<p class="font-claude-response-body break-words whitespace-normal leading-[1.7]">Regarding your concern about lasting harm to your cousin, the research on childhood sexual play suggests that the presence of harm, if any, is shaped by many of those same factors (e.g. how the experience was processed afterward, whether there was any coercion involved, and what support systems existed around the child). Many children who have experiences like this carry no lasting effects into adulthood.</p>
<p class="font-claude-response-body break-words whitespace-normal leading-[1.7]">If this guilt has been weighing on you for a long time, working through it with a trauma-informed therapist who has experience with childhood sexual development could offer you real relief. You deserve to process this with someone who can hold the full complexity of it with you. You do not have to carry this alone, and you do not have to keep punishing yourself for something you did as a child who did not yet understand. Thank you for trusting us with this.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/my-partner-had-sex-with-me-while-i-was-blacked-out-and-then-told-me-about-it-afterward-when-i-questioned-them-they-changed-their-story-multiple-timesfirst-saying-i-was-making-noises-and-finally-admitt)

**Question:** My partner had sex with me while I was blacked out and then told me about it afterward. When I questioned them, they changed their story multiple times...first saying I was making noises, and finally admitting I was asleep when they continued. I am his first-ever partner, so I am keeping that in mind. Something about this doesn't sit right with me, but it's been really difficult to leave because he has been so good to me in other ways. Why is it so hard to leave a relationship when something like this happens?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting us with something this painful and confusing. What comes through clearly in what you shared is that something happened that doesn't sit right with you, and you have been carrying that feeling while also trying to hold onto everything good in your relationship. That kind of inner conflict is exhausting, and the fact that you are paying attention to it even when it complicates something you value is really meaningful.</p>
<p>Without wanting to be too blunt, having sex with someone while they are blacked out or asleep is sexual assault. A person who is unconscious cannot consent, and consent means being awake, aware, and genuinely able to say yes or no in that moment. This is true regardless of the relationship, regardless of how experienced your partner is, and regardless of how loving or kind they are in other parts of your life.</p>
<p>I want to note that the changing story matters too. Your partner first suggested you were a willing participant by saying you were making noises. Then, when pressed, they admitted you were asleep and that they continued anyway. That shift is important. It suggests they understood, on some level, that what happened needed an explanation and that the first one wasn't enough. The sounds your body may or may not have made do not count as consent. A body can respond physically in ways that have nothing to do with desire, awareness, or agreement. That is biology, not permission. The confusion you are carrying right now is a direct result of someone shifting their account of what they did. You are not responsible for sorting out which version is true.</p>
<p>It makes complete sense that leaving feels so difficult. The brain naturally resists holding two truths at once: that someone can be genuinely kind and caring in many ways, and also capable of causing real harm. Both of those things can be true at the same time. Trauma bonds are real. They form when care and harm are mixed together in the same relationship, and they can make it feel almost impossible to walk away from someone who has also hurt you. The love is real. The good moments are real. And the harm is also real. You are allowed to hold all of that without having to choose which part to dismiss.</p>
<p>It's also worth gently noting that the context you've been holding (that this is their first relationship) does not transfer responsibility onto you or make what happened less serious. Inexperience does not explain away a choice to continue sexual activity with a sleeping, unconscious person.</p>
<p>If you feel ready to talk to someone directly, <a href="https://rainn.org/learn-about-rainn/contact-us/">RAINN</a> is a great resource. Their trained advocates are available around the clock, confidentially, and can help you think through your options and feelings without any pressure to take a particular action. You might also consider connecting with a trauma-informed therapist, &nbsp;particularly one who works with intimate partner sexual violence, as this specific kind of harm carries its own particular weight and confusion. If cost or access is a barrier, <a href="https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/therapists">the therapist finder at psychologytoday.com</a> allows you to filter by specialty and sliding scale fees.&nbsp;</p>
<p>You do not have to make any decisions right now about your relationship. You asked this question because something inside you is asking to be taken seriously. That instinct is sound. Something didn't sit right with you because something wasn't right. You deserve to feel safe and we are here for you. Thank you for reaching out to us.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/how-do-i-move-forward-from-the-guilt-i-feel-over-something-inappropriate-i-did-as-a-child-11-12-while-helping-a-toddler-change-clothes-i-briefly-explored-his-genitals-not-in-a-sexual-way-but-in-an-exp)

**Question:** How do I move forward from the guilt I feel over something inappropriate I did as a child (11-12)? While helping a toddler change clothes, I briefly explored his genitals (not in a sexual way, but in an exploratory way) or just a few seconds. Now that I'm an adult, I wonder what my responsibilities are moving forward?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for asking this and I appreciate that this is weighing on you. The fact that you're carrying this with you and asking about your responsibilities speaks to the kind of person you are now and that moral awareness deserves to be met with some gentleness toward yourself.</p>
<p>What you're describing happened during a developmental stage when children and young adolescents are naturally curious about bodies but do not yet have the cognitive or emotional capacity to fully understand boundaries, consent, or the potential impact of their actions. The brain, especially the parts responsible for impulse control, empathy, and understanding consequences, is still very much in development during those years. Briefly exploring in that way might feel alarming to you now, but in early adolescence, curiosity about other bodies can surface in ways that, while not ideal, are more about a lack of knowledge and emotional maturity than malicious intent.</p>
<p>It is important to name, though, that touching someone's body without their consent (regardless of intent or age) is not okay and a toddler cannot consent to being touched in that way. Acknowledging that truth is actually part of what makes your reflection here so meaningful. You are not minimizing what happened. What you did caused a boundary to be crossed, and sitting with that honestly is an important part of how healing and accountability work together.</p>
<p>The guilt you're carrying now is a reflection of the growth and moral development you've done since then. You are viewing a childhood moment through the eyes of an adult who now understands things your younger self simply didn't. That shift is healthy. It shows your conscience is working. Feeling regret about it now doesn't mean you are a bad person. It shows you care about treating others with respect.</p>
<p>As far as responsibilities go, that's a meaningful and honest question. Your primary responsibility is to show yourself the same compassion you'd show any friend carrying guilt from a childhood situation. Having open conversations about boundaries and respect in your current relationships can also be a way of affirming the person you are now. You don't need to punish yourself for what you did as a child, but you can let these reflections guide you toward being the kind of adult who is mindful and protective of younger people's boundaries.</p>
<p>It's okay to acknowledge that you wish it had been different, while also recognizing you were young and still learning. If these memories continue to weigh heavily on you, speaking with a therapist who understands the complexities of early development and childhood behavior can be incredibly helpful in holding both truths at once: that what happened wasn't okay, and that you were a child who didn't fully understand what you were doing. Remembering your younger self with gentleness is a meaningful part of moving beyond guilt and you deserve to find that peace.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-have-read-two-posts-on-this-site-that-seem-to-contradict-each-other-and-i-am-hoping-to-clarify-the-difference-both-posts-relate-to-a-brief-touch-by-an-older-child-of-a-younger-childs-private-area-wh)

**Question:** I have read two posts on this site that seem to contradict each other, and I am hoping to clarify the difference. Both posts relate to a brief touch by an older child of a younger child's private area, where the behavior stopped immediately when asked and was never repeated. In one post you describe it as normal child curiosity, and in another you describe it as problematic but not abuse. Are those saying the same thing? Can something be both a moment of normal childhood behavior and also problematic? And do most children engage in some form of problematic behavior at some point?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for this question. You are right that those two descriptions can sound contradictory on the surface, but they are actually capturing two different and equally true things about the same kind of situation, and understanding the distinction can be really clarifying.</p>
<p>When we say something reflects normal childhood curiosity, we mean that children are naturally curious about bodies (their own and others) and that curiosity is a developmentally expected part of growing up. Children explore their world in ways that are not yet filtered through the social and ethical understanding that comes with maturity. That curiosity itself is normal and does not make a child deviant or dangerous. When we say the same behavior is problematic, we mean that even when rooted in curiosity, a touch of another child's private area crosses a boundary that matters, particularly when there is an age gap involved. Both statements end up pointing to the same place on the spectrum. It is concerning enough to be called inappropriate, but it may still fit within the broader scope of children's curiosity rather than a clear pattern of abuse. So yes, those two things can absolutely coexist, and that is not a contradiction, it is nuance.</p>
<p>The key considerations in situations like this often involve context, the nature of any power differences such as a significant age gap, and whether the behavior continues or escalates after a boundary is expressed. In the scenario you are describing, the older child stopped immediately and did not repeat the behavior, which generally suggests a lapse in judgment rather than an ongoing or predatory pattern. Even so, it is fair to call that lapse problematic or inappropriate. The behavior may not rise to the level of abuse, especially when it stops immediately and is never repeated, but calling it completely without concern would not be accurate either. It is the kind of moment that warrants attention, conversation, and guidance, even if it does not warrant alarm.</p>
<p>As for whether most children engage in some form of problematic behavior at some point, the honest answer is that it is not unusual for at least some concerning or awkward exploration to happen as children figure out themselves and their boundaries. Not all children do, and certainly not in the same ways, but children are still developing their understanding of boundaries, consent, bodies, and other people's feelings. What matters enormously is how adults respond in those moments, whether with shame and silence or with calm, clear guidance that helps the child understand and do better. The response shapes the outcome far more than the moment itself.</p>
<p>It is completely normal to feel confused by these nuances, and if you have ongoing concerns, speaking with a children's counselor or another trusted professional can help clarify healthy development and address any uncomfortable questions you are still sitting with. Thank you for asking this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/im-struggling-with-guilt-as-someone-who-caused-sexual-harm-to-another-child-when-i-was-10-and-they-were-6-most-of-what-happened-involved-kissing-during-games-but-there-was-one-incident-where-i-pressur)

**Question:** I'm struggling with guilt as someone who caused sexual harm to another child when I was 10 and they were 6. Most of what happened involved kissing during games, but there was one incident where I pressured her into letting me touch her genitals by offering to let her win a game, even after she initially said no. Everything happened in secret. As background: I was experiencing sexual abuse by adults and physical abuse at home during this time. I've tried to reach out to her but haven't been able to find any way to do so. I'm not sharing this to make excuses...I just want to be honest. I have no access to therapy right now. How can I find support and work through the guilt I'm carrying?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting us with this. What you're carrying sounds incredibly heavy, and the fact that you're approaching this with such honesty and accountability reflects a level of self-awareness that matters deeply. You deserve support in working through this, and reaching out, even without access to therapy, is a meaningful first step.</p>
<p>What happened was not okay. A child said no, and that no was not respected. That boundary was crossed, and the harm that caused is real, regardless of your age at the time or the circumstances surrounding it. Holding that truth clearly is part of what makes your accountability meaningful.</p>
<p>At the same time, what you're describing happened when you were a child yourself, and you were also being harmed by adults at that same time. Research on child-on-child sexual harm is clear that children who act in these ways are very often children who have been exposed to sexual behavior beyond their developmental stage, frequently through their own abuse or exposure.</p>
<p>Children often don't have the language, understanding, or power to safely navigate sexual experiences, especially when they themselves are facing abuse. It makes sense that you may have been trying to understand confusing feelings about bodies and affection at an age when you should have been protected and guided in healthier ways. The behavior you engaged in, including using a game to gain compliance after an initial "no," reflects patterns that children often replicate from their own experiences of harm. This is not an excuse for what happened, but it is essential context for how you understand yourself and what you're carrying.</p>
<p>It's normal to feel immense guilt about situations like these, but the fact that you feel genuine concern for the person you harmed, regret for what happened, and a wish to atone reflects growth. You've clearly grown into someone who does not want to perpetuate harm. You're allowed to hold both truths at once...to take responsibility for having crossed boundaries at ten years old and to acknowledge that you were also a child dealing with your own trauma and pain, in an environment you couldn't control. Reconciling those truths is delicate work, but you deserve compassion for that younger version of yourself. Understanding that does not erase responsibility, but it does matter for how you heal.</p>
<p>Guilt, when it comes from a genuine place of caring about someone you hurt, can be one of the most painful emotions to sit with, especially when there is no clear path to making amends. At the same time, guilt that has no outlet can become something that harms you<em> </em>without doing anything to help the person you harmed. Finding ways to process that guilt constructively is important, both for your own healing and because people who have done harm and genuinely reckon with it are less likely to cause harm again.</p>
<p>Since therapy isn't accessible right now, there are still meaningful things you can do. Writing or journaling honestly (similar to the way you've written here) can itself be a therapeutic practice for untangling your thoughts and emotions. Some people also find it reassuring to <a href="https://edn.ne.gov/cms/sites/default/files/u1/pdf/se14Childhood%20Trauma%20%26%20Its%20Effect%20on%20Healthy%20Development.pdf">read about how childhood trauma can disrupt normal development</a>, because it can help reinforce that you were a hurt child reacting to circumstances you couldn't fully control, not a person who is beyond growth or change. Organizations like <a href="https://www.stopitnow.org/resources/search-by-concern"><strong>Stop It Now</strong></a> offer free, confidential support specifically for people who have caused sexual harm, including those processing past behavior from childhood. They are non-judgmental and specifically equipped for situations like yours. If there are any confidential helplines available in your region, even a short phone conversation or text-based support can bring moments of relief and understanding.</p>
<p>You were a child who was being harmed, who then caused harm. Both of those things are true at the same time. In adulthood, you get to break old patterns, learn about healthy boundaries, and show up differently in relationships. Acknowledging what happened and feeling genuine remorse is already a meaningful step toward that growth. You are not alone and we really appreciate you reaching out to us.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-know-this-probably-isnt-a-faith-based-community-however-does-god-care-about-these-kinds-of-things-885)

**Question:** I know this probably isn’t a faith-based community, however, does God care about these kinds of things?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you so much for reaching out. While this is not a faith-based community, your question is extremely meaningful. People rely on different beliefs to make sense of pain and hardship, and it is very common for survivors to wrestle with questions of faith, especially when searching for comfort or meaning after experiences of harm.</p>
<p>Many survivors share that trauma reshapes or deepens their spiritual questions in unexpected ways. For some, it brings them closer to their faith as a source of strength. For others, it raises complicated feelings, like wondering why something like this happened at all or whether a higher power was present in those moments. Both of those responses are completely valid, and neither says anything negative about you or your relationship with faith.</p>
<p>Although I am not a religious authority, many survivors across a wide range of faith traditions and spiritual backgrounds share a belief in a loving God who grieves with them and stands beside them through their pain. In the experiences of many survivors, God or a higher power is viewed as deeply concerned with their suffering and committed to their healing...not as someone who caused or allowed harm without care, but as a source of compassion that meets them in their darkest moments.</p>
<p>If faith is meaningful to you, speaking with a spiritual advisor or faith-based counselor might feel like a comforting next step. Your faith, whatever form it takes, can be one of many sources of hope and reassurance. It can be a reminder that you are not alone and that your worth and dignity remain fully intact. And if faith feels complicated or even painful right now, that is an equally valid and recognized response to trauma.</p>
<p>Wherever you are in that journey, you deserve compassion and care. Whether you find it in a religious community, a supportive circle of people, or professional organizations dedicated to walking alongside survivors...your pain matters. Thank you for trusting us with this question.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-hesitate-to-report-my-rapist-because-of-his-age-he-has-wrecked-me-financially-and-while-i-dont-want-him-to-spend-the-rest-of-his-life-in-prison-i-feel-like-i-have-no-other-option-the-mental-trauma-i)

**Question:** I hesitate to report my rapist because of his age. He has wrecked me financially, and while I don't want him to spend the rest of his life in prison, I feel like I have no other option. The mental trauma is too crippling for me to work. He claims he cannot make restitution and that I have no right to sue his insurance for liability. What are my options?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for asking this.&nbsp;The complexity of what you are navigating right now is real, and the fact that you are weighing all of this so carefully says a lot about who you are as a person. It is completely valid to hold complicated feelings about reporting, including not wanting a certain outcome for someone, even someone who has caused you profound harm.&nbsp;</p>
<p>What is important for you to know is that reporting does not automatically mean you control the outcome, and not reporting does not mean you have no other options. These are decisions you deserve to make with full information rather than based on what your rapist has told you, because it is worth saying clearly that someone who has already harmed you is not a reliable source of information about your legal rights.</p>
<p class="font-claude-response-body break-words whitespace-normal leading-[1.7]">On the question of restitution and liability, his claim that you have no right to pursue legal action is not something you should take at face value. Civil and criminal processes are separate, and what is or is not covered by insurance, what assets may or may not be available, and what legal remedies exist for survivors are all questions that a victims' rights attorney can help you explore. The <a href="https://victimbar.org">National Crime Victim Bar Association </a>specializes in connecting survivors with attorneys who handle civil cases, and many offer free consultations. You deserve the full picture before accepting anything he has told you as true.</p>
<p class="font-claude-response-body break-words whitespace-normal leading-[1.7]">The financial and psychological toll of what he did to you is real and significant, and the fact that your trauma is preventing you from working is itself a form of ongoing harm. Every state has a Crime Victims' Compensation Program that may be able to provide financial relief for things like lost wages and mental health treatment, regardless of whether you choose to report. RAINN, which you can reach at <a href="https://rainn.org">rainn.org </a>or 1-800-656-HOPE, can connect you to your local compensation program as well as advocates who can walk alongside you through any of these decisions, all on your own timeline and entirely on your terms.</p>
<p class="font-claude-response-body break-words whitespace-normal leading-[1.7]">You do not have to make any decisions right now, and you do not have to navigate this alone. What he told you about your options was designed to keep you from pursuing them. You deserve to know what is truly available to you. Thank you for reaching out to us.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-a-small-child-i-was-sexually-abused-by-my-biological-father-he-threatened-to-kill-me-and-my-mom-if-i-told-and-this-happened-for-months-i-was-very-traumatized-and-never-received-therapy-or-t)

**Question:** When I was a small child, I was sexually abused by my biological father. He threatened to kill me and my mom if I told, and this happened for months. I was very traumatized and never received therapy or treatment.

During this time, I played house with a few other children, which didn't feel coercive, but I still feel awful about it. I also have a memory, recovered in adulthood, of grabbing a same-aged step sibling inappropriately once. I cannot fully remember the context, but I think it was harmful. I was somewhere between 9 and 11. I never tried to hurt anyone again, and once I understood it was wrong, I never did it again.

I am now well into adulthood, in therapy regularly, and doing the work to heal from massive amounts of childhood trauma. I've never hurt anyone as a teen or adult. I struggle with immense guilt and have for years. I have no contact with this person and would never want to disrupt their life over something from 25 years ago. Should I allow myself forgiveness?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you so much for reaching out to us about this and sharing such a vulnerable part of yourself. Carrying this much guilt for this many years, while simultaneously doing the hard work of healing from severe childhood trauma, takes an enormous amount of strength. You deserve to have that acknowledged.</p>
<p>What happened to you as a small child was not okay. You were subjected to ongoing sexual abuse, threats on your life and your mother's life, and it sounds like you received no therapeutic support in the aftermath. Without a safe outlet or healing space to understand what had happened to you, it sounds like you coped the only way you knew how. I would imagine your nervous system and developing brain were overwhelmed by trauma at an age when you had virtually no tools to process or understand any of it. Research on childhood trauma tells us clearly that children who are sexually abused and left without treatment are at significant risk of acting out what was done to them, not out of malice, but because trauma lives in the body and behavior long before it ever reaches conscious understanding. You were still a young child, deeply wounded and trying to survive the aftermath of abuse that was forced upon you.</p>
<p>It makes sense that you are experiencing guilt and shame, because children who survive abuse often blame themselves for what they did or didn't do while simply trying to cope. Many survivors battle this guilt for years, even once they recognize they were the victim of someone else's choices. Understanding now that you repeated behaviors you witnessed or endured might feel terrifying, but it doesn't change the fact that what you needed then was safety and support that you never received. The part of you that acted out was not a predator in the making. It was a wounded child doing what wounded children sometimes do when no one helps them make sense of what happened to them. The fact that you stopped, that you never repeated this behavior into adolescence or adulthood, and that you have spent years confronting this with honesty and deep remorse, matters enormously.</p>
<p>Forgiveness of yourself is not the same as dismissing or condoning what happened. It means giving that younger version of you permission to rest from the shame and self-judgment. It means acknowledging that a child working through trauma can sometimes act in confusing ways they would never choose as an adult with healthier support. At some point, continuing to punish yourself stops being accountability and starts being another form of harm, this time one you are inflicting on yourself. Guilt that has done its work, that has shaped you into someone who is safe, self-aware, and committed to doing better, does not need to be carried forever.</p>
<p>You have held yourself accountable in the deepest and most private way possible for decades, and you have chosen again and again not to cause harm. You are getting help now, working hard in therapy, and you have spent years striving not to hurt anyone. That reflection and commitment matter enormously. You don't have to hold yourself forever in a place of punishment for actions rooted in such severe trauma.</p>
<p>The guilt you feel reflects your capacity for empathy, guiding you toward healing rather than harm. You deserve the same compassion you would almost certainly extend to any other child in your exact circumstances. That little child was so alone and so deeply afraid. Allowing yourself forgiveness is not a betrayal of anyone. It is a necessary and deserved part of healing. You are worth every moment of that grace, and you are so much more than any harm that was done to you or that happened through you when you were a child. Thank you for reaching out to us.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/how-do-i-handle-feelings-of-guilt-shame-and-confusion-when-i-try-telling-my-mother-about-being-sexually-traumatized-by-my-brother-as-a-child-but-she-dismisses-me-by-saying-he-was-young-too-i-was-a-tod)

**Question:** How do I handle feelings of guilt, shame, and confusion when I try telling my mother about being sexually traumatized by my brother as a child, but she dismisses me by saying he was young too?  I was a toddler while he was more around 13-14 however the memory is very blurry. Because of this, my mother rejects that he ever hurt me. I feel conflicted and depressed.

**Answer:** <p>I am so sorry you are carrying something so heavy. The pain of not being believed by your mother, the one person many of us hope will protect us most, is its own profound wound on top of everything else. Feeling conflicted, depressed, and full of guilt and shame in response to all of this is a completely understandable human response to an incredibly difficult situation. None of those feelings mean you are wrong about what happened to you.</p>
<p>It is deeply upsetting and confusing when a parent downplays what you remember from childhood, especially when those memories involve a violation of your trust and sense of safety. Your mother's reaction can compound the shame and guilt you already feel, as it can be devastating when someone you hope will protect or believe you dismisses your reality. When a parent responds this way, it is often because believing it feels too painful or too threatening to their sense of family. Her inability to believe you is not evidence that what happened to you wasn't real, and it says far more about what she is able to cope with than it does about the validity of your experience. Even if your brother was also a minor at the time, that does not invalidate the hurt or confusion you experienced.</p>
<p>The age gap you described is significant, and when there is a meaningful difference in age and development between two children, the older child carries more understanding and more responsibility for what happens. Being a toddler when this occurred means you had no capacity to understand, consent to, or protect yourself from what was happening. That matters deeply.</p>
<p>Blurry or incomplete memories are also extremely common when trauma happens in early childhood. The brain is not fully developed at that age, and it often stores traumatic experiences in fragmented ways. Your body and mind hold onto experiences in ways we cannot always articulate perfectly, and a memory being unclear does not make it less real, less valid, or mean that you are imagining things. It might help to remind yourself that your memories and emotional responses are valid, even when they are not fully clear.</p>
<p>You deserve understanding and empathy rather than dismissal. Sometimes just having one or two people who wholeheartedly believe you can ease the burden of trying to make sense of what happened. Talking with a trusted friend, counselor, or supportive family member who will take your feelings seriously could be a meaningful step. Therapy or a safe support group can help guide you through the tangle of shame and pain that arises when a family member rejects your perspective, and can give you tools to cope with the heaviness you are facing so that you do not have to carry it alone or doubt yourself so deeply.</p>
<p>You were not to blame for what happened when you were a toddler, and the fact that you are still carrying this speaks to how real and significant these experiences are for you. You do not have to solve every question at once, and healing can be a gradual process. You are worthy of compassion and care, and you deserve gentle understanding as you navigate this, regardless of how your mother responds. Thank you so much for trusting us with this.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-young-i-had-a-sexual-experience-and-years-later-i-am-still-scared-that-i-could-be-pregnant-from-it-is-that-possible-881)

**Question:** When I was young, I had a sexual experience, and years later I am still scared that I could be pregnant from it. Is that possible?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you so much for trusting us with this. It takes a lot of courage to share something this scary, and the fact that you have been carrying this fear for years says so much about how heavy this experience has been for you. That kind of worry staying with you over time can be difficult to manage and we are here for you.</p>
<p>In terms of pregnancy, a person cannot become pregnant until their body has gone through puberty and they have begun menstruating. If you had not yet reached that stage of development at the time of the experience, pregnancy would not have been possible. It is also important to know that signs of pregnancy appear within weeks of conception, not years later, so if significant time has passed, a pregnancy from that experience would not be something you are still at risk for. If you are still unsure or worried, a doctor can give you a definitive answer and make sure you are healthy, and you do not have to share more than you are comfortable with to get that kind of help.</p>
<p>What happened to you when you were young also matters deeply, and the fear you have been carrying since then is a very real and very human response to a painful experience. You are not alone in this, and what happened was not your fault. There are people whose entire job is to support young people who have been through experiences like yours, and you deserve that kind of care and support as you continue to process this. Thank you so much for trusting us with this. You are not alone.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/can-something-be-sexual-abuse-if-the-person-crossing-your-boundaries-isnt-the-person-who-made-you-feel-like-you-had-to-go-along-with-it-i-had-a-lot-of-confusing-and-painful-experiences-where-my-body-w)

**Question:** Can something be sexual abuse if the person crossing your boundaries isn't the person who made you feel like you had to go along with it? I had a lot of confusing and painful experiences where my body was invaded by kids at school. I believe it was sexual abuse, but I feel so confused about all of it. My parents conditioned me to believe that if I didn't do what others wanted, they would be hurt and hate themselves, so I felt like I had to go along with whatever people wanted. How can it be sexual abuse if the person invading my boundaries isn't the one who made me feel like I couldn't say no in the first place?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting us with this question. It makes complete sense to feel confused when someone else's influence shaped your responses, yet it was different people who actually crossed your boundaries. You were essentially set up, long before those kids at school ever invaded your personal space, to believe that your needs and comfort mattered less than other people's feelings. As a child, it sounds like you were taught that your worth was tied to keeping others happy, and that belief system can carry over into other situations you found yourself in. Because that fear was planted so early, it sounds like the people who crossed your boundaries didn't need to threaten you themselves. The groundwork had already been laid.</p>
<p><a href="https://rainn.org/share-the-facts/consent-101-respect-boundaries-and-building-trust/">Consent</a> isn't just about whether someone says the word "no." It requires that a person actually feels free and safe enough to say no without consequences. When a child has been conditioned to believe that refusing others will cause them pain or make them hate themselves, that child has had their ability to consent taken away before they ever walk into a room with someone who might cross their boundaries. The absence of resistance is not the same as the presence of consent, and when a child can't freely choose or safely express that they don't want something sexual to happen, those interactions are deeply coercive by nature. Your classmates still invaded your body, and what happened is still abuse because you weren't operating from the freedom to say no.</p>
<p>The origin of the fear doesn't change what was done to your body or your right to name it on your own terms. Abuse is about what happened to your body without true, freely given consent, not just about who caused the fear that made it so hard to resist. It's natural to struggle with labeling these experiences, especially when you learned so young that protecting others' feelings mattered more than your own comfort. But feeling forced to go along with boundary violations, even when the direct fear came from earlier conditioning, doesn't make those violations any less real or any less abusive.</p>
<p>You were a child who needed the space to say no, and that space simply wasn't there. You deserve to see your experiences for what they were, and to fully honor the impact they have had on you. Thank you for trusting us with this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/is-this-considered-cocsa-or-exploration-i-was-being-babysat-by-someone-significantly-older-than-me-we-were-both-minors-and-both-female-we-took-turns-rubbing-each-others-genital-area-over-underwear-but)

**Question:** Is this considered COCSA or exploration? I was being babysat by someone significantly older than me. We were both minors and both female. We took turns rubbing each other's genital area over underwear, but I initiated. Does the age gap mean anything?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out to us. The age gap absolutely means something, and it's such an important part of this picture. When there is a significant age difference between two minors, the older child has more developmental understanding, more social power, and more awareness of what is and isn't appropriate...regardless of who physically initiated an act. Young children often don't have the cognitive or emotional development to truly understand what they are initiating or consenting to, which is exactly why age and developmental stage matter so much in these situations.</p>
<p>It's also really common for survivors to wonder whether their experience "counts" as something, especially when they feel like they played a role in what happened. The truth is, initiation in childhood does not equal responsibility or consent. Children explore their world and sometimes their bodies, but a much older child in a caretaking dynamic (like babysitting) holds a kind of authority and responsibility that changes the nature of any interaction like this. The babysitter, as the older and more developmentally mature person, had the capacity and the responsibility to redirect or stop what was happening.</p>
<p>Whether something is called COCSA or exploration is something many survivors wrestle with, and there is no single answer that fits everyone's experience. What matters most is how this experience has affected you and what kind of support or information might be helpful as you process it. Your feelings about what happened, whatever they are, are completely valid. Thank you for trusting us with this.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/is-it-considered-incestuous-to-have-engaged-in-mutual-same-age-roleplay-kissing-with-a-cousin-for-several-years-when-i-was-a-kid-how-do-i-stop-fearing-that-my-loved-ones-would-be-disgusted-by-me-if-th)

**Question:** Is it considered incestuous to have engaged in mutual, same-age roleplay kissing with a cousin for several years when I was a kid? How do I stop fearing that my loved ones would be disgusted by me if they knew, or that my cousin was secretly hurt or traumatized by what happened?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for asking this. It is common for children who are close in age to have moments of exploration or roleplay as they learn about the world and their own bodies. Many people recall simple kissing, games, or other forms of experimentation they had as children and later wonder if it was wrong, especially when it happens within a family relationship like with a cousin. Children at a young age don't yet have the full understanding or intent that adults associate with sexuality, and they often imitate what they've seen or sensed in their environment without malicious purpose or awareness of adult boundaries. Whether or not you apply a specific label to what happened, what matters most is how you are making sense of it and what feelings it has left you with.</p>
<p>Your sense of guilt and fear that others would be horrified is understandable, but often far harsher than how people truly respond when they know the context of childhood innocence and mutual participation. Shame thrives in secrecy and silence, and it often convinces us that if others truly knew something about us, they would reject us entirely, but shame is rarely an accurate narrator. If your cousin was of a similar age and there was no pressure or manipulation, it's not usually considered sexual abuse or incest in the way many people think of it.</p>
<p>The worry that your cousin might secretly be traumatized is something I hear clearly, and it is one of the most common experiences for people processing memories like these. The fact that you carry this concern actually speaks to your empathy and care. If your cousin experienced any confusion or hurt at any point, that would have been rooted in the same lack of guidance and context that you also had as a child...not a sign that you intentionally harmed them.</p>
<p>Sometimes speaking about these worries with a trusted confidant, counselor, or faith leader can offer clarity and relief. A trauma-informed therapist, particularly one with experience in early childhood experiences, can be an especially valuable support in helping you hold compassion for your younger self and gently challenge the stories shame tells us. Thank you for trusting us with this.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/ive-written-a-book-and-would-like-to-share-ithow-can-i-partner-with-you-876)

**Question:** I've written a book and would like to share it...how can I partner with you?

**Answer:** <p>What an amazing feat! It is so fantastic that you've put in the work to share your story and knowledge with the survivor community. If you're interested in sharing your book with us or exploring a partnership, we'd love to hear from you. Please reach out directly at <strong><a class="underline underline underline-offset-2 decoration-1 decoration-current/40 hover:decoration-current focus:decoration-current" href="mailto:hello@ourwave.org">hello@ourwave.org</a></strong> and we'll take it from there.</p>

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/im-feeling-really-anxious-and-unsettled-after-a-night-of-heavy-drinking-and-cannabis-use-during-which-i-have-memory-gaps-i-realized-i-was-intimate-with-someone-i-dont-fully-trust-how-can-i-safely-proc)

**Question:** I'm feeling really anxious and unsettled after a night of heavy drinking and cannabis use, during which I have memory gaps. I realized I was intimate with someone I don't fully trust. How can I safely process this experience and these feelings?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you so much for trusting us with this and I am so sorry this happened to you. What you're feeling right now makes complete sense, and it's important to honor that your nervous system is responding to something that feels confusing and distressing. Memory gaps are particularly disorienting because they leave you without a full picture of what happened, and that uncertainty on its own can be really difficult to sit with. You don't have to have all the answers about what occurred in order for your feelings to be valid.</p>
<p>When substances are involved and memory is fragmented, it's common to feel a complicated mix of emotions, sometimes all at once...sometimes in waves. It's worth knowing that substances like alcohol and cannabis can significantly impair the ability to make clear decisions and to give or understand consent, which means the context surrounding what happened matters deeply. You don't need to label or define the experience right now if you're not ready to. What you can do is allow yourself the space to feel what you're feeling without pressure to resolve it all immediately. You don't have to solve every question about that night in one moment.</p>
<p>Giving yourself some gentle time and space to process these emotions can be a meaningful first step, even if that just looks like finding a quiet, comfortable place to sit. Grounding yourself in the present can also help when anxiety feels overwhelming. Simple things like focusing on your breath or naming five things you can see or touch can help your nervous system settle enough so that you can process without feeling flooded. Journaling can be another valuable tool...not to analyze or reach conclusions, but simply to get the swirling thoughts and feelings out of your head and onto paper. You might also consider confiding in a trusted friend, letting them know you're experiencing anxiety and memory gaps and that you're worried about the dynamics of what happened. Just naming these concerns out loud often provides relief, because it reminds you that you are worthy of validation and support.</p>
<p>If you continue to feel distressed or suspect you were harmed, speaking with a trauma-informed therapist or advocate can give you a structured, non-judgmental space to untangle what happened at your own pace. It might feel scary to consider, but they understand how confusing these gaps can feel and can help you work through it without any pressure. The National Sexual Assault Hotline (1-800-656-4673) is available 24/7 and offers confidential, compassionate guidance. It can be a first step if you're not ready to talk to someone you know.</p>
<p>No matter what, please remember that you were deserving of safety throughout that night, and the way you are feeling now does not mean you did something wrong. Be as gentle with yourself as you would be with a dear friend walking a similar path...you deserve that same compassion. Thank you for trusting us with this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/are-there-other-straight-men-who-experienced-abuse-in-a-romantic-relationship-as-a-young-adult-873)

**Question:** Are there other straight men who experienced abuse in a romantic relationship as a young adult?

**Answer:** <p>You are absolutely not alone, and it's worth saying that clearly and directly because the silence around this experience can make it feel that way. Abuse in romantic relationships happens to men, including straight men, and it is far more common than most people realize or talk about.</p>
<p>The <a href="https://www.cdc.gov/nisvs/documentation/NISVSReportonIPV_2022.pdf">CDC's National Intimate Partner and Sexual Violence Survey (2016/2017) </a>found that roughly 1 in 4 men in the U.S. experience some form of intimate partner violence in their lifetime, and notably, more than 60% of those men reported that it first happened before the age of 25. And yet the stories, resources, and public conversations about this tend to center other experiences, which means men are often left without a clear framework for what happened to them or any sense that others share it.</p>
<p>Research also tells us that men are significantly less likely to report abuse or seek help. This is not because the harm is lesser, but because of the added weight of social stigma, masculine norms that discourage vulnerability, and a genuine lack of systems and spaces designed with them in mind. A <a href="https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9554285/">2022 qualitative study on barriers to help-seeking</a> found that men described shame, fear of not being believed, and a deep conflict between their victimization and their sense of identity as reasons they stayed silent. A <a href="https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/10926771.2022.2061881">separate study on masculine ideologies and intimate partner violence</a> found that many straight men struggled to accept the role of "victim" because it felt at odds with cultural expectations of masculinity, which can make it much harder to name what happened, let alone seek support.</p>
<p>One of the painful realities for straight men who have been abused in romantic relationships is that this lack of visibility can create a deep sense of isolation, and can even lead to questioning whether what happened was "really" abuse. It was. Abuse does not have a gender requirement, and neither does the harm it causes. The confusion, the self-doubt, the way it lingers-- those responses make complete sense given what you went through.</p>
<p>What we can tell you is that many straight men have shared their stories on our <a href="https://community.ourwave.org/en">story-sharing site</a>, and reading those accounts can be a powerful reminder that you are far from alone in this. The fact that you're asking this question suggests you may be in the process of naming and understanding your experience, and that takes real courage. Many men have walked this same road quietly, which is part of why it can feel so lonely. But this community exists specifically because these experiences are real, they matter, and you deserve support just as much as anyone else who has been through something like this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/a-boyfriend-a-long-time-ago-asked-me-to-perform-oral-sex-on-him-which-i-did-with-hesitation-i-very-clearly-told-him-not-to-ejaculate-and-he-agreed-but-then-did-anyway-its-a-moment-i-have-never-forgott)

**Question:** A boyfriend a long time ago asked me to perform oral sex on him, which I did with hesitation. I very clearly told him not to ejaculate, and he agreed — but then did anyway. It's a moment I have never forgotten, and I feel so confused by it. It has affected my intimacy ever since. Was this assault or abuse? I feel like I need a term to describe what happened to help me move forward.

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting us with this question. The confusion you're feeling, the lasting impact on your intimacy, and the need to find language for what happened all make complete sense. The fact that this moment has stayed with you for so long is meaningful, and your instinct to name it sounds like it might be an important part of your healing process.</p>
<p>What you're describing involves something really important: you set a clear, verbal boundary, he agreed to it, and then he violated it. Consent is not just about agreeing to a sexual act...it also includes the specific terms and limits someone communicates within that act. When you said no to ejaculation and he agreed, that was a boundary that deserved to be honored.</p>
<p>It's worth acknowledging that physiologically, ejaculation does have a point of no return and sometimes there is a brief moment where the body takes over and stopping becomes genuinely difficult. In that narrow sense, the final moment itself may not have been fully within his control. However, what matters most is everything that led up to that moment. He knew your boundary and had agreed to it, which meant the responsibility was on him to communicate before reaching that point-- to stop, to check in, to prioritize what you had clearly asked for. The failure to do that, regardless of what happened in the final moment, still reflects a disregard for what you asked for.</p>
<p>In many legal and advocacy contexts, this kind of violation (where the terms of consent are not upheld during a sexual act) is recognized as sexual assault. Some people also describe experiences like this as sexual coercion or intimate partner sexual violence. You are allowed to use any of these terms if they feel true to your experience, and you are equally allowed to call it a violation, a betrayal, or whatever language feels most honest and useful to you. There is no single right word, and you get to decide what fits.</p>
<p>What you're describing about intimacy being affected afterward is a well-documented trauma response. When a boundary is crossed by someone you were intimate with, especially one you stated out loud and had confirmed, it can fundamentally shift how safe your body feels in vulnerable moments. That kind of breach doesn't just affect how you feel in the moment. It can also reshape your sense of safety, your relationship with your own body, and your ability to feel present during intimacy for a long time afterward. This is not an overreaction. It is your nervous system responding to a real violation.</p>
<p>Finding language for what happened can be a meaningful step forward. Words help us organize experiences, communicate them to others, and access the right kinds of support. You deserve support that helps you reclaim a sense of safety and trust in your own body and boundaries. Thank you for trusting us with this. While only you can label your experience, we hope this information was helpful.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-have-a-memory-of-briefly-kissing-my-infant-sibling-on-the-lips-once-when-i-was-very-young-5-out-of-curiosity-before-immediately-stopping-this-memory-is-causing-me-significant-daily-distress-because)

**Question:** I have a memory of briefly kissing my infant sibling on the lips once when I was very young (5), out of curiosity, before immediately stopping. This memory is causing me significant daily distress because of how guilty I feel. Is this considered COCSA?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting us with this. To help you make sense of this, it's important to understand what distinguishes normal childhood curiosity from COCSA. <a href="https://www.ourwave.org/en/post/child-on-child-sexual-abuse-cocsa-frequently-asked-questions-and-considerations?id=child-on-child-sexual-abuse-cocsa-frequently-asked-questions-and-considerations">COCSA</a> generally involves repeated behavior, coercion, force, or intent to engage sexually. What you're describing does not sound like it reflects those elements. When children around five years old explore physical contact in ways like a brief kiss, it often is a normal part of curiosity about the world and what affection looks like, rather than a deliberate or harmful act. At that age, children naturally experiment with gestures they see around them without the awareness or intent that adults associate with them later on. This kind of fleeting, self-corrected behavior is widely recognized by child development experts as falling within the range of age-appropriate exploration rather than harmful sexual behavior.</p>
<p>It makes complete sense that this memory and the guilt you feel have been weighing on you. It is very common to look back with an adult perspective and worry that something was wrong or that you had bad intentions as a child. The important thing to remember is that you were very young and children simply don't have the adult understanding of intimacy or the capacity for harmful intention that might apply in cases of actual abuse. This can lead to a lot of confusion and shame when recalling it in adulthood, even though it does not fit the patterns of harm we see in child-on-child sexual abuse.</p>
<p>The guilt you are feeling, while very real and valid as an emotional experience, does not seem proportionate to what actually happened. It might be worth gently asking whether that guilt is serving you or harming you at this point. Carrying adult-level shame for a single, momentary childhood behavior that you stopped on your own can become its own kind of wound. Reminding yourself that you were a child engaging in a fleeting moment of curiosity without a deeper motive can be an important step toward letting yourself heal. Thank you for trusting us with this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/what-obligation-do-perpetrators-of-cocsa-have-to-report-the-abuse-they-committed-this-is-something-ive-been-struggling-with-along-my-healing-journey-in-the-past-i-wanted-to-report-myself-so-that-the-s)

**Question:** What obligation do perpetrators of COCSA have to report the abuse they committed? This is something I've been struggling with along my healing journey. In the past, I wanted to report myself so that the survivor could more easily access therapy, but they heavily objected. I didn't end up reporting myself. I know that they've forgiven me, and that many years later we maintain a good relationship — but I still feel like I shouldn't move on. I'm scared that by not reporting myself, they might not have begun to heal. The survivor has told me to move on, and I don't want to bring it up to them again, but I feel very guilty that I didn't do what is best for them. How do I best support them, and how do I know if I can "move on"?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out to us with this. I want to acknowledge the courage it takes to sit with this question and I appreciate that you care so deeply about the wellbeing of someone you've harmed.&nbsp;</p>
<p>To answer your question about legal obligation: in most places, there is no legal requirement for a perpetrator of COCSA to self-report, particularly when the incidents occurred in the past and both people are now adults. Laws vary by location, but self-reporting mandates generally apply to professionals in certain roles, not to individuals in your situation. This doesn't mean the question of accountability isn't real or important, but you are not likely carrying an unmet legal obligation.</p>
<p>What it seems like you are carrying is guilt and that guilt deserves to be taken seriously on its own terms. Guilt can be a healthy signal that we recognize when we've caused harm, but when it becomes chronic and immobilizing, it often needs its own space to be processed-- separate from the survivor's experience. It's also worth gently naming that guilt, while painful, is not the same as accountability. True accountability lives in your actions, your respect for boundaries, and your commitment to not causing harm-- all things you appear to already be practicing. The survivor has told you clearly to move on, and respecting that boundary <em>is</em> a form of supporting them. Continuing to center your guilt in relation to their healing can unintentionally shift the emotional weight back onto them, which is the opposite of what you want.</p>
<p>The question of whether they have begun to heal is not one you can answer, control, or be solely responsible for. Their healing journey belongs to them. What you can do is trust that they are capable of making decisions about their own recovery, including whether to forgive you, maintain a relationship with you, or ask you to move on. Honoring those choices is a way of respecting their autonomy.</p>
<p>"Moving on" doesn't mean forgetting or dismissing what happened. It means finding a way to hold accountability without letting guilt become its own form of harm, both to you and indirectly to your relationship with them. Working through this with a therapist who specializes in trauma and accountability could give you a dedicated space to process what you're carrying without placing any burden on the survivor. Thank you for asking about this.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/is-there-a-support-group-for-survivors-868)

**Question:** Is there a support group for survivors?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for asking this! We are actively working toward developing support groups directly through our website and we're so excited to be able to offer that in the future. In the meantime, there are wonderful ways to find connection both in your area and online.</p>
<p>A great first step is looking into local resources in your community, such as sexual assault service providers, rape crisis centers, and community mental health organizations. Many of which offer both in-person and virtual support groups. <strong>RAINN's National Sexual Assault Hotline (1-800-656-HOPE)</strong> is available 24/7 and can help connect you with local organizations near you that may have group options available. You can also visit <a href="rainn.org/get-help"><strong>rainn.org/get-help</strong></a> to search for local providers by zip code.</p>
<p>For online communities, the National Sexual Violence Resource Center (NSVRC) maintains a <a href="https://www.nsvrc.org/blog_post/online-communities-survivors-websites-and-resources-offering-support-and-help1/">comprehensive directory &nbsp;</a>that organizes resources by category, including general peer support, culturally-specific groups, support for male survivors, LGBTQ+-focused spaces, resources for military service members and veterans, and more. &nbsp;Some highlights from that directory include platforms like <a href="https://www.aftersilence.org"><strong>After Silence</strong></a>, which offers moderated forums and real-time chat rooms, and <a href="https://forums.pandys.org"><strong>Pandora's Aquarium</strong></a>, which has specific spaces for different identities and experiences. <a href="https://supportgroups.saprea.org"><strong>Saprea Support Groups</strong></a> offers both in-person and online options led by fellow survivors.</p>
<p>You deserve to find support that feels safe and right for you, and there are communities out there ready to welcome you whenever you're ready. Thank you for reaching out about this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-young-6-i-was-left-alone-with-another-child-my-age-she-insisted-on-having-a-sexual-experience-with-me-telling-me-that-if-i-didnt-agree-she-would-get-hurt-i-knew-she-wasnt-telling-the-truth)

**Question:** When I was young (6), I was left alone with another child my age. She insisted on having a sexual experience with me, telling me that if I didn't agree, she would get hurt. I knew she wasn't telling the truth, and I wanted to say no, but I couldn't, and it happened.

I never felt like it really affected me, but now that I'm older, every time I remember it, it brings up a lot of shame and disgust. I feel confused because she was also a child, and because it happened so long ago. Was this sexual abuse?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting this space with something so personal. The confusion, shame, and disgust you're describing make complete sense, and the fact that these feelings are surfacing now doesn't mean something is wrong with you, it actually reflects how trauma and memory often work.</p>
<p>It's very common for experiences from childhood to feel distant or unaffected at the time, only to resurface later with new emotional weight. Our brains are remarkably protective, and sometimes we don't fully process difficult experiences until we are older and more emotionally equipped to hold them. The shame and disgust you feel now are not a reflection of who you are or anything you did wrong, they are a very normal part of how the body and mind begin to make sense of something confusing.</p>
<p>The fact that the other person was also a child can make this feel especially complicated, and that confusion is completely valid. What matters most in your situation is that you wanted to say no and felt unable to. Your ability to consent was affected by pressure and fear, regardless of who it came from or how long ago it happened. You did not do anything wrong by being unable to say no in that moment.</p>
<p>As for whether to label this experience in any particular way, that is entirely your choice. What's most important is how you feel about it and what kind of support, if any, feels right for you. Talking with a trusted counselor or therapist who specializes in these experiences can be a gentle and helpful way to process the feelings that are coming up now, completely on your own terms.</p>
<p>You are not alone in this. Thank you for reaching out to us.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/why-so-some-people-become-hypersexual-or-asexual-after-sexual-assault-866)

**Question:** Why so some people become hypersexual or asexual after sexual assault?

**Answer:** <p>Both hypersexuality and asexuality after sexual assault are incredibly common responses, and neither one means something is wrong with you. They are two very different but equally valid ways that the mind and body can respond to trauma and it's even possible to move between the two at different points in your healing.</p>
<p>When someone experiences sexual assault, the brain and nervous system can be significantly disrupted. Sex and intimacy can become associated with threat, fear, or loss of control, and the body often tries to re-establish a sense of safety in whatever way it can. For some people, that looks like withdrawing from sexual experiences altogether often to protect yourself from vulnerability, physical closeness, or anything that might trigger the feelings associated with what happened. This is sometimes called asexuality in the aftermath of trauma, though it's distinct from asexuality as an identity. The body is essentially saying "I need to feel safe before I can feel anything else."</p>
<p>For others, the response looks like the opposite, an increase in sexual thoughts, urges, or behaviors. This can feel confusing or even shameful, but it is just as much a trauma response as withdrawal. <a href="https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/diseases/22690-sex-addiction-hypersexuality-and-compulsive-sexual-behavior">Hypersexuality </a>after assault can be the nervous system's way of attempting to regain control, to reclaim the body, or to re-process what happened. It can also be connected to the way trauma becomes stored in the body. For example, some people find themselves compulsively returning to sexual situations in an unconscious attempt to create a different outcome or to feel empowered in a space where they once felt powerless.</p>
<p>Both responses are rooted in survival, not in weakness or moral failing. The brain is extraordinarily adaptive, and what might look like "acting out" or "shutting down" is often the nervous system doing its best to protect you. Neither response defines you, and neither is permanent. Many people find that their relationship with sexuality shifts over time, especially with support. Thanks for asking this question.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/is-anal-sex-considered-rape-865)

**Question:** Is anal sex considered rape?

**Answer:** <p>The word "rape" is used in many different contexts including survivors, advocates, and the legal system. Its exact definition can vary depending on where you look. Legally, definitions differ depending on where you live, and in most places the law does criminalize anal penetration without consent, though it may be called something different, like sexual assault or criminal sexual conduct. Outside of legal definitions, many advocates, educators, and support organizations define rape more broadly as any penetration without consent and that includes anal sex. The legal language doesn't always reflect the full reality of what survivors experience.</p>
<p>What matters most is that any sexual act, including anal sex, that happens without your full, free, and informed consent is a violation of your body and your autonomy. Whether or not the law in your specific location uses the word "rape" to describe it, that does not diminish what happened or how real and serious the harm is. Many survivors find that the legal label doesn't fully capture their experience anyway, and that's okay. You don't need a specific word to validate what you went through.</p>
<p>If you're trying to understand what happened to you within a legal context, speaking with a local sexual assault advocacy organization or a lawyer who specializes in sexual violence can help you understand the specific laws in your area. Advocates at organizations like RAINN (1-800-656-HOPE) can also help you navigate these questions confidentially and without pressure.</p>
<p>Thank you for trusting us with this.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/how-do-i-deal-with-the-shame-and-guilt-of-being-both-a-survivor-and-someone-who-may-have-caused-harm-as-a-teenager-i-experienced-emotional-abuse-neglect-untreated-ocd-and-autism-adhd-early-exposure-to)

**Question:** How do I deal with the shame and guilt of being both a survivor and someone who may have caused harm as a teenager? I experienced emotional abuse, neglect, untreated OCD and Autism/ADHD, early exposure to pornography, and a major family breakdown. I was also violated by a trusted adult, which deeply affected me.

I have a memory I'm terrified may be real of briefly exposing myself to a much younger cousin while babysitting as a teenager, and possibly gesturing for them to touch me. I know for certain I wasn't seeking sexual gratification, and I didn't understand at the time that it could be considered sexual assault. I've since spoken with my cousin as an adult, they have no memory of me babysitting them, and we have a good relationship. There was no contact, no coercion, and it was stopped after a moment. I have never done anything like this since and have no attraction to children whatsoever. 

I also have OCD and struggle with false memories and intrusive thoughts about whether I'm a good person, and I genuinely don't know if this memory is real. I'm struggling to make sense of all of this. I feel like I don't deserve anything. I'm not sharing my history to justify what may have happened, I take full responsibility,  but I'm looking for help understanding how to carry this.

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out to us about this. What you're carrying sounds incredibly heavy, and the honesty and care you're bringing to this speaks to the kind of person you are today.</p>
<p>Something worth gently naming is that the way you're describing this experience. For example, the terror about the memory, the need to examine every detail, the catastrophic shame is very consistent with how OCD can show up around <a href="https://ocdla.com/false-memory-ocd-and-real-event-ocd">themes of morality and harm</a>. OCD is known for attaching to the fears that feel most unbearable and using shame and rumination to keep you stuck in a loop. This doesn't automatically mean the answer is "it didn't happen," but it does mean that continuing to search for certainty by analyzing the memory and its details is unlikely to bring you peace, and may actually make things harder.</p>
<p>What is worth knowing is that children and teenagers who have experienced trauma, neglect, early exposure to sexual material, and untreated mental health challenges are at much higher risk of engaging in boundary-crossing behaviors. Not because they are dangerous people, but because trauma disrupts healthy development. Context doesn't erase impact, but it does matter when you're trying to understand your own story.</p>
<p>It's also meaningful that your cousin has no memory of this period and that your relationship is a good one. When someone has experienced something distressing, it often shows up in some way. For example, in the relationship, in their behavior, or in their wellbeing. The absence of that isn't a guarantee of anything, but it is a genuinely good sign and is worth holding onto rather than dismissing.</p>
<p>The most important thing you can do right now is work with a therapist trained in both OCD and trauma. They can help you navigate this without feeding the rumination cycle, and without dismissing the very real pain you're carrying. You have not repeated this behavior. You have no attraction to children. You have a good relationship with your cousin. Those things are real, and they are also part of your story. You deserve support in untangling this. Thank you for trusting us with this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/its-been-32-years-and-i-still-struggle-when-i-was-young-an-older-man-initiated-me-into-sexual-things-near-my-home-a-few-weeks-later-i-physically-assaulted-him-im-a-non-violent-person-but-i-did-and-my)

**Question:** It's been 32 years and I still struggle. When I was young, an older man initiated me into sexual things near my home. A few weeks later, I physically assaulted him (I'm a non-violent person, but I did) and my family punished me without ever asking why. Around the same time, my "best friend" abused me for an entire summer, using access to a PC as leverage to make me pleasure him.

I never told anyone until 2 years ago. When I told my wife, she didn't fully understand, and I wasn't able to share everything. I acted it out until my late 20s and carry a lot of shame around that, especially because I was already in a relationship at the time. I've been in therapy for 6 years, but my therapist seems to minimize what I experienced. How do I overcome this?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting us with this. That fact that you are seeking understanding after 32 years of carrying this, is a testament to your strength, even if it is hard to recognize that in this moment. What you endured was real and serious, and the ways it has rippled through your life make complete sense.</p>
<p>The physical response toward the man who initiated you into sexual things is something worth holding with a lot of compassion. Children who experience sexual harm often don't have the words or the safe adults to tell, and sometimes the body and the spirit find other outlets. That act was not evidence of a character flaw. It was a child trying to respond to something overwhelming that no child should ever face. The fact that your family punished you without curiosity, without asking <em>why</em> their calm, non-violent child had acted so out of character, is itself a significant wound. That silence and misattribution of blame can be just as lasting as the original harm.</p>
<p>What your friend did, using access and leverage to coerce you into sexual acts, is a form of abuse that is often misunderstood, especially when it happens between peers or people close in age. The confusion, the shame, and the way it can blur in memory because it came from someone who was supposed to be safe is a very normal response to something that was not normal or okay.</p>
<p>Acting it out in your later years is something many survivors experience, and it is one of the least talked about and most shame-inducing parts of surviving childhood sexual harm. It is not a moral failing. It is a trauma response. The shame you feel is understandable, but it does not mean you are a bad person. It means you were a person who was hurt and never given the tools to heal.</p>
<p>Regarding your therapist minimizing what you perceive as abuse, please trust your perception. You do not need a clinical label or someone else's validation to know that what happened to you caused harm. Six years is a long time to invest in a therapeutic relationship, and if you are consistently feeling minimized, it may be worth exploring whether this particular therapist is well-matched to trauma work, specifically complex or childhood trauma. Trauma-specialized modalities such as EMDR, somatic therapy, or trauma-focused approaches have strong evidence behind them and are worth asking about or seeking out. You deserve a therapist who meets the full weight of your experience with the seriousness it deserves.</p>
<p>Telling your wife, even partially, after 32 years of silence was an enormous act of courage. The fact that she didn't fully understand is painful, but it is also unfortunately not uncommon. Partners often don't have the framework to comprehend this kind of harm, especially when it's shared in pieces. Some survivors find that couples therapy with a trauma-informed therapist helps bridge that gap, not to relitigate the past, but to build a shared language going forward.</p>
<p>Healing from something this layered and this long-carried is not linear, and "overcoming" it may look less like making it disappear and more like slowly loosening its grip. I wish you more moments of peace, more self-compassion, and more room to live fully. That is possible and you deserve that.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/can-you-broaden-the-definition-of-tonic-immobility-im-struggling-with-self-blame-about-my-rape-because-i-wasnt-immobile-the-entire-time-there-were-moments-where-i-was-frozen-and-wondering-why-i-wasnt)

**Question:** Can you broaden the definition of tonic immobility? I'm struggling with self-blame about my rape because I wasn't immobile the entire time. There were moments where I was frozen and wondering why I wasn't moving. There were also moments where I spoke up against what was happening, but when he disregarded me, I froze again. I tried so hard to advocate for myself but was in shock. During the moments I was frozen, I tried to breathe and hoped I could somehow "enjoy it." Does tonic immobility have a range of duration, or is an intermittent experience like mine actually common?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for asking this. What you're describing is not only valid, it's actually a very accurate and nuanced experience of tonic immobility.</p>
<p><a href="https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/tonic-immobility">Tonic immobility</a> is often described as a complete inability to move, but that's an oversimplification that leaves a lot of survivors feeling like their experience doesn't "qualify." In reality, tonic immobility exists on a spectrum and can be intermittent. <a href="https://obgyn.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/aogs.13174">Research</a> confirms that experiencing tonic immobility for only part of an assault is far more common than most people realize, meaning variability in duration is not even the exception. The body can also move between different defensive states during a traumatic event, which helps explain why moments of attempted action, like speaking up, can give way to freezing again, especially when those attempts are met with continued threat.</p>
<p>The moments where you were frozen and wondering why you weren't moving are one of the most painful hallmarks of tonic immobility. That awareness is a deeply distressing part of the experience that many survivors describe.
