It is also one of the reasons self-blame can feel so convincing, because it can feel like you "should" have been able to do something. But that sense of being stuck while aware is a neurological event, not a reflection of your willingness or desire.</p>
<p>Trying to breathe through those frozen moments, or hoping to find some way to tolerate what was happening, is also a recognized survival response. When the nervous system determines it cannot escape a threat, it will sometimes shift toward any strategy that makes the experience more survivable, including attempts at dissociation, or trying to find something, anything, to endure the moment. This is not enjoyment, and it is not consent. It is your mind trying to protect you from the full weight of what was being done to you.</p>
<p>Your did what you needed to do to survive at the time. Self-blame often focuses on what we didn't do, but your body and mind were actively working to protect you throughout. That deserves acknowledgment, and it deserves compassion.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/what-do-i-do-when-i-cant-remember-what-happened-or-how-i-felt-during-childhood-i-know-my-sister-and-i-engaged-in-sexual-play-on-an-ongoing-basis-when-i-was-young-i-can-remember-two-instances-of-active)

**Question:** What do I do when I can't remember what happened or how I felt during childhood? I know my sister and I engaged in sexual play on an ongoing basis when I was young. I can remember two instances of actively feeling uncomfortable and not knowing how to get away, but I don't remember any emotions tied to the other experiences. In general I remember very little of the events of my childhood and even less of how I felt during it.

I have a lot of anxiety and depression and am several years into therapy, but I just remembered these two experiences. It's making me question what my childhood was actually like, and I have no idea how to find that answer. It's been distressing trying to figure out if my childhood was actually scary and whether it's the reason I feel so unhappy so much of the time.

I'm autistic and know I never talked to anyone about my feelings growing up. Because of the autism, I wonder if I was more sensitive, meaning things that seem relatively normal could have been extremely distressing for me personally, but I can't remember. I also don't know if I could have hidden my discomfort well enough for it to go unnoticed when I was that young.

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting us with this. To start, I want you to know that what you're sitting with right now is one of the hardest places to be in, and it makes complete sense that it feels so unsettling. Memory gaps around childhood experiences, particularly ones that may have been confusing or uncomfortable, are incredibly common and do not mean something is wrong with you or that your experiences weren't real.</p>
<p>Memory, especially from early childhood, is not like a recording. Our brains, particularly at ages 4 through 7, are still developing the structures responsible for storing and organizing explicit memories. When experiences carry emotional weight, the brain sometimes stores them in fragmented ways. This can result in a feeling here, a flash of sensation there, without a clear narrative connecting them. This is not a failure of your mind. It is actually your mind doing what minds do, especially when something feels too big or confusing to fully process in the moment. The two instances you do remember (where you felt uncomfortable and didn't know how to get away) are meaningful data points, even without a full picture surrounding them.</p>
<p>It's also worth knowing that people with autism often process and store emotional experiences differently. Sensory and emotional sensitivity can make certain experiences register more intensely, and the tendency to mask or suppress distress can begin very early. It is absolutely possible that you experienced significant discomfort that went unspoken and unnoticed externally, even as a young child. That doesn't mean something was wrong with how you handled it. It means you were doing the only thing available to you at the time.</p>
<p class="font-claude-response-body break-words whitespace-normal leading-[1.7]">The distress you're feeling as you try to reconstruct your childhood is coming from a real place. But it's also important to gently hold the reality that you may never have complete clarity about what happened or how you felt and that the absence of that clarity doesn't have to prevent healing. Many people work through the impact of early experiences without ever recovering a full memory of them. Your nervous system, your anxiety, and your depression all carry the weight of experiences even when your conscious mind doesn't have access to the full story. Therapy that is attuned to trauma and to autistic experiences can help you work with what your body and emotions are telling you, even without a complete narrative.</p>
<p class="font-claude-response-body break-words whitespace-normal leading-[1.7]">Reaching out to family for factual information is something only you can decide whether to pursue, and there is no right answer. It may offer some pieces of context, or it may raise more questions than it answers. Either way, your healing does not depend on outside confirmation of what happened. What you remember is enough to take seriously and enough to bring into your therapeutic work. Thank you for reaching out to us.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/why-do-i-feel-so-disgusted-by-myself-its-been-months-since-he-touched-me-and-days-since-i-was-stalked-and-i-still-feel-so-uncomfortable-in-my-own-skin-why-cant-i-move-past-this-858)

**Question:** Why do I feel so disgusted by myself? It's been months since he touched me and days since I was stalked, and I still feel so uncomfortable in my own skin. Why can't I move past this?

**Answer:** <p>There is nothing wrong with you for still feeling this way. The disgust you're feeling is not about who you are...it's about what was done to you. But trauma has a cruel way of turning that outward violation inward, making you feel like the wrongness lives inside your body rather than in the actions of the person who harmed you.</p>
<p>When someone touches you without your consent, it can fundamentally disrupt your sense of ownership over your own body. Your skin, which is supposed to be yours alone, was treated as though it belonged to someone else. That violation can leave a residue that feels almost physical, like something is on you or in you that you can't wash off. The disgust you're describing is your mind and body's way of saying "this wasn't supposed to happen to me," and that reaction is actually a healthy, protective response, even though it feels unbearable right now.</p>
<p>And here's something important: you're not just healing from one thing. You mentioned being stalked just days ago. Stalking is its own form of violation. It strips away your sense of safety, privacy, and control over your own space and movements. When someone is stalking you, your nervous system doesn't get the chance to come down from high alert. Your body can't begin to fully process the trauma of being touched when it's simultaneously dealing with the ongoing threat of being watched and followed. These experiences are compounding each other, and it makes complete sense that you feel uncomfortable in your own skin right now, because multiple people have treated your body and your presence as something they're entitled to.</p>
<p>The timeline of healing is not measured in months. There is no expiration date on how long you're "allowed" to feel the impact of what happened to you, and the fact that the stalking is still so recent means your system is actively coping with ongoing threat, not just processing something from the past. Please try to be gentle with yourself about where you are right now. You are not behind. You are carrying the weight of someone else's actions in your body, and your body is asking for time, safety, and support to let that weight go. If you're able to access a trauma-informed therapist or advocate, they can help you work through both of these experiences and begin rebuilding that sense of safety and home within yourself. You deserve to feel comfortable in your own skin again, and with the right support, that is absolutely possible.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/why-did-i-become-hypersexual-around-males-after-being-sexually-assaulted-could-this-be-connected-to-the-assaults-or-to-growing-up-with-an-abusive-father-or-is-this-just-who-i-am-857)

**Question:** Why did I become hypersexual around males after being sexually assaulted? Could this be connected to the assaults or to growing up with an abusive father? Or is this just who I am?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting us with this. I want to start by addressing your last question first...no, this is not "just who you are." The fact that you're asking this question at all tells me that this behavior feels foreign to you, like something that doesn't quite align with who you know yourself to be underneath it all. That instinct is worth trusting.</p>
<p>Hypersexuality is one of the most common and most misunderstood <a href="https://www.helpguide.org/mental-health/ptsd-trauma/sex-and-intimacy-after-sexual-trauma">responses to sexual trauma</a>. After being sexually assaulted, your brain and body went through something that fundamentally disrupted your sense of safety, control, and autonomy. Hypersexuality can develop as the mind's attempt to regain some of that control by approaching the thing that was used to harm you on your own terms. This can create an illusion of power over what happened. It can also be a way of seeking validation, connection, or proof of your own worth through sexual attention, particularly if the trauma left you feeling powerless or devalued. Sometimes it's the body replaying a trauma pattern almost compulsively, not because you want it, but because your nervous system got stuck in a loop it doesn't know how to break.</p>
<p>The fact that this pattern specifically involves males adds another important layer, especially given that you also experienced abuse from your father. Growing up with an abusive father can deeply shape how you understand your value in relation to men (e.g. what you believe you're worth to them, what you think they want from you, and what you feel you need to do to earn safety, love, or approval). When sexual assault is layered on top of that foundation, hypersexual behavior around men can become a place where both wounds intersect. It may be difficult to untangle exactly how much comes from the assaults versus your father's abuse, and the truth is, it's likely both working together. These experiences don't exist in isolation. They build on each other and create patterns that can feel impossible to step outside of.</p>
<p>What I want you to hear most clearly is that this response does not define you. It is not a character flaw, and it is not evidence that something is wrong with who you are at your core. It is your mind and body responding to painful experiences in a way that made sense given what you've been through. <a href="https://community.ourwave.org/en/answer/why-can-people-who-experience-sexual-harm-experience-hypersexuality-after-these-experiences-97">Many survivors experience this</a>, and many have found their way to a different relationship with their sexuality...one that feels more like their own. A trauma-informed therapist can help you gently explore where these patterns come from and support you in building a sense of sexuality and connection that feels authentic to you, rather than driven by what was done to you. You deserve that.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-previously-asked-about-self-touch-during-a-storytelling-game-with-my-sister-ive-since-apologized-and-she-laughed-it-off-saying-she-was-willing-however-during-a-severe-ocd-episode-i-had-an-intrusive)

**Question:** I previously asked about self-touch during a storytelling game with my sister. I've since apologized and she laughed it off, saying she was willing. However, during a severe OCD episode, I had an intrusive thought or possible memory of my sister trying to change the subject while I continued with the story and self-touch. I don't know if this memory is real or a product of my OCD. I'm afraid to bring it up to my sister in case she repressed it, but I'm also too scared to talk to a therapist because I'm worried about what I might have done. What should I do?

**Answer:** <p>Thanks for following up about this. First, I want to gently acknowledge that OCD is doing exactly what it does best right now...finding a new angle to pull you back into the cycle of doubt, guilt, and <a href="https://www.treatmyocd.com/blog/the-rumination-trap?utm_source=google&amp;utm_medium=cpc&amp;utm_campaign=NOCD_PM_US&amp;gad_source=1&amp;gad_campaignid=14264737132&amp;gbraid=0AAAAADEWRhZ2VlICpq3jGOCmJMwU76e9E&amp;gclid=CjwKCAiAncvMBhBEEiwA9GU_flF6xJIxjC8X_BsfQbVnxNKv1J5jWL_uIlge5k5NcGtUycoHpQHUNBoCzkcQAvD_BwE">rumination</a>. That's important context for everything you're feeling in this moment.</p>
<p>One of the most well-documented features of OCD is its ability to generate intrusive thoughts that feel like memories, or to distort and rewrite actual memories until you can no longer tell the difference. This is especially true during severe episodes. The uncertainty you're feeling (e.g. "I don't know if this is real or OCD") is itself a hallmark of how OCD operates. It thrives in that gray area and wants you to keep searching for certainty you may never find.</p>
<p>Here's what I want you to hear clearly: talking to a therapist, specifically one experienced in OCD is the single most important step you can take right now. A therapist trained in OCD will not judge you. They hear these kinds of fears regularly, and they understand that the terror you feel about being a bad person is the OCD talking, not evidence of who you are. You do not need to have all the answers about this memory before seeking help. In fact, trying to figure it out on your own is likely feeding the cycle.</p>
<p>Reaching out to your sister for reassurance right now, while in an OCD spiral, would likely function as a compulsion rather than a genuine resolution, and it carries the risks you've already identified. A therapist can help you work through this in a way that protects both you and your sister. You deserve that support, and seeking it is not an admission of guilt...it's an act of courage. Thank you for trusting us with this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/can-it-still-be-considered-sexual-assault-or-abuse-if-the-person-was-the-same-age-as-me-they-didnt-directly-pressure-me-into-intimacy-in-the-moment-but-they-had-been-manipulating-me-into-doing-what-th)

**Question:** Can it still be considered sexual assault or abuse if the person was the same age as me? They didn't directly pressure me into intimacy in the moment, but they had been manipulating me into doing what they wanted in other ways for three years leading up to it. Does the history of manipulation matter?

**Answer:** <p>This is such an important question thank you for asking it.&nbsp;When we talk about <a href="https://rainn.org/share-the-facts/consent-101-respect-boundaries-and-building-trust/">consent</a>, it's easy to focus narrowly on what happened in the moment: Was there direct pressure? Did someone say no? But true consent requires so much more than the absence of force or explicit coercion in a single instance. Consent requires the genuine freedom to say yes or no without fear of consequences, and that freedom can be deeply compromised by patterns of manipulation that have been built up over time. Three years of someone conditioning you to comply with what they wanted creates a dynamic where your ability to freely choose is fundamentally affected, even if they didn't overtly pressure you during the intimate encounter itself.</p>
<p>Manipulation works precisely because it's often subtle and cumulative. Over time, it can reshape how you see yourself in relation to another person. For example, what you feel you owe them, what you believe will happen if you don't go along, how much space you feel you have to assert your own needs and boundaries. When someone has spent years training you to prioritize their wants, your "choice" in an intimate moment doesn't exist in a vacuum. It exists within the entire context of that relationship and the power dynamic that was carefully constructed long before that moment arrived.</p>
<p>The fact that this person was the same age as you does not disqualify your experience from being harmful, abusive, or assaultive. Peers can absolutely create power imbalances through manipulation, emotional control, and other coercive behaviors. Age equality does not automatically equal power equality. What matters is the dynamic between you and a dynamic shaped by three years of manipulation is not one built on mutual respect and freely given choice.</p>
<p>You don't need anyone else to label your experience for you. Only you can determine what feels true about what happened. But I want you to know that if what you're describing doesn't sit right with you (if it feels like something was taken from you or like your ability to choose was truly compromised) those feelings make complete sense given what you've shared. You deserved a relationship where your boundaries and autonomy were respected, not one where years of manipulation laid the groundwork for someone to get what they wanted. Whatever you decide to call this experience, your feelings about it are valid, and you deserve support as you process it.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-younger-i-became-friends-with-a-new-girl-at-school-over-time-she-began-crossing-my-physical-boundaries-in-small-waystouching-my-waist-and-putting-her-legs-on-mine-she-also-isolated-me-from)

**Question:** When I was younger, I became friends with a new girl at school. Over time, she began crossing my physical boundaries in small ways...touching my waist and putting her legs on mine. She also isolated me from my other friendships, turned people against me, and told me I was selfish if I had friends besides her. She tried to manipulate my parents and took over my hobbies and interests. She made it clear that if I didn't do what she wanted, there would be consequences, and I felt powerless and alone. During this same time, I was also being sexualized and objectified by two other girls at school. Eventually, after years of this dynamic, we were talking about the sexual experiences our friends were having, and I could tell she wanted something to happen between us. I felt like I didn't have a choice, so I suggested it, and we kissed. This happened three times before I told her I didn't want to do it anymore. My mother firmly believes this was sexual abuse. But I'm the one who suggested it, so how could it not be my fault? After years of her manipulation and mind games, was my ability to consent compromised? Am I to blame?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for sharing something so deeply personal and painful. I can hear how much weight you've been carrying around this. What you're describing is a pattern of behavior that unfolded over years before anything sexual ever took place. It sounds like this person systematically crossed your physical boundaries, isolated you from your other friendships, manipulated the people around you, took over your interests and identity, and made you believe that failing to comply with her wishes would result in losing even more. By the time the sexual contact occurred, you had already spent years being conditioned to believe that your needs didn't matter and that resisting her would only lead to more loss. That context is essential to understanding what happened.</p>
<p>You mentioned that you were the one who suggested it, and I understand why that feels so significant to you. But I want to gently reframe what you described. You said yourself that she made it clear there would be repercussions if you didn't do what she wanted. You said you could tell she wanted something sexual to happen. You said you felt like you didn't have a choice. And your reasoning in the moment (that if you did this, maybe she would leave the boy you loved alone) reveals that this wasn't a free, enthusiastic choice. It was a survival strategy. You were trying to protect something that mattered to you from someone who had already taken so much.</p>
<p>True consent requires the freedom to say no without fear of consequences. When someone has spent years manipulating you, isolating you, and punishing you for not complying, that freedom doesn't exist. The fact that the words came from your mouth doesn't mean the choice was truly yours. Coercion doesn't always look like someone physically forcing you...it can look exactly like what you described: years of control, manipulation, and fear that slowly erode your sense of agency until you feel like going along with what someone wants is the only option you have. Your ability to freely consent was absolutely compromised by the dynamic she created.</p>
<p>It may also help to understand that young people who are navigating their own identity, including their sexual orientation, sometimes act out in ways that are confusing or harmful to others, particularly when they don't have the language, support, or safe spaces to explore those feelings openly. It's possible that this girl was struggling with her own feelings and identity in ways she didn't know how to express or process, and that some of her behavior was rooted in that confusion. This doesn't excuse what she did to you...none of that was okay. The harm it caused you is real regardless of what she may have been going through internally, but holding space for that complexity can sometimes help make sense of a situation that otherwise feels impossible to understand. Her struggles don't minimize yours, and your experience deserves to be honored fully on its own terms.</p>
<p>It's also important to acknowledge that all of this was happening while you were simultaneously being sexualized and objectified by two other girls at school. You were navigating multiple situations where your boundaries and autonomy were not being respected, which would have made you even more vulnerable and exhausted.</p>
<p class="font-claude-response-body break-words whitespace-normal leading-[1.7]">I also want to address something about your mother's perspective. It's clear that she loves you and is trying to protect you. Her belief that this was sexual abuse comes from a place of care. However, only you get to decide what language feels right for your experience. Having someone else label what happened to you (even someone who loves you) can sometimes add to the confusion rather than ease it, especially when you're still processing. You may come to agree with your mother, or you may find that different words feel more accurate to you, and either is okay. What matters is that you are the one who gets to define your own experience, in your own time.&nbsp;</p>
<p class="font-claude-response-body break-words whitespace-normal leading-[1.7]">If these feelings of shame and self-blame are weighing heavily on you, a trauma-informed therapist who understands the dynamics of coercion and manipulation can be an incredibly valuable support in helping you work through them. You deserve to process this experience with someone who can help you see what so many of us can already see...that this was not your fault. You deserve that compassion, especially from yourself. Thank you for trusting us with this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-am-having-memories-come-up-from-when-i-was-younger-and-im-beginning-to-wonder-if-what-happened-could-be-considered-cocsa-my-cousin-and-i-were-playing-a-gamei-think-we-were-playing-house-i-dont-remem)

**Question:** I am having memories come up from when I was younger, and I'm beginning to wonder if what happened could be considered COCSA. My cousin and I were playing a game...I think we were playing house. I don't remember what happened before, but I remember him pinning me down on the ground and trying to kiss me. He wouldn't let me get up and kept asking until I did. I can't remember much else as the memory is really fuzzy. I remember feeling trapped and confused and like I couldn't tell anyone. I don't know if this could be considered child-on-child sexual abuse or if I'm overreacting. I don't want to call it something it's not, but it felt that way. It's also confusing because I can't remember all the details. Could this be COCSA?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for sharing something so personal with us. It takes a lot of courage to put these kinds of memories into words, especially when they feel incomplete or uncertain. What you're describing does involve some elements that are commonly associated with COCSA (<a href="https://www.ourwave.org/en/post/understanding-cocsa-and-breaking-cycles-of-childhood-harm">child-on-child sexual abuse</a>). COCSA generally refers to sexual behavior between children that involves force, coercion, a power imbalance, or a lack of mutual consent. The key details you remember (e.g. feeling trapped, being physically held down, and being pressured until you gave in) suggest that this interaction was not mutual or consensual on your part, even within a game. It seems like there were some elements of coercion here. &nbsp;</p>
<p>I also want to gently address your concern about overreacting. It's incredibly common for survivors to question whether their experience "counts" or whether they're making too much of it, especially when the other person involved was also a child. But your feelings about what happened are important and valid. Those emotional responses are telling you something meaningful about how this experience affected you and they deserve to be taken seriously regardless of what label you ultimately choose to use.</p>
<p>The fact that your memories are fuzzy and fragmented is also completely normal. Trauma, especially childhood trauma, can affect the way memories are stored and recalled. When something distressing happens, our brains don't always record events in a neat, linear way. You may remember certain details vividly while other parts remain blurry or missing entirely. This doesn't mean your experience didn't happen or that it wasn't significant. Many survivors describe their memories in exactly this way, and incomplete memories do not make your experience any less real or valid.</p>
<p>You get to decide what language feels right for you when describing this experience. Whether you call it COCSA, whether you use other words, or whether you're still figuring that out...all of that is okay. What matters most is that you honor what you felt then and what you're feeling now. If these memories are causing you distress, speaking with a trauma-informed therapist can be incredibly helpful in processing fragmented memories and the emotions that come with them, at whatever pace feels safe for you. You deserve that support.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/how-do-i-know-who-answered-my-question-can-i-assume-the-same-person-answered-both-the-original-question-and-any-follow-up-questions-in-the-comments-851)

**Question:** How do I know who answered my question? Can I assume the same person answered both the original question and any follow-up questions in the comments?

**Answer:** <p>Great question! The author of each answered question will always be listed, so you'll always know who provided the original response. However, we cannot guarantee answers to follow-up questions in the comments at this time. Occasionally, our team may try to respond, but the primary purpose of the comments section is for community discussion rather than expert follow-up. We wish we could answer every follow-up, but our current bandwidth doesn't allow for it. We appreciate your understanding, and we're grateful you're here and engaging with the community.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/how-can-our-organization-become-a-part-of-your-directory-do-you-offer-an-html-that-can-be-embedded-on-our-website-850)

**Question:** How can our organization become a part of your directory?  Do you offer an HTML that can be embedded on our website?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for asking this! We appreciate your organization&rsquo;s desire to connect with us and become part of our resource directory. We do accept requests from groups that share our commitment to supporting survivors and we have a straightforward process for reviewing and adding new organizations. Please email us at <a href="mailto:hello@ourwave.org">hello@ourwave.org</a> to get started. Once you reach out and provide the relevant information about your mission, location, and services, a member of our team will meet with you to guide you through any additional steps. Regarding the HTML you mentioned, we offer options to embed our platform on external websites. Please reach out to <a href="mailto:hello@ourwave.org">hello@ourwave.org</a> about that request as well, but we are always happy for your to list us as a resource to your audience. Our main goal is to maintain clear and reliable listings that serve people seeking help, and we appreciate the chance to explore ways to extend our collective reach even further!</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-a-child-8-an-older-sibling-12-showed-me-child-pornography-over-the-course-of-a-few-weeks-eventually-when-no-adults-were-home-they-told-me-to-take-photos-of-myself-imitating-the-pornography)

**Question:** When I was a child (8), an older sibling (12) showed me child pornography over the course of a few weeks. Eventually, when no adults were home, they told me to take photos of myself imitating the pornography they had shown me. They also showed me their genitals, let me touch them, and then performed a sexual act on me. Adults came home in the middle of it, but I can't clearly remember what happened next...I just know I refused to talk to anyone about it. I didn't fight or say no, and I think part of me felt curiosity and even wanted to seem mature. Does this count as COCSA or sibling abuse, or was it just normal children stuff? It also bothers me that I don't know what happened to those photos. Is it reasonable to be upset about that even though I don't know what they were used for? I'm also confused because after that period, my sibling went back to acting completely normal, and it feels like I'm the only one still affected.

**Answer:** <p>I'm so sorry you've been wrestling with these questions. Thank you for trusting us with this. Nothing about what you described sounds like "normal children stuff." What you are describing seems to go far behind normal childhood curiosity and I would love to share more about my thoughts around it.</p>
<p>Children naturally have some level of curiosity, but it sounds like this reached a level that went well past simple interest or games. It involved explicit sexual content, a difference in ages and understanding, and behaviors that you weren't old enough to consent to. A four-year age difference between siblings does create a power imbalance, and when an older child repeatedly introduces sexual content or requests, it typically goes beyond normal childhood exploration. These behaviors reflect planning and escalation, which are hallmarks of child-on-child sexual abuse, or <a href="https://www.ourwave.org/en/post/understanding-cocsa-and-breaking-cycles-of-childhood-harm">COCSA.</a> Normal childhood sexual curiosity tends to be mutual, spontaneous, and between children of similar ages and developmental stages. What your sibling did sounds like it involved deliberate grooming...slowly desensitizing you with pornography over a period of weeks before escalating to direct contact. That pattern of behavior is concerning.</p>
<p>Having been only eight years old when this occurred, it was never your job to recognize or resist a situation that felt confusing or unsafe. The fact that you didn't fight, didn't say no, or even felt some curiosity does not change what this was. You were a child who had been gradually exposed to sexual content you had no framework to understand. Children naturally try to make sense of confusing situations. They often seek attention and affection any way they can. Feeling like you were being "mature" is a completely understandable response from a child in that position. Many survivors describe similar feelings, and it does not mean you were a willing participant or that this was somehow your fault. The responsibility always lies with the older child who crossed those boundaries. Your sibling was older, had more knowledge and power in that dynamic, and made choices that you (as a younger child) were not equipped to consent to or fully understand.</p>
<p>Your discomfort and anger about the photos make perfect sense, even if you don't know exactly whether they were kept or used. You do not need to know what happened to those photos in order to feel upset about their existence. It is reasonable to have lingering distress about images that were taken without your true, informed consent. The act of directing a child to create those images was harmful in and of itself, and you have every right to feel disturbed, angry, or worried about this.</p>
<p>As children, we don't know how to process something so confusing, which is why you might have refused to talk about it back then. That makes perfect sense. Your mind was trying to protect you in the only way it could at that time. It also makes sense to feel stuck now that you're an adult with more perspective on what occurred. Holding on to upsetting details doesn't mean your healing has stalled. Many survivors revisit childhood experiences and make new sense of them when they have the emotional tools to do so. Replaying everything now, as an adult, is a natural response to realizing how serious those actions were.</p>
<p>The fact that your sibling later acted like a normal sibling again does not change that the behavior you described crossed serious boundaries. You have every right to experience those emotions, and it's not your fault if you still think about them, even if your sibling seems to have moved on. Please remember you aren't burdening anyone by asking questions or expressing your anger. It's okay to need time and possibly supportive conversations, whether with loved ones, a counselor, or other survivors, to make sense of it all. If you find it helpful, a counselor specialized in trauma could provide a space to talk openly without judgment and help you sort through these painful memories. You deserve to voice your truth, be believed, and feel supported. Thank you for trusting us with this. You are not alone, and what happened to you was not your fault.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-my-boyfriend-was-12-he-briefly-touched-his-younger-cousins-private-area-over-their-clothes-for-a-few-seconds-when-his-cousin-asked-him-to-move-his-hand-he-immediately-stopped-and-nothing-happened)

**Question:** When my boyfriend was 12, he briefly touched his younger cousin's private area over their clothes for a few seconds. When his cousin asked him to move his hand, he immediately stopped and nothing happened again. He describes this as childhood curiosity from when he was going through puberty. I'm trying to understand whether this falls under typical childhood sexual exploration or if it's something more serious. Is this a common experience? He has OCD and I'm wondering if I should be concerned about this.

**Answer:** <p>We appreciate you reaching out with this question and understand how confusing it can be to navigate this kind of disclosure from someone you care about. It's completely natural to want to understand what happened and what it means, both for your boyfriend's younger cousin and for your relationship.</p>
<p>What you're describing exists in a complex space. Not all sexual contact between children falls into the same category and context matters significantly. Researchers and clinicians typically consider several factors when evaluating these situations: the age difference between the children involved, whether there was coercion or force, the frequency and duration of the behavior, and how the children responded to boundaries.</p>
<p>In the situation you've described, there are some elements that distinguish it from more concerning patterns. The interaction was brief, it happened once, your boyfriend was in early adolescence when puberty-related curiosity is developmentally common, and most significantly, he stopped immediately when his cousin communicated a boundary. Children and young adolescents are still developing their understanding of consent, boundaries, and appropriate behavior, and the ability to respond to a "no" and never repeat the behavior suggests he was capable of recognizing and respecting that boundary once it was communicated.</p>
<p>That said, there was an age difference that likely meant a power imbalance existed between your boyfriend and his younger cousin, and any sexual contact between children with significant age or developmental differences raises valid concerns about the younger child's experience. The cousin was old enough to communicate a boundary, and your boyfriend respected it, but we can't know how that moment affected them. Many concerning sexual behaviors in childhood don't happen just once or stop when a boundary is stated, so the pattern you're describing is different from behaviors that typically cause alarm among professionals.</p>
<p>Regarding whether this is "common," research shows that sexual curiosity and exploration are normal parts of child development, though the specific behaviors and ages vary widely. Many adults recall moments from their own childhoods involving sexual curiosity that they now recognize as inappropriate, even if they occurred in the context of normal developmental exploration. The difference between problematic sexual behavior and typical childhood curiosity often lies in the pattern, the response to boundaries, and whether the behavior continues.</p>
<p>It's also worth noting that your boyfriend's OCD may be causing him to ruminate on this memory or experience distress about it disproportionate to the actual event. People with OCD often fixate on past behaviors and question their own morality intensely, sometimes confessing things repeatedly or seeking reassurance about whether they're a bad person. If he's bringing this up frequently or seems consumed by guilt, that pattern itself might be worth addressing with support from a mental health professional who understands OCD.</p>
<p>What matters now is how your boyfriend has grown since that moment, how he understands consent and boundaries in his current relationships, and whether he recognizes why that kind of contact wasn't appropriate even though he was young. If you're feeling uncertain about your relationship because of this disclosure, that's valid, and you get to decide what feels right for you. You might also consider whether his current understanding of consent and respect for boundaries aligns with the values you need in a partner. Thank you for trusting us with this. We hope this gave you a little insight to support your decision making going forward.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-around-14-i-had-consensual-experiences-with-friends-that-involved-dry-humping-while-fully-clothed-during-one-instance-when-a-friend-initially-declined-to-experiment-i-made-a-comment-out-of)

**Question:** When I was around 14, I had consensual experiences with friends that involved dry humping while fully clothed. During one instance, when a friend initially declined to experiment, I made a comment out of embarrassment saying "all the popular people do it." We did experiment at a later date, and our friendship has continued into adulthood. As an adult, I asked her if she had ever felt pressured by me, and she said no and laughed it off. My therapist, who has a complex understanding of my history and emotional development at that time, does not believe I pressured or abused anyone. However, my OCD convinces me otherwise. Have I pressured or abused somebody?

**Answer:** <p>I hear how much distress you're carrying about this, and I want to start by saying that I appreciate your willingness to reflect on your past interactions. What you're describing, however, sounds like a situation where OCD is causing you to catastrophize a moment of adolescent awkwardness into something far more serious than what actually occurred.</p>
<p>From what you've shared, you were 14 years old and your comment about "popular people" sounds like exactly what it was: an awkward, immature attempt to navigate social discomfort when you felt embarrassed. Adolescents say clumsy things all the time, especially in vulnerable moments, and that doesn't transform typical teenage behavior into abuse. The fact that your friend declined initially, that you didn't push further in that moment, that she later chose to engage with you, and that she's maintained a positive relationship with you into adulthood all point toward mutual, age-appropriate experimentation rather than coercion.</p>
<p>Your therapist, who knows your full history and developmental context, has told you this wasn't abusive. Your friend, who was actually there and experienced the interaction firsthand, has told you she never felt pressured. OCD specializes in taking ambiguous situations and turning them into catastrophic certainties, and it sounds like that's exactly what's happening here.&nbsp;</p>
<p>Pressure and abuse involve persistent coercion, intentional manipulation of power imbalances, or disregarding someone's stated boundaries. The evidence, both from the people involved and from professionals who understand trauma, consistently points toward this being typical adolescent exploration, not abuse. You deserve to trust the people who were there and the professionals who understand your situation, rather than the part of your mind that's trying to convince you of something that didn't happen. Be gentle with yourself.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/is-it-wrong-or-creepy-to-make-my-original-characters-experience-the-same-things-that-happened-to-me-i-use-this-as-a-way-to-process-and-relate-to-my-experiences-since-i-havent-met-other-survivors-and-d)

**Question:** Is it wrong or creepy to make my original characters experience the same things that happened to me? I use this as a way to process and relate to my experiences since I haven't met other survivors and don't feel comfortable talking about it. A friend recently called me a creep for doing this, even though they knew what I was going through when it was happening. I don't feel like it's fair if my characters don't suffer the way I did. I need something to relate to. Am I weird for this?

**Answer:** <p>You are absolutely not a creep, and you are not weird. What you're describing is actually a common and legitimate way that many trauma survivors process their experiences. Using creative expression, including writing characters who have gone through similar experiences of sexual assault, is a recognized coping mechanism and can be part of healing.</p>
<p>Many survivors find it difficult or impossible to talk directly about what happened to them, especially when they haven't found safe people to share with or when previous attempts to open up were met with dismissive responses like being called "dramatic." When direct conversation feels unsafe or unavailable, channeling these experiences into creative work allows you to externalize what happened, explore it from different angles, and maintain some sense of control over the narrative. This isn't weird. It's resourceful and shows emotional awareness about what you need to survive.</p>
<p>Your friend's response says more about their own discomfort or lack of understanding than it does about you. It's particularly painful that someone who was present during a vulnerable time would later use that knowledge to shame you. That response was unkind and uninformed, but it doesn't make your coping strategy inappropriate or harmful.</p>
<p>The feeling that it wouldn't be "fair" if your characters didn't experience what you did speaks to something many survivors experience which is a need to see your reality reflected somewhere and to have it acknowledged and validated even if only in fiction you create. When the world around you hasn't properly witnessed or validated your pain, sometimes you need to create that witness yourself. That's not creepy... that's you trying to make meaning from what happened and to not feel so alone in it.</p>
<p>You deserve support from people who understand trauma, not judgment from those who don't. Your creative expression belongs to you, and using it to process your experiences is both valid and valuable. You're surviving in whatever ways you can, and that takes strength. Thank you for trusting us with this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/is-my-experience-valid-my-best-friend-pressured-me-into-sex-when-we-were-both-teens-she-would-threaten-to-run-away-and-harm-herself-if-i-didnt-comply-when-i-went-to-the-police-they-told-me-they-couldn)

**Question:** Is my experience valid? My best friend pressured me into sex when we were both teens. She would threaten to run away and harm herself if I didn't comply. When I went to the police, they told me they couldn't do anything and to get over it.

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting us with this and I am so sorry you experienced such painful responses to the harm you experienced. You did not deserve that. Your experience is absolutely valid. What you're describing sounds like a form of <a href="https://www.loveisrespect.org/resources/what-is-sexual-coercion/">sexual coercion</a>, and it can be deeply traumatic regardless of the gender of the person who harmed you or your ages at the time. The fact that the police dismissed your experience doesn't make it any less real or harmful. Their response was inadequate and wrong, and I'm truly sorry that happened to you when you were seeking help and validation.</p>
<p>When someone uses threats of self-harm to pressure another person into sexual activity, they are manipulating that person's care and concern to override their consent. This creates an impossible situation where you felt responsible for someone else's safety and wellbeing, which is an enormous burden to carry, especially as a young person. That kind of pressure removes genuine choice from the equation. You were placed in a position where you felt you had no option but to comply, and that is coercion.</p>
<p>It's important to know that sexual harm between young people is real and can have lasting impacts. The trauma you experienced doesn't disappear because the person who harmed you was the same age as you or because she was your friend. Betrayal by someone you trusted, especially during formative teenage years, can be particularly painful and confusing. Many survivors of peer-on-peer sexual violence struggle with having their experiences validated because there are misconceptions about what assault looks like and who can cause harm.</p>
<p>Sexual violence can happen between people of any gender, but there are particularly harmful myths that minimize or dismiss harm when it occurs between women or girls, or when the person who caused harm is female. These myths suggest that women can't be sexually harmful or that same-gender experiences are somehow less serious. These beliefs are false and deeply damaging to survivors. Sexual coercion is about power, control, and the violation of consent. Those dynamics exist regardless of gender. You experienced real harm, and the gender of the person who hurt you doesn't make that harm any less significant or deserving of recognition.</p>
<p>The response you received from the police added another layer of harm. Being told to "get over it" is a form of institutional betrayal that can compound trauma, and it's possible that gender assumptions played a role in how dismissively they treated your report. You deserved to be believed, supported, and treated with dignity. The fact that they didn't respond appropriately says everything about failures in the system and nothing about the validity of your experience.</p>
<p>Your feelings about what happened, whatever they are, deserve space and acknowledgment. Healing from coercion and betrayal takes time, and there's no right way to process what you've been through. If you're able to access support through a counselor who specializes in sexual trauma or through survivor support services, that might help you work through these experiences at your own pace. You deserved better then, and you deserve support and compassion now. Thank you for trusting us with this.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/how-long-does-it-take-for-your-mind-to-feel-calm-again-after-leaving-a-relationship-i-broke-up-with-my-boyfriend-4-months-ago-and-my-mind-feels-so-busy-with-memories-of-when-he-hurt-me-emotionally-and)

**Question:** How long does it take for your mind to feel calm again after leaving a relationship? I broke up with my boyfriend 4 months ago and my mind feels so busy with memories of when he hurt me emotionally and sexually, mixed with memories of when he was nice to me. I feel alone and tired 💔

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting us with your feelings. You are not alone.&nbsp;It is entirely normal to wish for a calmer mind and to wonder when you'll finally get some peace from such painful, conflicting memories. While four months can feel like an eternity when you are struggling, it makes sense that your mind still feels busy and exhausted. It sounds like you have a lot to process. Your brain is doing important work right now, even though it feels overwhelming and tiring.</p>
<p>I wish I could give you a defined amount of time, but unfortumately there isn't a universal timeline for when your mind will feel calm again because healing isn't linear and everyone's journey looks different. Four months can sometimes seem like ages, but it often takes longer than we expect for our minds and hearts to quietly process what happened. You have been through a deeply distressing experience, and it's understandable that your thoughts still feel like they're spinning. The tiredness you're feeling is real and valid.</p>
<p>Healing from emotional and sexual harm can feel like riding an emotional rollercoaster. Your memories of the hurt may crash into thoughts of tender times that make you question everything. Many survivors notice waves of regret, anger, sorrow, and even occasional nostalgia, all intermingling. This does not mean that the difficult times weren't real or that your experiences weren't valid. Our minds are wired to recall the good alongside the bad, which can be confusing, yet it's also part of how we gradually come to terms with what we endured. When someone who hurt us was also sometimes caring or loving, it can create a kind of mental tug-of-war. You might find yourself questioning your decision, missing certain aspects of the relationship, feeling angry about the harm, or all of these things at once. All of those reactions are normal responses to a complicated situation.</p>
<p>Over time, these waves usually become less intense, and small moments of calm begin to expand. Though it may not feel like it in the moment, the busyness in your mind can subside. Healing tends to come in steps rather than in giant leaps, and often it's only in looking back that we realize our minds and hearts have become notably quieter. Processing memories of harm, especially when they're tangled up with moments of care or connection, takes enormous emotional energy, and it's genuinely exhausting work.</p>
<p>What often helps people move toward feeling calmer is having support during this process. The feeling of being alone that you mentioned can make everything feel heavier. If you can, consider talking to a support system you trust--someone who can hear you without dismissing your feelings. Reaching out to professionals or organizations that specialize in trauma support may also help you find edges of calm. These conversations can be a chance to unravel the thoughts that haunt you and learn gentle tools for coping. The loneliness you feel now can lessen when you allow yourself to share what you're going through, at your own pace and with people who truly understand. You don't have to process all of these memories and feelings by yourself.</p>
<p>It's not easy to handle so many memories. You deserve compassion from yourself just as much as from others. Until then, be kind to yourself as best you can, and remember you are not alone. There are people who care and who stand ready to remind you that you deserve peace, gentleness, and a future free from the chaos of these memories. Thank you for trusting us with this. We are here for you along your journey towards healing.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-9-i-played-with-my-5-year-old-neighbor-where-we-imitated-what-we-saw-on-a-reality-tv-showa-man-and-woman-sitting-together-and-flirting-i-think-it-was-my-idea-and-i-remember-pretending-to-ki)

**Question:** When I was 9, I played with my 5-year-old neighbor where we imitated what we saw on a reality TV show—a man and woman sitting together and flirting. I think it was my idea, and I remember pretending to kiss but not actually kissing. We were caught and stopped, and I felt really bad at the time. Now as an adult, I can't help but feel like I abused them. I have OCD, which might be making this worse because I keep thinking maybe I coerced them or that it could have escalated if we hadn't been stopped. I recently learned about COCSA and can't help but feel like I committed it. Did I do something wrong?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out to us. It sounds like you&rsquo;re carrying a lot of worry about a childhood memory that has resurfaced as you&rsquo;ve learned more about sexual abuse and tried to fit your experience into that understanding. At nine years old, you were both children, still learning about boundaries and relationships. Many kids explore what they see on TV or around them without fully grasping adult concepts like consent or the real significance of those actions. It&rsquo;s also very common for people with OCD to get stuck analyzing old memories, especially those involving any kind of developmentally normal curiosity, and to feel intense guilt or shame that doesn&rsquo;t necessarily match what was actually happening at the time.<br /><br />Looking back with an adult perspective can make childhood play feel more serious or sinister than it really was, especially if you&rsquo;re anxious about the possibility of having caused harm. There&rsquo;s a difference between children experimenting or imitating something they don&rsquo;t fully understand and a situation where a child uses power, force, or coercion in a harmful way. If you recall stopping immediately when adults intervened, and if there was no ongoing pressure or manipulation, that suggests it was more a moment of childish imitation rather than a calculated decision to harm someone. Feeling bad at the time also shows you recognized something wasn&rsquo;t right, which is normal for children suddenly realizing they&rsquo;re doing something they shouldn&rsquo;t.<br /><br />If these thoughts are spinning and causing you distress, it might help to talk to a mental health professional, particularly someone who understands OCD, so you can work through any guilt or intrusive worries. Remember that you were a child yourself, operating with a child&rsquo;s understanding and impulsivity, not an adult intention to harm. You deserve compassion for the younger version of you who simply copied a situation you saw on TV without grasping its adult implications. It doesn&rsquo;t make you an abuser to look back and regret or feel unsettled about a past game you didn&rsquo;t truly comprehend at the time. You have the right to acknowledge that experience, process any lingering guilt, and recognize you were just a child exploring something you didn&rsquo;t fully understand. Thank you for trusting us with this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/would-touching-a-sleeping-9-year-olds-breast-and-genitals-be-considered-cocsa-or-criminal-behavior-does-the-age-of-the-older-child-14-15-versus-11-12-make-a-difference-in-how-this-would-be-viewed-i-sa)

**Question:** Would touching a sleeping 9-year-old's breast and genitals be considered COCSA or criminal behavior? Does the age of the older child (14-15 versus 11-12) make a difference in how this would be viewed? I saw another post where brief touching out of curiosity by an 11-12 year old wasn't considered COCSA. What makes situations different. Is it the sleeping aspect, the age difference, or a combination of factors?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out with this thoughtful question. The scenario you're describing (where a 14 or 15 year old touched a sleeping 9 year old's breast and genitals) would generally be considered concerning harmful sexual behavior and could fall under the category of child-on-child sexual abuse (COCSA). There are several factors that make this situation different from the brief curiosity-driven touch you referenced. The combination of the significant age gap (five to six years), the developmental stage of the older child, the intimate nature of the areas touched (both breast and genitals), and critically, the fact that the younger child was asleep creates a very different context.</p>
<p>When a child is asleep, they cannot consent, respond, set boundaries, or indicate discomfort. This removes the younger child's ability to participate in what's happening to their body in any way. The sleeping aspect is significant because it suggests intentionality and awareness on the part of the older child. They knew the younger child was unconscious and unable to respond. At 14 or 15, youth are typically well past the age of normative sexual curiosity exploration and have developed an understanding of privacy, consent, and boundaries. They're also in a very different developmental stage than an 11 or 12 year old.</p>
<p>In contrast, brief touching that immediately stops when a boundary is indicated, especially by a younger child still navigating early puberty, may reflect developmentally normal (though still inappropriate) curiosity rather than abusive behavior. The key differences include the responsiveness to boundaries, the age and developmental gap, whether the touch was sustained or exploratory, and whether the younger child had agency in the moment. Criminal definitions vary significantly by jurisdiction, but many places do have laws addressing sexual contact with minors, even when perpetrated by other minors, particularly when there are substantial age differences and when the behavior involves deliberate touching of intimate areas while the victim is unable to consent.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/why-did-everyone-believe-him-the-person-who-harmed-me-and-not-me-even-my-best-friend-who-had-just-met-him-838)

**Question:** Why did everyone believe him (the person who harmed me) and not me, even my best friend who had just met him?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for asking this question to us. I'm so sorry this happened to you. Being disbelieved is one of the most painful experiences a survivor can face, and when it comes from people you trusted (especially your best friend) that betrayal cuts incredibly deep. What you're describing is unfortunately common, and I want you to know that the fact people believed him says nothing about the truth of what happened to you. It says everything about how our society has been conditioned to respond to disclosures of sexual violence.</p>
<p>There are several dynamics that often contribute to this painful reality. People tend to believe those they perceive as charismatic, confident, or likeable, and perpetrators are often skilled at presenting themselves in ways that seem trustworthy. They may have practiced their denials, had time to craft their narrative, or presented themselves as calm while you were understandably emotional. Our culture also has deeply ingrained myths about what "real" victims and perpetrators look and act like, which can lead people to make judgments based on their own biases rather than on what actually happened. Additionally, believing a perpetrator often feels safer and easier for bystanders. It allows them to avoid the discomfort of confronting the reality that someone they know could cause harm, and it doesn't require them to take action or examine their own complicity.</p>
<p>The fact that even your best friend, who had just met him, chose to believe him over you speaks to how powerful these dynamics can be. Sometimes people make snap judgments based on first impressions, charm, or their own discomfort with the truth. Her choice to believe him is about her own limitations, biases, and perhaps her inability to sit with the difficult reality you shared with her. It is not a reflection of your credibility or the validity of your experience.</p>
<p>You deserved to be believed. You deserved support. And regardless of who did or didn't believe you, what happened to you was real, and your truth matters.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/did-i-commit-cocsa-child-on-child-sexual-abuse-im-dealing-with-guilt-and-shame-about-sexual-experiences-i-had-during-childhood-between-ages-6-14-i-engaged-in-sexual-behaviors-with-family-members-close)

**Question:** Did I commit COCSA (child-on-child sexual abuse)? I'm dealing with guilt and shame about sexual experiences I had during childhood. Between ages 6-14, I engaged in sexual behaviors with family members close to my age, including a cousin my age, my younger sister (who is 3 years younger), and my nephew. These experiences included playing inappropriately with dolls, humping each other occasionally, and sometimes touching and licking. An older family member also performed oral sex on me during this time, which felt secretive and sometimes happened even when I didn't want it. Around age 12, I was groomed by a 23-year-old man. The behaviors with my sister and nephew stopped when I was around 14 because I felt gross and was developing more compared to them. I've asked my sister and nephew about these experiences, and they said we were being curious and that I should let it go. I want to become a teacher and help people, but I'm struggling with these memories. Was this abuse?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you so much for trusting us with this. I can hear how much pain and confusion you're carrying about these childhood experiences. The guilt and shame you're feeling make sense given everything you're trying to process, but I want you to know that what happened is more complicated than the story your shame is telling you.</p>
<p>Here's what really stands out to me... it sounds like you were being sexually abused during all of this. An older family member was performing oral sex on you and doing things even when you didn't want to, and at 12 you were being groomed by a 23-year-old adult. These weren't just things happening in the background. It sounds like they were actively shaping how you understood your body, boundaries, and sexuality during the exact same years that the sexual behaviors with your sister and nephew were occurring.</p>
<p>When children are sexually abused, it's actually really common for them to engage in sexual behaviors with other kids. It's not because they're abusers themselves. It's because they're trying to make sense of something that's deeply confusing and that they don't have the developmental capacity to understand. Your brain was probably working overtime to process experiences no child should ever have to process, and sexual play with peers can be part of that. It doesn't make you someone who intentionally hurt others. It makes you a kid who was hurting.</p>
<p><a href="https://www.ourwave.org/en/post/understanding-cocsa-and-breaking-cycles-of-childhood-harm">Child-on-child sexual abuse (COCSA)</a> generally looks different from what you're describing. It usually involves clear power imbalances, coercion, force, or one child continuing behaviors even when the other child is clearly distressed or saying no. What you're describing with your sister and nephew sounds more like sexual behaviors between kids who were relatively close in age. I know you and your sister had a three-year gap, but you were both children, and the behaviors you mention like humping each other and playing inappropriately with dolls sound mutual rather than forced. Yes, it sounds like there were times when touching and licking happened, but context matters here. You weren't an adult taking advantage of younger children. You were all kids, and you yourself were being harmed.</p>
<p>What really matters here is that you've actually talked to your sister and nephew about this. They told you they see it as curiosity and that you need to let it go. That's their experience of what happened, and their perspective counts. They're not telling you they feel harmed or violated. They're telling you they're okay and that you're holding onto something they don't need you to carry.</p>
<p>I also want to acknowledge that the shame you're feeling might actually be about your own abuse. Sometimes it's easier to blame ourselves for things we feel we had some control over than to sit with the painful reality that we were victimized and powerless. Feeling guilty about the experiences with your sister and nephew might feel more manageable than feeling the full weight of what was done to you by your older family member and that adult who groomed you. But you were a child navigating an impossible situation, and you deserve the same compassion you'd give to any kid in those circumstances.</p>
<p>Your dream of becoming a teacher and helping people isn't something you should give up because of childhood experiences that happened while you were being abused. Plenty of people who've been through trauma go on to do beautiful, meaningful work supporting others. You've already shown a lot of courage in facing these memories and actually checking in with your sister and nephew about their experience. That takes real care and accountability. Those are exactly the qualities that make someone good at helping others.</p>
<p>You were a child who was being hurt, and you were doing your best to survive and make sense of a world that wasn't safe. That's the truth here. Thank you for trusting us with this. We are rooting for you.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/im-struggling-with-feelings-of-guilt-and-self-blame-about-my-past-relationship-while-i-recognize-my-ex-did-some-questionable-things-i-feel-responsible-for-staying-and-for-not-being-more-explicit-about)

**Question:** I'm struggling with feelings of guilt and self-blame about my past relationship. While I recognize my ex did some questionable things, I feel responsible for staying and for not being more explicit about my boundaries. I had mentioned being open to sex at one point (though we never had it), and I worry that confused him and led to his behavior. I feel manipulative for "leading him on," and when I talk to others, they ask why I didn't just leave. Am I a bad person?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you so much for trusting us with this. You are absolutely not a bad person. It is understandable to feel this wave of self-doubt, questioning whether you somehow caused or contributed to your ex's actions. The feelings you're describing are incredibly common responses to experiencing harm in a relationship, and they don't reflect who you are as a person. They reflect how difficult and complex these situations truly are.</p>
<p>You used the word "questionable" to describe some of the things your ex did, and I want you to know that your instinct matters here. While you haven't shared the specific details of what happened, if something felt questionable to you (e.g. if it made you uncomfortable, confused, or unsure) that feeling is worth trusting. Sometimes we minimize our own experiences or second-guess ourselves, especially when we care about someone or feel conflicted about the relationship. But your gut reaction is telling you something important, and you don't need to have all the answers or the "right" words to describe it for your feelings to be valid.</p>
<p>You are not a bad person for staying with him or for feeling unsure about how to communicate your boundaries. Relationships can be filled with hope and confusion at the same time, especially when you care about someone and genuinely believe they can change or respect you if you just explain things better. The fact that you remained in the relationship does not make you responsible for someone else's decision to act in a way that hurt you. Many of us want to see the best in our partners, fear conflict, or worry that leaving might cause more harm than staying. None of these reasons reflect poorly on you. They reflect the complexity of human relationships. You cared enough to give him space to respect your boundaries, and he chose not to. That is not your fault.</p>
<p>Expressing openness to something at one point doesn't give anyone permission to cross your boundaries or do things that felt wrong to you at a later point. Consent and healthy boundaries involve ongoing communication, but even if you felt uncertain, that does not excuse how he treated you. Consent isn't a blanket statement that covers all future interactions. It's an ongoing conversation that can change at any moment. It's natural to wonder if you gave mixed signals or "led him on," yet the responsibility lies with him to honor your comfort level, rather than assume unlimited permission because of one conversation about sex. You are allowed to explore your feelings without it meaning you deserve mistreatment. Feeling uncertain or not knowing how to say no in the moment does not mean you invited whatever followed.</p>
<p>When people ask why you didn't leave, they are simplifying a very real emotional dynamic. The question "why didn't you just leave?" ignores the reality that leaving is often far more complicated than it appears from the outside, and it unfairly places responsibility on you rather than on the person who caused harm. You mentioned worrying that you were manipulative or that you led him on. Changing your mind, having mixed feelings, or being uncertain about what you want are all completely normal human experiences-- they're not manipulation. Manipulation involves intentionally deceiving or controlling someone for your own benefit. Navigating your own feelings and boundaries, even imperfectly, is not the same thing.</p>
<p>What you're experiencing sounds much more like the painful aftermath of a confusing and harmful relationship, combined with the unfair judgments of others who may not understand trauma responses. You have every right to put your own well-being first and to find support where you feel valued and respected. You deserve kindness and compassion. From others and from yourself. You are not alone, and you do not deserve to carry guilt for someone else's choices.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-10-years-old-i-initiated-sexual-play-with-a-younger-female-friend-who-was-3-or-4-years-younger-than-me-the-first-time-i-asked-if-she-was-comfortable-and-she-said-yes-and-we-kissed-for-a-min)

**Question:** When I was 10 years old, I initiated sexual play with a younger female friend who was 3 or 4 years younger than me. The first time I asked if she was comfortable and she said yes, and we kissed for a minute or two. About a week later, I asked again but she said no, and I was okay with that. But then I suggested we play a game where if I won, I could kiss her, and she agreed. After I won, we kissed one last time before her parents found out. Her parents forgave me, but I was beaten by my mother. I was physically abused throughout my childhood, witnessed my parents argue and engage in physical violence, was sexually abused by a female teacher, and was exposed to adult content starting around age 6. I'm older now and struggling with guilt about what happened when I was 10. How do I understand this?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting this space with something so heavy and painful. The fact that you're reaching out shows tremendous courage, and I want you to know that you deserve compassion as you work through these complicated feelings.</p>
<p>When children experience abuse and are exposed to sexual content before they're developmentally ready, it profoundly impacts how they understand boundaries, relationships, and appropriate behavior. This doesn't excuse harmful actions, but it's essential context for understanding how a 10-year-old child might engage in sexualized behavior without fully comprehending what they were doing.</p>
<p>At 10 years old, your brain was still developing the capacity to understand consent, power dynamics, and the potential impact of sexual behavior. Children who have been abused or exposed to adult content often re-enact what has happened to them or what they've seen, not because they want to harm anyone, but because they're trying to make sense of confusing experiences. This is a recognized pattern in trauma research. You were a child who was still learning what was okay and what wasn't, navigating new feelings without the knowledge or emotional tools you have today. The age difference you mention and the fact that you created a game to get around her initial "no" are concerning behaviors, but they occurred when you yourself were a child who had been harmed and was likely acting out of confusion rather than malicious intent.</p>
<p>Child-on-child sexual behavior can be incredibly complicated, especially when children have been exposed to violence or have had difficult family environments. It sounds like some of the time you were both curious in the way children often are, yet there was also a moment where she said no, and later she agreed under conditions of a game. It's understandable to look back now, with your current understanding of right and wrong, and feel deep regret or wonder about the nature of what happened. This doesn't minimize the younger child's perspective, but it does recognize that you were also a child doing your best to navigate feelings and situations you weren't equipped to handle.</p>
<p>The guilt you're carrying is actually a sign that you understand something harmful could have occurred, even if you didn't fully understand it at the time. That awareness matters deeply. Many survivors of childhood sexual abuse struggle when they realize they also engaged in problematic sexual behavior as children. It creates a painful duality of being both someone who was harmed and someone who caused harm. Both truths can exist simultaneously, and holding both doesn't make you irredeemable or beyond help. Your willingness to reflect on this and ask hard questions now shows that you care about doing the right thing. That recognition matters a lot.</p>
<p>If thinking about this weighs on you or you notice it affecting your self-esteem or relationships, that's a sign you deserve trustworthy support. Consider speaking with a trauma-informed therapist or counselor who specializes in childhood sexual abuse and complex trauma. They can help you process both what was done to you and what happened with your friend, without judgment or condemnation. Talking through emotions like guilt, confusion, shame, or regret in a safe and confidential space can bring real relief. Many people discover that sharing in this kind of environment helps them see their younger self in a more forgiving light and understand how their earliest experiences shaped them.</p>
<p>Healing isn't about deserving criticism. It's about understanding the full picture of how trauma affected you, developing genuine accountability, and learning healthy ways to relate to others moving forward. You were a child who needed protection, guidance, and appropriate boundaries, and you didn't receive those things. You deserve the opportunity to heal and grow into someone who treats others with the respect and care you didn't always receive. It was brave of you to reach out, and seeking help and understanding is part of taking good care of your emotional well-being, both now and in the future. You are not alone in questioning events that happened when you were a child, and you deserve kindness, understanding, and support as you navigate this difficult terrain. Thank you for trusting us with this.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-around-6-years-old-another-child-of-similar-age-asked-me-to-reenact-a-bedroom-scene-from-a-movie-with-her-at-school-in-a-secret-location-she-undressed-me-by-removing-my-backpack-and-had-me)

**Question:** When I was around 6 years old, another child of similar age asked me to reenact a bedroom scene from a movie with her at school in a secret location. She "undressed" me by removing my backpack and had me lay on a raised platform, and at one point she either kissed or simulated kissing my chest area over my clothes. This may have happened a couple of times. I knew this incident always existed but never thought much about it. I don't know why I keep remembering this now with so many details after many years, and I'm struggling to understand what this experience was and what it means. I also feel ashamed when I think about it. Can you help me make sense of this?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out to us and sharing this difficult memory with us. First, I want you to know that whatever you're feeling about this experience is valid, including the shame and confusion you mentioned. These feelings don't mean you did anything wrong. They're common responses when we're processing experiences that felt uncomfortable or that we didn't fully understand at the time.</p>
<p>When children see adult situations before they can fully understand them, it can lead to moments of curiosity or mimicry that may not feel significant at the time, yet can later create confusion and shame. When children engage in sexual behaviors with other children, it exists on a spectrum. Some childhood sexual behaviors are developmentally normal exploration, while others can be harmful even when both children are young. The fact that you were both young children, close in age, often means neither child truly grasped adult ideas about boundaries or consent. However, your present-day feelings still matter deeply.
