What often determines whether an experience was harmful isn't just what happened, but how it felt to you (for example, whether there was pressure, secrecy, confusion, or an uncomfortable power dynamic) and whether you truly wanted to participate or felt you couldn't say no. The fact that this took place in a "secret location" and involved someone directing you to do things suggests there may have been elements that didn't feel entirely comfortable or consensual to you, even if you couldn't name that at the time. Your body and mind may have registered something about this experience that didn't feel right, and that can be enough for it to have been impactful.</p>
<p>The shame you're feeling is unfortunately very common, but I want to be clear: you were a child, and children are never responsible for sexual experiences initiated by others, even when those others are also children. You didn't do anything wrong by being in that situation. Shame often attaches itself to experiences that felt confusing or that violated our boundaries, even when we weren't at fault. Sometimes shame appears because when we look at these memories through our adult eyes, we apply our current understanding of intimacy and appropriate behavior to a moment when we were too young to have that awareness. Your reactions do not make you responsible for what happened, nor do they lessen whatever discomfort you felt then or now.</p>
<p>It's also completely normal for memories like this to resurface years later, especially with increased detail and emotional intensity. Regaining memories or suddenly recalling new details is very normal. Sometimes our minds revisit events we did not analyze before when we feel safer or have more insight into what happened. This often happens when we reach a developmental stage where we can better understand what occurred, when something in our present life triggers the memory, or when we finally feel safe enough to process it. Your brain may have protected you from fully examining this memory until now, and the fact that it's coming up doesn't mean something is wrong with you. It may actually mean your mind is ready to make sense of it.</p>
<p>You get to define what this experience means to you. You don't have to label it in any particular way or meet anyone else's criteria for it to matter. If this memory is causing you distress, if it's affecting how you feel about yourself or your relationships, or if you simply want support in processing it, those are all valid reasons to seek help. Your sense of confusion does not take away from the fact that you were six years old, discovering something unfamiliar and complicated for the first time. You deserved a chance to learn about boundaries, bodies, and affection in a context free from secrecy and shame.</p>
<p>Even though many years have passed, you have every right to acknowledge that this memory has impact, and it is okay to seek some clarity and comfort now. Speaking with a trauma-informed therapist or advocate who specializes in childhood experiences could provide you with a safe space to explore these feelings without judgment and to find ways to move forward that feel right for you. You deserve support in making sense of this, you deserve to release the shame that doesn't belong to you, and above all, you are not alone.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-suffer-from-complex-ptsd-and-have-endured-psychological-and-religious-abuse-from-my-family-since-childhood-multiple-sexual-assaults-and-power-harassment-i-was-misdiagnosed-denied-my-trauma-and-treat)

**Question:** I suffer from complex PTSD and have endured psychological and religious abuse from my family since childhood, multiple sexual assaults, and power harassment. I was misdiagnosed, denied my trauma, and treated poorly by several private physicians who downplayed my symptoms and suffering. Even in these circumstances, counselors focused on somatic approaches to trauma without providing psychological education or stabilization, resulting in medical trauma and re-traumatization. As a result, I was unable to seek proper treatment until recently. My health continued to deteriorate, and I was unable to continue working, so I left. I have a trusting relationship with my current counselor and doctor, and I am currently at the same hospital, but due to certain circumstances, I am likely to change doctors and hospitals. While my sleep disorders have continued to improve, I suffer from rapid dissociation, hyperarousal, and high-functioning, high-introspection symptoms that are difficult to break down in public. I have also had chronic suicidal ideation since childhood, and when my symptoms are severe, I sometimes wish I could die. My divorce coincided with the past few months, and I was feeling unwell. For the past two months, I would experience hyperventilation, trembling, and dizziness when I called for a consultation or thought about separation. In the past, I would experience physical and emotional flashbacks of abuse, power harassment, and bullying, even before they worsened. These flashbacks would occur for hours, even when I was alone. Treatment has helped me feel much better, but I'm now afraid of contact from my family. I still have flashbacks of the sexual assault trauma, sometimes waking up in the middle of the night or during the day. My sleep has been disrupted, and I've been unable to sleep at all lately. I've had to adjust my medication several times, and now I can't maintain five to six or seven hours of sleep without taking six different medications, including antipsychotics, mood stabilizers, and anti-anxiety medications. Even before my appointment, I would experience symptoms and be forced to rest in bed. I'm taking anti-anxiety medication within the limit a few times a day. University hospitals have been considered, but given my symptoms, would it be better to see a doctor there? Finding a trauma specialist for complex PTSD and a specialized clinic is a struggle. Even when I speak to the public health center, they only give me a search page, so I can only rely on their website, which I don't really understand. Just thinking about a specialized clinic scares me to tears because of past medical trauma. Nothing has been decided yet, so we'll have to start next year and discuss it, and I don't think I'll be doing all the paperwork myself, but I'm constantly anxious and scared, and it makes me cry. There are times when I'm so exhausted I can't even read my favorite books, and I'm also going to have to discuss mental health welfare certificates and disability pensions. If I need to change my doctor or hospital, would it be better to consider a university hospital? I live in the second largest metropolitan area in Japan, divided into eight blocks.

**Answer:** <p data-start="103" data-end="268">You've been carrying a lot on your own for a long time.<br data-start="135" data-end="138" /> What you've shared with us—your long journey seeking trauma-sensitive support, your repeated encounters with misdiagnoses and inadequate support, and your chronic hypervigilance and flashbacks—speaks to how much your mind and body have been through.</p><p data-start="270" data-end="472"> Having had repeated experiences of being hurt in medical settings where they should have received help, it is only natural that they would be cautious and wary of new medical professionals.<br data-start="354" data-end="357" /> Larger medical institutions, such as university hospitals, may have departments and teams that specialize in complex trauma-related conditions and use research-based approaches, including psychologists and psychiatrists who understand complex PTSD and its underlying causes.</p><p data-start="474" data-end="653"> However, this does not necessarily guarantee that you will be able to receive "immediate relief" or "trauma-informed interaction that is tailored to you." Whether you find a truly supportive clinical environment, whether at a university hospital or a private clinic, ultimately depends largely on whether the doctors and support staff are willing to respect your pace, listen carefully to what you have to say, and not rush you into unnecessary treatment.</p><p data-start="655" data-end="783"> The trust you have built with your current counselor, despite the many difficulties you have experienced up until now, is a great source of strength for you. If you must change hospitals for some reason, one way to go about it is to ask your current supporter if they can introduce you to a reliable specialist institution or medical professional.</p><p data-start="785" data-end="983"> Even when considering a large medical institution, it can be helpful to communicate in advance what you need to feel at ease. For example, asking them to review your medical history before your first visit, ensuring sufficient consultation time, and sharing your triggers and points to consider in advance. Having a trusted person accompany you in situations where flashbacks or hyperarousal are likely to occur is also an important way to protect yourself.</p><p data-start="985" data-end="1139"> Given your past experiences, it is natural to feel very anxious about changing medical providers. However, please remember <strong data-start="1039" data-end="1063">that your peace of mind and safety are of utmost importance.</strong><br data-start="1063" data-end="1066" /> If you ever feel pressured or misunderstood in a new relationship with a supporter, you always have the right to stop and reconsider.</p><p data-start="1141" data-end="1326"> As you make these choices, you may experience waves of fatigue and other physical reactions. If it's difficult to move, ask those around you for help with administrative tasks like finding and contacting medical institutions. Strong physical and mental reactions during change don't mean you're weak. They're a natural response to having been in "survival mode" for so long.</p><p data-start="1328" data-end="1568"> You continue to search for an environment that will lead to recovery and healing while respecting the path you have walked. Please cherish the sense of trust you have with your current counselor and take it with you to your new place.<br data-start="1418" data-end="1421" /> The fact that you've made it this far is a testament to your strength. Even in the midst of deep fear and despair, your resilience is there. Even when the road ahead feels uncertain, it's not impossible to find someone who will treat you with compassion and respect. You deserve that care.

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/what-should-i-do-about-my-anger-towards-the-perpetrator-824)

**Question:** What should I do about my anger towards the perpetrator?

**Answer:** <p data-start="133" data-end="306">First, let me tell you that feeling anger towards someone who has hurt you is a very natural and valid reaction.<br data-start="188" data-end="191" /> There is no need to blame yourself or feel ashamed of your feelings, thinking, "I shouldn't be feeling this way" or "I should forgive the other person." The fact that you feel anger speaks to the fact that you have been hurt.</p><p data-start="308" data-end="532"> Sometimes, anger can come over you in waves so strong that it's hard to process. Anger is a natural response to injustice and unfairness, especially when your important boundaries and dignity are violated.<br data-start="433" data-end="436" /> If you are aware of your anger, it means that you are trying to feel and value your own emotions. Your emotions are born to protect you. Please don't forget that.</p><p data-start="534" data-end="667"> You don't have to force yourself to forgive, or try to dismiss or minimize your anger, but it's also important to find ways to gradually let it out in a way that doesn't cause you more pain or burden you with shame or guilt.</p><p data-start="669" data-end="821"> Some people find it supportive to have someone they can trust listen to them without being judged or rejected. This can be a friend, family member, counselor, trauma care professional, or anyone else. Putting your feelings into words and entrusting them to someone who will simply listen can be an important step in giving your anger and pain a place to live.</p><p data-start="823" data-end="945"> For others, writing in a journal, expressing themselves through art or music, or gently moving their body by taking a walk can help them safely release the emotions that have built up inside them. What works for each person is different, so it's okay to choose a method that feels even slightly comfortable to you.</p><p data-start="947" data-end="1098"> Anger is not an emotion that needs to be "turned off" in order for trauma to heal. It is an important part of your recovery process that should be acknowledged and respected. With time and support, anger often changes form, and even if it doesn't go away completely, it may become more manageable and less overwhelming.</p><p data-start="1100" data-end="1278"> Furthermore, in the process of recovering from such betrayal or deep hurt, it is not uncommon for other emotions, such as sadness or fear, to quietly exist beneath the anger. These are all natural reactions, evidence that the mind and body are responding to the deep wounds.<br data-start="1203" data-end="1206" /> If you feel that it is too difficult to deal with on your own, one important option is to seek professional support, such as a counselor who understands trauma.</p><p data-start="1280" data-end="1348"> You deserve support to work through these strong emotions without hurting anyone else, and most importantly, without hurting yourself.</p><p data-start="1350" data-end="1428"> Any page is fine.<br data-start="1364" data-end="1367" /> I hope that you can gently give yourself permission to feel that way, treat yourself kindly, and move forward little by little, at your own pace.

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-7-i-had-a-sleepover-with-a-friend-also-7-we-watched-a-movie-with-a-scene-involving-truth-or-dare-and-skinny-dipping-which-led-us-to-play-a-game-that-included-being-nakeddoing-things-like-ki)

**Question:** When I was 7, I had a sleepover with a friend (also 7). We watched a movie with a scene involving Truth or Dare and skinny dipping, which led us to play a game that included being naked—doing things like kicking a ball. It ended with us pretending to get married, which involved kissing on the lips and a peck on the butt. Her parents discovered us, and I could hear them screaming at her while she sobbed. They made me leave immediately, and I felt deeply ashamed even though I didn't understand why. I have an OCD diagnosis, and after learning about COCSA, I'm now doubting my memories. Was it my idea? Did I pressure her? The memories are choppy, and I'm anxious that I might have done something terrible. How do I make sense of this experience?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you so much for reaching out about this. Based on what you shared, what you're describing sounds like age-appropriate childhood play. When children around 7 years old engage in exploratory behavior, especially behavior inspired by something they saw in media, it typically reflects natural curiosity rather than harmful intent. The elements you described (e.g. playing pretend, imitating what you saw, mutual participation in a game) are consistent with normal childhood development. Children at that age are learning about their bodies, social roles, and boundaries, often through imitation and play.</p>
<p>The trauma you experienced that night appears to have come from the adults' reaction, not from the play itself. The screaming, your friend sobbing, the abrupt removal from the home, and the profound shame you felt--these are the elements that left a lasting impact. When adults respond to childhood exploration with anger and punishment rather than gentle redirection, it can create deep confusion and shame in children who didn't understand they were doing anything wrong. That shame you felt, even without understanding why, makes complete sense given how the adults reacted. Their response taught you that something terrible had happened, even though the behavior itself was developmentally normal.</p>
<p>The intensity of the parents' reaction may have been amplified because you were both girls. Many people hold beliefs that same-sex behavior, even between young children, is inappropriate or concerning in ways that similar behavior between boys and girls might not be. If the family held conservative values, they may have responded not just to the sexual exploration itself, but to discomfort around the same-sex nature of it. This adds another painful layer to your experience. You may have internalized not just shame about childhood curiosity, but shame connected to same-sex interaction.</p>
<p>It's important to recognize that childhood exploration between same-gender peers is completely normal and happens among children who grow up to identify in all different ways--some as LGBTQ and many who don't. Exploring with another girl as a child doesn't mean anything definitive about your identity, and it certainly wasn't wrong. The discomfort existed in their reaction, not in what you were doing.</p>
<p>Your OCD is likely latching onto this memory because there's ambiguity and uncertainty around it, which OCD tends to exploit. The "what ifs" you're experiencing (e.g. What if it was my idea? What if I pressured her? What if I'm remembering wrong?) are characteristic of OCD's need for certainty about moral responsibility. The choppy, vague nature of childhood memories is completely normal. Our brains don't form detailed, linear memories at that age, especially of events that seemed unremarkable at the time. The fact that your memories are fragmented doesn't mean you're forgetting something sinister. It means you were 7.</p>
<p>Nothing in what you've described suggests coercion, pressure, or harm. You were both the same age, you were both participating, and you were both imitating something you'd seen. It doesn't seem to me that you were doing something terrible. It sounds like you were a child being a child.</p>
<p>The shame belongs to that experience of being shamed, not to the play that preceded it. If you feel ready, sharing this with your therapist, particularly one who understands both OCD and childhood development, might help you process both the traumatic reaction you experienced and the way your OCD is using this memory against you now. You deserve compassion for what you went through, both as a 7-year-old and as an adult trying to make sense of it. Thank you for trusting us with this.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-younger-i-was-assaulted-by-a-15-year-old-who-told-me-we-were-playing-a-game-i-dont-remember-all-the-details-but-i-became-more-physical-with-my-own-body-afterward-i-recently-learned-that-whe)

**Question:** When I was younger, I was assaulted by a 15-year-old who told me we were playing a game. I don't remember all the details, but I became more physical with my own body afterward. I recently learned that when I was 4, I had problematic sexual behavior with my younger brother. The guilt is overwhelming, and I'm constantly anxious about it. Now I'm more aware of my body, and even though I know I'm not aroused, I keep worrying "what if I am?" I feel gross and disgusting, and I keep thinking: am I a pedophile? Is this normal?

**Answer:** <p>I'm so sorry you've been carrying this heavy sense of guilt and fear. I want you to know that what you're experiencing are all understandable responses to a really painful and confusing situation. First, let me be clear: you are not a pedophile. What happened when you were a child does not define who you are now, and the fact that you're so distressed by these memories actually shows how much you care about doing what's right.</p>
<p>When you were four years old, your brain hadn't yet developed the ability to fully understand boundaries, consent, or the impact of your actions. At that age, children are often just beginning to learn the difference between appropriate and inappropriate touch, and any sexual behaviors can arise more from curiosity or mimicry than from a true desire to harm someone. This doesn't excuse actions that hurt others, but it can help explain them.</p>
<p>Being sexually assaulted by the 15-year-old during your own childhood may have further complicated how you perceived and explored your body as a child. When children experience sexual abuse or exposure to sexual content beyond their developmental understanding, it's not uncommon for them to engage in sexual behaviors with other children. This is often a way that young children try to make sense of confusing experiences or recreate what happened to them. What you did as a four-year-old was not the same as an adult making a conscious, predatory choice. That doesn't erase the potential impact on your brother, and your feelings of guilt show you understand that, but it's crucial to recognize that you were also a child who had been harmed.</p>
<p>The intrusive thoughts you're having (e.g. the "what if I am aroused" fears) are a common symptom of trauma, especially when guilt and shame are involved. These are called intrusive thoughts, and they're not reflections of your true desires or identity. Feeling worried about arousal doesn't mean you're actually attracted to children. Our bodies can respond in unanticipated ways when we're revisiting trauma or uncertainty, even if our minds are deeply uncomfortable. Your heightened awareness of your body and the anxiety around it are trauma responses.</p>
<p>When we've experienced sexual harm, our relationship with our own bodies can become complicated. Sometimes we become hypervigilant, constantly checking in with ourselves, trying to control or understand sensations that feel scary or shameful. These difficulties often reveal the complexities of how the brain and body process experiences when we're too young to understand them clearly. The more you try to push them away or analyze them, the stickier they can become. It's important to know that having these thoughts doesn't mean they're true, and it doesn't mean you want them to be true.</p>
<p>It can help to know that child maladaptive sexual behaviors, especially at four years old, rarely predict harmful adult behavior. You deserve the chance to explore these conflicting emotions with kindness toward the child you once were, because that child was coping with things that shouldn't have happened in the first place. Healing from this will likely involve working with a trauma-informed therapist who can help you process what happened to you, address the guilt you're carrying, and learn skills to manage the intrusive thoughts and anxiety.</p>
<p>You deserve support and compassion as you navigate this, and you deserve to understand that your worth as a person isn't defined by what happened when you were a child. You're asking these questions because you care deeply about being a good person, and that matters. You are not alone in these feelings, and they do not define you. Be patient with yourself, and remember that what happened in your past does not decide who you are today.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/if-a-12-year-old-briefly-touched-and-penetrated-a-10-year-olds-vagina-without-permission-while-in-the-shower-would-this-be-considered-cocsa-if-this-were-shared-with-a-therapist-now-could-mandatory-rep)

**Question:** If a 12-year-old briefly touched and penetrated a 10-year-old's vagina without permission while in the shower, would this be considered COCSA? If this were shared with a therapist now, could mandatory reporting requirements apply? Is that because penetration makes this more serious than other types of touch?

**Answer:** <p>What you're describing does sound like it falls under the umbrella of child-on-child sexual abuse (COCSA). <a href="https://www.ourwave.org/en/post/child-on-child-sexual-abuse-cocsa-frequently-asked-questions-and-considerations">COCSA</a> refers to any sexual interaction between children where there is a significant difference in age, development, power, or where one child doesn't or cannot consent. The two-year age gap between a 12-year-old and a 10-year-old, combined with the lack of permission and the nature of the contact, leads me to believe that this situation meets those criteria.</p>
<p>When it comes to mandatory reporting, the answer is complex and depends on several factors. Therapists and other mandated reporters are required to report suspected child abuse, but how they assess a situation like this varies. They consider things like the ages of the children involved, the nature of the contact, whether there are ongoing safety concerns, and the context in which the behavior occurred. Penetration is often viewed as more serious in reporting guidelines, yes, but it's not solely about the type of touch. It's also about whether there's current risk of harm to any child involved.</p>
<p>If you were to share this with a therapist now, they would likely assess whether there are any current safety concerns. If both individuals are now adults, the risk assessment changes significantly. Some therapists may not file a report if everyone involved is now an adult and there's no indication of ongoing abuse or risk to other children. However, laws vary by location, and some jurisdictions have stricter reporting requirements that could apply even to historical abuse. It's worth knowing that many therapists will discuss their reporting obligations with you before you share details, giving you some control over what you choose to disclose and when.</p>
<p>Regardless of your role in this situation, what happened likely carries weight for you, and you deserve support in processing it. COCSA is complicated because it impacts everyone involved, often in different but equally significant ways. If you were the younger child, you may be carrying confusion, shame, or questions about what happened and why. If you were the older child, you may be struggling with guilt, shame, or trying to understand why you engaged in that behavior. Often children who act out sexually are responding to their own experiences of trauma, exposure to sexual content, or developmental challenges they didn't have the tools to manage.</p>
<p>Both positions can leave lasting emotional impacts, and both deserve compassionate, informed support. If you're considering therapy, it might help to ask a potential therapist about their approach to mandatory reporting and how they handle disclosures of childhood experiences before sharing details. You deserve to feel safe and in control of your healing process, whatever your experience was. Thanks for trusting us with this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-younger-a-substitute-teacher-in-my-theater-class-took-me-into-the-hallway-to-work-on-projection-for-my-narrator-role-i-dont-remember-much-except-him-getting-really-close-to-me-and-making-me)

**Question:** When I was younger, a substitute teacher in my theater class took me into the hallway to work on projection for my narrator role. I don't remember much except him getting really close to me and making me very uncomfortable. I don't know if he touched me, but I remember going back to my friends feeling very freaked out and scared. I completely forgot about this until recently, and I'm struggling to make sense of it while not knowing everything that happened. I don't know how to feel about the situation or how to go about processing it.

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting us with this.&nbsp;What you're experiencing is incredibly common among people who have experienced harm, and I want you to know that your confusion and distress are completely valid. It sounds like this memory caught you by surprise, especially since it resurfaced after so many years. It is common for certain details or entire experiences to stay hidden in our minds until something in our adult life brings them back. The fact that you felt scared and freaked out in that moment tells you something important. It sounds like your body and instincts recognized that something was wrong, even if you can't remember all the details now.</p>
<p>Memory gaps around uncomfortable or frightening experiences are actually a normal part of how our brains protect us from overwhelming situations. When something distressing happens, our minds sometimes file away only fragments (e.g. sensations, feelings, or snapshots) rather than a complete narrative. Not remembering everything can feel frustrating or unsettling, yet it does not diminish how uncomfortable and frightened you felt. Your body and emotions recognized that something in that moment was wrong or alarming, and those feelings alone are important. This doesn't mean your experience wasn't significant or that your feelings about it aren't real. The fear you felt then and the discomfort you're experiencing now are valid regardless of whether you can recall every detail. There is no right or wrong way to feel about what happened or what you recall.</p>
<p>Many people find that healing can involve giving themselves permission to explore the emotions they do have, rather than forcing every memory to return in perfect order. You may never have a complete picture of that day, and that is okay. Healing doesn't require a complete picture of what happened. It often requires honoring what you do know and how you felt. If you find that thinking about this moment is causing you distress, it might help to confide in someone supportive who understands or seek professional guidance from someone able to talk through these mixed emotions with empathy and understanding.</p>
<p>Some people find it helpful to talk with a therapist who specializes in trauma, as they can provide tools for processing fragmented memories and managing the feelings that come with them. Others find healing through journaling, creative expression, or simply acknowledging that something happened that made them feel unsafe. Sharing your feelings, at your own pace, can often bring more clarity or at least more peace around the lingering questions.</p>
<p>Above all, trust that your response back then and the instinctive ways you went about creating safety. You get to decide how you want to move forward with this, and you're allowed to take your time figuring out what feels right for you. What matters most is that you're listening to yourself and taking your experience seriously, because it deserves to be taken seriously. Thank you for trusting us with this. We are here for you.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/my-father-has-kept-me-trapped-at-home-since-my-childhood-i-cannot-go-out-without-his-monitoring-even-though-i-have-never-done-anything-wrong-now-that-i-am-at-university-i-still-cannot-go-anywhere-with)

**Question:** My father has kept me trapped at home since my childhood. I cannot go out without his monitoring even though I have never done anything wrong. Now that I am at university I still cannot go anywhere without his control and I feel like I am suffocating. What should I do?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting us with this. I'm really sorry you have lived under such tight control for so long, and I hear how suffocating it is to feel you have no freedom. Feeling trapped at home with no ability to go out, even now that you are in university, is not a small issue and can affect not just your emotions but your sense of who you are.</p>
<p>What you're experiencing is a form of controlling behavior that can have real impacts on your wellbeing, confidence, and ability to develop independence. You mentioned that you've never done anything wrong, and I want you to hear this clearly: the behaviors you're describing are a reflection of your father's choices, not a result of anything you did wrong. You did nothing to deserve being kept home or constantly monitored.</p>
<p>The suffocating feelings you're experiencing make complete sense. Humans have a fundamental need for self-determination and the ability to make choices about their own lives. When that need is consistently denied, it can lead to feelings of helplessness, anxiety, frustration, and even depression. Your emotional response isn't a sign of weakness. It's your internal system recognizing that something important is being taken from you. You deserve respect, care, and the chance to grow without constant oversight.</p>
<p>If you can, consider finding someone trustworthy to confide in. Maybe a university counselor, a mentor, or someone at a campus resource center. It might feel overwhelming to take this step, but having a safe place to talk can make a real difference. A counselor could provide emotional support and help you explore what options might be available based on your specific circumstances. They may also know of confidential ways to help you navigate your situation. In many areas, there are support organizations that help individuals dealing with controlling family members. Reaching out for guidance isn't disloyal or wrong. It can be a critical step toward feeling safer and more in control of your future.</p>
<p>Only you can assess what choices are realistic and safe for you right now, and your situation may have cultural or familial dimensions that make it more complex. As you navigate this, it may help to identify small ways you can cultivate a sense of agency within constraints. This could include making choices about your studies, building relationships with trusted peers, or finding meaningful activities within your allowed parameters. Building even one trusted connection can make a significant difference in how isolated you feel.</p>
<p>You deserve a space where you can live as the person you want to be. Whatever steps you're able to take, even small ones, are valid and important. Continue to hold onto your inner strength, and know that understanding people exist and are ready to support you. Thank you again for trusting us with this.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/im-a-survivor-of-childhood-sexual-abuse-and-cocsa-im-scared-that-if-i-ever-decide-to-explore-relationships-romantically-and-sexually-im-going-to-harm-others-everyone-i-see-talks-about-concerns-of-bein)

**Question:** I'm a survivor of childhood sexual abuse and COCSA. I'm scared that if I ever decide to explore relationships romantically and sexually, I'm going to harm others. Everyone I see talks about concerns of being assaulted again, never about fearing becoming an assailant themselves. Am I broken for this? I feel damaged in a way that I'll only hurt anyone who comes close to me. Am I a monster? What do I do?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting us with this. What you're experiencing is actually more common than you might think, though it's talked about far less openly. Many survivors carry the worry that the abuse they experienced might somehow have "infected" them or made them dangerous to others. This fear doesn't make you broken, and it certainly doesn't make you a monster. In fact, the very anxiety you're feeling often indicates the opposite: you care deeply about not causing harm, which is fundamentally incompatible with being someone who would actually hurt others. The part of you that worries so strongly about causing harm is also proof that you want to do the right thing.</p>
<p>There's an important distinction between having intrusive thoughts or fears about causing harm and actually wanting to cause harm or being at risk of doing so. Your fear is a form of hypervigilance that many trauma survivors experience. Your brain is trying to protect you and others by staying alert to any possible danger, including the idea that you yourself might become a source of harm. This could be your mind's way of trying to maintain control in a world that once felt terrifyingly out of control. The fact that these thoughts distress you so much is actually evidence that they don't reflect your true character or intentions. Feelings of guilt or fear of hurting others can be a common reaction to sexual trauma when you've known violation so intimately and dread doing anything that remotely resembles it.</p>
<p>The relationship between experiencing abuse and later perpetrating it is far more nuanced than many people realize. The vast majority of survivors never harm anyone. Most research shows that being harmed in childhood does not automatically turn survivors into perpetrators, especially for those who grow up aware of boundaries and truly don't want to hurt anyone. While it's true that many people who do harm others have histories of trauma, most trauma survivors do not go on to hurt others. What makes the crucial difference is awareness, the desire not to cause harm, and the willingness to be reflective about your behaviors and their impact--all things you're already demonstrating by even asking this question. The fact that you're thinking about this so seriously means you're taking responsibility for your actions, which is the opposite of how abusers behave.</p>
<p>Feeling damaged comes from the trauma you've been through, not from any real sign that you pose a threat. The painful shock of that abuse can twist your sense of self into believing there must be something inherently wrong with you. You are not alone in having these thoughts. They usually come from wanting to protect others from pain you yourself know all too well. You deserve to be treated with kindness and understanding, both by others and by yourself. Even if you don't fully believe it yet, you do deserve love, closeness, and genuine peace in all parts of your life.</p>
<p>When you're ready to explore relationships, what will matter most is bringing your whole self, including your awareness of your history and your concerns. Healthy relationships are built on communication, respect for boundaries, ongoing consent, and the ability to recognize and repair when we've made mistakes. Because all humans sometimes miss cues or misunderstand situations. The goal isn't perfection-- it's attentiveness and care. Your awareness of the potential for harm, combined with a genuine desire not to cause it, can actually help you be more conscientious and attuned to your partners than many people who've never questioned these things about themselves. If you do start exploring romantic or sexual connections, it may help to move slowly and choose settings where clear communication, consent, and boundaries feel comfortable for you.</p>
<p>You're not irreparably damaged, and it's possible to build healthy, respectful connections that honor both your own experiences and the well-being of those you care about. If these fears continue to feel overwhelming, working with a trauma-informed therapist who specializes in sexual trauma can be incredibly valuable. They can help you distinguish between the fears your trauma has created and the reality of who you are, and support you in building confidence in your capacity for healthy, consensual connections with others when and if you choose to pursue them. Thank you for reaching out to us. We appreciate you.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/from-ages-6-or-7-until-10-my-sister-and-i-would-act-out-sexual-acts-together-mimicking-what-we-saw-in-movies-we-stopped-around-age-10-when-we-realized-it-was-inappropriate-at-age-12-we-briefly-re-enac)

**Question:** From ages 6 or 7 until 10, my sister and I would act out sexual acts together, mimicking what we saw in movies. We stopped around age 10 when we realized it was inappropriate. At age 12, we briefly re-enacted sexual acts and again realized it was inappropriate. Later, my mother's husband engaged in inappropriate behavior with me including inappropriate conversations, comments about my body, and eventually sending pornography and sex toys when I was 14. My mother knew about his behavior. After receiving these items, my sister told me we were alone in the world and must give each other sexual experiences. I said no but she persisted, and eventually performed oral sex on me. I tried to reciprocate but couldn't, and nothing further happened between us. I know what my mother's husband did was grooming and abuse, but what about my sister? She has apologized but I don't think she understands the impact. I can't have partners perform oral sex on me to this day and have anxiety around sex. Is what happened with my sister abuse?

**Answer:** <p>I'm so sorry for everything you experienced. I want to start by naming that what happened with your mother's husband sounds like grooming and sexual abuse. He was an adult in a position of power who deliberately created situations to sexualize you, violated appropriate boundaries, exposed you to pornographic material, and manipulated the relationship between you and your sister through his actions. Your mother's failure to protect you and her participation in sharing private information about your body may have compounded this harm. None of what he did was your fault. His grooming didn't just directly harm you, it also may have created an environment that influenced how your sister understood relationships, boundaries, and sexuality as well.</p>
<p>Regarding your sister, what you're describing is complex because it involves different circumstances at different ages. The sexual play in early childhood, when you were both very young children engaging in similar behaviors together, often falls into a category that reflects childhood curiosity rather than abuse. What changed at age 14 is significant. By that time, you were both teenagers who had been exposed to your mother's husband's grooming, pornography, and the explicit message that you should provide sexual experiences for each other. When your sister told you that you were alone in the world and must give each other sexual experiences...when she persisted after you said no...and when she performed oral sex on you despite your reluctance, she crossed a boundary that caused you harm.</p>
<p>Even when siblings are close in age, sexual contact that involves pressure or coercion can be a form of sibling sexual abuse, especially when one person feels unable to refuse or when the environment has stripped away the possibility of true, informed consent. It sounds like you had very little room to say no in an already unstable situation. Your sister was also harmed by the adults in your environment, yet her actions still hurt you, and both of these truths can exist simultaneously.</p>
<p>The lasting impact you describe (e.g. your inability to experience pleasure when partners perform oral sex on you, your anxiety around sex) reflects how deeply this experience affected you. Your body remembers saying no and being pressured anyway. Your body remembers that first experience of oral sex being connected to coercion rather than genuine desire and safety. This is a trauma response, and it's completely understandable given what you went through.</p>
<p>Your sister's apology suggests some awareness that she caused harm, even if she may not fully grasp the depth of its impact on your life. You get to decide what feels true for you about this experience and what terminology, if any, feels right to describe it. What matters most is acknowledging the harm you experienced and recognizing that you were a child who needed guidance and protection from adults that you simply didn't receive. Any blame you feel about what happened belongs instead to those who created and maintained an abusive environment. You deserve support that honors the complexity of your story, as well as your right to find a sense of safety, boundaries, and deeper understanding around intimacy now. It's possible to heal at your own pace while making sense of how the past shaped your life. Thank you for trusting us with this.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-experienced-sexual-abuse-as-a-child-by-two-uncles-i-believe-i-have-ptsd-depression-and-developed-ocd-i-feel-very-bad-because-i-think-that-sometimes-i-fantasized-about-my-two-uncles-when-i-was-a-teen)

**Question:** I experienced sexual abuse as a child by two uncles. I believe I have PTSD, depression, and developed OCD. I feel very bad because I think that sometimes I fantasized about my two uncles when I was a teenager and a few years ago (around age 21-22). I'm not sure if this is real because my OCD involves false memories. But I seem to remember that while dancing with one uncle, I liked feeling him close. I'm wondering if this happens with other survivors.

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting us with this. What you're describing is something that many survivors experience, and I want you to know that having these kinds of thoughts or feelings does not make what happened to you any less wrong, nor does it mean you wanted or caused the abuse. The confusion you're feeling is a common and understandable response to childhood sexual abuse, especially when the abuse was perpetrated by family members.</p>
<p>When abuse happens during childhood and adolescence, it can deeply affect how our brains and bodies develop and respond to closeness, touch, and arousal. Children and teenagers are still learning about boundaries, relationships, and their own sexuality. When abuse occurs during these formative years, it can create confusing associations between the person who hurt you and feelings of attention, closeness, or even physical sensations that your body might have responded to. This doesn't mean you wanted the abuse or that you're responsible for it in any way. It means your developing brain was trying to make sense of something that should never have happened. Trauma can sometimes blur the lines between what we feel in a moment of survival and how we later interpret or remember that experience, especially when another condition like OCD is also at play.</p>
<p>It's also important to acknowledge what you've shared about having OCD with false memories. This adds another layer of complexity because OCD can make it incredibly difficult to trust your own recollections and can create intrusive thoughts that feel real but may not reflect actual events or genuine desires. The uncertainty you're experiencing about whether these memories or feelings are real is part of how OCD can challenge survivors, making it hard to distinguish between what actually happened, what you actually felt, and what your OCD is making you question. When you have OCD, it can add extra layers of uncertainty and self-doubt about what is or is not real.</p>
<p>Many survivors speak of similar moments of confusion, questioning their experiences or feeling shame when they recall any instance where they might have welcomed closeness, even briefly. Having a brief sense of comfort or excitement during a dance, or later recalling fantasies about the person who hurt you, does not mean you consented to the abuse or that you are responsible for it in any way. It is common for survivors to carry guilt or shame about natural human responses and fleeting moments of warmth they might remember, such as enjoying a dance, seeking approval, or wanting comfort. These thoughts do not erase what was done to you, nor do they diminish the impact of the abuse. Trauma can create complicated feelings toward the people who caused harm, especially when those people are family members who may have also shown you kindness or attention at times. This doesn't change the fact that what they did was abuse and was not your fault. You were a child when the abuse began, and children cannot consent or be responsible for adult behavior toward them, regardless of any feelings or responses they may have had.</p>
<p>Be gentle with yourself and recognize that no part of you ever asked to be harmed. Having this awareness may be upsetting, but it does not define you or your healing journey. You did nothing wrong by seeking or remembering moments of closeness, and acknowledging these thoughts or fantasies now is just another step toward understanding your past on your own terms. Your healing journey is yours, and you deserve support as you process these difficult and painful experiences. You are worthy of support, care, and the opportunity to unpack these feelings at a pace that feels right for you.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-13-i-was-on-a-trip-with-my-uncle-and-we-shared-a-room-he-invited-me-to-take-off-my-pajamas-and-he-was-naked-too-he-touched-me-and-fingered-me-and-i-had-my-first-orgasm-he-told-me-to-say-sto)

**Question:** When I was 13, I was on a trip with my uncle and we shared a room. He invited me to take off my pajamas, and he was naked too. He touched me and fingered me, and I had my first orgasm. He told me to say stop if I felt uncomfortable, but I didn't say anything even though I felt terribly uncomfortable. It only happened once, and he's acted completely normal ever since, like it never happened. We still see each other at family gatherings. I don't know what to think about this. Sometimes it feels like nothing really happened because we didn't have sex and it only happened once. But I also have a lot of feelings of guilt and shame because I didn't say stop. What should I think about this experience?

**Answer:** <p>I'm so sorry you went through such a painful and confusing experience. It can be deeply upsetting to look back on an event like that and question everything, especially when it's someone in your family who made you feel uncomfortable. What your uncle did to you was sexual abuse. You were a child, and he was an adult in a position of trust and authority who had much more power and responsibility. Even if there was no visible force or "traditional" form of rape, what happened to you was still a violation.</p>
<p>It wasn't your job to know how or when to say stop, and he should never have placed you in a situation where you felt so conflicted. The fact that he told you to "say stop if you felt uncomfortable" was a manipulation tactic that placed the burden of his behavior onto you. This is not how consent works, and it's certainly not how a safe adult behaves with a child. He knew exactly what he was doing when he created conditions that made it nearly impossible for you to speak up--the shock, the fear, the power imbalance, the complete unexpectedness of the situation, and your natural instinct to freeze in the face of danger all worked against you.</p>
<p>The freeze response you experienced is one of the body's automatic survival mechanisms when faced with threat or danger. Your brain and body were doing exactly what they're designed to do to keep you safe in an impossible situation. Not saying "stop" doesn't mean you wanted this or allowed this to happen. It means your nervous system recognized that you were trapped and vulnerable, and it protected you in the only way it could in that moment. Many survivors describe feeling frozen, unable to move or speak, and this is a normal trauma response, not a choice or a failure on your part. The feelings of guilt that come when we think we "should have" said no are very common, but you did not fail or do anything wrong. It's important to recognize that your age and vulnerability put you in a position where you couldn't fully understand or control what was taking place.</p>
<p>I also want to acknowledge something that may be adding to your confusion and shame: the fact that you experienced an orgasm during the abuse. Having an orgasm doesn't mean you wanted what happened or that you were at fault in any way. Our bodies can sometimes react to non-consensual touch, and it's natural to feel deep confusion and shame about that. Physical arousal and orgasm are involuntary physiological responses. Your body responded to physical stimulation in the way bodies are designed to respond. This is purely biological and says absolutely nothing about whether you wanted or consented to what was happening. Many survivors experience arousal or orgasm during abuse, and it often becomes a source of deep shame and confusion. But having a physiological response does not mean you wanted the abuse, that you enjoyed it, or that it wasn't abuse. Your body's automatic reactions do not reflect your consent, your desires, or your responsibility. The shame you feel about this belongs to your uncle, not to you.</p>
<p>You might feel as though it "wasn't that bad" because there was no intercourse or it only happened once, but any sexual involvement an adult has with a minor is never okay, regardless of frequency or what specific act happened. Our culture often presents a very narrow picture of what sexual abuse looks like, which can make survivors question the validity of their own experiences. But what happened to you was serious. Your uncle sexually violated you, and the fact that it happened once doesn't make it less harmful or less significant. Single incidents of abuse can have profound and lasting impacts, and what you experienced was undeniably abuse.</p>
<p>The guilt and shame you're carrying belong to your uncle, not to you. He was the adult. He was responsible for maintaining appropriate boundaries. He had a responsibility to protect you, and he chose to cross a boundary that never should have been crossed. The fact that he's acted like nothing happened can be painful and isolating, especially when you see him at family gatherings. It's normal to feel conflicting emotions around him and to question your own memories&mdash;many survivors do. This is another aspect of how perpetrators often operate--they rely on this normalcy to keep survivors silent and confused, and to maintain their own reputation and access to family systems.</p>
<p>Your feelings are valid, and your experience matters. You didn't do anything wrong. You were a child who was put in an impossible situation by an adult who should have protected you. The discomfort, guilt, and shame you feel are normal responses to what was done to you, and they don't reflect the truth of what happened, which is that your uncle abused you and you survived. You deserve support and understanding, and you deserve space to cope with the aftermath of such a serious boundary violation and to heal at your own pace.</p>
<p>If possible, consider finding someone you trust to talk to about these feelings, perhaps a close friend or a helping professional who understands how these experiences can shape your thoughts and emotions. Letting someone else bear witness to your story can bring relief and remind you that what happened matters. Above all, please remember that your voice matters, your experience matters, and there is nothing you could have done to "deserve" this. You are not alone, and help is out there if and when you want it. Thank you for trusting us with your story.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/does-this-organization-help-women-in-pakistan-who-are-facing-rights-issues-other-than-sexual-assault-818)

**Question:** Does this organization help women in Pakistan who are facing rights issues other than sexual assault?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out with this question. While I'm honored you're thinking of this resource, I want to be transparent about what support is available here. This platform is specifically designed to provide information and support around sexual violence, trauma, and related healing. The focus is intentionally centered on these experiences because they require specialized, trauma-informed responses.</p>
<p>That said, many organizations work on intersecting issues of women's rights, and some may be able to help with the specific concerns you're thinking about. If you're looking for support related to other rights issues in Pakistan, I'd encourage you to connect with organizations that focus on women's rights more broadly in that region. Groups like the Aurat Foundation, War Against Rape Pakistan, or the Human Rights Commission of Pakistan may have resources or referrals that could be helpful, depending on the specific situation.</p>
<p>If the rights issues you're concerned about are connected to or have resulted from experiences of sexual violence, harassment, or abuse, then this platform would be a relevant place for support and information. Sometimes the impact of trauma can intersect with legal rights, family dynamics, economic concerns, and other areas of life, and those connections are absolutely within scope here.</p>
<p>I recognize that accessing support can feel complicated, especially when systems aren't always designed to meet the full range of what people are going through. Whatever you or someone you know is facing, you deserve care, safety, and respect. Thank you for taking the step to seek information and resources.</p>

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/if-it-happened-over-clothes-and-i-didnt-get-the-chance-to-say-no-because-she-would-grab-and-hold-me-when-she-thrusted-her-hips-onto-mine-does-this-still-count-as-cocsa-817)

**Question:** If it happened over clothes and I didn't get the chance to say no because she would grab and hold me when she thrusted her hips onto mine, does this still count as COCSA?

**Answer:** <p>I&rsquo;m really sorry you&rsquo;re feeling so unsure and overwhelmed about what happened. Even if it was over clothing and it happened quickly, any unwanted sexual contact where you didn&rsquo;t have the chance to say no can be deeply unsettling. COCSA (Child-on-Child Sexual Abuse) isn&rsquo;t determined by how long something lasts or whether it happened over clothing. It&rsquo;s about whether you truly had a choice in the situation and felt comfortable and safe in your body.</p>
<p>If she held you so you couldn&rsquo;t move or speak up, that takes away your ability to give or refuse consent. No one should thrust their hips onto you or make sexual contact without making sure you&rsquo;re okay with it. It&rsquo;s normal to doubt or minimize your experience, especially if it happened quickly or didn&rsquo;t involve force in the way people often imagine. However, your discomfort and the fact that you never got the chance to voice how you felt are significant signs that your boundaries weren&rsquo;t respected.</p>
<p>Even brief moments of unwanted contact can leave you feeling confused, powerless, or distressed afterward. If these memories are weighing on you now, it might help to talk to someone you trust or to seek out a professional who understands how complex these experiences can be. You deserve support, reassurance, and a space to process your feelings, and it&rsquo;s okay to reach out for help when you&rsquo;re ready.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-have-a-memory-from-when-i-was-young-where-my-dad-let-me-rub-his-chest-to-help-me-fall-asleep-im-not-sure-how-to-feel-about-this-memory-or-whether-i-should-try-to-forget-it-how-do-i-deal-with-this-81)

**Question:** I have a memory from when I was young where my dad let me rub his chest to help me fall asleep. I'm not sure how to feel about this memory or whether I should try to forget it. How do I deal with this?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting us with this memory and these difficult feelings. It is completely normal to feel unsure about a confusing childhood memory, especially when it revives strong emotions or discomfort.&nbsp;</p>
<p>At four to seven years old, you were still learning about boundaries and appropriate touch, and you depended on the adults around you to guide and protect you. Children naturally seek comfort and closeness in many different ways, and needing soothing touch to fall asleep is completely normal developmental behavior. Many children find comfort in tactile experiences, and parents often allow various forms of physical contact that help children self-soothe and feel secure. Chest contact can be particularly comforting for children due to the warmth, heartbeat, and closeness it provides. Having a memory of this experience and feeling uneasy now does not make you flawed or mistaken; it reflects the natural process of recalling early experiences and questioning what they meant.</p>
<p>It's also completely normal for memories to surface unexpectedly, sometimes years later. Our brains sometimes bring up memories when we're trying to make sense of our past or understand ourselves better. The fact that this memory has come up now doesn't mean something was necessarily wrong...it may simply be a moment your mind is revisiting as you process your childhood.</p>
<p>If you find yourself unable to stop thinking about this, it might help to talk with someone you trust, such as a counselor trained in understanding childhood experiences, or even a close friend or family member who respects your feelings. Sharing the memory in a supportive environment can offer the validation you may need. It can also help you sort out why the memory is resurfacing, how it affects you now, and what steps feel right to manage any lingering distress. You are not obligated to forget or dismiss something that makes you uncomfortable. You deserve the option to explore these feelings in a compassionate way, at a pace that feels safe for you. Sometimes, just acknowledging the memory and allowing yourself time to reflect can bring some relief.</p>
<p>Remember that you were a child at the time, with little understanding or control over the situation, and what happened was not your fault. You deserve care and acceptance as you decide whether, and how, you want to deal with this recollection. You get to decide what this memory means for you, and your feelings, whatever they are, are valid. Thank you for trusting us with this. You are not alone.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-6-years-old-my-grandfather-touched-my-penis-one-night-and-had-me-touch-his-he-didnt-force-me-and-at-that-time-i-didnt-feel-uncomfortable-with-what-was-happening-or-what-he-asked-me-to-do-i)

**Question:** When I was 6 years old, my grandfather touched my penis one night and had me touch his. He didn't force me, and at that time I didn't feel uncomfortable with what was happening or what he asked me to do. I even looked forward to it happening again the next night. I felt curious and wanted more. Does this make me complicit or guilty?

**Answer:** <p>I'm so sorry you went through something that's left you feeling uncertain about your role in it. It can be deeply confusing to look back on childhood experiences with adult eyes. Although you may have been curious or even welcomed the attention at the time, it was never your fault. Children are wired to seek affection and approval from adults, and it's very common for them to mistake harmful behavior for something acceptable when it comes from a trusted older figure. At six years old, you could not have fully understood what was happening or made an informed decision, and the responsibility for maintaining safe, appropriate boundaries always lies with the adult.</p>
<p>Feeling as though you "wanted it" back then does not make you complicit or guilty. A child's curiosity is natural, and that curiosity does not justify an adult's choice to engage in sexual behavior. You were not in a position to consent or truly know what was going on. Your grandfather was the adult in this situation, and he had all the power and all the responsibility. He knew what he was doing was wrong, and he chose to do it anyway. The fact that he didn't use physical force doesn't change the reality that he violated your trust and crossed boundaries that should never have been crossed with a child.</p>
<p>It's understandable that this might feel confusing, and you might blame yourself for not saying no or for expecting it to continue, but the fault and responsibility lie solely with your grandfather. Children's brains aren't developed enough to understand the true nature of sexual contact or its long-term implications. Some children may even experience physical sensations that feel confusing, or they may seek out the attention because they crave connection with that adult. None of this means you wanted the abuse or that you share any responsibility for what happened. Your body's natural responses and your childhood feelings of curiosity do not equal consent. Children cannot consent to sexual contact with adults... period.</p>
<p>You were a child who deserved to be protected and cared for, not to be made to feel guilty for something you could not control. If you find yourself struggling with these thoughts, you might find it helpful to talk with someone you trust or reach out to a professional who focuses on helping people work through the effects of childhood trauma. You're not alone in feeling this way, and with the right support you can better understand that none of it happened because of anything you did wrong. You were never an accomplice. You were a child who deserved safety and understanding.</p>
<p>Please take gentle care of yourself, and know that you are not at fault. You deserved protection, and your curiosity and willingness to please do not put any blame on you. You are worthy of compassion and support as you process these memories.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/how-do-i-know-if-i-am-ready-to-face-my-trauma-814)

**Question:** How do I know if I am ready to face my trauma?

**Answer:** <p>It's natural to wonder how you'll recognize that you're prepared to explore painful memories and experiences more openly. Often, it begins with little signs of curiosity about what happened or a sense that you're tired of avoiding certain thoughts, places, or conversations. You may also notice a growing desire to understand why you respond to situations in certain ways, or an urge to reconnect with parts of yourself that you've kept hidden. Feeling the weight of the trauma lingering and wanting relief can be another signal of readiness. Sometimes readiness looks like simply feeling tired of being stuck, or recognizing that the coping strategies that once helped you survive are now getting in the way of the life you want to live.</p>
<p>At the same time, it's normal to question whether these signs mean you're truly prepared. There's no perfect moment or universal checklist. Instead, it's about noticing whether you have a bit more energy for self-reflection and whether you feel supported, either by a friend, a professional, or a caring community, in talking about what happened. One of the most important factors is having enough stability in your present life. This might mean you're in a relatively safe living situation, you have some support around you, and your basic needs are mostly being met. It doesn't mean your life needs to be perfect, that's an impossible standard, but you generally need enough solid ground beneath you to weather the difficult emotions that can surface when working through traumatic experiences.</p>
<p>Even with that support, feeling anxious doesn't mean you're not ready; it often means you're stepping into unfamiliar territory, which can be unsettling but also a step toward healing. If you sense you can talk or think about your trauma without feeling entirely overwhelmed, or if you believe you can reach out for help on days that feel tougher, these can be gentle indications that facing your trauma is possible.</p>
<p>It's also worth noting that healing doesn't have to be all or nothing. You can dip your toes in, work on smaller pieces, build your resources and coping skills, and approach the deeper work gradually. A trauma-informed therapist can help you pace this process in a way that doesn't overwhelm your system. You deserve a pace that respects your comfort, and it's absolutely okay to move slowly or take breaks when you need them. Over time, many find that while confronting what happened can be difficult, it can also be an important path toward feeling more at peace and in control of their own story. Thank you for asking this!</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-17-i-worked-at-a-camp-and-my-boss-who-was-in-his-early-twenties-started-hooking-up-with-me-when-the-situation-got-reported-my-mother-told-me-i-should-have-known-better-friends-in-high-schoo)

**Question:** When I was 17, I worked at a camp and my boss (who was in his early twenties) started hooking up with me. When the situation got reported, my mother told me I should have known better. Friends in high school didn't really react when I shared the story. I thought it made me cool. In college, friends responded like I had been sexually abused, which I started to believe but didn't fully understand. Now in my mid-20s, I see how my actions and choices were shaped by the concept of power. Is this still considered sexual abuse? My mother framed it as more of a mistake or wrongdoing by him rather than grooming or purposeful harm. I also didn't want to press charges at the time, and neither did my mother.

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting us with your story. It sounds like your perspective on what happened has shifted over time as you've grown and reflected on the power imbalance involved. What you're describing is a situation where power imbalance and authority played a significant role, and many would recognize this as sexual abuse or exploitation. The fact that he held a position of authority over you as your boss created an inherent power dynamic that affected your ability to freely consent, regardless of how you felt about the situation at the time.</p>
<p>At seventeen, you were a minor, and your boss was an adult who held clear authority in that camp environment. Even if you felt mature and capable in certain moments, the adult in the situation was responsible for respecting boundaries and recognizing that you were still under the age of adulthood. The responsibility for maintaining appropriate professional and ethical limits belonged to him, not to you. The fact that your friends in high school reacted casually does not diminish how serious the situation may have been. It's completely understandable that your perspective has evolved over the years. Your high school friends might not have reacted because they didn't yet have the tools to recognize harm in situations involving power imbalances, while your college friends' responses likely reflected their growing awareness of these dynamics.</p>
<p>It's also common for parents to place blame on their children or dismiss situations like this as "poor judgment" rather than addressing them as potential sexual abuse or grooming. The fact that your mother told you that you "should have known better" placed responsibility on you that wasn't yours to carry. That doesn't make it any less valid to see it now for the violation it was. A parent's initial response, especially one that minimizes or misplaces blame, doesn't determine the reality of what happened or how harmful it was. Parents sometimes respond from their own discomfort, their own lack of understanding about power dynamics, or their desire to make sense of something they don't want to believe happened to their child.</p>
<p>A power difference, especially in a workplace setting, can make it hard for someone younger to see how they might have been taken advantage of. Often people don't fully recognize that until they gain more life experience. Whether or not someone "intended" to cause harm doesn't change the impact of their actions. Grooming doesn't always look predatory or calculated in the moment. It can feel flattering, special, or mutual. But the inherent power imbalance between a boss and an employee, especially when one person is a minor, means that true equality and free choice weren't possible. You can acknowledge that you made choices within that situation while also recognizing that those choices were constrained by the power he held over you.</p>
<p>Your choice back then not to press charges does not mean you "consented," and it doesn't mean the dynamic was acceptable. Many survivors decide against legal action for valid reasons that feel right at the time, such as not wanting to relive the events or fearing they won't be believed. The decision about whether to press charges was yours to make then, just as the decision about how to name and understand this experience is yours to make now. Not pursuing legal action doesn't make what happened less significant or less harmful, and it doesn't erase the fundamental idea that an older boss engaging with a teenager romantically or sexually violates a boundary.</p>
<p>Feeling confusion about whether you were "truly harmed" is normal. Recognizing that he was the adult who should have maintained professional and ethical limits may help validate whatever uncomfortable or uneasy feelings you have now. This is not your fault. You had limited experience and were influenced by both his position and society's messages about age and relationships. Looking back from your mid-twenties, it's understandable that you see it more clearly as an abuse of power, and that insight is significant and valid.</p>
<p>You deserve space to process any lingering doubts or regrets in a supportive environment. Listeners who understand the complexities of power, grooming, and coercion can offer a safe place to work through these thoughts. Whether you call it sexual abuse, grooming, or a serious mistake by that older man, the key point is that your feelings and experiences deserve validation, and you get to decide what healing looks like for you now. You get to decide what language feels right for your experience, and your understanding of what happened is allowed to be complex, layered, and different from how others understood it at the time. Even if you didn't hold him accountable legally, you can still acknowledge the harm and seek resources or professional support to talk about the impact this has had on you. You're not alone, and gaining clarity about it now doesn't mean you were overreacting or na&iuml;ve. It simply means you're seeing the situation with the perspective you've gained in the years since it happened. Thank you again for trusting us with this.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-about-6-a-friend-asked-me-to-kiss-her-i-said-no-at-first-but-she-explained-she-had-done-it-with-another-friend-and-said-it-was-okay-so-i-agreed-later-i-was-alone-with-my-cousin-who-was-slig)

**Question:** When I was about 6, a friend asked me to kiss her. I said no at first, but she explained she had done it with another friend and said it was okay, so I agreed. Later, I was alone with my cousin who was slightly younger than me, and I asked her to kiss me. She said no at first and looked uncomfortable, but I told her my friend and I had done it and it was okay. She then said yes, but my grandmother walked in before anything happened—or at least I think so. I feel extremely guilty about this and wonder if I pressured my cousin or crossed a boundary. I'm not sure how to process this experience, especially since my memory is fuzzy about what actually happened.

**Answer:** <p>It sounds like this memory has been weighing heavily on your mind, and it's understandable to feel conflicted and uneasy when looking back on childhood experiences through the lens of your adult understanding. Thank you for trusting us with this memory and the feelings you're carrying about it. I can hear how much guilt and uncertainty you're holding, and I want you to know that what you're describing sounds like developmentally typical childhood sexual behavior, not child-on-child sexual abuse.</p>
<p>At around six years old, children are still learning about boundaries and relationships in ways that aren't yet shaped by adult ideas. When children are very young, it's completely normal for them to be curious about bodies, physical affection, and boundaries. Your brain was still developing the capacity to understand concepts like consent, power dynamics, and social appropriateness. What may seem concerning from your current perspective was very likely part of normal childhood exploratory behavior, where young kids try to interpret the world using whatever information they have at the time. Children at that age often mimic behaviors they've seen or experienced, and they're learning about the world through exploration and imitation. What you described, asking for a kiss after another child had normalized it for you, fits within this developmental stage. You weren't operating with the awareness, intent, or power that would characterize harmful behavior.</p>
<p>Reviewing these memories can often stir up guilt, especially if you recall moments where someone hesitated or where you feel you "should have known better." The fact that your cousin initially said no and you felt unsure indicates there was confusion in that moment, which is common among children who haven't yet learned the intricacies of consent. While you mentioned she looked uncomfortable at first, she ultimately agreed, and nothing physical happened because your grandmother interrupted. The fact that you explained your reasoning to her, just as your friend had done with you, shows that you were trying to make sense of something you didn't fully understand yet. It doesn't sound like you were trying to harm her. You were navigating a confusing situation with the limited tools a 6-year-old has. The power balance between two kids so close in age is also quite different from how we think of power dynamics in adulthood. While you're understandably concerned about having pressured her, it's important to remember that at six years old, you were also just a child without the more complete understanding you now hold as an adult.</p>
<p>The worry that you might have hurt or harmed your cousin points toward your caring nature and sense of responsibility. The guilt you're feeling now is coming from your adult perspective, where you understand consent and boundaries in ways you simply couldn't have at that age. Many survivors of sexual harm (especially those who experienced something confusing or boundary-crossing in their own childhoods) can be hypervigilant about their own behavior and fear they might have caused harm to others. This makes sense, but it doesn't mean your childhood behavior was abusive or that you did something wrong. However, a child's exploration, guided by incomplete information and peer examples, is typically not considered the same as adult wrongdoing.</p>
<p>If your memory feels fuzzy, that's also understandable. Memory works differently in early childhood, and the passage of time, along with the emotions you're experiencing now, can make it harder to recall exact details. What matters most is how you're making sense of this now and what you need to move forward. It sounds like you're carrying a heavy burden of responsibility for something that was part of normal childhood development, and you deserve to be gentle with yourself about that.</p>
<p>If these memories continue to weigh on you or cause guilt and anxiety, reflecting on them with a trauma-informed counselor or someone you trust might help you find clarity and self-compassion. Talking it through can remind you that you only had a child's understanding of the situation, and that it's possible to acknowledge what happened without condemning yourself.
