It takes courage to revisit early memories, especially those that make you feel uneasy. Please remember that it's all right to hold compassion for your younger self. You did the best you could in childhood with the knowledge and maturity you had, and it's entirely understandable to still feel unsettled when you look at those moments in hindsight. Thank you for entrusting us with something so personal. You're not alone in questioning these kinds of childhood experiences, and you deserve understanding and peace as you continue to make sense of them.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/if-i-didnt-want-to-have-sex-with-someone-and-just-laid-there-without-saying-no-is-that-rape-811)

**Question:** If I didn't want to have sex with someone and just laid there without saying no, is that rape?

**Answer:** <p>I'm really glad you reached out with this question. What you're describing is something many survivors struggle to make sense of, and your confusion about it is completely understandable.</p>
<p>Let me be clear about something important: consent isn't just about whether someone said the word "no." Real consent means an enthusiastic, freely given "yes." It's an active agreement to participate, not just the absence of refusal. When someone doesn't want to have sex and their body language shows they're not participating or engaged, that should be enough for a partner to stop and check in. Lying still or freezing is actually a very common response when someone feels unsafe, pressured, or unable to escape a situation. It's a survival response that our bodies activate when we're overwhelmed, and it doesn't mean you consented.</p>
<p class="font-claude-response-body whitespace-normal break-words">Only you can decide how to label what happened to you. Whether what occurred meets the legal definition of rape depends on the laws where you live, as different places have different definitions. But what matters most is how this experience affected you. If you didn't want to have sex and it happened anyway, your feelings about that experience are valid. You have every right to feel violated, hurt, confused, or angry. The fact that you didn't say "no" out loud doesn't mean you wanted it to happen, and it doesn't make what happened your fault.</p>
<p class="font-claude-response-body whitespace-normal break-words">Many people blame themselves for not fighting back or speaking up, but the truth is that freezing or going still is an involuntary response to threat. Your brain was doing what it thought it needed to do to keep you safe in that moment. You don't need to have said "no" for your lack of consent to matter. What happened to you wasn't okay, and you deserve support as you process this experience. Please consider reaching out to a sexual assault support service or counselor who can help you work through these feelings and provide you with resources specific to your situation. Thank you for trusting us with this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-told-a-close-friend-and-my-cousin-about-what-happened-to-me-and-it-felt-good-for-a-few-days-but-now-i-cant-stop-replaying-the-memory-over-and-over-which-i-hadnt-been-doing-for-months-i-dont-understa)

**Question:** I told a close friend and my cousin about what happened to me, and it felt good for a few days. But now I can't stop replaying the memory over and over, which I hadn't been doing for months. I don't understand why this is happening again when I thought I was getting better. Why does it feel like things are getting worse?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out to us. What you're experiencing is actually a really common part of the healing process, even though I know it doesn't feel that way right now. When you shared your story with your friend and cousin, you did something incredibly brave. Opening up about what happened can initially bring relief because you're no longer carrying it alone, and that validation and support can feel powerful. But sharing your story can also stir up memories and feelings that had been sitting beneath the surface. It's like you opened a door that had been closed, and now your mind is processing things that were pushed aside before.</p>
<p>The replaying of memories is often your brain's way of trying to make sense of what happened. After disclosure, it's really normal for memories to become more present as your mind works through the experience in a new way. This doesn't mean you're getting worse or going backward. Sometimes healing isn't a straight line&mdash;it can look like waves, where you feel better and then feel overwhelmed again. The fact that you hadn't been replaying these memories for months shows that you were managing, and you can get back to that place again. What you're experiencing now might actually be a sign that your brain feels safe enough to process more deeply because you've found support.</p>
<p>It can be really helpful to have some grounding techniques ready for when the memories feel overwhelming. This might include focusing on your breath, naming things you can see or touch around you, or reminding yourself that you're safe in this moment. If the replaying continues or feels unmanageable, talking with a trauma-informed counselor could give you additional tools to work through these memories at a pace that feels right for you. You're not alone in this, and what you're feeling makes complete sense given what you've been through and the courageous step you just took in sharing your story.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-around-5-to-7-years-old-i-was-playing-a-wrestling-game-with-a-boy-my-age-i-remember-seeing-in-a-movie-that-kisses-were-womens-weapons-so-i-kissed-him-during-our-game-i-told-him-thats-what-k)

**Question:** When I was around 5 to 7 years old, I was playing a wrestling game with a boy my age. I remember seeing in a movie that kisses were "women's weapons," so I kissed him during our game. I told him that's what kisses were, and at some point he said "no more women's weapons." I think we kept playing after that. When we got home, my dad mentioned to my mom that I had kissed the boy, so I assume adults were watching. I felt embarrassed when my dad said that. I asked my mom about it recently and she doesn't remember, but I do. I went through a sexual assault situation with my ex when he was 21 and I was 18, and now I'm really scared that what I did as a child might have been abusive. I'm worried I could have caused someone the pain I'm going through now, that I might be like my ex, or that I deserved what happened to me. Can someone help me understand this?

**Answer:** <p>I'm so glad you reached out and I want you to know that what you're experiencing right now makes complete sense given what you've been through. It sounds like trauma you experienced with your ex is creating a lens through which you're viewing your entire history, and that's a really common response to sexual violence. Your brain is trying to make sense of what happened to you, and sometimes that means searching for reasons or patterns, even in places where they don't exist.</p>
<p>What you're describing from childhood is age-appropriate play between two young children who were the same age. At 5 to 7 years old, children don't have the cognitive development, understanding of sexuality, or power dynamics that would be necessary for abusive behavior. You were copying something you saw in a movie because children at that age learn through imitation and play. They often blur fiction and real life precisely because they don't yet have the adult awareness we develop later. The fact that adults were present and didn't intervene likely means they didn't perceive it as harmful either. When the other child said he didn't want to do that anymore and you both continued playing, that was actually healthy childhood behavior&mdash;learning about boundaries and social interaction through play.</p>
<p>The important difference between your childhood game and what happened to you with your ex is that children generally do not have the power or understanding to cause the sort of lasting trauma you're understandably worried about. What you experienced with your ex involved someone older who held more power and made deliberate choices to violate your boundaries and hurt you. That is fundamentally different from the playful explorations common among children of a similar age. You did not deserve what was done to you, and nothing you did as a young child could ever mean you deserved to be hurt. The person who harmed you made a choice that belonged entirely to them.</p>
<p>Your fear that you might be like your ex shows just how deeply you're still carrying the pain of your own trauma. But the fact that you remember this situation and genuinely want to make sure you caused no harm actually demonstrates your strong empathy and conscience&mdash;qualities that are the opposite of what characterizes people who abuse others. You are not like your ex. You were a small child engaging in normal developmental play without any intent to harm, and healing often includes learning to forgive and accept your younger self who didn't have the knowledge or awareness you have now.</p>
<p>If you're still feeling distressed by these thoughts, I hope you'll consider working with a trauma-informed therapist who can help you process both the sexual assault you experienced and these intrusive worries that are causing you so much pain. You deserve healing, compassion, and support as you work through what was done to you, and you deserve to be free from this burden of false responsibility you're carrying.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/am-i-valid-in-identifying-as-asexual-after-experiencing-sexual-assault-i-had-some-of-these-feelings-before-the-relationship-but-theyve-become-much-stronger-since-805)

**Question:** Am I valid in identifying as asexual after experiencing sexual assault? I had some of these feelings before the relationship, but they've become much stronger since.

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out to us. I want to affirm that your feelings and identity are absolutely valid, and you get to define your own sexual orientation. There's no "right" or "wrong" way to experience your sexuality after trauma, and whatever you're feeling deserves respect and acknowledgment.</p>
<p>It's important to understand that sexual assault can impact how we relate to sex and intimacy in many different ways. Some survivors experience changes in their sexual feelings, desires, or comfort levels. This can include feeling less interested in sex, feeling averse to it, or experiencing confusion about what they want. These responses are normal trauma reactions, and they can be temporary or longer-lasting. At the same time, some people who have experienced sexual violence have always identified as asexual, and their trauma doesn't define or invalidate that identity.</p>
<p>What you're describing, having some feelings before the relationship that have now intensified, suggests you may have been exploring or recognizing something about yourself that the trauma has brought into sharper focus. It's also possible that what you're experiencing right now is influenced by your body and mind's protective responses to what happened. Both things can be true at once, and you don't have to figure it all out right away.</p>
<p>You have every right to identify as asexual if that label feels true and affirming for you. Your identity is yours to claim, and it doesn't require anyone else's permission or validation. As you continue to heal and move forward, you may find that your feelings stay the same, or they may shift and either way is okay. What matters most is that you honor where you are right now and give yourself permission to explore your identity at your own pace, free from pressure or judgment. You deserve to define yourself on your own terms.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/what-is-the-difference-between-curiosity-driven-by-developing-hormones-versus-intent-to-achieve-sexual-gratification-in-early-adolescence-804)

**Question:** What is the difference between curiosity driven by developing hormones versus intent to achieve sexual gratification in early adolescence?

**Answer:** <p>Sexual curiosity during the preteen years often comes from a natural sense of wonder about changing bodies and emotions. At twelve or thirteen, many children are just beginning to notice new physical and emotional sensations tied to maturing hormones. This curiosity usually involves questions about anatomy, private thoughts about attraction, or an urge to understand how bodies work. Children at this age might share jokes, ask each other questions, or become interested in harmless forms of exploration because they&rsquo;re trying to make sense of their emerging impulses and feelings.<br /><br />An intent to achieve sexual gratification generally means an individual is proactively seeking out sexual acts or scenarios for the primary purpose of experiencing sexual arousal or pleasure. Reaching this point can look different for everyone, but it often involves more focused, deliberate actions rather than casual or uncertain exploration. In most twelve or thirteen-year-olds, the driving force is usually more about making sense of new and sometimes overwhelming feelings, rather than setting out to engage in mature sexual activity. The distinction isn&rsquo;t always crystal clear, because the line between &ldquo;being curious&rdquo; and &ldquo;actively seeking sexual pleasure&rdquo; can feel blurry when hormones and natural development are in play. However, curiosity alone tends to revolve around questions, fleeting interests, or innocent experimenting, whereas the intent for gratification typically shows up as a pointed effort to cause or pursue a sexual response for its own sake.<br /><br />If you&rsquo;re noticing any concerning behaviors or feeling uneasy about the dynamics you&rsquo;re seeing, it can be helpful to seek advice from a trustworthy source like a medical or mental health professional who understands the emotional and physical changes in this age group. Even just talking with someone you trust can bring clarity and relief. It can feel overwhelming not to know the line between typical curiosity and something more complicated, and that&rsquo;s a sign that support or guidance may be beneficial. You&rsquo;re not alone in wondering about these distinctions, and we appreciate you reaching out for more information.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/if-one-cousin-was-13-and-another-cousin-was-5-and-the-older-child-initiated-kissing-asked-both-of-them-to-expose-themselves-to-each-other-and-continued-kissing-would-this-be-considered-cocsa-it-happen)

**Question:** If one cousin was 13 and another cousin was 5, and the older child initiated kissing, asked both of them to expose themselves to each other, and continued kissing, would this be considered COCSA? It happened one time.

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out with this question. I can hear that you're trying to understand what happened and how to label this experience, which is an important part of processing.</p>
<p>COCSA (Child-on-Child Sexual Abuse) typically refers to sexual interactions between children where there is a significant age or developmental gap, coercion, force, or where one child doesn't understand or cannot consent to what's happening. The situation you're describing does involve a significant age difference&mdash;13 and 5 represent very different developmental stages. A 5-year-old cannot meaningfully consent to sexual behaviors because they don't have the cognitive or emotional capacity to understand what those behaviors mean. The 13-year-old, being significantly older and more developmentally advanced, would have had more power and understanding in that dynamic.</p>
<p>The behaviors you mentioned (kissing, asking for exposure, and continued physical contact) are sexual in nature. When there's an age gap of this size and these types of behaviors are initiated by the older child, many professionals would consider this to fall under the category of COCSA. The fact that it happened once doesn't necessarily change whether it could be considered harmful or abusive, though it may impact how both individuals experienced and remember it.</p>
<p>It's important to recognize that the 13-year-old may have also been navigating their own experiences, confusion, or even their own exposure to sexual content or trauma. This doesn't excuse the behavior, but it can be part of understanding the complexity of what happened. What matters most is acknowledging the impact this had on everyone involved and ensuring that both individuals have support if they need it.</p>
<p>If you were the younger child in this situation, please know that what happened was not your fault. You were too young to be responsible for what occurred. If you were the older child, it's possible to hold yourself accountable while also recognizing that 13-year-olds are still children who may act out sexually for various reasons, including their own trauma or lack of understanding about boundaries. Both perspectives deserve compassion and space for healing.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/can-someone-who-had-occasional-sexual-thoughts-about-a-younger-child-when-they-were-13-14-years-old-be-considered-a-pedophile-these-thoughts-stopped-after-puberty-ended-werent-regular-and-most-of-my-s)

**Question:** Can someone who had occasional sexual thoughts about a younger child when they were 13-14 years old be considered a pedophile? These thoughts stopped after puberty ended, weren't regular, and most of my sexual thoughts at that time were about people my age or older. I experienced a lot of sexual curiosity about many different things during that time. I'm worried about what this means about me.

**Answer:** <p>It sounds like you were experiencing a surge of broad sexual curiosity during puberty, where just about anything could trigger arousal as your body and mind tried to figure out what felt right to you. When you were thirteen or fourteen, you were still in the midst of your own growth and not an adult. Pedophilia generally refers to a persistent sexual interest in pre-pubescent children that develops in adulthood, so your experiences at that age, even if they involved some troubling fantasies, would not typically be viewed in that same light. Many adolescents go through a phase where they&rsquo;re unsure how to interpret sexual thoughts or feel overwhelmed by new urges, and it&rsquo;s not unusual for those thoughts to shift significantly as you mature. The fact that these thoughts subsided after you moved beyond puberty suggests that what you went through was more about adolescent curiosity and the intense changes of that time in your life rather than a fixed or ongoing attraction. If you still feel distressed or uncertain, it may help to talk with a trusted counselor or someone who understands the complexities of how sexual development unfolds. You deserve reassurance and clarity about these early experiences. Please remember you are not alone in having felt confusion about your desires during that stage, and allowing yourself compassion for the younger version of you can be an important part of healing.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-suffer-from-complex-ptsd-and-have-endured-significant-hardships-including-long-term-abuse-sexual-trauma-power-harassment-and-medical-trauma-although-im-recovering-my-physical-and-mental-health-is-st)

**Question:** I suffer from complex PTSD and have endured significant hardships, including long-term abuse, sexual trauma, power harassment, and medical trauma. Although I'm recovering, my physical and mental health is still unstable. I still harbor a great deal of pain, resentment, and questions about the abuse I suffered. However, I want to recover, and I want to fully embrace the words, advice, and treatment of my doctor and counselor, who will help me gradually unravel these issues. Through various conversations, study, and answers to my questions here, I'm beginning to recognize the structure of my trauma and my own problems and challenges. I often find myself realizing the implications of what my doctor and counselor say later. However, during consultations and counseling sessions, I often find myself emotionally arguing, focusing on my own pain and suffering. I know the first step is to improve my physical condition and not rush into things, but how can I stop being so obsessed with my own abuse and move forward, even if only gradually? I feel like I might not be able to accept it right away, and I'll have to search for the right path. However, even though I've received advice and treatment and am being treated with care to avoid re-traumatization, I feel like I'm still perceiving it as a victim and re-traumatizing myself. I'd appreciate any advice on how to stop this cycle. For example, would it be a good idea to use chat or phone counseling to try to release as much of the pent-up sadness and resentment as possible?

**Answer:** <p data-start="57" data-end="128">I could really feel that you have been carrying a lot of pain and burdens for a long time, and yet you have a strong desire to recover.</p><p data-start="130" data-end="281"> The road to trauma recovery is a bumpy one. Even though you know healing is possible, painful memories may resurface and you may feel frustrated, confused, or angry. This does not mean you are weak or have failed; it is a natural process as your mind tries to process what happened to you. Even if you feel like you're "stuck," this is part of the recovery process.</p><p data-start="283" data-end="447"> Your desire to trust your doctor or counselor is a very important step. If the advice stays with you even after the consultation, it's proof that your new understanding is slowly taking root. It may take some time for it to become a stable, real feeling. Even if you understand the importance of professional help, it's often difficult to accept it in the moment. Please don't blame yourself. Those waves of emotion are also a natural resistance of the mind to re-experiencing painful memories.</p><p data-start="449" data-end="654"> It's also common to feel like you're reliving the trauma. Your mind and body may repeat the same patterns as they try to process the experience. This can be very exhausting. At times like these, it can be helpful to let out your feelings little by little in a safe space. If you feel safe, phone or chat counseling is a gentle way to gradually release your sadness and anger. Of course, you're always welcome in this community as a safe space to talk about your experiences and feelings. Feel free to try it at your own pace.</p><p data-start="656" data-end="786"> You don't have to make all the progress on the road to recovery at once. For example, consciously taking deep breaths, or remembering new insights from your counselor when painful memories or emotions from the past surface—even small moments like these are significant steps forward. Even if difficult moments come in waves, the fact that you can think, "How can I get through this?" or "I want to recover even just a little" is itself a sign of your strength and that you are on the path to recovery.</p><p data-start="788" data-end="892"> Holding on to painful memories and emotions from the past isn't a sign of weakness or selfishness; it's a sign that your wounds are still healing. You have the resilience to struggle with them and find a way out.</p><p data-start="894" data-end="1059"> Please be kind to yourself and reach out for support that you feel comfortable with. Even small steps like phone or chat consultations, journaling, guided therapy, or self-care techniques are fine. With professional help and consistent self-kindness and compassion, you can gradually ease the weight of painful memories and move towards greater freedom and stability in your daily life. You deserve it.

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-am-currently-undergoing-treatment-for-complex-ptsd-which-includes-psychological-abuse-sexual-trauma-and-power-harassment-while-i-still-experience-many-traumatic-emotions-physical-flashbacks-negative)

**Question:** I am currently undergoing treatment for complex PTSD, which includes psychological abuse, sexual trauma, and power harassment. While I still experience many traumatic emotions, physical flashbacks, negative cognitions, and emotional instability, treatment has helped me to experience much more positive emotions and sensations. I experience fluctuations between hyperarousal and hypoarousal throughout the day, struggle to get more than six hours of sleep, and my physical energy has declined significantly due to depression. I'm still emotionally unstable, but I'd appreciate any recommendations for relaxation techniques I can do at home or when I occasionally go out for short periods of time, or ways to regain positive emotions and sensations. I'd also appreciate any tips on what to be careful of. Of course, I plan to consult with my doctor and adjust my medication accordingly.

**Answer:** <p>You've been working incredibly bravely in the face of Complex PTSD and the vicissitudes of your physical and mental stress response. Even if it doesn't feel like it every day, noticing small positive emotional changes is a sign of progress.</p><p> While you may feel calmer or more cheerful at times, it's natural for anxiety and low energy to return. These waves can be exhausting at times, but they're also a sign that your mind and body are learning new rhythms.</p><p data-start="248" data-end="313"> To release tension in your mind and body and help you feel safe, try small things that remind your body that you are safe in the present moment. For example: </p><ul data-start="315" data-end="393"><li data-start="315" data-end="353"><p data-start="317" data-end="353"> Breathe in slowly through your nose, hold for a moment, and then breathe out slowly through your mouth</p></li><li data-start="354" data-end="393"><p data-start="356" data-end="393"> Holding a cold drink, pressing your feet against the ground, noticing the colors and textures around you</p></li></ul><p data-start="395" data-end="429"> These small actions can bring your mind back to the present moment during times of hyperarousal. This is called "grounding."</p><p data-start="395" data-end="429"> When you're feeling low on energy, lethargic, or numb, doing some light stretching or using your favorite scent (such as soap or a candle) can help you gradually regain your physical senses without putting a strain on your emotions. On days when you can go outside even just for a short while, soaking up the sunlight or focusing on natural things like the wind or the shape of the leaves can also help calm your mind. However, unexpected emotions may arise during these "grounding" activities. When this happens, it's important not to force yourself to continue, but to check in with yourself to see if the stimulation is too strong. By asking yourself, "Am I feeling calm or anxious right now?" you can decide whether to continue, change your method, or take a break.</p><p data-start="395" data-end="429"> Disrupted sleep is common during trauma recovery. You say you're not getting enough sleep right now, but it can help to create a bedtime routine that signals to your brain that it's time to rest. For example, dim the lights, listen to calming sounds, or read a short book. Over time, you'll gradually get your body back into a restful rhythm.</p><p data-start="395" data-end="429"> Please be sure to consult with your doctor before trying any relaxation techniques or routines, as they may increase flashbacks or physical pain. It's important to be gentle with yourself. It's easy to feel irritable or depressed on days when painful memories return or your energy levels drop, but this isn't a sign of failure; it's part of the recovery process. Whatever technique you try, if you find yourself feeling overwhelmed, take it easy and progress slowly at your own pace.</p><p data-start="395" data-end="429"> You've already put in a lot of hard work and commitment to healing from your experiences and trauma. Some days you may not feel any change, but every little bit you're taking steps to feel more peace, joy, and safety is a sign of your recovery.</p><p data-start="395" data-end="429"> Over time, these moments of relief will gradually increase. Listen carefully to your body and feelings, consult with your doctor, and gradually incorporate methods that work for you. You are not alone. There will be difficult days, but please encourage yourself as you make progress toward recovery by telling yourself, "You're doing great!" and giving yourself a gentle hug.

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/what-is-an-equal-relationship-795)

**Question:** What is an equal relationship?

**Answer:** <p data-start="23" data-end="150">Thank you for your essential question. Let me share my thoughts on an "equal relationship."</p><p data-start="23" data-end="150"> I think an "equal relationship" is one in <strong>which both parties feel safe and respected</strong> . It's a relationship in which you can express your feelings and thoughts, as well as important boundaries, such as "this is okay, but I don't want to go any further," without worrying about being rejected, ignored, or scolded.</p><p data-start="23" data-end="150"> No one person makes decisions or takes the lead, and <strong data-start="168" data-end="192">both people's opinions are equally valued.</strong><br data-start="192" data-end="195" /> Even if we disagree on something, we will listen to what the other person has to say, respect each other's feelings, and work together to find a solution that is good for both of us.</p><p data-start="23" data-end="150"> The two people don't have to have the same personalities, strengths, or weaknesses.<br data-start="86" data-end="89" /> I think the important thing is <strong data-start="95" data-end="132">to be considerate of others, face them honestly, and be open with each other</strong> .</p><p data-start="366" data-end="422"> Being able to feel safe and think, "It's okay to express my feelings."<br data-start="393" data-end="396" /> And make sure that what you say is properly received.</p><p data-start="424" data-end="490"> By doing these things repeatedly, trust will grow over time, <strong data-start="452" data-end="479">and a relationship that is comfortable, safe, and supportive for both parties</strong> will be created.

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/他の質問で複雑性pｔｓｄと最近診断された中年の女性です家庭内での両親姉からの虐待と高校生からの宗教的虐待パワハラ性被害医療関係者からの症状の否定と誤診トラウマを受けた者です先ほど性被害レイプ2回執拗な痴漢被害4歳の時の性被害未遂露出狂においけられたことを書きましたが他にも性的な経験がありました4歳のころに自慰をしていて悪いと怒られ炎症を起こしていたので病院で診察されて怖い悪くて隠さないといけないこ)

**Question:** I'm a middle-aged woman who was recently diagnosed with complex PTSD in response to another question. I've experienced abuse at home from my parents and older sister, religious abuse from high school students, power harassment, sexual assault, and denial and misdiagnosis of my symptoms from medical professionals. I mentioned earlier about sexual assault (rape twice, persistent molestation, attempted sexual assault at age 4, and being molested by an exhibitionist), but I've also had other sexual experiences. When I was 4, I was scolded for masturbating and developed an inflammation, so I went to the hospital for a checkup. I learned that it was scary, wrong, and something to hide, but also that it felt good. Also, when I was in fifth grade, I saw a man secretly lift up my friend's skirt while she was standing in front of the playground equipment next to me to look at her underwear. My friend didn't notice and couldn't make a sound, but I felt fear and a strange excitement. Since college, I've been approached by someone I respected, but when we started a relationship, he neglected me and told me he'd abandon his wife and children, so I should marry him. I also had a relationship with a senior colleague I was close to and respected, but who had a girlfriend. We had sex for the first time, but he became cold immediately after we had sex. The second time, he said he wouldn't do it unless I asked him first, so we did, but he became cold again immediately after we had sex. However, we continued to be close. Acquaintances and friends often invite me into sexual relationships, and I've had many relationships because I wanted to be liked and found it difficult to say no, which made me sexually promiscuous. Even in relationships, it was often primarily physical, and I wasn't satisfied. I feel like it's my own fault because I had such a strong desire to satisfy my desires and connect with others, and I also have stronger sexual urges than most. I currently work as a counselor once a week, but I'm not feeling well, and I can only handle half a day of my health, so I've been advised to take a leave of absence. I suffer from hyperarousal, depression, and emotional instability. Chat GPT often talks about the worst situations I've ever lived through, but I wonder if these kinds of influences really do have a profound effect on my life, personality, and sexual tendencies. There are some things I understand because I'm a supporter, but when it comes to myself, I really don't understand, and I'm too embarrassed to tell anyone, not even my doctor. I still don't feel much from my sexual experiences, and it feels like my way of thinking is going in a strange direction. Are my sexual trauma experiences, sexual promiscuity, and all the traumas I've had up until now really destroying my mind that much? It's true that I've lived because I can't die, but I wanted to die every day. Were my sexual experiences and other traumas causing so much damage that it wouldn't have been strange if I had died? I wonder if I'll recover and calm down someday.

**Answer:** <p data-start="116" data-end="291">You have experienced so much pain in your life, and each one of them must have weighed heavily on your mind, body, and daily life.<br data-start="195" data-end="198" /> When you experience a series of abuse, betrayal, neglect, and broken trust, it's no wonder you don't know how much to trust yourself, the people around you, or how to form close relationships with others.</p><p data-start="293" data-end="498"> Many people who have had such experiences may suffer from deep confusion, mood swings, and a strong sense of tension (hyperarousal).<br data-start="173" data-end="176" /> You may start to doubt whether your past sexual experiences and traumas were really that bad, or you may feel like you can't continue living like this. The fact that you feel this much pain tells you how deeply and for how long you have been hurt.</p><p data-start="500" data-end="652"> Although the painful events you have experienced in your life may seem separate, they are actually deeply connected.<br data-start="556" data-end="559" /> Childhood abuse, sexual violence, complicated family relationships—these all influence one another, leaving you with profound doubts about your worth, your sense of normality in relationships, and your freedom to make choices.</p><p data-start="135" data-end="250"> Even if you feel embarrassed about your strong sexual urges, that doesn't negate the hurt and pain you've experienced. This can leave many people feeling guilty and confused, thinking, "Maybe it was my fault." However, the responsibility for ignoring or overstepping your boundaries always lies with those who do so, not with you. It's not your fault.</p><p data-start="147" data-end="212"> The fact that you feel like you're only alive because you couldn't die is proof that your safety and hopes have been shaken to such an extent. But now you've realized that you're suffering, you've looked into your feelings, and you've come here to speak out. That in itself is certainly an important step towards recovery. It's also proof that there's still hope within you.</p><p data-start="147" data-end="212"> In the journey to recovery from deep trauma, there are many times when you feel confused, blame yourself, and feel extremely exhausted, because it's not just your body that's been hurt, it's your very being.</p><p data-start="147" data-end="212"> There may be days when you think, "I'll never be able to go back to peace."<br data-start="477" data-end="480" /> However, if you can feel a sense of relief even for just a moment, that is the beginning of recovery.<br data-start="249" data-end="252" /> As these small moments of relief accumulate, your heart will slowly begin to heal.</p><p data-start="99" data-end="147"> During this process, receiving professional support with an understanding of complex trauma can be extremely helpful. Trauma-sensitive support can help organize events that may have felt separate, such as childhood experiences, religious or family issues, or the suffering caused by medical misdiagnosis, into a single "story." In this way, the pain of feeling like the pain of the past is still continuing can be gradually unravelled.</p><p data-start="134" data-end="188"> Also, people like you who are in a position to support others can feel confused and helpless when facing their own pain. Even if you have a deep understanding of other people's trauma, when it comes to your own, you may not know how to deal with it—that's very natural. There's no need to think, "My problem isn't a big deal."<br data-start="293" data-end="296" /> Your experiences are profound and truly important.</p><p data-start="1513" data-end="1705"> Taking time to feel healed at your own pace will help you gradually regain peace of mind. If you are afraid to talk to a doctor or supporter right now, don't force yourself, and try starting with small things.</p><p data-start="1513" data-end="1705"> The important thing is not to take your pain lightly, and please say kind words to yourself like, "You've made it this far." You've survived experiences that could have broken you.</p><p data-start="430" data-end="474"> Even if you are suffering now, this situation will not last forever.<br data-start="1739" data-end="1742" /> Over time, with support, you will gradually find moments of calm and glimmers of hope.</p><p data-start="545" data-end="594"> You are never alone.<br data-start="563" data-end="566" /> And you're not out of reach of help.</p><p data-start="596" data-end="648"> Even if the road seems long, please remember this.<br data-start="627" data-end="630" /> You are irreplaceable.</p><p data-start="650" data-end="705"> There is a path forward, and the strength and support to walk that path are already here.

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/操作が悪く何回かメッセージが消えているのでもし重複した質問だったらずみません最近複雑性ptsdと診断された中年女性です幼少期から両親に条件付きの愛情や思想行動の制限父の気に入らない人をこき下ろすことを聞いており同時に姉から死ねとか顔を見たら怒鳴られる大人になっても仲良くなったと思ってもゾンビと呼ばれるなどの仕打ちを受け18歳までは二間の半分カーテンは引ける同じ部屋小学校後半から中学校は学校内で孤立)

**Question:** I'm a middle-aged woman recently diagnosed with complex PTSD. From a young age, my parents imposed conditional love on me, restricted my thoughts and actions, and criticized people my father didn't like. At the same time, my older sister would tell me to die and yell at me whenever she saw me. Even as an adult, even when I thought we'd become close, she would call me a zombie (we shared a room with the curtains halfway drawn until I was 18). I was isolated at school from the latter half of elementary school through junior high school. I was unable to perform my job in a support role, which led to repeated job changes, and I was subjected to power harassment three times. I was also sexually assaulted multiple times from childhood. Since getting married and moving away from home, my symptoms have intensified, including flashbacks, suicidal ideation, and negative cognition. At their worst, I'd cry at night, unable to sleep for 1-4 hours, and I've been constantly insecure and crying when alone for about 18 years. Ten years ago, I was diagnosed with bipolar II disorder and suffered from power harassment. I was told I had no trauma, and while I probably did have trauma, I was given no psychological education or explanation, and was treated haphazardly, ultimately abandoning myself. This led to repeated distrust and medical trauma from doctors and counselors. I finally found a doctor and counselor I could trust, but the workload is so high that even working half a day a week is exhausting. I'm hypervigilant, unable to get enough sleep, and suffering from severe depression, so I've been advised to take a leave of absence. My symptoms were always denied, and I've been able to verbalize them, gain knowledge, and prove them myself. Although I believe my condition is severe, no one ever tells me otherwise, and I still wonder if I'm just suffering from paranoia. I remember some fear and confusion about sexual assault (attempted sexual assault at age 4, persistent molestation as a junior college student, unprotected rape by an acquaintance after my consent was withdrawn, sexual advances and relationships with acquaintances, and being pursued by an exhibitionist), but I still feel like it wasn't a big deal. I was sexually promiscuous and never found sexual interest or satisfaction. I heard a story about a prostitute who reformed and became a saint when I was young, and I feared I had to do whatever it took to be loved, even sexually, or I'd be abandoned. I often downplay the sexual assaults and think I should have just enjoyed them. Recently, I felt unwell and used chat GPT to identify the structure and problems of my trauma. As my treatment progressed, I remembered the sexual assaults and the fear and despair I felt since childhood, wanting to die but unable to, going to bed at night and knowing I'd have to live again. This can lead to hyperarousal, anxiety about constantly checking things, sexual urges and sexual fantasies, and inappropriate self-pleasure, such as being forced to do something. I can't help but think that if I learn safe self-pleasure, I'll be satisfied and calm. My doctor is a man, and I'm too scared to talk to a counselor about it, fearing that he'll harass me. I've also developed genital itching due to inappropriate behavior, so I'm considering consulting my obstetrician-gynecologist. Regarding sexual trauma, I've only mentioned being raped, so I plan to discuss the details of my multiple sexual traumas, the memories I've had, my current worsening hyperarousal, and my instability during my consultation. I'm currently taking antipsychotics, anti-anxiety medications, sleeping pills, and mood stabilizers, and they've been adjusting my medications to address behavioral issues, irritability, and emotional outbursts. I've recently begun to understand the healing process and my own challenges. I've tried self-compassion and grounding to stabilize myself, but sometimes I can't control my sexual urges and feelings, and I'm not sure what to do. It's difficult to talk about with your doctor, and even for medical professionals, it's a complex, delicate, and difficult-to-understand topic for healthy people, so I think it would be difficult to explain it, so would it be better to seek advice in places like this?

**Answer:** <p data-start="0" data-end="127">You've been carrying a lot of pain and confusion for many years. It's natural to worry about how to talk about it and how much you can talk about. You've worked so hard up until now, and now you're looking deep inside yourself for even a little healing - that in itself is a great sign of courage.</p><p data-start="0" data-end="127"> If you feel afraid to talk to someone directly, it might be a good idea to start by speaking out in an online support group or community. Of course, in these online community spaces, your stories and questions are always welcome. In such places, you can talk at your own pace, and people who have had similar experiences may be able to comfort you and say, "I understand." By talking, you may gradually start to feel like you're not alone.</p><p data-start="0" data-end="127"> And when you are mentally ready, professional support will also be available. Trauma-understanding counselors, doctors, consultants, and supporters will be able to listen to your experiences, feelings, and physical reactions without blaming you. It's okay to experience strong emotions, flashbacks, or physical discomfort. These reactions are not "strange" but are evidence that your mind and body have been desperately trying to protect you.</p><p data-start="0" data-end="127"> The pace at which you speak and how much you talk are entirely up to you. There's no need to force yourself to say things you don't want to say. It's enough if you can find a safe place and, little by little, you can start to feel at ease.</p><p data-start="0" data-end="127"> If you are experiencing physical discomfort (itchiness, pain, insomnia, shortness of breath, etc.), it is fine to simply tell a trusted healthcare professional, such as an obstetrician-gynecologist or psychiatrist, that "stress may be related." Even this is an important step.</p><p data-start="796" data-end="884"> You deserve to ask for help and receive support in whatever form it comes in. The pain you've felt is real, and trying to ease that pain is also legitimate and very important.</p><p data-start="796" data-end="884"> If you'd like, our website also provides information on trauma support organizations that you can use with confidence, so please take a look.</p><p data-start="886" data-end="1009"> Please remember.<br data-start="899" data-end="902" /> You are never alone.<br data-start="920" data-end="923" /> There are always people who care about you and want to protect your heart.<br data-start="957" data-end="960" /> And no matter how slow your progress may seem, every step you take is surely leading to recovery.

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/what-does-it-mean-to-be-loved-790)

**Question:** What does it mean to be loved?

**Answer:** <p data-start="71" data-end="189">Thank you for your question. It's a fundamental question.</p><p data-start="71" data-end="189"> I think being loved means feeling truly safe when you're with someone and feeling like "the other person is really paying attention to me."<br data-start="131" data-end="134" /> It's a relationship where your feelings, boundaries, and needs are respected without being denied.</p><p data-start="191" data-end="288"> True love and compassion is not about getting special favors.<br data-start="223" data-end="226" /> It's in the little things that accumulate every day, such as the other person being there for you and accepting you as you are.</p><p data-start="290" data-end="387"> Love may mean someone listening carefully when you share your fears.<br data-start="321" data-end="324" /> It may be that they offer kind words of encouragement when you are going through a difficult time.<br data-start="350" data-end="353" /> And I think it's about creating a place where you can be yourself.</p><p data-start="389" data-end="505"> A loving connection isn't a relationship where only one person has to work hard.<br data-start="417" data-end="420" /> It's a relationship where we trust each other and feel comfortable opening up to each other.<br data-start="444" data-end="447" /> Even if I have days when I lose confidence in myself, that person's warmth reminds me that I am someone who is cherished.</p><p data-start="507" data-end="601"> Feeling loved helps us realize that it's okay to value our feelings and that our voices have power.<br data-start="560" data-end="563" /> And above all, it makes you feel like you don't have to live alone.</p><p data-start="603" data-end="673"> Someone is there for you, respects your experience, and genuinely cares about you.<br data-start="644" data-end="647" /> I think that's what it means to be loved.

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/ive-been-reconnecting-with-my-sexuality-and-watching-porn-but-i-came-across-a-video-of-a-woman-being-assaulted-it-triggered-memories-of-my-own-experience-and-now-i-feel-disgusted-with-porn-im-not-read)

**Question:** I've been reconnecting with my sexuality and watching porn, but I came across a video of a woman being assaulted. It triggered memories of my own experience, and now I feel disgusted with porn. I'm not ready to fully process my own trauma right now. How can I navigate this?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting us with something so personal. I'm really sorry you experienced this, and I can tell how upsetting it must have been to come across that material unexpectedly. Stumbling upon assault depicted in pornography can be deeply disturbing, especially when you're a survivor yourself. It's understandable to feel disgusted or even ashamed, given the painful memories it triggered. Sometimes, when we're trying to explore our sexuality again, even small reminders can bring us back to moments that were traumatic. It can be hard to let yourself feel curious or open when those triggers resurface. It makes sense that this has shifted how you feel about porn. What you witnessed violated the consensual, pleasurable sexuality you were seeking, and it connected painfully to your own experience.</p>
<p>First, please know that you get to take all the time you need before you're ready to fully reckon with your trauma. This process of reconnecting with your sexuality after trauma is not a straight path, and it can have moments of feeling good mixed with sudden setbacks. Healing isn't linear, and sometimes we need to focus on managing the present moment rather than diving into deeper processing. That's not avoidance. That's honoring where you are right now and what feels manageable. It might help to remember that healing doesn't require you to face everything at once. It's okay to acknowledge the pain without diving into it before you feel ready.</p>
<p>In terms of navigating this situation, it might help to give yourself permission to step back from pornography entirely for now if that feels right. If you can, consider pausing activities that bring up strong triggers, like certain types of explicit videos, at least until you feel more stable or have coping tools that work for you on hand. Your comfort and sense of safety matter, and if porn no longer feels like a positive part of reconnecting with your sexuality, you can set it aside without judgment. Many survivors find that their relationship with sexual content shifts over time, and that's okay. You might return to it later, or you might discover other ways of exploring your sexuality that feel better aligned with where you are.</p>
<p>If and when you do feel ready to engage with sexual content again, you have options that might feel safer and more aligned with your values. You might consider seeking out ethical porn created by survivor-centered or feminist producers who prioritize consent, authenticity, and the wellbeing of performers. Ethical porn often involves transparent practices around performer consent, fair compensation, and content that centers mutual pleasure rather than eroticizing harm. Some survivors find this helps them feel more connected to sexuality in a way that doesn't replicate violence or exploitation.</p>
<p>There are also other forms of sexual content that some people find less triggering or more manageable, such as audio erotica, written erotica, or even educational content about pleasure and sexuality that focuses on connection and consent. Audio erotica, in particular, can allow you to use your imagination and maintain more control over the pacing and imagery, which some survivors find less overwhelming than visual content. Written erotica offers similar benefits, letting you engage at your own pace and step away whenever you need to. These alternatives can provide ways to explore your sexuality that feel gentler and more within your control. But there's no pressure to make that decision right now.</p>
<p>What happened when you encountered that video wasn't your fault, and the fact that it triggered you doesn't mean you're failing at healing or that you've lost progress.&nbsp; Feeling revulsion or disgust right now doesn't mean you're stuck in that place forever. It just means your mind and body are alerting you to a boundary that needs respect and gentleness.</p>
<p>For now, focus on what helps you feel grounded and safe. That might mean engaging with your sexuality in different ways, through your own touch, fantasy, erotica, audio content, or simply giving yourself space from sexual content altogether. Continue trusting how you feel in the moment, step away from what triggers you when you need to, and come back to exploring your sexuality only when it feels safe to do so. You're allowed to define what reconnecting with your sexuality looks like for you, and that can change as often as you need it to. Thank you for trusting us with this we are here for you.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-twelve-my-family-went-on-a-trip-together-with-my-parents-siblings-and-extended-family-including-aunts-uncles-and-cousins-while-we-were-all-sitting-around-a-table-one-of-my-cousins-who-was-t)

**Question:** When I was twelve, my family went on a trip together with my parents, siblings, and extended family including aunts, uncles, and cousins. While we were all sitting around a table, one of my cousins who was thirty-one at the time put his hand under my shirt and rubbed my back, stomach, shoulders, neck, and parts of my chest. No one at the table intervened. I stayed very still until he stopped and walked away. He then did the same thing to his girlfriend, which confused me. I have two questions: First, would this be considered molestation? It was uncomfortable, but I'm not sure if that word applies since I don't know if it was sexual and nothing more happened. This experience did affect how I feel around him. Second, my cousin is now a teacher and was recently elected as a councilman. I'm wondering if I should do something to protect the children he interacts with, or if I'm making too big a deal out of it since he's well-regarded in my family and community. I'm not sure what to do in this situation.

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting us with this. I'm really sorry you had to experience that, especially at such a young age when you were still processing other traumatic events. It's completely understandable to feel confused about whether your cousin's actions "count" as molestation, and to worry about his access to children now. What your cousin did to you when you were twelve was inappropriate, boundary-violating, and harmful. An adult putting their hands under a child's shirt to touch their body, including their chest, without consent can be a form of child sexual abuse. In many instances, sexual abuse doesn't necessarily include overtly sexual gestures as we might typically picture them. What does matter is the sense of violation, your age at the time, and how his touching made you feel both in the moment and afterward.</p>
<p>Accepting or using a term like "molestation" can be complicated, and only you can decide what language feels right for your story. You get to decide what words describe your experience, and whether you call it molestation, abuse, or something else is entirely your choice. What matters most is that you recognize this shouldn't have happened to you. It's natural to wonder whether you're overreacting, especially if nothing "worse" happened or if people around you seemed to dismiss it, but what you felt at the time (fear and that sense of freezing) indicates that you instinctively knew his behavior wasn't right. Your discomfort and the lasting impact you describe are completely valid responses to a violation of your body and boundaries. Even one uncomfortable incident can have real effects on how you feel around someone.</p>
<p>The fact that no adult at that table protected you compounds the harm. Children deserve to be safe in their families, and the adults present had a responsibility to intervene. Your cousin's behavior suggests he felt entitled to touch people's bodies without clear consent, and the context of touching a child in this way is particularly concerning regardless of his intentions. You don't need to know whether he experienced it as sexual to recognize that what he did was wrong and that it affected you.</p>
<p>Your instinct to be concerned about his access to children as a teacher and public official is worth taking seriously. You're not making too big a deal out of it. Your second question about whether you should warn others can bring up so many conflicting thoughts: not wanting to create a stir within your family, worrying you might be causing trouble for no reason, yet feeling a responsibility to protect children that your cousin might interact with. Those conflicting emotions are common. Adults who violate children's boundaries, especially in ways that involve intimate touching, do pose potential risks to other children. If you do ever feel the need to share your experience, especially with someone in a position to watch out for children's well-being, that doesn't mean you're making a big deal out of nothing. You have every right to voice your discomfort over what happened.</p>
<p>The decision about whether and how to act on this concern is deeply personal and complex. While the choice to speak up or not is yours alone, it may help to confide in someone you trust (perhaps a counselor, a friend, or a confidential helpline) just to sort through how you're feeling and talk about your options before making any big decisions. You might consider speaking with a counselor or advocate who specializes in childhood sexual abuse to help you think through your options, which could range from making a report to a school district or professional licensing board, to speaking with someone you trust about your concerns, to focusing on your own healing and letting this information simply inform your own boundaries with him.</p>
<p>However you decide to move forward, your feelings about this situation are valid, and you deserve to feel safe and supported. Being beloved in a family or community doesn't mean someone hasn't caused harm, and it doesn't erase what happened to you. Being in limbo about whether something truly "counts" as abuse often makes it harder to resolve, but your reaction, both then and now, tells you that it left an impact. You deserve support as you navigate both the impact of what he did and the difficult questions you're holding now about how to move forward. Your awareness and care for other children's safety speaks to your strength, and you deserve that same care and protection for yourself. Thank you for reaching out to us. You are not alone.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-13-i-briefly-placed-my-hand-on-a-younger-childs-crotch-and-moved-it-when-he-indicated-it-was-his-private-area-i-was-going-through-puberty-and-experiencing-curiosity-without-intent-to-do-any)

**Question:** When I was 13, I briefly placed my hand on a younger child's crotch and moved it when he indicated it was his private area. I was going through puberty and experiencing curiosity, without intent to do anything sexual. My therapist said this would be considered inappropriate exploration due to the age gap, but not COCSA, sexual assault, molestation, or criminal activity. I'm wondering if you agree with this assessment and whether there's an age at which exploration becomes something different.

**Answer:** <p>I can hear how much weight you've been carrying about this memory, and I'm glad you've been working with a therapist to process it.&nbsp;Your therapist's assessment aligns with what research tells us about childhood sexual development. When children and young adolescents explore bodies and boundaries, there is a wide range of what professionals consider typical sexual curiosity. For behavior to be labeled criminal or to be considered an assault, there generally must be intention to harm, a significant violation of one child's safety or autonomy, or a recognizable pattern of coercion. In your situation, you were young, still navigating new sensations and curiosities that come with early adolescence, and you stopped as soon as the younger child reacted. These factors are important in understanding why many would classify this as age-appropriate exploration that was nevertheless inappropriate due to the age gap, rather than malicious or assaultive behavior.</p>
<p>At 13, you were in the midst of puberty, a time when curiosity about bodies and sexual feelings is completely normal, even if it doesn't always lead to appropriate behavior. Questions about when "normal curiosity" crosses a line don't usually hinge on a single age but rather on the context, intent, and presence or absence of coercion.</p>
<p>It's common for preteens or young teens to experience heightened curiosity during puberty, and the way you describe this brief, awkward moment seems to reflect that. Therapists, especially those experienced with childhood development, often rely on guidelines that look at whether behaviors are mutual, motivated by equal curiosity, and promptly stop once a boundary is communicated. Even though there was an age gap here, you were still only 13, navigating a surge of new feelings and impulses, which often places this kind of event in a different category from predatory or forced situations where there's a clear power dynamic and intent to harm.</p>
<p>The age difference you've described does create a power imbalance that makes this interaction inappropriate, and it sounds like you understand that. However, inappropriate doesn't automatically mean the same thing as abusive or predatory. Teenagers are still developing their understanding of boundaries, consent, and appropriate behavior. This is why we provide guidance and education rather than assuming harmful intent in every situation. What distinguishes exploration from harmful behavior includes factors like intent, the nature of the interaction, whether force or coercion was used, and how someone responds when boundaries are communicated. You've described moving your hand immediately when the younger child indicated it was his private area, which shows you responded to his boundary and honored his signals by stopping. That points toward curiosity rather than abuse.</p>
<p>If your therapist feels it is within the realm of normal, yet inappropriate, developmental exploration, it sounds like they're acknowledging that it was not the ideal way to learn about bodies and boundaries, yet it also wasn't rooted in abuse or intent to victimize. It's completely understandable to question whether your therapist fully grasps these concepts, especially when you're seeking clarity about something so important. Therapists who specialize in sexual development and trauma do understand these nuances, though not every therapist has this specific training. If you feel uncertain about your therapist's expertise in this area, it would be reasonable to seek consultation with someone who specializes in childhood sexual development or adolescent behavior.</p>
<p>What you're describing sounds like a moment where your developing sexual curiosity led to inappropriate behavior that you stopped when a boundary was communicated. This doesn't mean the behavior was okay, and it doesn't erase the fact that it may have been confusing or uncomfortable for the other child. It does mean that you can hold both truths: that the behavior was inappropriate and that it doesn't define you as a person or predict future behavior. Many people have moments in their sexual development that they wish they could undo, and processing these memories with compassion for everyone involved, including yourself as a young teenager still learning, is part of healing. Thank you for trusting us with this.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-became-pregnant-after-being-repeatedly-sexually-abused-by-multiple-men-my-baby-was-stillborn-and-i-never-had-dna-testing-done-is-it-strange-that-i-wish-i-knew-who-her-father-was-788)

**Question:** I became pregnant after being repeatedly sexually abused by multiple men. My baby was stillborn, and I never had DNA testing done. Is it strange that I wish I knew who her father was?

**Answer:** <p>I'm so sorry for the overwhelming pain and loss you've been through. There is nothing weird or strange about wishing you knew who your daughter's father was. Your feelings make complete sense, and many survivors in similar situations share this desire to know.</p>
<p>When pregnancy results from sexual abuse, especially when multiple perpetrators were involved, not knowing paternity can add another layer of grief and complexity to an already devastating experience. You may wonder about this for many reasons. Perhaps to have a fuller sense of your daughter's identity, to understand her medical history, to find any fragment of meaning or connection after everything you endured, or simply because it feels like an important piece of information that was taken from you along with so much else. Wanting that piece of information can be a way of honoring her as well as acknowledging your own reality. The desire to know doesn't mean you're trying to maintain a connection to your abusers or that you're minimizing what happened. It's a completely natural human response to want to understand the full story of someone you carried and lost.</p>
<p>The loss of your daughter through stillbirth is a profound grief all on its own. When you add the trauma of how she was conceived and the uncertainty around paternity, you're navigating an incredibly complex web of emotions. You experienced violence, you experienced pregnancy, you experienced loss, and you're living with unanswered questions that can't be resolved. Each of these realities deserves acknowledgment and space. Survivors often feel a deep need to understand every aspect of what happened to them, no matter how complicated or hurtful the circumstances might have been.</p>
<p>Your feelings about wanting to know who her father was are valid, even if they feel confusing or uncomfortable at times. Grief and trauma don't follow predictable patterns, and there's no right or wrong way to feel about any aspect of this experience. None of your thoughts or wishes about your daughter's paternity diminish the depth of love and sorrow you carry for her. You deserve compassion and support as you continue processing everything you've been through.</p>
<p>If lingering questions are weighing on your heart and adding to your grief, reaching out for support can make a difference. Talking to someone who understands sexual abuse trauma, such as an advocate or counselor experienced with these issues, can help you process these very natural and human feelings. Thank you for trusting us with this. You are not alone.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/if-someone-who-was-12-13-placed-their-hand-on-a-younger-childs-fully-clothed-crotch-area-for-a-few-seconds-and-removed-their-hand-when-the-younger-child-said-something-would-this-typically-be-consider)

**Question:** If someone who was 12-13 placed their hand on a younger child's fully clothed crotch area for a few seconds, and removed their hand when the younger child said something, would this typically be considered criminal behavior or abuse/assault in the US?

**Answer:** <p>This situation involves inappropriate sexual contact with a significant age gap between the children involved. Whether this would be legally classified as abuse, assault, or criminal behavior depends on several factors, including the specific laws in your state, the ages involved, and the context of what happened. Different jurisdictions handle these situations differently, and some might address this through child welfare systems rather than the criminal justice system due to the ages of those involved.</p>
<p>What's important to understand is that even brief contact can cross boundaries, and the age difference between a 12-13 year old and a 5-6 year old is significant in terms of development and power dynamics. At the same time, adolescents are still developing their understanding of boundaries and appropriate behavior. Sexual curiosity is normal, but acting on it in ways that involve touching younger children is not okay, even if it was brief or the older child stopped when told.</p>
<p>If you're struggling with this situation, whether you were the younger child, the older child, or someone else affected by it, those feelings are valid. Many people carry confusion or distress about childhood experiences like this. Speaking with a therapist who understands child development and sexual behavior can help you process what happened and find a path forward.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-younger-9-someone-four-years-older-than-me-13-touched-my-breast-and-genital-area-while-i-was-sleeping-would-this-generally-be-considered-child-on-child-sexual-abuse-or-would-it-be-viewed-as)

**Question:** When I was younger (9), someone four years older than me (13) touched my breast and genital area while I was sleeping. Would this generally be considered child-on-child sexual abuse, or would it be viewed as criminal activity in most places in the US?

**Answer:** <p>This situation would generally be understood as child-on-child sexual abuse (COCSA). The four-year age gap, the nature of the touching, and the fact that one child was asleep are all significant factors that indicate this was an abusive situation rather than age-appropriate exploration.</p>
<p>Whether this would be considered criminal activity varies by state. Many jurisdictions have laws addressing inappropriate sexual contact between minors, though the legal system often handles cases involving younger individuals differently than cases involving adults. Some states might classify this as a criminal act, while others might address it through family court or child welfare systems. The legal response depends on the specific laws where this occurred and the particular circumstances of the situation.</p>
<p>It's important to recognize that sexual curiosity is a normal part of development, and at 13, someone is still learning and growing. However, this doesn't make the behavior okay. Touching someone sexually while they're asleep crosses important boundaries, regardless of the age of the person who did it. Understanding that harmful behavior happened is separate from understanding that young people can learn, change, and move forward in healthy ways.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/does-this-count-as-grooming-my-uncle-has-acted-in-ways-that-felt-strange-to-me-when-i-was-younger-he-would-insist-i-sit-on-his-lap-even-when-i-wanted-to-get-off-and-hed-call-me-back-he-also-insisted-i)

**Question:** Does this count as grooming? My uncle has acted in ways that felt strange to me. When I was younger, he would insist I sit on his lap even when I wanted to get off, and he'd call me back. He also insisted I mouth, suck, and chew on his ears, which confused me even then. There was always some kind of physical contact when we hung out. As I got older, these interactions felt increasingly uncomfortable. When he visited, he would crowd my space and get touchy-feely on my bed. I have clear memories of being on his lap and accidentally feeling what I knew was an erection. I didn't realize this wasn't normal until a family member pointed it out to my mother, which led to a family conflict. My mother stopped talking to him for a few months, but eventually everything went back to normal. Now I'm wondering if I misunderstood the situation and got him in trouble for no reason, even though I remember him leaping off my bed when he heard my mother coming.

**Answer:** <p>I want to start by saying that I'm really glad you're asking this question and examining these experiences, even though I know it's painful and confusing. What you've described reflects patterns that are absolutely consistent with grooming and sexual abuse. These do not seem like misunderstandings, and you didn't get anyone in trouble for no reason. Your uncle's behavior was inappropriate, violated your boundaries, and seemed to have caused you harm.</p>
<p><a href="https://health.clevelandclinic.org/what-is-grooming">Grooming</a> is a process where an older person repeatedly engages a child or adolescent in uncomfortable, sexually charged physical interaction&mdash;especially when the child feels compelled to go along with it. It often involves repeated patterns that confuse a young person about what is appropriate and may leave them blaming themselves when they sense something is wrong. What you're describing fits this pattern pretty clearly.</p>
<p>It sounds like he used physical closeness that felt confusing and uncomfortable, insisted on contact even when you wanted to stop, and engaged in behaviors that had no appropriate explanation&mdash;like having you put your mouth on his ears. That's not normal uncle-niece interaction. The fact that you felt his erection while on his lap is significant. Becoming sexually aroused by a child is never accidental or innocent, and it speaks to the sexual nature of his behavior toward you.&nbsp;</p>
<p>The fact that he jumped off your bed when he heard your mother coming is a sign he knew his behavior was not okay. People don't hide innocent behavior. The way your mind is second-guessing yourself right now is actually a very common response to abuse, especially when it happens within families. Children frequently feel unsure about speaking up for fear of causing a family rift or not being believed, which deepens their confusion. When the adults around us minimize what happened or return to "normal," it sends the message that maybe it wasn't that bad, maybe we overreacted, maybe we caused problems. It's understandable you felt guilty once things settled down in the family, but your discomfort and the details you describe suggest you were not imagining things, nor were you at fault. Your aunt saw something concerning enough to speak up. Your mother was upset enough to stop speaking to him. These reactions happened because what he did was wrong, not because you misunderstood.</p>
<p>Your reaction when you were older, recognizing that his behavior made you feel increasingly uneasy, is your own awareness telling you something was amiss. You deserve to trust that reaction. You mentioned feeling like you got him in trouble, but I want to gently redirect that thought. He got himself in trouble by choosing to behave inappropriately with you. Children and young people are never responsible for an adult's choices or the consequences that follow. Your job was never to protect him or manage his behavior. Your job was simply to be a kid, and he violated that.</p>
<p>The confusion you're feeling makes complete sense. Our brains aren't designed to hold the reality that someone we're supposed to trust could harm us, so we often create alternative explanations that feel less threatening. But your discomfort was real. Your body's signals that something was wrong were accurate. Trusting those feelings now, even retroactively, is an important part of honoring your own experience and moving toward healing.</p>
<p>If these memories still weigh on you or leave you feeling conflicted, it can be helpful to reach out for support from a counselor, advocate, or trusted confidant who respects your feelings and can help you process them in a safe space. You deserve to have your questions taken seriously. Ultimately, what you experienced was not normal, and your childhood self had every reason to feel uncomfortable. You deserved to be safe and at ease around your uncle, and his behavior was what broke that trust, not anything you did. Thank you for sharing this with us. You are not alone.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/is-it-normal-that-i-froze-when-a-man-masturbated-next-to-me-on-the-bus-while-looking-at-me-i-couldnt-move-until-i-got-home-783)

**Question:** Is it normal that I froze when a man masturbated next to me on the bus while looking at me? I couldn't move until I got home.

**Answer:** <p>I'm so sorry this happened to you. What you experienced was absolutely a normal reaction. Feeling like all you could do was stay completely still is a very common response to a frightening or upsetting situation, even if you think you "should" have done something else.</p>
<p>When we encounter danger or violation, our bodies automatically respond in ways we can't control. When confronted with an act that feels threatening or unexpected, the body sometimes reacts by freezing, which can serve as a protective mechanism to keep you safe. While many people have heard of "fight or flight," freezing is actually just as common, especially in situations involving sexual violation. This happens because your brain rapidly assesses the situation and determines that staying still is the safest option. In that moment on the bus, your body was protecting you in the way it knew how.</p>
<p>It's important to understand that freezing doesn't mean you consented, wanted it to happen, or did anything wrong. It doesn't reflect a failure on your part, and it doesn't mean you did anything wrong. You responded the best way you could in that moment. Many survivors struggle with guilt or confusion about why they couldn't move or speak up, but this response is neurobiological&mdash;it's not a choice or a failure on your part.</p>
<p>If you're feeling unsettled or distressed now, it's entirely normal to experience lingering effects, such as flashbacks or anxiety, even after the immediate danger has passed. Healing isn't linear and everyone's timeline is different. Should you ever feel like talking about it or seeking support, professionals at sexual violence hotlines or local resource centers can offer understanding and guidance without judgment. What happened to you was a violation, and that man's actions were wrong. Your reaction was not your fault. Your safety and well-being matter, and you deserve care and compassion as you process what happened. Thank you for trusting us with this.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/is-it-sexual-assault-if-i-said-no-several-times-but-the-other-person-kept-insisting-until-i-eventually-said-yes-782)

**Question:** Is it sexual assault if I said no several times, but the other person kept insisting until I eventually said yes?

**Answer:** <p>I'm so sorry you went through this. What you're describing is a situation where your initial boundaries were clear. You said no multiple times. When someone continues to pressure you after you've declined, wearing you down until you agree, that "yes" isn't freely given. <a href="https://care.ucr.edu/education/what-is-consent">Consent</a> requires that both people feel comfortable and respected in their decision.
