If you felt like you had to say "yes" just to stop the repeated insisting, or because you were exhausted by the pressure, it's understandable that this experience would leave you feeling violated.</p>
<p>Sexual assault doesn't only happen with physical force. It can also occur through coercion, where someone's repeated requests wear you down until you feel you have no other option but to agree. <a href="https://www.medicalnewstoday.com/articles/sexual-coercion">Coercion</a> removes genuine consent because real consent needs to be freely given, not obtained through persistence, pressure, or manipulation.</p>
<p>You did nothing wrong by initially refusing and later giving in under pressure. It's completely understandable to question what happened and whether it "counts" as assault. Many survivors struggle with this exact question, especially when they eventually said yes. It can be confusing to recognize that a reluctant "yes" doesn't automatically mean there was genuine consent. But here's what matters: you tried to hold your boundary. You communicated what you wanted, or rather, what you didn't want. The fact that someone kept pushing past those stated boundaries until you relented doesn't mean you truly consented. It means you were worn down.</p>
<p>Your feelings about what happened matter, and if it was distressing or felt forced, your discomfort is valid. What you experienced was a violation of your boundaries. Whether you call it sexual assault or not is entirely up to you. Some people find that naming it helps them process what happened, while others don't feel that label fits their experience. Both responses are okay. What matters most is that you weren't treated with the respect you deserved, and what happened to you wasn't right.</p>
<p>You deserve support and understanding as you sort through these complex emotions. It may help to find someone you trust or a professional resource to talk with about how this is impacting you. Thank you for trusting us with this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-younger-my-best-friend-would-touch-my-waist-and-legs-and-pull-me-close-to-me-which-made-me-uncomfortable-when-i-said-anything-to-her-she-would-tell-me-i-was-being-dramatic-and-self-absorbed)

**Question:** When I was younger, my best friend would touch my waist and legs and pull me close to me, which made me uncomfortable. When I said anything to her, she would tell me I was being dramatic and self-absorbed. Another time when we were talking about kissing, I could tell she wanted me to ask her to kiss, so we did. But I hated her, so it doesn't make sense to me that I genuinely wanted to. Was I groomed?

**Answer:** <p>I'm so glad you reached out, and I want you to know that what you're describing sounds incredibly confusing and painful. It sounds like these memories have been on your mind for a long time, and I'm so sorry you've been carrying confusion and distress around them.&nbsp;</p>
<p><a href="https://health.clevelandclinic.org/what-is-grooming">Grooming</a> often involves an older or more powerful individual creating trust or dependence, then crossing boundaries in a way that feels manipulative. In your situation, you and your friend were both children, which makes this more complicated, but the signals you're describing (e.g. her ignoring your discomfort, calling you "dramatic," and pressuring you into intimacy) do seem to reflect boundary crossings rather than healthy curiosity.</p>
<p>Children exploring crushes or gentle curiosity usually stop immediately if someone expresses uncertainty or seems upset. The fact that you sensed pressure and were then made to feel "self-absorbed" for voicing discomfort suggests that she disregarded your boundaries. That doesn't necessarily fit the classic picture of grooming, but it does point to a dynamic where she was pushing you into physical and emotional situations you didn't truly consent to.</p>
<p>It's understandable that you feel conflicted, especially if you remember moments where you felt you "wanted" her attention or felt compelled to go along with it even though you didn't enjoy it. Children often respond that way in confusing situations, and it's never an indication that you're to blame for what happened. It is normal to have wanted closeness or to have tried to meet a friend's expectations, even when it left you feeling confused or upset. It's really common for survivors to feel confused about why they went along with something they didn't want, or to question whether they "let it happen." But <a href="https://care.ucr.edu/education/what-is-consent">consent</a> isn't just the absence of saying no&mdash;it's an enthusiastic, freely given yes. When someone is being manipulated, dismissed, or pressured, their ability to freely consent is compromised. You were also very young, still learning about boundaries and relationships, which made you even more vulnerable to this kind of dynamic.</p>
<p>Whether we label it grooming or not, it's important to recognize the impact it had on you. Those reactions alone tell you the situation crossed lines. Your feelings (the discomfort, anger, and the sense that something was off) were valid. You deserve to have your experience validated and to process these feelings without needing to have the perfect label for what happened. What matters most is that you were hurt, your boundaries were violated, and you're carrying the weight of that now. Your feelings are real and they matter.&nbsp;</p>
<p>Even if it's many years later, talking about these memories with someone you trust or a professional who understands childhood boundary issues can help you make sense of why this still weighs on your mind. You deserve clarity, and you deserve respect for your instincts and boundaries, no matter what terms we use to describe your past experiences. There is support out there if you need it. I'm really sorry you went through this. You're not alone.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-younger-i-went-to-a-party-with-a-male-friend-who-had-made-multiple-advances-towards-me-that-i-had-clearly-rejected-at-the-party-he-brought-me-drinks-and-i-got-extremely-drunk-i-remember-kis)

**Question:** When I was younger, I went to a party with a male friend who had made multiple advances towards me that I had clearly rejected. At the party, he brought me drinks and I got extremely drunk. I remember kissing him and not resisting, but things are blurry after that. I remember being unable to walk due to intoxication and being carried out to the car, then sobering up in his bed the next morning. I told him afterward that I didn't want any physical relationship. I blamed myself for being drunk and making "dumb decisions," but my husband recently told me this was date rape and that my friend took advantage of me. I had never thought of it that way before. Is he right?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for sharing such a difficult and personal story. It makes a lot of sense that you walked away from that morning feeling embarrassed, even though what happened was completely not your fault. When someone has too much to drink, it can be very confusing to piece together what happened the night before, and it's natural for self-blame to take over. It sounds like you'd already made it clear to him many times that you weren't interested in anything intimate, and that matters deeply in understanding what happened.</p>
<p>What you're describing is a situation where you were too intoxicated to consent. When someone is unable to walk due to intoxication, they cannot give meaningful consent to sexual activity. The law recognizes this, and so does our understanding of healthy sexual interactions. <a href="https://care.ucr.edu/education/what-is-consent">Consent</a> requires the ability to make a clear, informed decision, and that capacity simply isn't present at that level of intoxication. Even if you remember moments of kissing or not resisting, that doesn't equal consent when you're that impaired. Being heavily intoxicated means you weren't in a position to freely decide what you wanted or didn't want.</p>
<p>Your husband is identifying something important. Your friend knew you weren't interested in anything sexual. It sounds like you had told him this directly and repeatedly. He also saw how intoxicated you were, to the point where you couldn't walk on your own. Choosing to engage sexually with someone in that state, especially someone who had already clearly communicated they weren't interested, is taking advantage. You deserve to define your own experience, but many would see this as a significant boundary violation where your earlier refusal was ignored and your impaired state was taken advantage of.</p>
<p>It's incredibly common for survivors to blame themselves, especially when alcohol is involved. Our society often puts the responsibility on people who've been harmed rather than on the person who chose to act. But being drunk doesn't make you responsible for someone else's decision to have sex with you when you couldn't consent. You didn't make a "dumb decision." Someone made a choice about your body when you weren't capable of making that decision for yourself. Feeling embarrassed or blaming yourself is a common response, but it doesn't mean you're at fault. He had a responsibility to respect your clearly stated boundaries, especially when you were unable to speak for yourself due to intoxication.</p>
<p>The feelings you had afterward (e.g. the embarrassment, the self-blame, the need to set boundaries with him) are all normal responses to a violating experience. It's also completely normal to only later see it from another perspective, as your husband did, and start questioning the fairness of what happened. Sometimes it takes time and distance, or another person's perspective, to recognize what happened for what it was. There's no timeline for when you "should" have understood this, and however you're feeling about it now is valid. You have every right to feel unsettled or angry about this.</p>
<p>If it helps, talking through these feelings with someone supportive, whether a counselor, a trusted friend, or a confidential helpline, can provide clarity and the compassionate understanding you deserve. You aren't alone, and what occurred does not change your integrity or your worth. It was never your fault that someone else chose to disregard your boundaries when you couldn't protect them yourself. Thank you so much for reaching out to us and take gentle care of yourself.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/ive-never-been-sure-if-what-i-experienced-was-cocsa-my-childhood-friend-used-to-ask-me-to-have-sex-with-her-she-asked-me-multiple-times-and-once-shed-pressured-me-she-would-get-on-top-of-me-and-grind)

**Question:** I've never been sure if what I experienced was COCSA, my childhood friend used to ask me to have sex with her, she asked me multiple times and once she'd pressured me she would get on top of me and grind onto me and ask me "doesn't it feel good?". She also used to watch R rated movies and tell me we should "watch it for the sex scene" we were the same age and stayed friends throughout school years until she moved away. I never told anyone about it until I got with my first boyfriend.

**Answer:** <p>What you're describing sounds really confusing to make sense of, and it's completely understandable that you've been uncertain about how to understand these experiences. It sounds like this has weighed on you for a long time, and the fact that you're asking this question shows you're trying to process something that has stayed with you.</p>
<p>Sexual curiosity and exploration between children of similar ages is a normal part of growing up, and this includes exploration between children of the same gender. Many children are naturally curious about bodies and what feels good physically. Same-sex exploration (apologies I am making an assumption about your gender based on quesiton context) in childhood doesn't mean anything definitive about someone's sexuality, and it's an experience many people have as part of normal development. What can make childhood sexual experiences feel harmful or uncomfortable is when one child repeatedly pressures the other, when requests continue after someone seems hesitant or unsure, or when the experience doesn't feel mutual or wanted. When sexual behaviors between children involve one child feeling coerced, unable to set boundaries, or uncomfortable, it can fall under <a href="https://www.ourwave.org/en/post/child-on-child-sexual-abuse-cocsa-frequently-asked-questions-and-considerations?id=child-on-child-sexual-abuse-cocsa-frequently-asked-questions-and-considerations">child-on-child sexual abuse</a>, even when both children share the same age.</p>
<p>Sometimes people carry extra layers of shame or confusion when childhood experiences that felt harmful also involved same-sex contact. It's important to separate these two things if you can. What matters is how the experience happened and how it made you feel. If you felt pressured or uncomfortable, that's about the pressure and lack of consent, not about the fact that this involved another girl.&nbsp;</p>
<p>It's understandable that these memories remain confusing, especially since you maintained a friendship with her for years afterward. Many people in similar situations feel torn because the person and the relationship held both good memories and unsettling moments. The important thing to remember is that none of this was your fault. Your sense that these encounters left you uneasy matters deeply, and what you felt at the time and how it affects you now are both valid.</p>
<p>You get to decide what this experience means to you and what language, if any, feels right for describing it. Some people find comfort in naming their experiences, while others prefer to simply acknowledge that something happened that affected them. There's no right or wrong way to understand your own story. Even without a formal label, your feelings are valid, and you aren't alone in wondering about experiences like this. It can help to talk with someone trained in these issues, like a support line or a trauma-informed counselor, so you can explore your feelings safely and at your own pace. Reaching out for understanding is an important step, and you deserve support from people who respect what you went through and help you move forward with self-compassion and acceptance.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-met-someone-online-who-has-been-pressuring-me-to-do-sexual-things-i-dont-want-to-do-they-send-me-explicit-content-pornography-and-make-me-feel-like-i-have-to-participate-is-this-exploitation-777)

**Question:** I met someone online who has been pressuring me to do sexual things I don't want to do. They send me explicit content (pornography) and make me feel like I have to participate. Is this exploitation?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out to us. It sounds like this person's behavior is placing you in situations where you feel pressured to do things you don't want to do, and that is absolutely not okay. What you're describing does sound like a form of exploitation. When someone pressures you into sexual activities you don't want to do, that's <a href="https://www.webmd.com/sex/what-is-sexual-coercion">coercion</a>, and it's never okay regardless of how you met them or what kind of relationship you have. Even if you are close in age, it's important to recognize that sending sexual content or pushing someone into any sexual activity without their clear agreement can be a form of exploitation or even abuse.</p>
<p>Feeling pressured and uncomfortable is a strong sign that what is happening is crossing your boundaries and violating your trust. <a href="https://www.stopchildabuse.org/education/what-is-online-child-exploitation">Exploitation </a>happens when someone takes advantage of an imbalance of power or uses manipulation to get you to do things that benefit them at your expense. Pressure is a form of coercion: it removes your genuine choice and consent. Real consent means you feel completely free to say no without fear of consequences, guilt, or continued pressure. When someone keeps pushing after you've expressed discomfort or hesitation, they're prioritizing their wants over your wellbeing and autonomy, which is not okay.</p>
<p>Being sent pornography and explicit content, especially when unwanted, is a form of sexual harassment. When this happens in an online relationship where someone is pressuring you for sexual interaction, it often serves to normalize sexual behavior, break down your boundaries, and groom you into compliance. This is a tactic used to make you feel like these requests are normal or that you owe them something.</p>
<p class="whitespace-normal break-words">It is not your fault that you feel uneasy. Any time someone forces or guilts you into sexual activities, even if it's online, that blame rests entirely on the person doing the pressuring. What's happening to you is not your fault. You deserve relationships, whether online or in person, where your boundaries are respected, where "no" is accepted the first time, and where you never feel pressured or obligated to do anything sexual. The fact that you're questioning whether this is exploitation shows your instincts are telling you something is wrong, and you should trust those instincts.</p>
<p class="whitespace-normal break-words">You might want to consider stopping communication with this person entirely. You don't owe them an explanation, and protecting yourself is what matters most. Ending contact with someone who is hurting you is a powerful way to reclaim your safety and boundaries. It's okay to block them, delete their messages, and remove yourself from a situation that makes you uncomfortable.</p>
<p class="whitespace-normal break-words">You deserve support. If you can, please consider talking to a trusted adult about what's going on, whether it's a parent, teacher, counselor, or another supportive figure you trust. Let them know you're uncomfortable with what this person is asking and the content being sent to you. You are not alone in feeling worried or confused, and getting help can make a huge difference in stopping this harmful behavior. A safe, caring adult can offer guidance, protect your privacy, and help you figure out the best steps to take so that you feel secure again. Thank you for trusting us with this. You are not alone.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/now-that-im-an-adult-i-understand-what-grooming-is-some-of-the-people-who-abused-me-i-thought-were-my-family-and-later-found-out-we-werent-even-related-what-i-thought-was-spending-time-with-my-grandmo)

**Question:** Now that I'm an adult, I understand what grooming is. Some of the people who abused me, I thought were my family, and later found out we weren't even related. What I thought was spending time with my grandmother was really her grooming me for my grandfather. How do I stop referring to them as grandma and grandpa? I know we weren't related from a very young age, but they were all I knew. I've tried to refer to them by their first names, but it just doesn't feel right. But I also don't like what happens to my chest when I say grandma and grandpa either.

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting us with this. What you're describing is such a profound betrayal on multiple levels. It sounds like these people manipulated not only your body and your boundaries, but also your understanding of who they were to you and what family meant. The grief and confusion you're experiencing around what to call them makes complete sense because language is tied to identity, memory, and meaning. It sounds like you're not just changing names. You're reworking your entire understanding of those relationships, and it's understandable to feel distress about how to address people who played a grandmotherly and grandfatherly role in your life but were in fact groomers.</p>
<p>The physical sensation in your chest when you use those familial terms is your body telling you something important. That tightness or discomfort is a trauma response, your nervous system recognizing the disconnect between the words you're using and the reality of what those people did to you. If you feel your chest tighten at certain words, that's your body's way of signaling distress, and it might need time to adjust to new terms or, if needed, to no terms at all. At the same time, using their first names doesn't feel right either because for so long, those weren't the names that held meaning in your world. You're caught between two truths: the relationship you believed you had and the reality of what was actually happening.</p>
<p>There's no right or wrong way to refer to them now, and it may help to experiment with different names before settling on something that feels dignified to you while acknowledging what they did. Some survivors find it helpful to create new terms entirely&mdash;sometimes simply saying "the people who raised me," "that couple," "that person," or "the man who hurt me" can be enough.</p>
<p>Others may choose entirely new labels based on first initials, using their first and last names together to create distance, or another reference that removes any endearing connotation. Some survivors alternate depending on context: using familial terms when talking about childhood memories because that reflects how you experienced it at the time, while using first names or other descriptors when talking about the abuse or your current understanding. Some people find that over time, one approach starts to feel more natural than others, while some continue to struggle with it, and both of those experiences are valid. It might feel strange at first, and that's completely normal. Healing often involves making changes that unsettle old patterns.</p>
<p>What matters most is giving yourself permission to navigate this however you need to, even if it's messy or inconsistent. You might use different terms on different days, or in different conversations, or even within the same sentence. Your healing doesn't require you to have this figured out perfectly. You're allowed to talk about them in any way that protects your peace.</p>
<p>If you find it difficult to navigate these feelings alone, confiding in a trained counselor or reaching out to a support service can be a helpful next step, giving you a space to process the complicated emotions around renaming people you once saw as part of your family. Above all, be patient and gentle with yourself while you find the words that feel safe and reflect the truth of your own story. Thank you for reaching out to us.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/for-a-long-time-i-was-afraid-to-have-kids-i-used-to-think-what-if-i-am-like-them-i-dont-want-to-harm-anyonethat-was-a-battle-into-my-late-20s-my-kids-are-now-14-and-17-and-im-so-proud-of-them-just-bei)

**Question:** For a long time, I was afraid to have kids. I used to think, "What if I am like them?" I don't want to harm anyone—that was a battle into my late 20s. My kids are now 14 and 17, and I'm so proud of them just being able to be kids. How do you tell your children your story? And how do you stop the verbal abuse from replaying in your mind at times when you weren't even thinking about it? Something on TV triggers a memory, and now you're just stuck. It's triggers everywhere, and every day I'm apologizing to my younger self because she didn't deserve any of that.

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out to us with this. First, I want to acknowledge the incredible strength it takes to break cycles and create a different experience for your children. Your pride in simply allowing your kids to experience their own uncomplicated childhoods is a testament to how deeply you value their safety and happiness, and that love will guide any conversations you choose to have with them.</p>
<p>The fact that you're proud of them for being able to be kids speaks volumes about the kind of parent you've become&mdash;one who prioritizes their wellbeing and childhood in ways you deserved but didn't receive. That fear you carried about repeating harm shows your commitment to doing things differently, and it's clear you've done exactly that. Many survivors worry about passing on harm, even unintentionally, and that concern shows how determined you've been not to repeat the pain you endured.</p>
<p>It can feel like standing at a crossroads when you think about sharing your past with your children, especially when your own memories are heavy and come rushing back uninvited. When it comes to sharing your story with them, there's no single right answer, and the decision is deeply personal. Some survivors choose to share age-appropriate information when their children are older, while others decide to keep their experiences private.</p>
<p>It might help to consider what you hope your children gain from knowing your story. Some parents find it meaningful to share in an age-appropriate way that they experienced tough times in childhood but are now in a much stronger, safer place. Others wait until their children ask or show readiness for deeper conversations. Some parents share their experiences to help their children understand family dynamics, to model resilience, to explain certain boundaries or behaviors, or to create openness about difficult topics. At 14 and 17, your children are at ages where they can understand complex situations, but you get to decide what feels right for your family.</p>
<p>You might start with broader conversations about respect, boundaries, and healthy relationships before deciding if or how to share your own experiences. Trust your instincts. Your lifelong desire to protect them will help you determine the details you feel comfortable including. The core message might be that hard things did happen, but you've worked to make sure they never experience the same. There's no obligation to share everything or anything at all. Your story is yours, and you get to choose who knows it and when.</p>
<p>The triggers you describe are a common and often upsetting part of healing from trauma. The intrusive thoughts and verbal abuse that replay in your mind are common trauma responses, and they don't mean you're stuck or that healing isn't happening. It can feel unfair, as though your mind presses "rewind" without your permission, forcing you to relive what happened.</p>
<p>When something triggers a memory, your nervous system is responding to a real threat you once faced, even though that threat isn't present now. These moments can feel overwhelming and disorienting, especially when they come out of nowhere. Recognizing that this is how trauma memories can operate is a start, but it doesn't make the flashbacks less painful. In those moments, something as simple as acknowledging, "I'm safe right now, even though my brain is remembering when I wasn't," can gently guide your thoughts back to the present.</p>
<p><a href="https://www.healthline.com/health/grounding-techniques">Grounding techniques</a> can help when you find yourself caught in a memory&mdash;engaging your senses by noticing what you can see, hear, touch, taste, or smell in the present moment, or noticing textures or familiar scents around you, can help remind your brain that you're safe now and that you're no longer in the place where the abuse occurred. Some survivors find it helpful to have a phrase they repeat to themselves, something like "that was then, this is now" or "I'm safe in this moment." It can be a real challenge not to blame yourself for having these reminders, but reminding the younger you she did nothing wrong can gradually ease some of that burden.</p>
<p>The apologizing to your younger self shows compassion and recognition that none of what happened was your fault, and that practice of honoring that younger version of yourself is actually part of healing. You're already using such a compassionate approach toward that child you once were, apologizing to her and acknowledging she didn't deserve any of it. That act is more powerful than it might seem. It's a way of gently undoing the shame she was forced to carry. Over time, it can reframe your sense of self so that when triggers do happen, you meet them with kindness toward who you used to be rather than anger or blame. Healing often means tending to yourself just as you'd care for one of your own children&mdash;offering reassurance, patience when memories flare up unexpectedly, and acceptance of all the complicated feelings that come along.</p>
<p>Living with triggers is exhausting, and it's okay to acknowledge how hard it is to navigate a world where reminders exist everywhere. If you ever feel uncertain about how to handle these conversations or find the triggers too overwhelming, many survivors find that working with a trauma-informed therapist, particularly someone trained in approaches like <a href="https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/22641-emdr-therapy">EMDR</a> or <a href="https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/therapy-types/trauma-focused-cognitive-behavior-therapy?msockid=1103576693d9679310b645d892bb66b2">trauma-focused cognitive behavioral therapy</a>, can help reduce the intensity and frequency of intrusive thoughts over time and can work alongside you in deciding how to communicate your experiences. These therapeutic approaches can help your brain process memories differently so they don't hijack your present moments as intensely.</p>
<p>You have a wonderful instinct for protecting your family, and it shows in the thought you've put into every word. You've already done so much work to create safety for yourself and your children, and you deserve support in managing these ongoing effects of what you survived. You're doing something remarkable by recognizing these patterns, showing yourself compassion, and continuing to move forward even when the past shows up uninvited. Thank you for sharing your experience with us.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/ive-been-having-memories-resurface-about-sexual-activities-with-my-older-cousin-when-i-was-young-and-im-trying-to-understand-if-this-was-cocsa-the-experiences-started-before-i-was-9-my-cousin-who-is-t)

**Question:** I've been having memories resurface about sexual activities with my older cousin when I was young, and I'm trying to understand if this was COCSA. The experiences started before I was 9 - my cousin, who is three years older, would ask me to touch him sexually. I remember feeling hesitant and uncomfortable at first. We also had games where we'd expose ourselves to each other and touch ourselves and each other. He showed me what penetration was, and while there was genital contact, I don't think penetration happened. Keeping it secret was part of the "game," and I was terrified of being caught. After the first hesitant times, I became less afraid of the activities themselves and more afraid of being discovered. The activities stopped when we got older and saw each other less often. I still have a good relationship with my cousin and don't think he remembers. I'm struggling to understand whether this was just exploration or something harmful, especially since I have a history of being groomed and my family has a habit of reacting to things in a way that does not help and sometimes traumatizes me. I'm also not sure if my distress comes more from fear of discovery or from the experiences themselves.

**Answer:** <p>I'm really sorry to hear how unsettling these memories have become for you, and I want to acknowledge how brave it is to share something that feels so private and confusing. What you're describing seems to contain elements that distinguish it from typical childhood sexual exploration, and it makes complete sense that you're struggling to categorize these experiences. When sexual behaviors involve children who are exploring bodies and boundaries, it can often leave people wondering if what they experienced "counts" as abuse or if it was simply childhood curiosity. The truth is that even when children are close in age, if one child feels coerced, uncomfortable, or pressured into sexual acts, it can be considered a form of <a href="https://www.ourwave.org/en/post/child-on-child-sexual-abuse-cocsa-frequently-asked-questions-and-considerations?id=child-on-child-sexual-abuse-cocsa-frequently-asked-questions-and-considerations">child-on-child sexual abuse</a>.</p>
<p>In healthy childhood exploration, children are typically close in age and developmental stage, activities are mutual and spontaneous rather than directed by one child, and either child can stop without pressure. What you're describing sounds a little different. Your cousin initiated and directed sexual activities, you felt uncomfortable and hesitant, and there seems to be an element of coercion even if it wasn't violent or explicitly threatening. The age difference and the repeated nature of what happened point to a dynamic where you were in a more vulnerable position, even if at times it felt like participation. The fact that he was teaching you about penetration and demonstrating it suggests knowledge and intent beyond what would be developmentally typical for his age, which raises questions about where he may have learned these behaviors. Children simply don't have the developmental ability to consent the way adults can, which means it wasn't only harmless exploration if one child felt hesitant or scared.</p>
<p>The secrecy, fear of getting caught, and sense of dread you remember are strong signals that your boundaries were being crossed, whether you understood it then or not. Your question about whether you became less afraid of the acts themselves and more afraid of being caught is really insightful. This shift doesn't mean the experiences were okay or that they weren't harmful. Many children in these situations adapt to protect themselves emotionally, and fear of discovery can actually be fear of the consequences you'd face for something that wasn't your responsibility. The secrecy being framed as part of the "game" is particularly concerning because it placed the burden of concealment on you when you were too young to understand what was happening or to consent to it. These uncertainties often reflect how children process trauma: they rarely know what is right or wrong in the moment when someone they trust initiates sexual behavior, but they do sense something is off, which can settle in as fear and anxiety.</p>
<p>The confusion you feel is shared by many survivors of child-on-child sexual abuse, especially when you still have a good relationship with the family member involved. It's not unusual to feel unsettled by these memories while also caring for your cousin. The fact that these memories have haunted you, that you spent years hoping you'd dreamed them up, and that they're now interfering with your daily life all suggest these experiences affected you significantly. Trauma isn't determined by whether something fits a specific definition but by how it impacted you. Your body and mind are telling you that something about these experiences wasn't right, and that deserves acknowledgment and care.</p>
<p>It's also completely understandable that you're uncertain about your feelings, especially given your history of being groomed. These factors can make it harder to trust your own perceptions of what happened. You don't need to have perfect clarity about every aspect of these experiences to deserve support. Whether your distress stems from the acts themselves, the fear surrounding them, or both, your feelings are valid and the impact is real.</p>
<p>It sounds like you've done what many of us do by pushing these experiences away for a long time, hoping they might fade. Now that the memories are coming back, it's understandable to feel haunted and worried about how others might react if they knew. You don't have to confront your cousin or disclose to family members who might respond poorly. Healing can happen through working with a trauma-informed therapist who specializes in childhood sexual experiences and child-on-child sexual abuse, even without confrontation or family involvement.</p>
<p>If you're comfortable doing so, talking with a professional could offer a private space to untangle what happened, understand how it affects you now, and guide you through the complexity of still caring about your cousin. A supportive professional can also help you develop ways to cope with the intrusive thoughts and decide if and how you want to communicate about it moving forward. Many survivors find that talking with someone who understands these complex dynamics helps them process their feelings and reduce the power these memories have over them.&nbsp;</p>
<p>What happened to you still matters, even years later, and those fearful or uncomfortable feelings weren't simply a misunderstanding&mdash;you were responding the best way you knew how. You didn't do anything wrong by being a child caught in a situation you weren't equipped to handle. You deserve kindness as you continue to sort through this. Healing often involves hearing that what you endured was real and that your feelings and memories are important, no matter how much time has passed. You deserve to find peace, clarity, and a sense of closure that honors both your fears and your experiences.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-around-7-another-child-who-was-about-a-year-older-introduced-me-to-sexual-activity-and-asked-if-i-wanted-to-participate-i-was-shy-and-a-people-pleaser-so-i-said-yes-without-understanding-wh)

**Question:** When I was around 7, another child who was about a year older introduced me to sexual activity and asked if I wanted to participate. I was shy and a people pleaser, so I said yes without understanding what it meant. We engaged in sexual behaviors together. I only recently realized this experience might have been harmful. Does this count as COCSA?

**Answer:** <p>I'm so glad you reached out, and I want you to know that it's completely understandable to feel uncertain about labeling this experience, especially given how young you both were at the time. When children as young as seven or eight engage in sexual behavior, it can be confusing to make sense of later in life, particularly when thinking about concepts like consent and understanding at that age. Children in that age range generally do not have the emotional or developmental capacity to fully understand or consent to sexual activities. Without realizing it, one child might be imitating behaviors they have encountered or trying to make sense of things they've seen, heard, or learned elsewhere, even though neither child fully grasps what they are doing.</p>
<p>The term<a href="https://www.ourwave.org/en/post/child-on-child-sexual-abuse-cocsa-frequently-asked-questions-and-considerations?id=child-on-child-sexual-abuse-cocsa-frequently-asked-questions-and-considerations"> "COCSA" (child-on-child sexual abuse) </a>sometimes applies when there is any type of sexual interaction between children that involves a power imbalance, coercion, confusion, or behavior that goes beyond typical childhood curiosity. What you're describing (e.g. not understanding what sex entailed and saying yes because you were shy and wanted to please) suggests you weren't in a position to give informed consent. However, it's also true that the other child was very young too and may not have understood the significance of what was happening either. Both of these things can be true at once.</p>
<p>What matters most is how this early experience has affected you. It's really common for people to spend years not thinking much about childhood sexual experiences, only to have them resurface later with new awareness or feelings. Our minds often wait until we feel safer or stronger to understand and process confusing childhood events. The fact that you're now recognizing this experience as potentially significant is part of understanding yourself better, not something you did wrong by not realizing sooner. If you're feeling unsure or distressed when remembering it, that matters. It signals that this experience has had an impact on you.</p>
<p>At the same time, not everyone who had childhood sexual experiences with other children feels harmed by them, and that's okay too. What you call this experience and how you feel about it is entirely up to you. Your emotions about this are valid, whatever they are. Some people feel confused because the other person was also young, or because they technically said yes, or because it didn't feel violent or scary at the time. Your response to this experience matters more than any specific label. You get to decide what this means to you.</p>
<p>If this memory continues to trouble you or if you have lingering questions, please consider reaching out for support. Talking it through with a trusted friend, counselor, or confidential resource that understands childhood sexual dynamics might help bring clarity and self-compassion. Many people find it helpful to work with a therapist who specializes in childhood experiences and can provide a nonjudgmental space to explore your feelings. &nbsp;You are not to blame for what happened, and it doesn't make you "bad" or responsible for someone else's actions at that age. You didn't do anything wrong, and you deserve care and healing as you make sense of this experience and its impact on your life. You deserve patience and kindness as you gain insight into this experience, and you don't have to figure it out alone.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/if-someone-agreed-to-have-sex-and-wanted-it-but-then-stayed-silent-and-still-during-the-encounter-because-they-were-afraid-of-being-caught-by-others-not-afraid-of-the-person-or-act-itself-does-that-af)

**Question:** If someone agreed to have sex and wanted it, but then stayed silent and still during the encounter because they were afraid of being caught by others (not afraid of the person or act itself), does that affect consent? The other person initiated actions and went further than expected, but there was no verbal response or physical engagement throughout. Why does this feel confusing, and how do you know if consent was present when someone chose to stay silent and still, even though the silence wasn't about fear of their partner?

**Answer:** <p>It sounds like this situation involved someone who genuinely wanted intimacy, yet felt paralyzed by the fear of being caught. Holding both truths at once can naturally create confusion. What you're describing captures something really important about consent that often gets oversimplified: consent isn't just about what someone wants in theory or what was agreed to before things started, and it's not just about saying "yes" or "no." It also involves feeling safe enough to communicate, set boundaries, and decide what feels right as the experience unfolds in real time. The confusion around this situation makes complete sense because there's a gap between the initial desire and what actually happened during the encounter itself.</p>
<p>Even though the fear wasn't directed at the other person or the act, the worry about punishment by others may still have limited how freely and comfortably someone could express themselves during the moment. When fear enters the picture, even if that fear is about external consequences rather than the person involved, it can fundamentally change the capacity to engage freely.</p>
<p>Fear has a way of freezing the ability to communicate, even when silence isn't consciously chosen as much as it becomes the only response that feels possible. Being still and quiet might have been the only way to cope with the fear of outside consequences. Sometimes people freeze not because they dislike the act or the other person, but because they're scared of what might happen if they speak or move. In that state, it's easy to go along with actions that might leave someone feeling neutral or uncertain afterward, especially if they think they "should" be fine with them. While part of someone may have thought they were "choosing" that response, it's worth considering whether fear was actually driving the body's response in ways that felt outside of conscious control. This is actually a really common neurobiological reaction to fear, even when the fear isn't about immediate physical danger.</p>
<p>The fact that someone wanted to be there initially doesn't mean that everything that happened afterward was okay or that the ability to consent was maintained throughout. Consent needs to be ongoing, and it requires the freedom to communicate, to say yes to some things and no to others, to slow down or stop. Real consent often feels like a sense of safety and presence, where someone knows they could stop or change course if they wanted, without fearing punishment or judgment. If silence became the default because of outside pressure, it might have prevented someone from fully expressing likes, dislikes, or any moment they wished to pause. Someone might still have been okay with sex overall, yet perhaps didn't have enough freedom in that moment to shape how it played out. When someone is silent and completely still throughout an entire sexual encounter, that's not typically a sign of enthusiastic participation. It's often a sign that something has shifted and the person is no longer able to engage freely.</p>
<p>What makes this particularly confusing is that one person went further than expected with actions that weren't explicitly agreed to. When someone continues sexual activity while their partner has gone completely silent and still, without checking in or getting clear signs of engagement and enthusiasm, they're not treating silence as consent&mdash;they're assuming it. Real consent involves active participation, communication, and mutual engagement. It's not just the absence of a "no."</p>
<p>It's also normal to feel confused if there was nothing overtly terrifying about what happened, yet afterward certain details still bother someone. Fear of being discovered can override the ability to communicate preferences, making the experience feel less clear-cut. Over time, these mixed emotions may linger precisely because they weren't able to be voiced in the moment. The confusion isn't a sign of overreacting or misunderstanding what happened. It's actually the mind and body trying to process an experience where things didn't align&mdash;where initial desire met a situation that became something different, where fear created silence, and where actions happened without full participation. That feeling of things "just happening" while being there but not really present is significant. It suggests that somewhere along the way, there was a loss of agency in the encounter.</p>
<p>Being unsure whether something was simply awkward or if it crossed a line doesn't mean anyone is imagining things. It means trying to understand whether everyone truly felt safe and at ease throughout, or if fear silenced important ways of communicating. Many people feel conflicted when they wanted sex on some level but were unable to communicate comfortably about what they did or didn't want. No one has to label this experience in any particular way right now. What matters is honoring the confusion and discomfort, because those feelings are telling something important. It's okay to recognize that even though someone wanted to be there initially, what unfolded wasn't what they wanted or expected. It's okay to feel conflicted about an experience where there was some desire but also an inability to communicate or participate fully. These complex feelings don't make the experience any less valid or the discomfort any less real.</p>
<p>Having a debrief conversation between the people involved can be really important to clarify boundaries and consent, especially when an encounter leaves someone with lingering questions or discomfort. Talking about what happened, what felt confusing, and what each person experienced can help bring clarity to the situation and ensure that future interactions involve clearer communication. If that conversation feels too difficult or unsafe, or if someone is still processing what happened, it could be helpful to confide in a trusted person, perhaps a friend, a professional, or a support line, and talk through the parts that feel confusing. Everyone deserves a space where they can explore the role that fear played, how it shaped what happened, and how they feel about it now. The fact that these questions are arising shows that concerns about consent and comfort matter, and everyone deserves clarity and understanding for their own sense of peace.</p>
<p>Many people describe exactly this kind of confusion when fear, silence, and unexpected actions intersect with some initial desire or agreement. Everyone deserves support in making sense of these feelings, and deserves to have their experience acknowledged for what it was&mdash;complicated, confusing, and worthy of care. Thank you for asking about this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-15-my-friend-who-was-11-insisted-we-compare-ourselves-physically-and-we-briefly-touched-each-other-for-a-few-seconds-before-stopping-it-wasnt-sexual-and-we-thought-it-was-funny-at-the-time)

**Question:** When I was 15, my friend who was 11 insisted we compare ourselves physically and we briefly touched each other for a few seconds before stopping. It wasn't sexual and we thought it was funny at the time. Am I a bad person because I was older? I'm feeling guilty and ashamed about this.

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting this space with something that's been weighing on you. It sounds like you've been carrying a lot of worry and shame about this memory for quite some time.&nbsp;</p>
<p>What you're describing sounds like an incident of sexual curiosity between young people who were both minors at the time. During adolescence, curiosity about bodies and exploring the differences between friends can happen in many different ways. Sexual curiosity and exploration are normal parts of adolescent development, and many young people engage in some form of mutual exploration with peers as they're trying to understand their changing bodies. Being a few years older doesn't automatically mean you intended something harmful or sexual, especially if you were both simply being curious without any sense of coercion or malice. Children often test boundaries or engage in playful exploration. This is a recognized aspect of how young people learn about themselves, even if it feels awkward or troubling to recall later in life.</p>
<p>The fact that this was brief, mutually initiated by your friend, not coercive, and stopped quickly are all important contextual details. The four-year age difference does create some developmental disparity, but you were also a young teenager yourself&mdash;still developing your own understanding of boundaries, sexuality, and appropriate behavior. It's significant that you remember this as something that felt funny rather than sexual in the moment, which suggests it existed in that confusing space of childhood curiosity rather than predatory intent. Harmful sexual behavior typically involves planning, secrecy, coercion, or an intent to use someone else for sexual gratification. From what you've shared, this doesn't appear to fit that pattern.</p>
<p>The self-blame and discomfort you're feeling now are common reactions when looking back with the empathy and understanding you've developed as an adult. Sometimes our adult understanding of consent and appropriate boundaries can cause us to look back at childhood or adolescent experiences with concern or shame, especially as we become more aware of the importance of age-appropriate behavior and consent. Feeling guilty doesn't mean you're a bad person; instead, it highlights the capacity you have for reflection and moral awareness. The fact that you're questioning yourself now, years later, actually speaks to your moral compass and your care about having acted appropriately. This reflection is part of growth and developing maturity.</p>
<p>You are not alone in questioning past interactions through an adult lens, and it's okay to seek reassurance that this one episode from your youth doesn't define who you are today. If this experience continues to trouble you deeply or if it's affecting your day-to-day life, speaking with someone you trust or a counselor who understands the complexity of childhood and teenage experiences could provide you with additional perspective and tools for processing these feelings. You deserve compassion for yourself as the teenager you were, who was also navigating development and learning about boundaries. Being able to reflect critically on our past while also holding appropriate context for our developmental stage is part of healing and growth.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/im-confused-about-terminology-some-people-tell-me-what-happened-was-rape-and-others-say-its-sexual-assault-when-i-was-a-child-someone-pushed-me-to-the-ground-forcibly-removed-my-clothing-held-me-down)

**Question:** I'm confused about terminology. Some people tell me what happened was rape, and others say it's sexual assault. When I was a child, someone pushed me to the ground, forcibly removed my clothing, held me down, and performed oral sex on me without my consent. Can someone help me understand these terms?

**Answer:** <p>I'm so sorry this happened to you. What you experienced was a serious violation, and I want you to know that the confusion you're feeling about terminology is completely understandable.&nbsp;</p>
<p>The truth is that different places use these terms differently. In some states or countries, "rape" has a legal definition that only includes penetration with a penis, while other places define it more broadly to include any forced sexual penetration--including with tongues, fingers, or objects. "Sexual assault" is typically used as an umbrella term that covers a wide range of unwanted sexual contact, from groping to rape. What you described, being forcibly held down while someone performed oral sex on you as a child, would be considered a serious sexual assault everywhere, and in many jurisdictions, it would legally qualify as rape. When he pushed you down, removed your clothing, and used his mouth and hands on you while holding you there, he crossed a line in a deeply harmful way that was forced on you against your will.</p>
<p>The fact that this happened when you were a child adds additional layers of concern and complexity. Children cannot consent to sexual contact under any circumstances. Your age alone meant you were inherently unable to agree to what happened, and the responsibility for this violation rests entirely with the person who harmed you. Child sexual abuse often carries profound impacts because it happens during crucial developmental years when you're forming your understanding of safety, trust, and boundaries. The power imbalance inherent in being a child made you especially vulnerable, and what happened to you was not only a violation but also a betrayal of the protection and care you deserved.</p>
<p>The word you choose to describe your experience is entirely up to you. Some survivors feel the word "rape" captures the gravity of what happened to them. Others prefer "sexual assault" or "abuse." Some don't use any specific label at all and simply describe what happened. All of these choices are valid, and you get to decide what feels most accurate and comfortable for you. The terminology doesn't change the reality of what you endured or the impact it's had on you.</p>
<p>What matters most is that you experienced something deeply traumatic as a child. Someone violated your body, your boundaries, and your safety in a way that no child should ever experience. It's painful and frightening to think about someone doing this to you, especially when you were young. Your reactions (whether they've been fear, anger, confusion, or even numbness) are valid responses to something that should never have happened in the first place. It's also common to question what happened or to wonder if the distress you feel means you're "overreacting," but the fact that you're still feeling affected tells you it was serious and that it mattered. You did not deserve this, and it wasn't your fault.</p>
<p>Your body and your story belong to you, and you get to name your experience in whatever way feels right. If you're seeking support or resources, most crisis centers and therapists understand that survivors use different terms, and they'll respect however you choose to talk about what happened to you. Speaking with someone who truly understands how trauma works can bring a sense of relief and help you process these feelings in a way that feels safe. You deserve to be heard, believed, and supported every step of the way. You're not alone in this, and there are people who genuinely want to help you find a path toward healing.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/im-a-survivor-who-is-now-studying-social-work-im-interested-in-connecting-with-other-survivors-to-learn-about-their-experiences-and-gather-demographic-information-for-my-studies-is-it-possible-to-conn)

**Question:** I'm a survivor who is now studying social work. I'm interested in connecting with other survivors to learn about their experiences and gather demographic information for my studies. Is it possible to connect with other survivors through this platform?

**Answer:** <p>It's wonderful that you're pursuing social work and want to use your own experiences to inform your understanding and support of others. That kind of personal insight can be incredibly valuable in this field. It's also completely understandable that you'd want to connect with other survivors, especially as someone who has walked a similar path. Many survivors do seek out community for shared understanding and to discover they're not alone.</p>
<p>This particular platform isn't designed as a space for survivors to connect directly with one another in an informal way, but we do sometimes partner with people who want to support survivors through research and other collaborative work. We have strict collaboration guidelines and privacy protections in place to ensure that survivors' wellbeing and autonomy are always centered. Survivors who share on our platform can opt into and out of research opportunities, which means they maintain control over whether and how their experiences are included in any studies or projects. This approach respects the fact that sharing one's story is a personal choice that can change over time, and survivors deserve to have agency over their own narratives and information.</p>
<p>If you're interested in exploring a potential research partnership or learning more about how we work with people in the social work and academic fields, you can reach out to hello@ourwave.org with questions. They can provide you with information about our collaboration process, ethical guidelines, and what opportunities might be available that align with your studies and goals. If you're hoping to gather information for research or academic purposes through other avenues, there may be ethical requirements, like obtaining approval from your institution's review board, to ensure the safety and anonymity of any survivors who choose to participate. Survivors often need to feel certain that their voices will be respected and that they won't be re-traumatized by sharing their stories.</p>
<p>As a fellow survivor, it's natural to bring empathy to these conversations, but it can also be emotionally taxing to absorb so many difficult histories. It's wise to ensure you have supportive resources in place for yourself too as you pursue this work. Thank you so much for reaching out and asking this!</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/how-do-i-start-telling-my-story-766)

**Question:** How do I start telling my story?

**Answer:** <p>Starting to tell your story is such a significant step. Thank you for asking us about this! Sharing your story can begin in quiet, gentle ways. You might find that writing down what happened feels more manageable at first, allowing you to release thoughts onto paper without worrying about anyone else's reactions. This could be in a journal, notes on your phone, or even just fragments of thoughts as they come to you. Some people find that creative expression through art, poetry, or music helps them access and communicate their experiences in ways that feel safer than direct words.</p>
<p>When you do feel ready to share with another person, you get to choose who that person is and how much you tell them. Some people begin by confiding in a single trusted friend or family member, choosing someone who's known to be compassionate and patient. Others find solace in connecting with a local crisis center or an advocacy group that understands the impact of trauma and provides a sense of safety and understanding. You might start with just the basics or share as much detail as feels right in that moment. It's completely okay to say "I'm not ready to talk about all of it yet" or "I need to take breaks while I'm telling you this."</p>
<p>It helps to remind yourself that you can decide what to share and when, and there's no fixed timeline or script you have to follow. Simply voicing a few feelings at a time can be enough. Some survivors find it easier to start their story from where they are now rather than from the beginning of what happened. You might talk about how you're feeling today, what support you need, or what prompted you to want to share. The chronological details can come later if and when you're ready.</p>
<p>If you feel nervous or overwhelmed, it can help to pause, take a few slow breaths, and remind yourself that your story belongs to you and should only be shared in ways that feel right to you. Starting small doesn't diminish the importance of what you've gone through. It just allows you to move at a pace your emotions can handle. Remember that telling your story isn't a one-time event. You might share different parts with different people at different times, and each time you tell it, you may remember new things or understand your experience differently. Over time, you might find that each time you speak or write about your experience, it feels a little less daunting, and you regain some of the control that was taken from you. You're in control of your own narrative, you deserve to be heard, and only you get to decide how that happens.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-younger-my-grandmother-engaged-in-inappropriate-sexual-behavior-with-me-over-many-years-i-didnt-receive-any-sex-education-growing-up-so-i-didnt-have-the-language-or-education-to-understand)

**Question:** When I was younger, my grandmother engaged in inappropriate sexual behavior with me over many years. I didn't receive any sex education growing up, so I didn't have the language or education to understand what was happening. At the time, I resisted—I would hit her and pull away—because I hated it, but it still felt normal since it had been happening for so long. Recently, I visited her and found myself dressing up and seeking her attention, wanting to prove to myself that the abuse really happened. She responded the way she used to—touching me inappropriately and making comments about my appearance—which confirmed my experiences, but now I feel terrible. I was so stressed during the trip that I was taking a lot of Benadryl to cope. I feel guilty, dirty, and confused about why I sought that validation. Why do I feel so awful after getting the answers I thought I needed?

**Answer:** <p>I'm so glad you reached out, and I want you to know that everything you're feeling makes complete sense given what you've been through. What you are experiencing now are common responses to childhood trauma&mdash;especially when that abuse came from someone who was supposed to protect and care for you.</p>
<p>First, I need you to hear this: you were a child. The resistance you showed (e.g. the hitting, the pulling away) those weren't cruelty. Those were a child's way of fighting back and trying to reclaim control when someone was violating your boundaries. You have absolutely nothing to feel guilty about. Your grandmother was the adult with all the power and responsibility, and she violated that trust profoundly. Your reactions, both then and now, are not your fault.</p>
<p>What you did during your recent visit (seeking validation that the abuse really happened) is something many survivors do, and it doesn't mean anything is wrong with you. When trauma happens over years and becomes normalized, especially without the language to name it, many people need concrete proof that their memories and experiences are real. The pull to make yourself "pretty" for her, despite the harm you've lived through, doesn't make you dirty or rotten. It shows how deeply we can want closure or understanding of something we should never have had to prove in the first place. Your mind was seeking confirmation, trying to make sense of something that felt confusing for so long. This doesn't make you responsible for her behavior. She made the choice to respond inappropriately, just as she made those choices when you were a child.</p>
<p>The awful feelings you're experiencing now are trauma responses. When someone who was supposed to protect you causes harm over so many years, it can create a powerful push-and-pull of both yearning and revulsion. These conflicting feelings, though they may feel exhausting, are very common for survivors. You're grieving the childhood that was stolen from you, and you're processing the reality that someone who should have loved you safely instead harmed you. Seeing glimpses of your younger self in the mirror is heartbreaking because you're connecting with that child who deserved so much better. The self-blame and shame you're experiencing, even the tears when you see your younger self, are your body and heart releasing all that you've had to bury for so long. It's not proof that you're tainted or broken. It's proof that what happened matters, and that you're trying to heal.</p>
<p>Using substances like Benadryl to cope with overwhelming feelings is understandable. It signals how desperate you are to quiet the storm in your mind. But you deserve healthier support and understanding, not just to numb the hurt. I strongly encourage you to connect with a trauma-informed therapist who specializes in childhood sexual abuse. Right now, confiding in someone you trust (e.g. a close friend, a counselor, or a support line) can help soften the sense of isolation and provide the steadiness your mind and body are craving. A therapist can walk you through strategies to manage flashbacks, shame, and those conflicting emotions about your grandmother. They can be a safe place to unpack how you feel without judging you, and help you find healthier ways to ground yourself when memories or guilt threaten to take over. Many survivors find that therapy, particularly approaches like EMDR or trauma-focused cognitive behavioral therapy, can help them work through these complex feelings.</p>
<p>You didn't deserve to be hurt when you were small, and you deserve kindness and patience from yourself now that you're confronting it. You are not dirty or rotten. You are a survivor making sense of something deeply painful, and everything you're feeling is a normal response to abnormal circumstances. Be as gentle with yourself as you would be with another person going through this kind of pain, because you deserve the compassion you were once denied. Give yourself permission to rest, to cry, to feel angry&mdash;in each of those moments, you are trying to shed the weight of what was never yours to carry in the first place. And with the right kind of support and a bit of time, that weight can begin to lift. Healing is possible, and you don't have to carry this alone.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/ive-been-through-a-lot-of-trauma-and-am-thinking-about-starting-a-blog-to-share-my-experiences-and-survival-do-you-have-any-advice-for-someone-wanting-to-start-writing-publicly-about-trauma-and-abuse)

**Question:** I've been through a lot of trauma and am thinking about starting a blog to share my experiences and survival. Do you have any advice for someone wanting to start writing publicly about trauma and abuse? Also, how did Our Wave come to be?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out to us! It sounds like you have a wealth of experiences and insights to share, and a desire to help others by opening up about them. Many people healing from trauma find it both empowering and comforting to write publicly, and readers often feel less alone when they see their own experiences reflected in someone else's words. Your unique story, and the resilience you've built, can truly touch others who are seeking hope, community, and practical understanding.</p>
<p>In terms of advice, I've seen that survivors who choose to blog often find it helpful to decide, before writing, on personal boundaries around what they're comfortable sharing. These boundaries can bring a sense of control over the process, which can make it feel less overwhelming if past memories stir up powerful emotions. Some people begin by writing drafts purely for themselves, giving time to see how it feels before publishing. Others choose to share in smaller online communities first. Whatever pace feels right to you will honor both your story and your well-being while letting you connect with an audience who appreciates you.</p>
<p>It's also worth thinking about your support system as you embark on this journey. Writing about trauma can bring up difficult emotions and memories, so having people you trust to talk to (whether friends, family, or a therapist) can be incredibly valuable. If you're not currently working with a mental health professional, you might consider connecting with one, especially as you begin this process. They can provide support as you navigate any feelings that come up through your writing.</p>
<p>When you do start blogging, remember that you're in control of your narrative. You don't owe anyone every detail of your story, and you can always adjust what you share as you go. Some bloggers choose to write anonymously or use pseudonyms to maintain privacy, while others use their real names. Think about what feels right for your situation, considering both your comfort level and any safety concerns. Be patient and compassionate with yourself throughout this process. There may be days when writing feels healing and days when it feels heavy. Both responses are completely normal.</p>
<p>As for Our Wave's story, it came to be out of recognition that many survivors, just like you, have important stories that need a supportive outlet. The notion of a digital community where survivors can share experiences and find validation (and also see that they're not alone) emerged several years ago among people who understood firsthand that no one should have to heal in isolation.</p>
<p>What followed was the formation of the core Our Wave team and years of conversation, listening, and learning. Through these discussions, we realized that the resources available to survivors are limited and often only accessible in person. Drawing on our backgrounds in technology, marketing, research, and data science, we recognized an opportunity to create a safe online space that could serve the needs of all survivors&mdash;a space free from guilt, blame, and judgment, providing research-driven and user-informed tools to help survivors of all identities find healing. In 2019, Our Wave was officially registered as a 501(c)(3) non-profit organization, and we launched our online healing platform. This spirit of safely sharing is very much aligned with what you're envisioning in your own blog.</p>
<p>I'm really glad you've decided to take this step, and I wish you much encouragement as you bring your ideas to life. You deserve to be heard, and others will benefit from reading not only of the pain you've endured, but of the resilience that carried you through. It can be a deep source of healing to acknowledge your own journey in a way that helps others recognize they, too, are more powerful than the traumas they have faced. Take good care of yourself while you take these steps forward, and remember that your story has immense value&mdash;both for you and for those who find comfort and validation in your words.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/what-is-the-difference-between-sexual-abuse-and-sexual-assault-is-sexual-abuse-a-broader-term-that-encompasses-sexual-assault-if-someone-said-what-happened-wasnt-sexual-abuse-does-that-mean-it-also-wa)

**Question:** What is the difference between sexual abuse and sexual assault? Is sexual abuse a broader term that encompasses sexual assault? If someone said what happened wasn't sexual abuse, does that mean it also wasn't sexual assault?

**Answer:** <p>I'm so sorry you've been grappling with these definitions and how they might apply to your own experience. These terms can feel confusing because they're often used in different ways depending on the context&mdash;legal definitions, clinical settings, and everyday conversation don't always align.</p>
<p>In many discussions, sexual abuse is used as a broader term that captures a wide range of unwanted or harmful sexual behaviors, including sexual violence, exploitation, and sometimes other forms of violation like showing explicit materials to a child. Sexual assault often refers to the part of that spectrum involving direct physical acts like forced touching, penetration, or any sexual contact that occurs without someone's consent or when consent cannot be given. Both terms point to a violation of trust and bodily autonomy, and legal definitions can differ across jurisdictions.</p>
<p>If someone told you that what happened wasn't sexual abuse, that doesn't automatically mean it wasn't sexual assault, or vice versa. Often, the confusion comes from how people label or interpret different behaviors. People use these terms differently based on their own understanding, comfort level, or even their own denial or minimization of what happened. Someone might reject one label while the experience could still fit under another term. The language someone else uses to describe your experience doesn't change what actually happened or how it affected you.</p>
<p>What truly matters is whether there was unwanted sexual contact, exploitation, or a power imbalance that made free, informed consent impossible. If any of those elements were present, then it can be described as a form of sexual violence, even if certain people or institutions refer to it by one term rather than the other. What matters most isn't getting the terminology perfect&mdash;it's recognizing that you experienced something that wasn't okay and that impacted you.</p>
<p>You get to choose the words that feel right to you when describing your own experience. Some people prefer one term over another because it resonates more with their understanding of what happened. Others don't use any specific label at all. All of those choices are valid. If you're questioning the words that were used, remember that your feelings and the impact of what happened are what truly count.</p>
<p>Whether someone calls it sexual assault, sexual abuse, or both, any violation of your boundaries and bodily safety is valid and significant. It may help to speak with a professional who understands these nuances&mdash;they can explain how definitions are used where you live so you can get clarity about your situation. The most important thing is recognizing that you deserve respect, safety, and support, no matter how others label the experience. You're not alone, and there are people who believe you and want to help. Thank you for trusting us with this question.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/im-a-man-in-my-mid-30s-trying-to-cope-with-the-impact-of-childhood-sexual-abuse-by-multiple-perpetrators-over-several-years-the-abuse-began-when-i-was-very-young-and-involved-different-people-in-vario)

**Question:** I'm a man in my mid-30s trying to cope with the impact of childhood sexual abuse by multiple perpetrators over several years. The abuse began when I was very young and involved different people in various contexts—including family members, someone in a position of authority, and others in my life. This has left me with severe anxiety, compulsive sexual behaviors, and a profound struggle with my identity. I'm reaching out to see if anyone has found a path to healing from complex and repeated trauma.

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting this space with your story. What you experienced was profound betrayal by multiple people who should have protected you, and the courage it takes to reach out and name these experiences is significant. You deserved safety, protection, and care throughout your childhood, and none of what happened to you was your fault.</p>
<p>What you're describing, abuse by multiple perpetrators across different environments and relationships, is often called complex trauma or polyvictimization. This type of trauma can be particularly devastating because it happened repeatedly during critical developmental periods when your brain and nervous system were still forming. When children experience sexual abuse at very young ages, especially by multiple people, it can profoundly impact their sense of safety, trust, boundaries, and identity. The symptoms you're experiencing are common responses to this kind of pervasive trauma. These aren't signs of weakness or damage; they're evidence that your mind and body have been working incredibly hard to survive and cope with unbearable circumstances.</p>
<p>The reactions your body showed during and after the abuse were your nervous system's way of expressing what was too overwhelming to process at the time. Children's bodies and minds respond to trauma in ways that help them survive, even when those responses later become sources of pain or shame. Your reactions were adaptive responses to impossible situations, not character flaws. It's completely natural to feel overwhelmed, or even disconnected from who you are. The fact that you survived such repeated violations reflects a deep resilience within you, even when it may feel like those experiences have taken over your life.</p>
<p>Healing from complex trauma is absolutely possible, though it's often not a linear journey. Many survivors find that healing involves working with trauma-informed therapists who specialize in childhood sexual abuse and complex PTSD. There are mental health professionals who specialize in working specifically with people who have faced childhood abuse and betrayal by loved ones.
