Approaches like <a href="https://www.apa.org/ptsd-guideline/treatments/eye-movement-reprocessing">EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing)</a>, <a href="https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/therapy-types/trauma-focused-cognitive-behavior-therapy?msockid=1103576693d9679310b645d892bb66b2">Trauma-Focused Cognitive Behavioral Therapy</a>, <a href="https://positivepsychology.com/somatic-experiencing/">Somatic Experiencing</a>, and <a href="https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/therapy-types/internal-family-systems-therapy?msockid=1103576693d9679310b645d892bb66b2">Internal Family Systems</a> have helped many people process traumatic memories and rebuild a sense of safety in their bodies and lives. For some, this might include approaches that concentrate on noticing and gradually releasing the physical impact of those early violations. For others, it might look like exploring the emotional and relational wounding that happened when trusted figures betrayed you. Whatever path you choose, you will not be alone if you decide to seek support from a counselor experienced in childhood sexual trauma.</p>
<p>Sharing your story in a confidential setting, whether with a therapist or in a supportive group specifically for male survivors of childhood sexual abuse, can help alleviate the intense shame and fear that abusers often imprint on survivors. There is something potent about being heard and believed&mdash;especially if you rarely felt that validation in childhood. Some survivors also find support groups to be powerful spaces for connection and validation. Part of healing often involves learning to recognize how trauma has shaped your nervous system and discovering tools to help regulate anxiety and compulsive patterns. Coping strategies you adopted as a child, like certain compulsive behaviors or dissociation, were once your mind and body's way of surviving. With the right help, many survivors learn new ways to navigate daily life without relying on the very patterns that now hold them back. This might include working with the body through movement, breathwork, mindfulness, or other somatic practices that help you reconnect with yourself in gentle ways. Many survivors also benefit from exploring their identity and sense of self separate from the trauma&mdash;reclaiming parts of themselves that existed before the abuse or discovering new aspects they want to cultivate now. This process can be messy and you may feel uncertain at times, but it often involves rediscovering the parts of yourself you had to bury or hide just to get through each day.</p>
<p>You asked if others have found paths to healing, and the answer is yes. It's understandable to wonder if healing is truly possible when trauma has included so many different people and settings, each betrayal reinforcing the last. Yet many who've lived through repeated abuses do find that with dedicated support, the sense of unrelenting pain can lessen and something closer to wholeness can emerge. Recovery doesn't necessarily mean the trauma never happened or that you'll never be affected by it, but many survivors do find ways to reduce the intensity of their symptoms, develop healthier relationships with themselves and others, reclaim their sexuality in ways that feel authentic and safe, and build lives that feel meaningful and connected. This doesn't happen overnight, and it often involves setbacks along the way, but many survivors speak of eventually feeling more stable, regaining a sense of identity, and recovering the capacity for genuine connection and trust. Healing happens in relationship&mdash;with supportive others and with yourself&mdash;and it happens at your own pace.</p>
<p>In your mid-thirties, taking this step toward voicing what happened and asking if healing is possible is a meaningful move in itself. If you can, consider gradually connecting with a professional who is comfortable addressing childhood sexual abuse, betrayal by loved ones, and the cycling anxiety or compulsive habits that stem from trauma. Look for someone who understands the long shadows that such early abuse can cast on adulthood&mdash;and who believes that people can change and grow, no matter how deep the pain. You deserve comprehensive, compassionate support as you navigate this healing journey, and reaching out like this is already a part of that healing process. What you've endured does not define your core worth, even if it sometimes feels all-encompassing. You have survived so much, and in that survival rests a strength that can carry you forward. You are not alone, and you deserve that same chance to heal at your own pace. Thank you for reaching out to us.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-young-6-years-old-another-child-my-age-asked-me-to-get-inside-a-playhouse-and-then-kissed-me-and-licked-my-mouth-repeatedly-during-playtime-i-remember-feeling-uncomfortable-from-the-moment)

**Question:** When I was young (6 years old), another child my age asked me to get inside a playhouse and then kissed me and licked my mouth repeatedly during playtime. I remember feeling uncomfortable from the moment he asked me to "be mommy" and later feeling horrified. I've been stressed about this experience for a long time. Was this child-on-child sexual abuse?

**Answer:** <p>I'm so sorry this happened to you. What you're describing sounds like it was deeply uncomfortable and violating, and I can understand why you've carried stress about this experience for so long. <a href="https://www.ourwave.org/en/post/child-on-child-sexual-abuse-cocsa-frequently-asked-questions-and-considerations?id=child-on-child-sexual-abuse-cocsa-frequently-asked-questions-and-considerations">Child-on-child sexual abuse (COCSA)</a> generally refers to sexual interactions between children where there is a lack of consent, an imbalance of power, or where one child is coerced or manipulated into sexual activity. It can also include situations where one child's boundaries are violated, even when both children are the same age. Only you can determine what language feels right for your own experience and whether this term resonates with what happened to you.</p>
<p>What you're sharing tells me that you felt uncomfortable from the very beginning and that those feelings intensified into horror. Those responses matter deeply. Even at six years old, our bodies and minds can recognize when something doesn't feel right or safe, and your discomfort was a valid signal that your boundaries were being crossed. At the same time, it's important to understand that six-year-olds are still in the very early stages of learning about boundaries, consent, and social cues. Young children are developing their ability to recognize and respond to other people's body language and verbal signals, and they may not yet have the skills to understand when someone is uncomfortable or to stop behavior that's harmful. This developmental reality doesn't change the impact this experience had on you or make what happened appropriate, but it does provide context for understanding that young children are still learning these essential social and emotional skills.</p>
<p>Regardless of how you choose to name this experience, what matters most is that it affected you. The fact that you felt uncomfortable, that you remember feeling horrified, and that you've carried stress about this for years tells us that this experience was significant and harmful to you. You don't need to fit your experience into any particular category for it to matter or for your feelings to be valid. Trauma isn't determined by labels or definitions&mdash;it's determined by how an experience impacts you. Your body remembers feeling unsafe, and the emotional weight you've carried is real and deserving of acknowledgment.</p>
<p>Many people who had similar childhood experiences struggle with questions about whether what happened "counts" or whether they have a right to be affected by it. The truth is that if this experience has stayed with you and caused you distress, then it was significant, and you deserve support in processing it. You have every right to seek help and to honor your own feelings about what happened. Healing often begins when we stop questioning whether our pain is valid and start giving ourselves permission to acknowledge the impact something had on us. You deserve care, validation, and support as you work through this. Thank you for trusting us with this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-am-18-and-my-parents-fight-frequently-and-my-father-often-curses-at-my-mother-recently-he-threatened-to-hit-her-in-front-of-guests-ive-tried-to-get-him-to-apologize-but-he-doesnt-seem-remorseful-how)

**Question:** I am 18 and my parents fight frequently, and my father often curses at my mother. Recently, he threatened to hit her in front of guests. I've tried to get him to apologize, but he doesn't seem remorseful. How do I handle witnessing this ongoing conflict between my parents?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting us with this. It sounds like you are going through an incredibly tense and distressing situation at home. It can feel scary, confusing, and frustrating to see your father threaten violence and show no regret for his words or behavior, especially when it happens right in front of others. It's understandable to want him to apologize and realize how much fear and hurt this is causing, both for your mother and for you. Having constant conflict at home, along with the threat of physical harm, can take a serious emotional toll and make you feel powerless.</p>
<p>You don't deserve to live in an environment where you are constantly worried about your parents fighting or concerned that someone may get hurt. When arguing escalates to cursing or threats, it crosses a line that can affect everyone's sense of safety. Even if no one has been physically harmed yet, it is still important to treat it as a serious warning sign and take care of your own well-being. When one person in a relationship regularly uses verbal aggression like cursing and makes threats of physical harm, especially in front of others, these behaviors often represent a pattern of control and intimidation rather than isolated moments of anger. The fact that your father shows no remorse when you've approached him suggests that he may not see these behaviors as problematic, which can be concerning.&nbsp;</p>
<p>Witnessing ongoing conflict and threats in your home can create lasting impacts, including hypervigilance, anxiety, difficulty trusting relationships, and a sense of responsibility for others' emotions that isn't yours to carry. It's completely normal to feel confused, scared, or frustrated about what you're seeing, and these feelings are valid responses to a difficult situation. If you have a trusted friend, family member, or counselor, talking with them about what's happening may feel like a small relief. Simply sharing the burden of what you're going through and having your perspective met with understanding can help you feel a bit less alone.</p>
<p>If possible, you might consider whether there is another relative or close family friend who can offer guidance, a safe space, or help you get professional support if you feel like you need it. If you're worried about your mother's safety or your own, it is completely reasonable to reach out to resources that deal with domestic conflicts. Many communities have helplines dedicated to people experiencing threats or abusive situations at home, and these services are often confidential and can guide you on local options for help. Organizations like the<a href="https://www.thehotline.org/"> National Domestic Violence Hotline</a> (1-800-799-7233) offer confidential support not just for people directly experiencing abuse, but also for family members and friends who are concerned about someone's safety.</p>
<p>You are 18, and that sometimes means you have a bit more ability to speak privately to a counselor, a legal advocate, or a friend's parent without needing permission. There is no shame in wanting more information about how to handle tense or violent behavior at home. Whether your father ever apologizes or not, what matters most is that you and your mother aren't put at risk. No one should be living in fear of a loved one's outbursts turning into harm.</p>
<p>It might help to remind yourself that the arguments and threats are not your fault, nor your responsibility to fix. You deserve to feel safe, and to have your concerns taken seriously. While your concern for your family is natural and loving, creating change in this situation is not your responsibility. Taking care of your own emotional needs and seeking support for yourself is not selfish. It's necessary and important. It takes courage to admit that something isn't right at home and to think about reaching out for support. If you decide to speak confidentially to a counselor or helpline, it doesn't mean you're betraying your family. It means you value everyone's safety and well-being. No one should have to carry this burden alone, and you don't have to. You are not alone in feeling upset and worried, and there are people who want to help you. Thank you for trusting us with this.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/im-remembering-more-about-a-childhood-event-and-wondering-if-it-could-be-considered-child-on-child-sexual-abuse-cocsa-a-boy-younger-than-me-asked-me-to-play-a-game-in-a-closet-i-felt-uncomfortable-and)

**Question:** I'm remembering more about a childhood event and wondering if it could be considered child-on-child sexual abuse (COCSA). A boy younger than me asked me to play a game in a closet. I felt uncomfortable and didn't want to, but he kept asking until I agreed. He wanted us to rub our stomachs and private parts together. I remember feeling scared and uncomfortable and not wanting to participate, but he kept asking. I can't remember anything else. I'm confused about whether this was just childhood curiosity or if it could be considered COCSA, and it's hard for me to think about.

**Answer:** <p>What you're describing sounds incredibly confusing and difficult to process, and your feelings about this memory are completely valid. It's completely understandable that you feel confused and unsure when you look back on that childhood memory. Many people struggle with the question of whether an interaction with another child was just innocent curiosity or if it crossed a line into something more harmful, and it takes courage to explore these memories, especially when they bring up uncomfortable emotions.</p>
<p><a href="https://www.ourwave.org/en/post/child-on-child-sexual-abuse-cocsa-frequently-asked-questions-and-considerations?id=child-on-child-sexual-abuse-cocsa-frequently-asked-questions-and-considerations">Child-on-child sexual abuse</a> (COCSA) can occur regardless of the age difference between children, and it's distinguished from typical childhood sexual exploration by several key factors. Children do explore, but they also have a responsibility to listen when someone is uncomfortable. Healthy childhood curiosity is usually mutual, brief, age-appropriate, and stops when one child says no or shows discomfort. What you're describing includes elements that seem to move beyond typical exploration -- particularly the persistence after you expressed discomfort, the fear you felt, and the fact that the activities continued despite your clear reluctance. The pressure you felt despite your unwillingness can mean a boundary was crossed.</p>
<p>Even if the other child was younger than you, it's significant that you felt scared and did not really want to participate but were persuaded into it anyway. Child-on-child sexual abuse does not necessarily depend on the ages of the children involved, as children can engage in harmful sexual behaviors for various reasons including their own exposure to inappropriate content or experiences. What matters most is whether someone's boundaries were ignored, if there was any force or pressure, and whether the experience left you feeling uncomfortable or distressed. It sounds like you were uneasy and felt compelled to go along, which may explain why it remains such a troubling memory.</p>
<p>The feelings you experienced are important signals that this interaction crossed boundaries for you. Your body and mind recognized that something didn't feel right, even as a child. The fact that he "kept asking" after you showed resistance suggests he wasn't respecting your comfort level or boundaries. Rubbing private body parts together under repeated pressure is certainly more concerning than the sort of mutual, playful exploration some kids might engage in when they are equally curious and stop the moment anyone feels uneasy.</p>
<p>You are not overreacting by feeling confused or unsettled about it. Your confusion about this experience is completely understandable, and many survivors of childhood sexual experiences, including COCSA, struggle with questions about whether what happened "counts" or was "serious enough." It can help to acknowledge these feelings rather than dismiss them. Sometimes just naming your discomfort can be the first step in feeling more at ease with your own emotions. You didn't do anything wrong by feeling frightened, and the fact that he was younger doesn't erase the sense of violation you experienced. The impact an experience has on you matters more than how others might categorize it.</p>
<p>If this memory is causing you distress and affecting your well-being, then it deserves attention and care, regardless of labels. If these thoughts continue to trouble you, you might consider confiding in someone you trust or seeking out a professional who understands childhood boundary violations. Many people find it helpful to work with a trauma-informed therapist who specializes in childhood experiences and can help you navigate these memories and feelings in a safe space. You deserve support from people who can offer understanding, validation, and kindness. Healing can take time, and it's okay to want clarity about confusing childhood events. You're not alone in this, and your feelings are important.</p>

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/does-it-count-as-sexual-assault-if-another-child-touched-me-inappropriately-when-i-was-young-even-though-i-wasnt-raped-754)

**Question:** Does it count as sexual assault if another child touched me inappropriately when I was young, even though I wasn't raped?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for asking this. What you experienced could absolutely count as sexual assault depending on the context. <a href="https://www.britannica.com/topic/sexual-assault">Sexual assault</a> is any unwanted sexual contact, and it doesn't require penetration or rape to be valid or harmful. When someone touches your body in sexual ways without your consent, whether they're an adult or another child, that can be characterized as sexual assault, and your experience and feelings about it matter completely.</p>
<p>It's important to understand that children engaging in sexual behaviors with other children exists on a complex spectrum that's influenced by developmental stages. Young children naturally engage in some body exploration and curiosity, like looking at or briefly touching each other's bodies, which is generally considered normal when it's between children of similar ages and stops when redirected. However, as children get older, the expectations for understanding boundaries and consent increase significantly.</p>
<p>When sexual behavior between children involves a significant age gap, a power imbalance, coercion, force, threats, secrecy, or behaviors that are far beyond what would be developmentally expected, it becomes problematic sexual behavior that can absolutely be traumatic. Children who engage in these behaviors may have been exposed to sexual content or experiences themselves, but this doesn't minimize the impact on you or make your experience less valid.</p>
<p>Additionally, even if the behavior might have been within a "normal" developmental range, your individual response to it is what matters most. Some children may experience typical childhood sexual curiosity as distressing or violating based on their temperament, family values, or other factors, and those feelings are completely legitimate.</p>
<p>Your body and your boundaries belonged to you then, just as they do now. If someone crossed those boundaries in a way that felt uncomfortable, scary, confusing, or wrong to you, those feelings are completely valid regardless of the age of the person who hurt you or their developmental stage at the time. Trauma doesn't discriminate based on the age of the person who caused it, and healing doesn't require meeting some arbitrary threshold of "serious enough."</p>
<p>Many survivors of childhood sexual assault, particularly when it involved other children, struggle with questions about whether their experience "counts" or was "bad enough" to warrant their feelings or need for support. This minimization is often reinforced by societal messages that dismiss or normalize inappropriate childhood sexual behaviors. Your experience was real, your feelings about it are valid, and you deserve support and healing regardless of the specifics of what happened or who was involved.</p>
<p>If this experience is impacting you today, please know that support is available. Many therapists specialize in childhood sexual trauma and can help you process these experiences in a safe, non-judgmental space. You deserve care and compassion as you navigate your healing journey. Thanks for trusting us with this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/ive-written-to-our-wave-a-lot-love-yall-lol-about-some-childhood-abuse-but-there-were-always-things-i-could-remember-that-couldnt-have-happened-during-that-time-period-things-that-didnt-align-with-the)

**Question:** I've written to Our Wave a lot (love y'all lol) about some childhood abuse, but there were always things I could remember that couldn't have happened during that time period-- things that didn't align with the physical reality of those involved or my body at that age. Recently, after reading a story, I suddenly had a vivid memory of my swimming instructor from when I was around 10-11, someone I hadn't thought about in years. After this happened, I dissociated and have been struggling with dissociation since. I'm questioning whether this memory is real and looking for advice on how to process this experience and figure out what happened.

**Answer:** <p>Hey! We love you too, and it means so much that you keep coming back to share with us. Your trust in this community is really beautiful, and we're honored that you feel safe enough to keep processing these difficult experiences here with us.</p>
<p class="whitespace-normal break-words">What you're experiencing, the confusion about fragmented memories and the dissociation that followed your sudden recall, makes complete sense given what you've already shared about your childhood experiences. It's really common for survivors to have memories that feel inconsistent or confusing, especially when parts of those memories don't align with what you know about your body or situation at different ages. Your brain has been working incredibly hard to protect you, and trauma can store experiences in ways that come back as images, physical sensations, or flashes of emotion rather than clear, linear memories.</p>
<p class="whitespace-normal break-words">The fact that reading something triggered such a vivid image of your swimming instructor, followed by dissociation, shows that your nervous system had a strong response to something. This kind of reaction can happen for various reasons. Sometimes it's because something significant happened with that person, or perhaps their behavior was problematic in ways that felt uncomfortable or unsafe to you, even if you couldn't fully understand it at the time. It's also possible that your previous childhood trauma experiences made your nervous system more sensitive to certain situations or interactions, and something about this instructor or that environment triggered those earlier feelings of vulnerability.</p>
<p class="whitespace-normal break-words">When you suddenly recalled his face and then dissociated, that was your mind and body's protective response kicking in. This kind of reaction often happens when something feels overwhelming or threatening in some way. Your body's wisdom is responding to something -- whether that's a specific memory, a feeling of unsafety you experienced then, or the intersection of your earlier trauma with later experiences.</p>
<p class="whitespace-normal break-words">Memory after trauma doesn't work like a video recording. It's more like scattered puzzle pieces that your brain stores in different ways to help you survive. When we've experienced trauma, especially at a young age, our minds develop powerful ways to protect us. This can lead to memory gaps, uncertainties, or complex layers of experiences that many survivors navigate, even when it's upsetting to feel unsure. It's completely normal to question these experiences and wish you could know exactly what took place.</p>
<p class="whitespace-normal break-words">Rather than focusing on determining what specific events may or may not have happened, it might be helpful to pay attention to what your body and emotions are telling you right now. Your nervous system's responses deserve care and attention. These responses often carry important information about your experiences, even when the cognitive details feel unclear.</p>
<p class="whitespace-normal break-words">If you feel ready and safe, it might be helpful to seek out a trauma-informed professional who can support you through these confusing feelings at a pace that feels comfortable for you. There are counselors who understand how challenging it can be when memories feel unclear or layered, and who can help you process the emotional impact of these experiences without pressure to determine specific details or rush to conclusions.</p>
<p class="whitespace-normal break-words">In the meantime, practice patience and kindness toward yourself. It's okay not to have all the answers right now. Feeling dissociated after that sudden recall makes sense -- your mind is protecting you. Paying attention to what kinds of activities, stories, or situations trigger strong responses might help you notice patterns and take care of yourself when you need it most.</p>
<p class="whitespace-normal break-words">Whatever you experienced deserves compassion and support. You don't need to figure everything out at once, and you deserve to be believed and supported as you navigate these confusing feelings at your own pace. You're not alone in this uncertainty, and gentle, caring help is available when you feel ready to reach for it. Thanks for reaching back out to us!</p>

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-keep-getting-triggered-by-childhood-memories-and-worry-i-may-have-engaged-in-cocsa-behavior-when-i-was-around-8-10-i-had-sexual-curiosity-and-would-sometimes-touch-myself-usually-in-private-but-occa)

**Question:** I keep getting triggered by childhood memories and worry I may have engaged in COCSA behavior. When I was around 8-10, I had sexual curiosity and would sometimes touch myself, usually in private but occasionally in public settings without realizing others might notice. One specific memory troubles me: when I was about 9, I was with a family friend who was a few years younger, and I touched myself while she was nearby. It wasn't directed at her and she wasn't forced to watch or participate, but I feel deep shame wondering if this could have impacted her, especially since neither of us understood what was happening at the time. We've remained friendly over the years when we see each other, but I can't stop worrying that I hurt her or committed some form of abuse. What exactly were my actions, and how can I better understand this situation?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out to us. It sounds like you're carrying a great deal of worry about what happened during a time when you were still learning about your body and its sensations. The unease and shame you feel are real and can be very upsetting, especially when you look back with the understanding you have now as an adult. Your courage in examining these childhood memories shows tremendous self-awareness and care for others' wellbeing, but it's important to view these experiences through the proper developmental lens.</p>
<p>In childhood, it is natural to have curiosity about your body, and at around nine years old, there's only so much understanding you can have of what you're actually doing. You were discovering sensations without the mature framework about sexuality and boundaries that you have today, and you're not alone in feeling embarrassed or unsettled by what you later recognize as sexual exploration from childhood. What you're describing sounds like normal childhood sexual exploration that occurred within your own developmental stage. Children between ages 8-10 commonly engage in self-stimulating behaviors as they discover their bodies, and doing this occasionally in the presence of others, without directing it toward them or involving them, falls within the range of typical childhood behavior.</p>
<p>When sexual behavior occurs between children of similar ages, professionals often look at whether there was a significant difference in maturity or a power imbalance, and whether the behavior was coercive, aggressive, or significantly beyond what's developmentally appropriate. In your memory, it doesn't sound like you specifically intended to engage the other child or expose her to something harmful. Instead, you described an internal focus, rubbing your thighs together for your own sense of comfort or curiosity, and you didn't involve her in any direct way. You also didn't coerce her, force her to watch, or make it about her in any manner that she had to participate in. The fact that this wasn't directed at the other child, didn't involve her participation, and wasn't something you were trying to get her to watch or engage with distinguishes it from harmful sexual behavior.</p>
<p>The shame you feel in the present might be your grown-up mind placing adult interpretations on a situation that occurred when you had a childlike view of the world. Children in that age range don't fully grasp the implications of sexual behaviors or consent, and they often explore their bodies with no intent to harm another person. It is very natural to feel unsettled when you look back at that exploration through adult eyes. The intrusive thoughts and shame spirals you're experiencing may actually be a trauma response themselves. Sometimes our brains latch onto childhood memories and reframe them through an adult lens, creating guilt about behaviors that were actually age-appropriate.</p>
<p>If your family friend appears comfortable around you and remains on good terms, it could indicate that your past actions did not register as hurtful or violating to her. For many people, simply naming these complicated feelings and learning that childhood sexual curiosity is not the same as abuse can bring relief. The fact that you are so worried about the possibility of harming someone suggests a strong moral compass, rather than an indication that you had the intention to cause any harm. You were a young person exploring sensations without understanding their meaning, and the empathy you're showing now, looking back, is a sign of your kindness and sense of responsibility toward others.</p>
<p>Your description of having "a tendency to overthink" suggests this pattern of ruminating and self-blame might be part of a broader pattern of anxiety or intrusive thinking that could benefit from professional support. If you continue to feel unsettled, focusing on how you behave now (e.g. showing respect and care in your relationships, and maintaining healthy boundaries) might help you make peace with the past.&nbsp;</p>
<p>It's okay to acknowledge those confusing moments in childhood, accept them as part of your learning process, and remember that you're allowed to find healing and self-forgiveness as the adult you are today. You deserve understanding and compassion, especially from yourself. Thanks for trusting us with this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-dont-think-i-was-abused-as-a-child-but-starting-in-high-school-i-began-having-fleeting-relationships-with-lots-of-sex-it-was-common-in-my-friend-group-ive-continued-a-pattern-of-being-attracted-to-p)

**Question:** I don't think I was abused as a child, but starting in high school I began having fleeting relationships with lots of sex. It was common in my friend group. I've continued a pattern of being attracted to people who treat me badly. How can I change this pattern?

**Answer:** <p>It sounds like you've been carrying a sense of frustration and maybe even some self-blame for being caught in a cycle of relationships that don't uplift or respect you. What you're describing reflects patterns that many people recognize in their own lives, even when they don't have clear memories of childhood trauma. These behaviors can develop as responses to various experiences, including emotional neglect, boundary violations that might not register as "abuse" at the time, or growing up in environments where healthy relationship modeling wasn't present.</p>
<p>When we start having sexual experiences very young, especially when it becomes a dominant way of connecting with others, it can sometimes indicate that we learned early on to use our bodies or sexuality as a way to feel valued, wanted, or in control. This isn't about making poor choices&mdash;it's often about adapting to circumstances with the tools and understanding we had available at the time. The pattern of being attracted to people who treat you badly might reflect what feels familiar or what you've internalized about your own worth and what kind of treatment you deserve.</p>
<p>It's understandable that, when these patterns start early, like in high school, you might feel they're too ingrained to change. The fact that you're asking how you can "change your pattern" shows you're ready to look at them and make choices that feel healthier for you. It may help to remind yourself that you deserve relationships where respect and genuine care are present. That truth doesn't vanish, no matter what your past has looked like or how many difficult relationships you've experienced.</p>
<p>Moving forward often involves being curious about what you look for in a partner, and paying closer attention to how those values line up with the kind of care and respect you want in your life. It might also mean stepping back from people who reinforce that old pattern of being treated badly, even if you've known them for a long time. Taking small but consistent steps toward healthier connections (like surrounding yourself with friends or communities who show respect and kindness, or talking your feelings through with someone you trust) can help break those long-standing cycles.</p>
<p>Breaking these patterns is absolutely possible, though it often requires patience with yourself and sometimes professional support to understand the underlying beliefs and experiences that shaped them. Many people find that therapy, particularly trauma-informed approaches, can be incredibly helpful in understanding these patterns and developing healthier ways of connecting with others. You're not locked into one path, even if it felt that way for a long time. Every time you reach out, set a boundary, or choose kindness toward yourself instead of blame, you're taking a step toward changing those patterns you're worried are set. You deserve relationships built on respect, care, and genuine affection and recognizing that these patterns exist is already an important step toward creating the change you're seeking.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/ive-experienced-multiple-instances-of-unwanted-sexual-contact-throughout-my-life-starting-when-i-was-young-with-a-classmate-who-would-touch-me-inappropriately-and-coerce-me-into-it-even-though-i-dont)

**Question:** I've experienced multiple instances of unwanted sexual contact throughout my life, starting when I was young with a classmate who would touch me inappropriately and coerce me into it. Even though I don't remember all the details clearly, I can feel the impact in my body. Recently, someone close to me touched me inappropriately multiple times when I was in a vulnerable emotional state - I had just been venting and almost crying to them. I felt too heartbroken to move or react in the moment and didn't even realize what was happening because I wasn't in the right headspace. When I got home, I felt disgusted with myself and violated. Now, whenever sexual topics come up or when boys talk about me in sexual ways like I'm some object, something in me just switches and I start breaking down. My body seems to react with fear even in situations that might seem safe to others - like when I was alone with an older student and felt terrified for no clear reason, with my leg shaking and heart beating out of my chest. Can you help me understand these reactions?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out to us. It sounds like you have been navigating deeply distressing experiences at various points in your life, some going back to when you were very young. It also sounds like you never received a real explanation for what happened to you or why you felt so shaken and fearful. Those questions you carry are understandable responses to repeated violations of your sense of safety. These reactions do not mean there is anything inherently wrong with you.</p>
<p>What you're describing are completely normal trauma responses, though understandably distressing. Your body and mind are responding exactly as they should to experiences of sexual violence and boundary violations. These reactions show that your internal protective systems are working to keep you safe, even when your conscious mind might not fully understand what's happening. They often happen because of trauma. Your mind and body learned early on that certain situations could lead to harm, so your system goes into protective mode, preparing to fight, flee, or freeze whenever something surfaces that reminds it of potential danger.</p>
<p>When you were little and your classmate touched you in ways you instinctively knew were wrong, you were put into a situation you had no tools to handle at that age. It makes sense that you remember so little. Young minds often block out or partially remember hurtful events in order to cope and go on living. What you're describing as "feeling it in your bones" is your body's memory of that trauma. Our bodies hold onto traumatic experiences even when our minds don't have clear recollections, and this can manifest as physical sensations, emotional reactions, or a general sense that something was wrong. In the absence of clear answers, you only knew you felt unsafe.</p>
<p>Years later, being alone with an older boy in a room might have triggered those same fear pathways buried in your body, causing your leg to shake or your heart to pound, even if you didn't fully understand why. Your intense fear response when alone with that older student was what's called a trauma trigger. Your nervous system recognized similarities to previous unsafe situations and activated your body's alarm system to protect you. That physical trembling reflects how your body can sometimes redirect your panic into something you can't control, an automatic reaction to perceived danger. This <a href="https://health.clevelandclinic.org/hypervigilance">hypervigilance</a> is a survival mechanism that develops after trauma.</p>
<p>It also sounds like your recent experience, being felt up by someone you trusted while you were already hurting and emotionally vulnerable, added another painful layer to all of this. When you were in such a fragile state, that person took advantage of you, leaving you in shock and disbelief. Your reaction of feeling frozen and unable to move is called a freeze response, which is one of the most common reactions to sexual assault. Feeling numb or immobilized during that sort of violation is common and does not mean you consented or gave permission. It simply reflects the way brains often respond to extreme stress. When our fight-or-flight systems become overwhelmed, our bodies sometimes shut down as a protective mechanism. This is not a choice you made or a sign of weakness-- it's an involuntary neurobiological response to threat.</p>
<p>You might still be replaying that moment, asking yourself why you didn't move or call out, but freezing can occur when your nervous system goes into defense mode. This is the body's way of protecting you, even though it leaves you feeling guilty or ashamed afterward. The sense of disgust and violation you describe once you got home shows you fully grasp the wrongness of what happened, even though it took time for your mind and body to catch up to each other. The disgust you feel toward yourself after these experiences is unfortunately common among survivors, but it's important to understand that this shame belongs with the people who harmed you, not with you.</p>
<p>The breakdowns you mention when sexual topics come up or when boys objectify you are also a sign of deeply rooted trauma re-surfacing. These intense emotional reactions are your mind and body working together to process trauma, not signs that something is fundamentally wrong with you. Trauma memories aren't stored like everyday memories, so reminders (even simple things like certain words, physical closeness, or someone commenting on your body) can suddenly bring back the alarm and helplessness you felt during the assault. This "switch" you describe is an important signal from your body, telling you it recognizes danger or the hint of danger. It can leave you feeling overwhelmed and convinced something is wrong with you personally, when in reality, you are responding to repeated boundary violations that no one should have experienced in the first place.</p>
<p>You're not crazy or broken for feeling all these emotions and fears. Your experiences represent a pattern of sexual violence and boundary violations that have understandably left lasting impacts on your nervous system and emotional well-being. Often, healing involves understanding how your body and mind adapted to past harm so you could survive it. Your reactions are evidence of your resilience and your body's determination to protect you, not signs of something being wrong with you.</p>
<p>Some people find it helpful to talk with a professional who specializes in childhood or sexual trauma because they're equipped to explain why these responses happen and to guide you through practical ways to re-establish your sense of safety. A trauma-informed therapist who specializes in sexual violence can help you process these experiences, understand your body's responses, and develop coping strategies that work for you. Many survivors find therapies like <a href="https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/22641-emdr-therapy">EMDR</a>, <a href="https://www.verywellmind.com/what-is-somatic-experiencing-5204186">somatic experiencing</a>, or <a href="https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/therapy-types/trauma-focused-cognitive-behavior-therapy?msockid=1103576693d9679310b645d892bb66b2">trauma-focused cognitive behavioral therapy</a> particularly helpful. Others seek out confidential hotlines or support groups to share their stories with people who truly understand.</p>
<p>What's most critical is recognizing that you are worthy of support and that these intense feelings are valid responses to the violations you've endured. Your response to trauma does not define who you are; it shows that you are a survivor whose body is trying hard to protect itself. You have done nothing wrong in any of these situations. The people who violated your boundaries and assaulted you are responsible for their actions, not you.</p>
<p>Though it may feel overwhelming, know that healing is a gradual process. You may find strength in reminding yourself that these reactions are not your fault; they are your mind's way of safeguarding you from further hurt. Over time, and with the right help, be it from empathetic friends, trauma-informed counselors, or survivor resource centers, many people discover they can diminish the power these memories hold over them and learn to feel a sense of peace, autonomy, and safety again. You deserve to feel safe in your body and in relationships. You deserve to find that peace as well, and you do not have to face these emotions or memories all on your own. Seeking understanding about these experiences, as you're doing now, is an important step in your healing journey. Thanks for trusting us with this. We appreciate you.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/if-you-use-a-service-to-remove-non-consensual-intimate-images-do-the-people-working-there-have-to-look-at-the-photos-when-you-submit-them-i-want-to-use-one-of-these-services-but-im-worried-about-more)

**Question:** If you use a service to remove non-consensual intimate images, do the people working there have to look at the photos when you submit them? I want to use one of these services but I'm worried about more people seeing the images.

**Answer:** <p>Thank you so much for asking this. It's completely natural and understandable to feel worried about having anyone else see those photos.&nbsp; The good news is that the most reputable organizations devoted to helping survivors like you have developed systems specifically designed to honor your privacy and keep you in control. Services like <a href="https://stopncii.org/">StopNCII.org</a> and<a href="https://takeitdown.ncmec.org/"> Take It Down</a> understand exactly why privacy matters so much to survivors, and they've built their entire approach around protecting you from any additional exposure or harm.</p>
<p>These organizations use something called "on-device hashing," which means all the processing happens right on your own device, in your web browser. Your photos never actually leave your computer or phone. Instead, the system creates a unique digital "fingerprint" of the image - kind of like a mathematical recipe that represents your photo without anyone being able to see or recreate the actual image. Only that code gets sent to the service, and it's technically impossible for anyone to turn it back into your photo.</p>
<p>Here's what's important to know: while the removal service staff never see your images, when these services work with social media platforms to find and remove content, those platforms may have moderators who review flagged material to confirm it should be removed. These are people who are specially trained to handle sensitive content respectfully, but I want you to know this step exists so you can make the most informed decision for yourself.</p>
<p>Typically the process is completely anonymous. You don't need to give your name, email, or any personal information. You just get a case number that only you know. These organizations have spent years earning the trust of survivors and advocates, and they're regularly recognized for putting privacy and dignity first.</p>
<p>You have every right to ask any service about exactly how they handle your information and what their privacy protections look like. A trustworthy organization will be completely transparent about their process and happy to answer your questions. Trust what feels right for you. Your sense of safety and comfort matters more than anything else.</p>
<p>Taking this step to reclaim control over your images is an act of incredible courage and self-advocacy. You deserve to feel safe, respected, and heard throughout this process. Whatever you decide, know that prioritizing your wellbeing and taking time to feel confident about your choice is exactly the right approach. Thank you for trusting us with this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/im-struggling-to-differentiate-between-normal-childhood-curiosity-and-sexual-abuse-these-are-two-hypothetical-situations-1-a-twelve-year-old-started-touching-and-kissing-a-four-year-old-she-asked-them)

**Question:** I'm struggling to differentiate between normal childhood curiosity and sexual abuse. These are two hypothetical situations: 1. A twelve-year-old started touching and kissing a four-year-old. She asked them to stop. This happened twice. 2. A twelve-year-old briefly inappropriately touched a four-year-old's private area over clothing, and when asked to stop, they immediately did and nothing more happened ever again. Would situation 1 be viewed as sexual assault/abuse, and situation 2 be viewed as normal childhood curiosity?

**Answer:** <p><strong>Thanks for asking aboout this. </strong>The distinction between normal childhood curiosity and problematic sexual behavior involves several important factors, with age differences and genuine mutuality being key considerations.</p>
<p>A key difference lies in the age gap between the children and the presence or absence of genuine mutuality. When a four-year-old is interacting with a twelve-year-old, there is already a significant disparity in power, understanding, and development. Children that young do not have the capacity to consent, and a much older child typically has a level of knowledge and influence that makes true "curiosity" difficult to separate from coercion. Normal childhood curiosity typically involves peers of similar ages and developmental stages exploring together with mutual interest, stopping immediately when anyone feels uncomfortable, and generally involving brief, exploratory behaviors rather than repeated actions.</p>
<p>In the first scenario, the younger child begged the older child to stop, but it happened twice anyway. Begging for it to stop indicates a clear refusal that was ignored, and that crosses a line into abusive behavior. Several concerning elements are present: the persistence despite clear communication to stop, the repetition of the behavior, and the significant age and developmental gap between the children. The fact that the behavior continued after the younger child asked it to stop indicates a disregard for boundaries that moves beyond typical curiosity.</p>
<p>In the second scenario, while the initial touching of private areas given the substantial age difference represents problematic sexual behavior that goes beyond typical peer curiosity, the fact that the older child stopped immediately when asked is significant. This immediate response to boundaries, while not excusing the inappropriate nature of the contact, suggests the behavior may have been more impulsive rather than predatory. The lack of repeated behavior after being told to stop is an important distinction that indicates the child was capable of respecting boundaries once they were clearly communicated.</p>
<p>True childhood exploration typically occurs among children of closer ages, tends to be mutual, and stops the moment a boundary is expressed. When a younger child expresses discomfort or says "no," yet the older child proceeds or even initiates such contact, it's generally viewed as a violation rather than harmless play. Both situations would likely benefit from adult intervention and guidance, though the approach might differ, to ensure that all children receive the support and education they need to develop healthy understanding of boundaries and appropriate interactions with others.</p>
<p>If you find yourself questioning these experiences, you are not alone. Many people struggle with how to label interactions that felt confusing or uncomfortable when they were very young.&nbsp; Above all, it is important to listen to your own sense of safety and comfort. Even in childhood, our reactions to being touched show us whether something was consensual exploration or a breach of boundaries that made us feel scared or powerless. Your feelings about these situations are valid regardless of how others might categorize them, and what matters most is how these experiences affected you and getting the support you need to process those feelings. Thank you so much for trusting us with this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-keep-seeing-my-old-swimming-instructors-face-in-my-head-over-and-over-and-it-makes-me-feel-sick-to-my-stomach-i-dont-remember-anything-specific-about-him-and-havent-thought-about-him-in-years-but-i)

**Question:** I keep seeing my old swimming instructor's face in my head over and over, and it makes me feel sick to my stomach. I don't remember anything specific about him and haven't thought about him in years, but I see his face so clearly and how he's looking at me. I've had other traumatic encounters before, and I'm scared there might be another one I don't remember. These thoughts are making me feel physically ill and giving me headaches. What could this mean?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out to us.&nbsp;It sounds like you're experiencing a lot of confusion and distress seeing this person's face so vividly, especially when you have no clear memory or understanding of why it's bothering you now. What you're going through right now is incredibly overwhelming, and I want you to know that your fear and physical reactions are completely valid, regardless of what they ultimately mean or represent.</p>
<p>The sudden, vivid mental images of someone's face accompanied by intense physical symptoms like nausea and headaches can have many different explanations. Sometimes these reactions can signal that something significant happened, but they can also occur when our previous trauma experiences have made us more sensitive to certain people, situations, or dynamics that remind us (consciously or unconsciously) of past harm. Your brain might be responding to something about this person or your interactions with them that felt uncomfortable or unsettling, which may or may not have involved any inappropriate behavior on their part.</p>
<p>Often, when the mind picks up on a possible reminder of past harm, whether it's connected to an actual harmful event or simply reminiscent of feelings from a different painful experience, the body reacts before we have the words to explain it. Your previous traumatic encounters may have created a heightened awareness that causes your nervous system to respond strongly to memories or situations that share certain elements with your past experiences. Feeling sick to your stomach, or having your head hurt when these thoughts arise, is a common way your body might express alarm or uncertainty when trying to process confusing or distressing memories.</p>
<p>It's important to know that having these intense reactions could mean different things. Sometimes these strong physical and emotional responses do indicate that something harmful happened that your mind and body are trying to process, even when you don't have clear conscious memories of it. At the same time, these reactions can also occur when our minds and bodies create strong responses to people or situations based on subtle feelings of discomfort, power dynamics, or other elements that reminded us of previous difficulties, even when no abuse occurred with that specific person. Both possibilities are valid, and there's no way to know for certain without time and possibly professional support to help you explore these feelings safely. Your distress is real and deserves acknowledgment either way, and you deserve support in working through these difficult feelings regardless of what they ultimately reveal about your experiences.</p>
<p>You don't have to push yourself to force a memory or determine definitively what happened. Sometimes these sensations come up when your mind is processing various experiences, and the meaning isn't always clear right away. Right now, focusing on grounding techniques and self-care can help manage these intense physical symptoms. When these images come up, try grounding yourself in the present moment through deep breathing, naming things you can see around you, or holding something with a comforting texture.</p>
<p>If the nausea or headaches become overwhelming, consider reaching out to someone supportive (e.g. a friend, a trusted family member, or a trained professional) who understands how complex trauma responses can be. If you have access to trauma-informed therapy, they can help you navigate this process safely and at your own pace, without pressure to reach any specific conclusions about what these experiences mean.</p>
<p>Above all, be gentle with yourself. The fact that you feel so unsettled tells us that you deserve compassion and patience as you sort through what's happening. There is no right or wrong timeline for understanding what these reactions might mean, and it's okay to take it in small steps. You aren't alone, and there are people who will believe you and want to help you find some peace as you work through these difficult feelings. You don't have to go through it alone, and you deserve support during this challenging time. Thank you for trusting us with this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/im-questioning-whether-what-happened-to-me-was-rape-i-met-someone-online-who-i-planned-to-hang-out-with-i-clearly-told-him-i-wasnt-interested-in-one-night-stands-and-he-said-he-felt-the-same-way-when)

**Question:** I'm questioning whether what happened to me was rape. I met someone online who I planned to hang out with. I clearly told him I wasn't interested in one night stands, and he said he felt the same way. When I went to his place, he eventually took me to his bedroom and started making moves on me. I told him no, but he used force to stop me from moving. I was scared he would hit me, so I let him have sex with me. Is this considered rape?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you so much for trusting us with this. Based on what you shared with me, what you experienced could certainly be <a href="https://rainn.org/get-the-facts-about-sexual-assault-rape/">classified as rape.</a> When someone uses force to prevent you from moving after you've said no, and you submit out of fear for your physical safety, that is absolutely sexual assault.</p>
<p class="whitespace-normal break-words">Your body's response to freeze or submit when faced with the threat of violence is a completely normal survival mechanism. Many survivors describe feeling like they "let it happen" or didn't fight back "enough," but compliance under threat or fear is not consent. True <a href="https://rainn.org/share-the-facts/consent-101-respect-boundaries-and-building-trust/">consent</a> requires the freedom to say no without fear of harm, and you were clearly denied that freedom when he used physical force against you.</p>
<p class="whitespace-normal break-words">The fact that you had previously communicated your boundaries about not wanting casual encounters makes his actions even more clearly a violation. He heard your boundary, agreed with it to gain your trust, then deliberately ignored your explicit "no" when the situation escalated. This pattern of reassurance followed by boundary violation is unfortunately common among perpetrators.</p>
<p class="whitespace-normal break-words">Your fear that he might hit you was not irrational. It was your mind and body recognizing a dangerous situation. When someone uses force to restrain you against your will, the threat of further violence becomes very real. Submitting to protect yourself from greater harm is a survival strategy, not consent.</p>
<p class="whitespace-normal break-words">Please know that none of this was your fault. You clearly communicated your boundaries both before arriving and in the moment. You have every right to seek support, whether through counseling, trusted friends, or survivor resources. Your experience and your feelings about it are valid, and you deserve care and healing as you process what happened to you. Thank you for trusting us with this.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/ive-engaged-in-self-mutilation-with-cutting-after-sexual-assault-i-target-areas-the-perpetrator-touched-or-aroused-my-thoughts-are-to-make-my-skin-look-different-than-the-body-they-touched-i-see-my-bo)

**Question:** I've engaged in self-mutilation with cutting after sexual assault. I target areas the perpetrator touched or aroused. My thoughts are to make my skin look different than the body they touched. I see my body in the mirror as my reflection right after the assault happened. Is this something common that doesn't get talked about? The more I scar myself, the more I feel like they're gone, or at least that's what I try to believe. I'm getting help through therapy for both the assault and self-injury. Is it normal to want to stop arousal and flashbacks by any means necessary?

**Answer:** <p>I'm so sorry you've been feeling this way, and I'm grateful you felt safe enough to share your experience. What you're describing is a trauma response that many sexual assault survivors experience but is rarely discussed openly. It's not unusual for survivors of sexual violence to feel an overwhelming need to reclaim their bodies in the aftermath, and self-harm can sometimes appear in these moments as a way to cope. When you mention cutting the very places he touched in order to make your skin look different, it sounds like it might reflect your attempt to regain control over a body that was violated. This is one way survivors may attempt to create physical distance from their trauma memories.</p>
<p>For many survivors, the particular kind of self-injury you describe doesn't always come up in everyday conversation, yet it can be more common than people realize. The distress you feel looking in the mirror and seeing yourself exactly as you were after he hurt you can be incredibly painful. Trauma can freeze certain images and sensations in our minds, making us feel stuck in that moment even when we're safe, and altering your skin may feel like a way to distance yourself from those memories and separate your present self from those traumatic moments.</p>
<p>Feeling that you will do whatever it takes to stop arousal or flashbacks is also a response many survivors recognize and is completely understandable. These involuntary bodily responses can feel like ongoing violations, making you feel as though your body is still connected to the assault in ways that are deeply distressing. Trauma can lead us to seek any means of relief when faced with overwhelming reminders, and self-harm becomes a desperate attempt to quiet that turmoil, blot out that sense of helplessness, or serve as a momentary release. Many survivors describe feeling betrayed by their own physical responses and desperately seeking ways to regain control. What you're feeling can certainly be a facet of the broader range of self-harm, and it doesn't make you any less worthy of help and compassion.</p>
<p>I hear that you're already getting therapy and I want you to know that seeking support is such a courageous and important step. It's encouraging that you're working with a therapist who understands both sexual trauma and self-injury. Speaking openly with a professional can help you develop healthier ways to cope and to gradually replace self-injury with a sense of safety and self-care. Recovery from sexual assault often involves learning new ways to manage flashbacks and reconnect with your body in positive ways through body-based therapies, grounding techniques, and trauma-focused treatments that can be particularly helpful in processing these specific experiences and developing coping strategies that still provide the sense of control and relief you're seeking.</p>
<p>When you feel ready, there are alternative ways to reclaim your body and manage intense emotions that can provide some of the same relief without causing physical harm. Some survivors find that using washable markers or temporary tattoos on areas they want to alter gives them a sense of changing their appearance without permanent damage. Ice cubes held against the skin or flicking the skin with a rubberband can provide intense physical sensation that interrupts emotional overwhelm. Creating new positive associations with your body through gentle self-massage with scented lotions, or covering areas with beautiful temporary decorations like henna, can help you reclaim ownership in nurturing ways. Intense physical exercise, cold showers, or progressive muscle relaxation can help discharge the energy that builds up before self-harm urges. For flashbacks specifically, grounding techniques like naming five things you can see, four you can touch, three you can hear, two you can smell, and one you can taste can help bring you back to the present moment and remind your nervous system that you are safe now. These are just some ideas, but only you can experiment with what works for you.</p>
<p>It doesn't mean these urges will disappear overnight, and it doesn't mean you can't experience setbacks, but it does mean there is a way forward that doesn't involve harming yourself. You deserve patience and kindness from yourself in the same way you give it to others, especially in the aftermath of such a painful violation. You're not alone in this, and the urge to separate yourself from those memories is something other survivors have felt. Your healing journey is valid, and the feelings you're experiencing are a normal response to an abnormal and traumatic situation. Healing might not always be straightforward, but you're taking courageous steps by acknowledging these feelings and working with someone who can guide you. With continued support and treatment, many survivors find ways to feel safe and at peace in their bodies again. You deserve to feel safe in your own skin again.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/does-it-count-as-sexual-assault-if-i-stopped-the-encounter-halfway-through-727)

**Question:** Does it count as sexual assault if I stopped the encounter halfway through?

**Answer:** <p>Thanks for reaching out to us. Without knowing the specific context of your situation--whether you initially consented and then changed your mind, were coerced from the beginning, or experienced something else entirely-- it's difficult to provide a definitive answer about your particular experience. However, I can share some general information about consent and sexual assault that may help you understand your situation better.</p>
<p>Sexual assault occurs when any sexual activity happens without ongoing, enthusiastic consent. Consent is not a one-time agreement. It's an ongoing process that can be withdrawn at any point during an encounter. If you indicated you wanted to stop an encounter and the other person continued or pressured you despite that indication, your boundaries were violated. This violation can constitute sexual assault, regardless of what happened before you decided to stop.</p>
<p>It's completely understandable that you might question whether your experience "counts" as sexual assault. Many survivors grapple with these doubts, especially when the situation doesn't match what they might have expected assault to look like. Feeling uncertain or questioning yourself is extremely common, and these feelings don't reflect the reality of what you experienced. Sexual assault exists on a spectrum, and every violation of your consent matters, regardless of duration or the specific circumstances.</p>
<p>Your body and your boundaries belong to you alone. When someone continues sexual activity after you've indicated you want to stop, whether through words, actions, or body language, they are disrespecting your autonomy. Your decision to stop, regardless of when it occurred or what led up to that moment, was your right and should have been immediately respected.</p>
<p>You deserve support and understanding. What happened was not your fault. If you find that these thoughts continue to trouble you or would like individual support, consider reaching out to someone you trust, whether that's a friend, an advocate at a local resource center, or a professional who is trained to help survivors. Organizations like <a href="https://hotline.rainn.org/online">RAINN </a>(1-800-656-HOPE) offer confidential support and can connect you with local resources. Thank you for trusting us with this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-around-13-my-mother-would-ask-me-to-massage-her-back-legs-and-bottom-area-while-she-was-unclothed-on-her-bed-she-would-use-a-plastic-tool-with-soap-im-wondering-if-this-was-appropriate-beha)

**Question:** When I was around 13, my mother would ask me to massage her back, legs, and bottom area while she was unclothed on her bed. She would use a plastic tool with soap. I'm wondering if this was appropriate behavior from a parent, especially since I feel like if a father asked this of a daughter, it would be seen as inappropriate.

**Answer:** <p>Thanks for reaching out to us. It sounds like this memory is creating a lot of discomfort and uncertainty for you, and that's completely understandable. Your instincts about this situation are absolutely valid, and I want to acknowledge how much courage it takes to ask this question. What you've described crosses important boundaries that should exist between parents and children. Being expected to massage a parent's body in an area that normally would be private can violate important boundaries between a child and an adult.</p>
<p>Although every family has different routines around touch, it is widely recognized that asking a child of your age to massage a parent's bare buttocks and legs crosses the line of what is generally considered acceptable caregiving or affection between a parent and child. You're right to recognize that this behavior was inappropriate. It involved unnecessary nudity and physical contact that placed you in an uncomfortable position as a child. It doesn't necessarily matter who is in the role of parent, mother or father, if the situation leaves you feeling that your boundaries were overlooked or that you were placed in an inappropriate position.</p>
<p>Healthy parent-child relationships maintain appropriate physical boundaries, especially as children enter adolescence. Parents should not put their children in situations that involve intimate physical contact or nudity, regardless of how it might be framed or justified. Children deserve to feel safe and respected, and that includes being able to say "no" to touch that feels uncomfortable. Being put in a position where it doesn't feel possible to say no suggests a breach of that safety.</p>
<p>It's normal to question this behavior, especially as an adult looking back, and to feel unsettled that it happened. If you're feeling lingering unease or confusion about these experiences, that in itself is significant and worth acknowledging. It's common for survivors of boundary violations to question their own perceptions, especially when the inappropriate behavior came from someone who was supposed to protect them. Your feelings about this experience, whatever they may be, are completely valid.</p>
<p>What you experienced was not your fault, and you deserved to have your boundaries respected. If these memories continue to weigh on you, it might help to talk about them with someone you trust or with a professional who understands childhood and family boundary issues. Organizations like <a href="https://hotline.rainn.org/online">RAINN</a> (1-800-656-HOPE) offer confidential support and can help connect you with local resources. You deserve support and clarity in coming to terms with any experiences that may have been confusing, distressing, or that simply never sat right with you. Your wellbeing matters, and there are people who understand and want to help.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-a-child-8-an-older-family-member-13-wanted-to-play-boyfriend-and-girlfriend-in-the-bathtub-during-this-game-they-kissed-me-and-touched-me-while-i-was-undressed-and-they-remained-clothed-at)

**Question:** When I was a child (8), an older family member (13) wanted to play 'boyfriend and girlfriend' in the bathtub. During this 'game,' they kissed me and touched me while I was undressed and they remained clothed. At the time, I thought it was just playing, but now looking back I realize how inappropriate that was. I don't know if this was COCSA and it's driving me crazy trying to figure it out.

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for sharing your experience and for the courage it takes to reach out about something that's been weighing on your mind. It makes sense to look back on this moment with confusion, especially now that you understand more about healthy boundaries and how children relate to each other. It's completely understandable that you're trying to make sense of what happened, especially when childhood experiences can feel confusing to process as an adult.</p>
<p>What you're describing could fall under <a href="https://www.ourwave.org/en/post/child-on-child-sexual-abuse-cocsa-frequently-asked-questions-and-considerations?id=child-on-child-sexual-abuse-cocsa-frequently-asked-questions-and-considerations">Child-on-Child Sexual Abuse</a> (COCSA), but only you can label your experience. Children at ages eight and thirteen are at very different stages of development. That five-year gap represents substantial differences in physical development, understanding of sexuality, and social power during childhood. In situations where an older child directs sexual play toward a younger child, it can cross into harmful territory even if it feels like a game at the time. That difference in maturity can create a power imbalance, which can be a key factor in what professionals call COCSA. The fact that you were undressed while they remained clothed, combined with the kissing and touching, suggests behavior that went beyond typical childhood play.</p>
<p>It's important to remember that your family member was also a child at thirteen, likely navigating their own confusion about sexuality and boundaries. At that age, children are still developing their understanding of appropriate behavior and may not fully grasp the impact of their actions on younger children. This doesn't excuse what happened or minimize your experience, but it can help provide context for understanding that children who engage in inappropriate sexual behavior are often acting from their own lack of understanding, exposure to inappropriate content, or even their own experiences of boundary violations.</p>
<p>This experience does not mean there was anything wrong with you at the time. You were only eight, and children often go along with what an older kid suggests because they don't have the same understanding of boundaries that an older child might have. The confusion you're experiencing about whether this "counts" is something many survivors of childhood sexual experiences struggle with. When we're children, we don't have the framework to understand when something crosses appropriate boundaries, especially when it's presented as a game by someone we trust. Your brain naturally tried to make sense of the situation in the only way it could at the time --as play. This doesn't mean what happened was okay, and it doesn't diminish the impact it may have had on you.</p>
<p>Looking back and feeling that it was odd or unsettling is a normal response once you're old enough to recognize these differences. The fact that you're reflecting on this experience now and questioning it shows your adult understanding recognizing that something wasn't right. You're not alone in questioning or analyzing childhood memories as you grow older. If the memory is causing you distress or if you're questioning it continually, it might help to speak with someone who understands these situations&mdash;perhaps a counselor, support line, or anyone trained in working through childhood boundary violations.</p>
<p>You deserve validation concerning your discomfort, and it's okay to name what happened in the way that feels right to you, whether or not you decide to call it COCSA. The most important thing is recognizing that you were a child who didn't ask for or truly understand what was happening. Your feelings now do not change that you did nothing wrong and did not cause what happened. Whatever you decide to call this experience, your feelings about it are valid, and seeking support to work through these memories can be incredibly helpful. If you find yourself feeling overwhelmed, seeking gentle, knowledgeable support might bring clarity and help you feel more at ease with this part of your past. Thank you for trusting us with this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-see-some-stories-on-the-our-wave-platform-have-updates-how-do-i-add-an-update-to-my-story-besides-posting-in-the-comments-740)

**Question:** I see some stories on the Our Wave platform have updates.  How do I add an update to my story besides posting in the comments?

Once you have that link, you will be able to update your story. Please reach out to <a href="mailto:hello@ourwave.org">hello@ourwave.org </a>if you have any additional questions related to this!</p>
