**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/what-are-some-helpful-strategies-for-managing-flashbacks-related-to-coercive-control-talking-through-the-experience-doesnt-always-feel-helpful-and-im-looking-for-additional-techniques-to-complement-pa)

**Question:** What are some helpful strategies for managing flashbacks related to coercive control? Talking through the experience doesn't always feel helpful, and I'm looking for additional techniques to complement pausing and allowing the feelings to pass.

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out and for sharing your preferences with us. It's very common and understandable to look for additional strategies to manage flashbacks beyond talking, especially to complement the practice of pausing and letting feelings pass. Actively engaging in certain practices can indeed be very helpful in regaining a sense of presence and safety when these difficult moments arise.</p>
<p>It's completely understandable that talking doesn't always feel helpful when you're experiencing flashbacks related to coercive control, and it shows great self-awareness that you're seeking strategies that work specifically for you. Flashbacks from coercive control can be particularly challenging because they often involve complex layers of emotional, psychological, and sometimes physical memories that can feel overwhelming.</p>
<p>One set of helpful strategies involves grounding techniques, which are designed to bring you back to the present moment and reconnect you with your current surroundings. The 5-4-3-2-1 technique involves identifying 5 things you can see, 4 things you can touch, 3 things you can hear, 2 things you can smell, and 1 thing you can taste. This helps anchor you in the present moment when your mind feels pulled back into past experiences. Some people find focusing on the sensation of their feet on the floor helpful, or holding a comfort object like ice cubes or different textures. Deep, deliberate breathing exercises can also be very grounding and can activate your body's relaxation response, particularly longer exhales than inhales. You might try slowly inhaling, holding your breath, and then slowly exhaling, perhaps counting to four for each step.</p>
<p>Engaging in self-soothing activities that provide comfort and a sense of safety can also be very beneficial. You could create a "crisis kit" or "comfort box" filled with items that soothe you, like a soft blanket, calming scents, or music that makes you feel peaceful. Simply wearing comfortable clothing or, if it feels right, taking a warm bath or shower focusing on the sensation of the water can also be helpful. Some survivors find that having a specific playlist ready, keeping comforting objects nearby, or having predetermined safe spaces where they can go provides structure during difficult moments.</p>
<p>Practicing mindful awareness and acceptance of the flashback can also be a useful approach. This involves acknowledging the memory or feeling without judgment, perhaps saying to yourself, "I notice I'm having this feeling right now." It can be helpful to remind yourself that thoughts and feelings, particularly those related to trauma, are often like clouds passing by and will eventually shift. Gently reminding yourself that you are safe in the present moment, even if the feelings are very intense, is a key part of this process.</p>
<p>Gentle movement and reconnecting with your body in a safe way can also complement pausing. Movement-based strategies can be powerful tools for discharging the physical tension that often accompanies flashbacks. If you feel up to it, gentle stretching, walking, or even simple movements like rolling your shoulders can help you feel more present. Some find bilateral movements, like alternating tapping their knees or crossing their arms and patting their shoulders, can help calm their nervous system. Practicing self-touch in a non-sexual, comforting manner, like gently massaging your hands or applying lotion, can bring awareness back to your body in a positive way.</p>
<p>Furthermore, redirecting your attention and engaging in activities that require some focus can be helpful. This might be something like a puzzle, a simple game, drawing, or other creative tasks that help ground you in the present moment. Some people find it helpful to remind themselves of current realities by looking at recent photos, reading affirming notes they've written to themselves, or touching objects that represent their current life and freedom.</p>
<p>Throughout this process, self-compassion is vital. Try to speak to yourself with the same kindness and understanding you would offer a good friend who was struggling. It's important to remember that healing from coercive control often involves your nervous system learning to trust that you're truly safe now. This process takes time, and having flashbacks doesn't mean you're not healing or making progress. Remind yourself that flashbacks are a normal trauma response and are not a sign of weakness. What happened was not your fault.</p>
<p>It's important to experiment with these strategies to find what works best for you, as coping is a very personal journey. Building a toolkit of these options can support you when flashbacks occur. Your instinct to pause and let feelings pass is already a healthy coping mechanism, and building on that foundation with additional strategies that resonate with you can strengthen your ability to navigate these challenging moments. If these experiences are frequent or significantly impacting your daily life, you might also consider speaking with a trauma-informed therapist, as they can help you develop more personalized strategies.</p>
<p>Remember, healing is a process, and finding what helps you manage these challenging moments is a significant and powerful step. We appreciate you trusting us with this question. You are not alone.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-young-two-family-members-sexually-abused-me-though-my-memories-of-this-are-unclear-and-distant-ive-questioned-whether-these-experiences-really-happened-because-the-memories-are-so-blurry-i)

**Question:** When I was young, two family members sexually abused me, though my memories of this are unclear and distant. I've questioned whether these experiences really happened because the memories are so blurry. I also experienced other inappropriate sexual exposure during childhood, including witnessing adult sexual activity and accessing explicit content without guidance or education. During this time, I engaged in sexual exploration with a cousin close to my age, and I'm deeply worried that I may have initiated or influenced these interactions because of what happened to me. I also experienced an unwanted physical response while holding my baby sister, which has caused intense distress and intrusive thoughts. I struggle with severe self-blame and wondering if my childhood experiences made me harmful to others. How do I process these complex childhood experiences and the intense shame and confusion I feel about them?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you so much for sharing your story with us. It takes immense courage to reflect on and talk about such difficult childhood experiences, especially ones that have been causing you pain and confusion for so many years.</p>
<p>What you've shared reflects the profound complexity of childhood sexual trauma and its lasting effects. From what you've described, it's clear that you were exposed to situations and content as a child that you weren't equipped to understand or process. Being sexually abused by family members at such a young age was a profound violation of your trust and safety, and these experiences are unfortunately common among survivors. The confusion, self-doubt, and shame you're feeling are natural responses to the trauma you endured, not reflections of your character or worth as a person.</p>
<p>Memory difficulties are extremely common with childhood sexual abuse, especially when it occurs at very young ages. It's common for memories of traumatic events from early childhood to be blurry or fragmented. Our minds sometimes protect us by making these memories less accessible, and the brain's memory systems are still developing during early childhood when trauma can significantly impact how memories are stored and retrieved. The fact that your memories feel distant, confusing, or unclear doesn't mean they didn't happen or that their impact isn't significant. Many survivors experience exactly what you're describing including fragmented memories, uncertainty about details, and periods of doubting their own experiences.</p>
<p>Witnessing adult sexual activity and accessing explicit content further exposed you to material long before you were ready to understand it. Without proper guidance or sexual education, it's natural that you tried to make sense of these experiences in the only ways you knew how. The sexual behaviors you engaged in as a child, including exploration with your cousin, are actually predictable responses to premature sexual exposure and abuse. When children are sexually abused or exposed to adult sexual content, they often exhibit what professionals call "sexual behavior problems" to make sense of confusing experiences and sensations they weren't developmentally ready to understand. Children often mimic behaviors they've seen without fully grasping their meaning, especially when they've been exposed to inappropriate material or abuse.</p>
<p>The interactions with your cousin were a part of this confusing landscape, and at such a young age, neither of you had the maturity or knowledge to fully understand what you were doing. The fact that you are now worried about having initiated those interactions reflects your deep sense of responsibility and care, but it's important to remember that as a child, you were not in control of the environment or the influences around you. You were navigating a world where boundaries were not properly set or respected by the adults in your life. Your deep concern about potentially initiating sexual activity with your cousin demonstrates your moral conscience and empathy. These qualities distinguish you from those who harmed you.</p>
<p>Feeling haunted by these memories and wondering if your childhood experiences made you harmful to others is a heavy burden to bear. However, there's a fundamental difference between the actions of an adult who abuses a child and a child's innocent exploration influenced by external factors they don't understand. You were a child trying to understand the confusing and inappropriate situations you were exposed to, not someone intentionally causing harm. Children who have been sexually abused often worry they've become abusive themselves, but engaging in age-inappropriate sexual behavior as a child victim is fundamentally different from an adult choosing to abuse a child. These behaviors don't make you responsible for your abuse, nor do they make you like your abusers.</p>
<p>The unwanted physical response you experienced while holding your baby sister is another example of how natural physical responses can be misinterpreted, especially when you're already dealing with guilt and confusion from past experiences. The intrusive thoughts and physical responses you've experienced are symptoms of trauma and possibly OCD, not indicators of dangerous desires or intentions. Physical sensations can occur without any malicious intent or desire, and trauma can cause the nervous system to react in ways that feel frightening and confusing, creating unwanted physical responses that have nothing to do with your actual feelings or intentions. The fact that this caused you distress and led to obsessive thoughts shows how deeply you care about not causing harm to others. These reactions are your trauma speaking, not your true self.</p>
<p>It's understandable that you've struggled with severe self-blame, feelings of shame, guilt, and unworthiness, but these are serious symptoms that deserve professional attention. The experiences you've had and the actions you took as a child do not define your worth as a person. You were placed in situations that no child should have to navigate, and you did the best you could with the limited understanding and resources you had at the time. You are not a bad person. You are a trauma survivor whose nervous system and thought patterns have been shaped by experiences that were never your fault.</p>
<p>You deserve kindness, compassion, and forgiveness especially from yourself. Healing from such complex childhood experiences can be challenging, but it's also possible. Working with a trauma-informed therapist who specializes in childhood sexual abuse can help you process these complex experiences, develop tools for managing intrusive thoughts, and rebuild a sense of safety within yourself. You don't have to carry this burden alone, and seeking help is a sign of strength and self-care.</p>
<p>Please know that you are not alone, and you are deserving of love, happiness, and all the good things life has to offer. Healing is possible, and you deserve support, compassion, and care - especially from yourself. Thank you again for trusting us with your story.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/does-it-count-as-sexual-abuse-if-we-were-the-same-age-and-i-said-yes-but-was-pressured-through-emotional-manipulation-this-person-would-threaten-to-cry-or-never-talk-to-me-again-to-get-their-way-and-w)

**Question:** Does it count as sexual abuse if we were the same age and I said yes but was pressured through emotional manipulation? This person would threaten to cry or never talk to me again to get their way, and would imply I'd be a bad friend if I didn't comply. I said yes but didn't want to. I just did it to keep the peace. I technically consented but never really enjoyed it or wanted to. Does that count as abuse?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting us with this. What you're describing absolutely counts as sexual abuse. The fact that you were the same age doesn't diminish what happened to you. Sexual abuse between peers, including children, is real and can cause lasting impact.</p>
<p>It's important to understand the difference between normal childhood sexual exploration and harmful sexual behavior. Children naturally explore their sexuality and intimacy as part of normal development - this typically involves mutual curiosity, play that feels good to everyone involved, and interactions that stop when someone says no or seems uncomfortable. However, what you're describing goes beyond normal exploration because it involved coercion, manipulation, and your clear discomfort that wasn't respected.</p>
<p><a href="https://rainn.org/articles/what-is-consent">Consent</a> isn't just about saying "yes." It's about freely choosing to participate without pressure, manipulation, or coercion. What you're describing shows clear patterns of emotional manipulation and coercion. Using threats of abandonment, emotional withdrawal, or making you feel like a "bad friend" to pressure you into sexual activity moves this beyond normal childhood exploration into harmful behavior. True consent happens when someone genuinely wants to participate and feels free to say no without consequences or retaliation.</p>
<p>It's important to recognize that when children engage in harmful sexual behaviors, it's often complex. Children who pressure others into sexual activity may themselves be acting out trauma, abuse, or exposure to inappropriate sexual content, or they may be struggling with understanding boundaries and appropriate ways to explore their developing sexuality. This doesn't excuse the behavior or minimize your experience, but it helps us understand that childhood sexual behaviors often emerge from a child's own pain, confusion, or lack of guidance about healthy relationships.</p>
<p>Many survivors, especially children, comply with situations not because they want to, but because they feel they have no other choice or because the alternative consequences feel too scary. This doesn't make you responsible for what happened. The responsibility lies with understanding that both of you were children navigating something neither of you should have been dealing with alone, but that doesn't change the fact that you were harmed.</p>
<p>The fact that this happened repeatedly over years, that you never enjoyed it or wanted it, and that it required emotional manipulation to continue are important indicators that this was harmful to you rather than mutual exploration. Your body and mind were telling you something was wrong, even if you couldn't name it at the time. Trust those feelings. They're valid and important, even while recognizing that the situation was complicated by both of your ages and developmental stages.</p>
<p>If you're able to access counseling or support services, speaking with a trauma-informed therapist who specializes in childhood experiences could be incredibly helpful. They can help you process both the harm you experienced and the complex feelings you might have about someone who was also just a child exploring their own development at the time. Thank you again for reaching out to us. You are not alone.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/im-very-confused-about-an-incident-from-my-childhood-and-whether-it-counts-as-cocsa-child-on-child-sexual-abuse-when-i-was-under-9-during-summer-sleepovers-a-friend-told-me-that-my-older-cousin-had-ma)

**Question:** I'm very confused about an incident from my childhood and whether it counts as COCSA (Child-on-Child Sexual Abuse). When I was under 9, during summer sleepovers, a friend told me that my older cousin had made her touch his genitals while she was sleeping. A few nights later during my sleepover with him, he tried to do the same thing to me while he thought I was asleep. I kept turning away because I didn't want him to make me touch him, but I eventually fell asleep and don't remember if anything actually happened after that. I'm confused about whether this counts as assault since I don't have clear memories of him actually doing anything to me. I also feel conflicted because I knew about his previous behavior but didn't try to change or cancel the sleepover. I never told anyone about this, not even my friend who was also affected, so this has never been acknowledged. I'm not angry or sad about it, just confused about how to understand what happened. My cousin and I have a good relationship now, and I don't know if he or my friend even remember these incidents.

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting us with this. Your confusion about this experience is completely understandable, and I want you to know that your feelings and questions are valid. What you've described involves concerning sexual behavior by an older child that crossed important boundaries, regardless of whether you have complete memories of what happened to you specifically.</p>
<p>The incident with your friend clearly constitutes inappropriate sexual contact. She was touched in a sexual way without her consent while she was sleeping. The fact that your cousin attempted the same behavior with you, and that you remember actively trying to avoid his advances by turning away, indicates that unwanted sexual contact was being initiated. Your lack of complete memory about what may have happened after you fell asleep doesn't diminish the significance of what you experienced or make your concerns less valid. Memory gaps around potentially traumatic childhood experiences are actually quite common and don't determine whether something "counts" as harmful.</p>
<p>It's important to understand that none of this was your fault. Children cannot be held responsible for preventing other children's harmful sexual behavior, even when they have some awareness of previous incidents. You were younger than your cousin and in a vulnerable position. The responsibility for appropriate behavior lay with him, not with you. Your decision not to cancel the sleepover doesn't make you complicit. You were a child processing complex information and navigating a difficult situation without adult guidance or support.</p>
<p>The fact that you never told anyone about this is also completely understandable. Many children who experience sexual harm don't disclose it, especially when it involves family members or when they're unsure about what happened. Children often lack the language to describe these experiences, fear they won't be believed, worry about family disruption, or feel confused about whether what happened was "serious enough" to report. Your silence doesn't make the experience less real or significant.</p>
<p>The fact that you're not feeling angry or sad doesn't mean your experience wasn't significant or that you're "being dramatic." People respond to potentially traumatic experiences in many different ways, and confusion is a completely normal response. Some survivors don't experience strong emotional reactions immediately or ever, and this doesn't invalidate their experiences. Your current good relationship with your cousin also doesn't negate what happened in the past - many survivors maintain relationships with people who harmed them, especially family members, for various complex reasons.</p>
<p>What you experienced represents a violation of your bodily autonomy and personal boundaries during a vulnerable developmental period. Whether or not you choose to label it with specific terms is entirely up to you. What matters most is that you're taking time to process and understand your experience, which is a healthy and important step in your healing journey. Thank you for reaching out to us. You are not alone.</p>
<p>&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/im-not-sure-how-to-understand-some-experiences-i-had-with-my-older-sister-when-we-were-children-and-adolescents-when-we-were-young-before-age-9-10-my-sister-showed-me-inappropriate-content-and-we-had)

**Question:** I'm not sure how to understand some experiences I had with my older sister when we were children and adolescents. When we were young (before age 9-10), my sister showed me inappropriate content, and we had some physical contact including briefly touching tongues. My parents didn't respond helpfully when they discovered some of this. As we got older and shared a room during her high school years, there were incidents where she exposed herself to her boyfriend online while I was present in the room, and later exposed herself directly to me. Over time, boundaries around sexual content became very blurred between us, and watching or reading sexual material in the same room became normalized. I'm realizing now that this might not be typical sibling behavior, and I'm feeling scared and confused about what these experiences mean. How do I make sense of what happened, and what should I do with these feelings?

**Answer:** <p>What you've described takes courage to share, and your feelings of confusion and concern are completely understandable. It's important to acknowledge that sibling relationships can sometimes involve boundary exploration, especially during childhood, and some fluidity in understanding appropriate limits is part of normal development. However, what matters most is how these experiences have affected you and what you need moving forward.</p>
<p>Looking back at childhood experiences through an adult lens can often bring up feelings of shame, confusion, or self-blame that weren't present when you were younger. This is a common experience for many people as they gain new understanding about boundaries and relationships. It's important to remember that as a child, you didn't have the knowledge or power to understand or control these situations. Any shame you might be feeling belongs to the circumstances, not to you.</p>
<p>The experiences you've outlined involve situations where boundaries became consistently blurred over time, creating an environment where sexual content and behavior became normalized in ways that are now causing you distress. Your feelings of confusion and your recognition that something feels "irregular" are valid responses to experiences that crossed typical sibling boundaries, particularly as they continued and escalated over the years.</p>
<p>Childhood experiences like these can have lasting impacts on how we understand intimacy, boundaries, and relationships. Many people describe feeling confused about what's "normal" because their early experiences shaped their understanding in ways that feel different from others. The fact that your parents didn't respond helpfully when they discovered some of these situations likely contributed to the ongoing boundary confusion and normalization of inappropriate behavior.</p>
<p>What's most important now is honoring your current feelings and recognizing that your distress about these experiences is completely valid. Your awareness that something feels off shows healthy instincts about boundaries and relationships. Many people who have experienced similar situations find it helpful to process these experiences with a trauma-informed therapist who specializes in childhood experiences and family dynamics. They can help you understand the impact these experiences may have had and support you in developing the healthy boundaries and relationships you deserve.</p>
<p>You deserve to have your experiences understood and validated. What happened matters because it's affecting you now, and seeking support or simply taking time to process these realizations is a sign of strength. You don't have to figure this out alone, and there are resources specifically for people navigating complex family situations and childhood experiences like yours. Thank you for reaching out to us. You are not alone.</p>

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-a-child-i-had-a-sleepover-at-my-cousins-house-my-cousin-who-was-about-a-year-older-would-often-pick-on-me-during-this-sleepover-while-we-were-sharing-a-room-my-cousin-pressured-me-to-imitat)

**Question:** When I was a child, I had a sleepover at my cousin's house. My cousin, who was about a year older, would often pick on me. During this sleepover, while we were sharing a room, my cousin pressured me to imitate sexual acts with her. I said no, but she wouldn't let me sleep until I participated. The memory is really foggy. I think we kept our clothes on and I remember being made to lay on top of her at some point. I felt very uncomfortable and just wanted it to be over so I could sleep. I never told anyone about it, and I don't really know how to talk about it when I don't remember everything clearly. Recently, I've been struggling and experiencing emotional flashbacks to that night. Does it count as child-on-child sexual abuse (COCSA) if it only happened once? Is it normal to not properly remember what happened?

**Answer:** <p>I'm so sorry you're experiencing these difficult flashbacks and struggling with this painful memory. It takes courage to reach out and share something so personal, especially when the memories feel unclear. Your feelings and your experience matter, and you deserve compassion and support as you work through this.</p>
<p>Only you can define and label your own experiences. That's entirely your choice and your right. What you're describing, however, contains several concerning elements. Normal childhood sexual exploration typically involves curiosity between peers of similar ages, happens with mutual interest or agreement, and doesn't involve coercion or distress. In contrast, your experience involved you clearly saying no, being pressured until you complied, feeling uncomfortable throughout, and wanting it to stop --these are significant aspects that point toward harmful behavior rather than normal exploration.</p>
<p>The fact that this happened only once doesn't diminish its impact or significance. Harmful experiences don't need to be repeated to be valid or to cause lasting effects. It sounds like your cousin used manipulation and refused to respect your boundary when you said no, which created a coercive situation regardless of the frequency.</p>
<p>It's completely normal and very common not to remember traumatic experiences clearly, especially those that occurred in childhood. Memory gaps, foggy recollections, and fragmented memories are actually typical responses to trauma. Your brain may have protected you by not storing every detail of a distressing experience. The emotional flashbacks you're experiencing now are also a common trauma response. Your body and emotions remember the impact even when your mind doesn't retain all the specifics.</p>
<p>Your feelings about this experience are valid, regardless of how much you remember or how you choose to understand it. The neglect you were experiencing at home may have made you more vulnerable to this situation and less likely to speak up about it afterward. The fact that you're experiencing distress about this memory now is significant and worthy of attention and care.</p>
<p>These recent emotional flashbacks suggest that your mind and body are ready to process this experience now, even though it happened years ago. Consider reaching out to a trauma-informed therapist who specializes in childhood experiences. They can help you navigate these memories and feelings in a safe, supportive environment, regardless of how you ultimately choose to understand or label what happened. You deserve care and support as you work through this experience, and you don't have to carry this alone. Thank you for reaching out to us.</p>
<p>&nbsp;</p>

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-younger-i-had-a-couple-of-experiences-with-other-children-that-im-wondering-about-in-one-situation-an-older-child-exposed-himself-and-tried-to-convince-me-to-do-inappropriate-things-though)

**Question:** When I was younger, I had a couple of experiences with other children that I'm wondering about. In one situation, an older child exposed himself and tried to convince me to do inappropriate things, though I didn't. In another ongoing situation with a child closer to my age, we would find private spots to expose ourselves to each other and there was touching involved. I'm wondering if these experiences might be considered child-on-child sexual abuse (COCSA)?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out and asking about this.&nbsp;Regarding your first experience, what you described (an older child exposing himself and attempting to coerce you into sexual contact) does align with characteristics of child-on-child sexual abuse. The significant age difference (approximately 6-7 years), the coercive nature of trying to convince you to do something you did not want to do and the inappropriate sexual behavior all suggest this was abusive. The fact that you didn't comply doesn't diminish the inappropriate and harmful nature of what occurred. Your instincts protected you in that moment, and that matters.</p>
<p>The second experience you described is more complex, as many childhood sexual experiences fall into gray areas. When children of similar ages engage in sexual behaviors, it can sometimes be considered normal childhood sexual exploration, particularly when it's mutual, age-appropriate, and not coercive. Your current uncertainty about how you felt and who initiated it, may also indicate this experience was problematic in some ways.</p>
<p>It's important to understand that regardless of the specific labels we might apply to these experiences, your feelings about them are completely valid. Childhood sexual experiences, whether clearly abusive or in gray areas, can impact us in various ways. Some people experience confusion, shame, difficulty with boundaries, or challenges in relationships. Others may have few lasting effects. There's no "right" way to be affected by these experiences.</p>
<p>Your brain was still developing during these experiences, which means your memories may be fragmented or unclear. This is completely normal for childhood trauma. The confusion you feel about these events doesn't reflect any failing on your part--it reflects the complexity of childhood experiences and how our developing minds process them.</p>
<p>If these memories are causing you distress or impacting your life in ways that concern you, speaking with a trauma-informed therapist who specializes in childhood sexual experiences can be incredibly helpful. They can provide you with personalized support and help you process these experiences in a safe environment. Remember that seeking support isn't about determining whether these experiences "count" as abuse, but about caring for yourself and any impact they may have had on your life.</p>
<p>Your experiences matter, your feelings about them are valid, and you deserve support and healing regardless of how these experiences might be categorized. Thank you for trusting us with this.</p>

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/how-can-responsible-citizens-effectively-support-survivors-of-gender-based-violence-in-their-communities-675)

**Question:** How can responsible citizens effectively support survivors of gender-based violence in their communities?

**Answer:** <p>Supporting survivors of gender-based violence requires both individual awareness and collective action rooted in respect for survivor autonomy and safety. One of the most impactful strategies is becoming an informed bystander who can recognize warning signs and respond appropriately when situations arise. This means learning about the dynamics of abuse, understanding that leaving an abusive situation is often the most dangerous time for a survivor, and knowing how to offer support without judgment or pressure. When someone discloses their experience to you, believing them unconditionally and following their lead about what kind of help they want is crucial. Sometimes the most valuable support is simply listening without trying to fix or advise, while other times it might involve helping them safety plan or connecting them with professional resources like local domestic violence and sexual violence organizations or the <a href="https://www.thehotline.org/">National Domestic Violence Hotline</a>.</p>
<p>Another essential strategy involves advocating for systemic change within your community and supporting organizations that serve survivors. This can include volunteering with or donating to local shelters and advocacy organizations, supporting legislation that protects survivors and holds perpetrators accountable, and challenging harmful attitudes and behaviors when you encounter them in your social circles. Many communities also benefit from citizens who educate themselves about trauma-informed approaches and work to implement these practices in their workplaces, schools, and community organizations. Remember that supporting survivors often means supporting long-term healing and empowerment rather than expecting quick solutions, and that the most effective help centers the survivor's own knowledge of what they need to be safe and heal.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-a-child-my-sister-would-create-private-spaces-where-she-would-make-me-kiss-her-and-touch-intimate-parts-of-her-body-and-she-would-touch-me-in-similar-ways-during-bath-time-im-wondering-if-t)

**Question:** When I was a child, my sister would create private spaces where she would make me kiss her and touch intimate parts of her body, and she would touch me in similar ways during bath time. I'm wondering if this would be considered sexual assault.

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for sharing your story.&nbsp;Only you can decide how to label your own experiences, and whatever words feel right to you are valid. However, the elements you've described (for example: being made to engage in sexual touching and kissing as a child, having someone touch your intimate parts without your consent) are concerning behaviors that many people would recognize as sexual abuse. These experiences can have lasting impacts regardless of what we choose to call them.</p>
<p>It's completely understandable that your memories might be fragmented or unclear. This is actually very common with childhood trauma, especially sexual trauma. Our brains often protect us by not storing every detail of painful experiences. The fact that you don't remember everything perfectly doesn't make what happened to you any less real or significant.</p>
<p>Many survivors of childhood sexual experiences involving siblings struggle with questions about whether their experience "counts" or was "serious enough." These doubts are normal, especially when the other person involved was also a child. What happened to you was not your fault, and you deserved to be safe and protected during your childhood.</p>
<p>The impact of sibling sexual experiences can be particularly complex because it often involves someone who was also a child at the time and may have been acting out behaviors they themselves had been exposed to or experienced. Children who engage in sexual behaviors with other children are often responding to their own trauma, exposure to inappropriate sexual content, or lack of proper boundaries and education. This doesn't excuse the harm caused, but it can help explain the context. Your sister, as a child herself, may not have fully understood the impact of her actions or had the developmental capacity to recognize the harm she was causing. Many children who engage in these behaviors later feel tremendous guilt and shame about their actions as they mature and better understand appropriate boundaries.</p>
<p>This complexity can create confusing feelings of loyalty, guilt, anger, and grief that are entirely normal responses. You might find yourself feeling protective of your sister while also feeling hurt by what happened, or struggling with anger toward someone you also care about. These contradictory feelings are completely understandable and don't diminish the validity of your experience or your right to feel hurt.</p>
<p>If you're finding that these experiences are affecting your life today, please know that healing is possible. Many survivors find it helpful to work with a trauma-informed therapist who has experience with childhood sexual experiences and understands the unique dynamics of sibling situations. You deserve support, validation, and the opportunity to process these experiences in whatever way feels right for you. Thank you for trusting us with this.</p>

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-often-feel-like-a-burden-to-others-and-wish-i-could-disappear-my-trauma-happened-long-ago-and-continued-for-an-extended-period-so-it-feels-normal-to-me-now-however-i-still-encounter-the-person-who-h)

**Question:** I often feel like a burden to others and wish I could disappear. My trauma happened long ago and continued for an extended period, so it feels normal to me now. However, I still encounter the person who hurt me regularly, and each time feels like reopening a wound. Shouldn't I be over this by now? How do I begin healing?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out to us with such deeply personal feelings. I want to start by saying that it takes immense strength to name the pain you're carrying, and I'm honored that you've trusted us with your story. What you're experiencing--all of it--makes complete sense, and you are not alone in feeling this way.</p>
<p>There is no timeline for healing from trauma, and the question "shouldn't I be over this by now?" is one that nearly every survivor asks themselves. The truth is that healing is not linear, and there is no expiration date on the impact of sexual violence, especially when it occurred repeatedly over time. Your feelings are valid, your timeline is your own, and there is no "right" way to process what you've been through.</p>
<p>When sexual violence happens over an extended period, it can become deeply embedded in how our nervous system functions and how we understand ourselves. Your brain adapted to survive those experiences, which is why they may feel "normal" even though they caused you profound harm. This adaptation was actually your mind's way of protecting you, though I know it may not feel protective now.</p>
<p>Having to see the person who hurt you regularly adds an incredibly painful dimension to your healing journey. Each encounter can activate your trauma response and bring all those feelings flooding back as if the harm just happened yesterday. This isn't a sign that you're not healing. It's your nervous system trying to keep you safe. Your body remembers, and that's not something you can simply will away.</p>
<p>Those feelings of being a burden or wanting to disappear are trauma responses that many survivors carry. They often stem from the shame and self-blame that trauma can create, but I need you to hear this clearly: you are not a burden. You are a human being who has survived something unimaginable, and you deserve love, support, and space to heal. The voice telling you otherwise is trauma speaking, not truth.</p>
<p>If you're having thoughts of wanting to disappear or harm yourself, please know that support is available right now. You can call or text 988 to reach the <a href="https://988lifeline.org/">Suicide &amp; Crisis Lifeline</a>, where trained counselors are available 24/7 to listen and help. For support specifically related to sexual violence, the <a href="https://rainn.org/resources">National Sexual Assault Hotline</a> at 1-800-656-4673 provides free, confidential support around the clock and can connect you with local resources.</p>
<p>Healing is possible, even when it feels impossible. It often begins with finding safe spaces and people who truly understand trauma. A trauma-informed therapist who specializes in sexual violence can help you process these experiences at your own pace and develop tools for managing the difficult moments. Many survivors find approaches like <a href="https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/22641-emdr-therapy">EMDR</a>, <a href="https://www.health.harvard.edu/blog/what-is-somatic-therapy-202307072951">somatic therapy</a>, or <a href="https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/therapy-types/trauma-focused-cognitive-behavior-therapy?msockid=1103576693d9679310b645d892bb66b2">trauma-focused cognitive behavioral therapy </a>particularly helpful in addressing the way trauma lives in both mind and body.</p>
<p>You've already taken a big step by reaching out and asking how to heal. That courage lives within you, even on the hardest days when you feel small and broken. Healing is not about forgetting or "getting over" what happened--it's about learning to carry your experiences in a way that allows you to live fully and authentically again. You deserve support, patience, and all the time you need to reclaim your sense of safety and worth. Thank you again for trusting us. You are not alone.</p>
<p>&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/im-starting-to-remember-an-incident-from-when-i-was-younger-i-was-at-my-uncles-house-with-my-cousins-and-a-family-friend-who-was-younger-than-me-the-memory-is-kind-of-hazy-but-i-remember-we-were-watch)

**Question:** I'm starting to remember an incident from when I was younger. I was at my uncle's house with my cousins and a family friend who was younger than me. The memory is kind of hazy, but I remember we were watching pornography on my phone for a little while before I turned it off. Afterwards he kept talking about how he was aroused and asked me to help him. I said no at first, but he kept asking and saying please so I gave in. I touched his private area with my hand for a little while until I felt nauseous about what I was doing, so I stopped and asked him if we could forget about it. This memory keeps coming back, and I feel in the wrong since I was older, like I should've known better. I feel like some sort of creep now. Did I abuse him?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out to us. What you're experiencing --the confusion, guilt, and questioning --is actually very common for survivors who are processing childhood experiences. It's important to understand that what you've described doesn't sound like abuse on your part, but rather an inappropriate situation that involved two children who were both too young to fully understand the implications of sexual activity.</p>
<p>The feelings you're having are understandable, but they don't reflect the reality of what happened. At your age then, you were still a child yourself, and children naturally explore and are curious about sexuality as part of normal development. However, what made this situation problematic wasn't your actions, but the circumstances. Children of your ages engaging in sexual activity, regardless of the age difference, indicates that appropriate boundaries and education weren't in place.</p>
<p>The fact that this other child persisted after you initially said no, and that you felt pressured to comply, suggests that you may have been experiencing coercion yourself. Children don't have the emotional, psychological, or developmental capacity to consent to sexual activities, which is why these situations are considered harmful regardless of who initiates them. The power dynamics between children are complex and don't operate the same way as adult relationships.</p>
<p>Your feelings of nausea and wanting to stop show that your instincts were telling you something was wrong. That you stopped when you felt uncomfortable demonstrates your own internal sense of boundaries, even as a child. The shame and guilt you're carrying now are unfortunately common responses, but they don't mean you did something wrong or that you're responsible for the situation.</p>
<p>It might be helpful to speak with a trauma-informed counselor who specializes in childhood experiences. They can help you process these memories without judgment and work through the complex feelings you're experiencing. Remember that healing is possible, and you deserve support and compassion --especially from yourself. Thank you for trusting us with this.&nbsp;</p>

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-a-young-child-i-would-kiss-my-grandmother-on-the-lips-with-tongue-she-never-stopped-me-or-said-it-was-wrong-just-made-comments-about-how-the-boys-would-love-me-when-i-got-older-i-never-thou)

**Question:** When I was a young child, I would kiss my grandmother on the lips with tongue. She never stopped me or said it was wrong, just made comments about how "the boys would love me" when I got older. I never thought this was strange until recently when I told a friend, and they said it was very wrong and she shouldn't have allowed it. I didn't think it affected me, but I started crying when I typed this out. I don't think it was abuse, just that it was wrong, but I'm struggling to process it. I understand she should have stopped me, but I feel like it was my fault because I initiated it. How do I make sense of these feelings and this experience?

**Answer:** <p>I'm so sorry to hear that you're experiencing these confusing and painful emotions.&nbsp;Your tears when writing this out tell you something important. Your body and emotions are responding to a recognition that something wasn't right. That reaction is meaningful and deserves to be honored.</p>
<p>What you're describing involved inappropriate physical contact that an adult should have redirected. As a young child, you were naturally exploring ways to show affection, which is completely normal childhood behavior. Children often test boundaries or mimic what they see as part of their development. The responsibility to guide appropriate behavior and maintain healthy boundaries always belongs to the adults, never to the child.</p>
<p>Your grandmother should have gently redirected this behavior toward appropriate expressions of affection. Instead, she allowed it to continue and made comments connecting your childhood behavior to future romantic relationships. This was inappropriate and harmful, regardless of her intentions. Adults have a responsibility to protect children's innocence and guide their understanding of appropriate physical boundaries.</p>
<p>The confusion and self-blame you're experiencing are common responses when people begin to reexamine childhood experiences. Many people wonder if they somehow caused inappropriate situations, but children cannot be responsible for adult failures to maintain proper boundaries. Your natural childhood affection was normal. The failure was in how it was handled by the adult.</p>
<p>Processing these realizations often brings complex emotions. Some people find it helpful to work with a trauma-informed therapist who understands childhood boundary violations. Others benefit from learning about child development, connecting with support resources, or practicing self-care when emotions feel intense.</p>
<p>You're taking an important step by examining this experience and trusting your emotional responses. Many people successfully work through similar childhood experiences and find ways to move forward while honoring their own healing process. Thank you for trusting us with this.&nbsp;</p>

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-have-blurry-memories-from-when-i-was-a-young-girl-that-always-make-me-uncomfortable-and-anxious-during-a-time-when-my-parents-were-divorced-i-lived-with-my-father-who-was-dating-a-woman-with-two-chi)

**Question:** I have blurry memories from when I was a young girl that always make me uncomfortable and anxious. During a time when my parents were divorced, I lived with my father who was dating a woman with two children. We often had sleepovers, and I would spend time with her son who was close to my age. He frequently brought up sex-related topics when we were alone, taught me about sex using our toys despite my discomfort, and insisted on showing me his genitals during bath time when adults weren't supervising. He only did these things when grown-ups weren't around, and I told him to stop but he didn't listen. Although there was no physical contact, could what I experienced have been COCSA? I feel disgusted and ashamed about it today as an adult, even though I know I did nothing wrong.

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out and trusting us with this. The behaviors you're describing sound like significant boundary violations that were inappropriate and harmful. Several aspects of your experience align with what some might consider <a href="https://www.ourwave.org/post/child-on-child-sexual-abuse-cocsa-frequently-asked-questions-and-considerations">childhood-on-childhood sexual abuse </a>(COCSA). For example, the deliberate timing when adults weren't present, the continuation of these behaviors despite your clear discomfort and requests to stop, and the introduction of sexual content to someone who had no prior exposure to it.</p>
<p>Only you can determine how to label or understand your experiences. For some survivors, having a name like COCSA for what happened can be validating and helpful in understanding their reactions and finding appropriate resources. For others, labels might feel limiting or not quite right for their specific situation. What matters most is how these experiences affected you and continue to impact you today, regardless of what term might be applied to them.</p>
<p>The fact that you felt uncomfortable at the time and continue to experience distress about these memories as an adult speaks to the real impact these experiences had on you. Your body and mind recognized that something wasn't right, even when you were too young to fully understand what was happening. It's completely understandable that you feel disgusted and ashamed, even while knowing intellectually that you did nothing wrong. These feelings are common trauma responses and don't reflect anything about your character or actions. You were a child who was placed in an uncomfortable situation by another child who violated your boundaries repeatedly.</p>
<p>The blurry nature of your memories is also very typical of childhood trauma. Our minds sometimes protect us by making difficult memories less clear or accessible. This doesn't make your experiences less valid or real. Your emotional responses to these memories are important indicators that something significant happened that affected you deeply.</p>
<p>If you're experiencing ongoing distress from these memories, you deserve support in processing these experiences. Speaking with a trauma-informed therapist who understands childhood sexual experiences could be helpful in working through the feelings these memories bring up and developing coping strategies that feel right for you. You are not alone.</p>
