**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/if-i-kissed-other-children-without-consent-when-i-was-a-child-because-i-didnt-know-it-was-wrong-and-thought-it-was-normal-did-i-commit-sexual-assault-i-was-exposed-to-sexual-content-when-young-and-tho)

**Question:** If I kissed other children without consent when I was a child because I didn't know it was wrong and thought it was normal, did I commit sexual assault? I was exposed to sexual content when young and thought kissing was something people just did. I didn't pressure anyone but didn't ask for consent either. I would just peck other children on the mouth for no reason. I'm unsure if this counts as sexual assault or childhood experimentation. I hate myself for this and feel disgusted by my actions.

**Answer:** <p>I can hear how much pain and self-disgust you're carrying about these childhood memories, and I want you to know that your distress is understandable while also being much harsher on yourself than the situation warrants. What you're describing falls clearly within the realm of normal childhood development and experimentation, not sexual assault.</p>
<p>Sexual assault involves intentional harm, power dynamics, coercion, or actions taken with knowledge that they're wrong or harmful. What you're describing is a young child who was exposed to adult content beyond your developmental understanding, trying to make sense of what you saw by mimicking behaviors you thought were normal. This is actually a very common response when children are exposed to sexual content they're not developmentally ready to process. They often try to recreate what they've seen because they don't yet understand the context or boundaries around those behaviors.</p>
<p>The key factors that distinguish your experience from sexual assault include your age and developmental stage, your lack of intent to harm, your genuine belief that this was normal behavior, and the absence of power dynamics or coercion. Young children naturally explore social behaviors through imitation, and when they're exposed to content beyond their understanding, they may incorporate those behaviors into their play or social interactions without any awareness of inappropriate boundaries.</p>
<p>Your early exposure to sexual content was not your fault, and the way you processed and responded to that exposure as a child was a normal developmental response to abnormal circumstances. Children who are exposed to sexual content often exhibit sexualized behaviors because they're trying to understand and integrate what they've witnessed. This doesn't make them perpetrators. It makes them children who needed appropriate guidance and protection that they didn't receive at the time.</p>
<p>The intense shame and self-hatred you're experiencing is actually causing you more harm than the original childhood behaviors ever did. This level of self-condemnation is disproportionate to actions that were developmentally normal responses to inappropriate exposure. Many adults look back on childhood behaviors with some embarrassment or concern, but the extreme distress you're feeling suggests that these intrusive thoughts may have taken on a life of their own.</p>
<p>It's important to understand that sexual assault requires intent, knowledge of wrongdoing, or exploitation of power differences, none of which were present in your childhood situation. You were a child trying to navigate social interactions with incomplete and inappropriate information about what was normal. The fact that you now understand boundaries and consent, and feel genuine concern about these past actions, actually demonstrates your healthy moral development.</p>
<p>Consider speaking with a trauma-informed therapist who specializes in childhood development and can help you process both the original exposure to sexual content and the current shame you're carrying. They can help you understand child development, work through the impacts of early sexual exposure, and develop healthier ways to view your childhood self. You deserve compassion and understanding, especially from yourself, rather than the harsh self-judgment you're currently experiencing. Thank you for trusting us with this.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/im-struggling-with-some-unclear-memories-from-when-i-was-in-primary-school-and-wondering-if-i-may-have-committed-cocsa-child-on-child-sexual-assault-i-have-vague-memories-of-kissing-a-boy-without-cons)

**Question:** I'm struggling with some unclear memories from when I was in primary school and wondering if I may have committed COCSA (Child-on-child sexual assault). I have vague memories of kissing a boy without consent (a teacher supposedly saw and told me to stop) and kissing a distant cousin without asking, but these memories are very blurry unlike my usual detailed recall. I'm not sure if these memories are real or not, and I'm scared and don't know what to do or how to find out if I actually did these things.

**Answer:** <p>What you're experiencing sounds incredibly distressing, thank you for trusting us with this. The anxiety and confusion you're feeling about unclear childhood memories is actually quite common. You're not alone in grappling with these difficult questions.</p>
<p>Childhood sexual behaviors exist on a spectrum, and many behaviors that occur between children of similar ages are considered normal developmental exploration rather than sexual assault. Young children are naturally curious about bodies and may engage in behaviors like kissing, touching, or "playing doctor" as part of normal development. These behaviors typically happen between children of similar ages and developmental stages, involve curiosity rather than power or coercion, and stop when adults redirect them.</p>
<p>The memories you're describing sound like they may fall into this category of childhood exploration, especially given that you were in primary school at the time. Children this age are still learning about boundaries, consent, and appropriate behavior. When adults intervene in these situations, it's typically to teach and redirect rather than to punish, which aligns with what you described about the teacher telling you to stop.</p>
<p>Memory is inherently unreliable, particularly for childhood events and especially when we're experiencing anxiety or intrusive thoughts about them. Our minds can construct scenarios based on fears, things we've heard about, or fragments of different experiences. The distress you're feeling about these potential memories can actually make them feel more real and significant than they may actually be.</p>
<p>What's most important right now is addressing the anxiety and distress these thoughts are causing you. This kind of intrusive worry about past actions is something many people experience, and it doesn't reflect who you are as a person today. Consider speaking with a counselor or therapist who can help you work through these concerns in a supportive environment. They can help you understand the difference between childhood developmental behaviors and harmful actions, and provide strategies for managing intrusive thoughts and anxiety.</p>
<p>Remember that your current concern and distress about the possibility of having caused harm actually demonstrates your moral compass and empathy. People who commit intentional sexual violence typically don't experience this kind of genuine worry about their past actions. Your care and concern for others is evident in your question, and that speaks to your character today. Thank you for tarusting us with this. You are not alone.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/is-it-possible-for-childhood-sexual-experiences-to-affect-me-as-an-adult-i-had-some-confusing-sexual-experiences-as-a-child-with-neighborhood-kids-before-i-really-understood-sexuality-i-developed-what)

**Question:** Is it possible for childhood sexual experiences to affect me as an adult? I had some confusing sexual experiences as a child with neighborhood kids before I really understood sexuality. I developed what I think might be hypersexuality and have struggled with compulsive sexual behaviors, including viewing pornography from a young age. I feel like I can't be loved without sexual undertones, and I've engaged in sexual behaviors online to fill an emotional void. Early puberty also affected how I feel about my body and being female. I'm confused about these experiences and feel ashamed. I don't know who I am without this trauma. How can I bring up sexual trauma with my therapist in a way that feels less awkward?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for trusting us with this question. Your childhood experiences can absolutely have lasting effects into adulthood, and what you're describing makes complete sense from a trauma perspective. When children are exposed to sexual experiences before they have the cognitive and emotional development to understand them, it can profoundly impact how they relate to their bodies, sexuality, and relationships throughout their lives.</p>
<p>The confusion you're feeling about these early experiences is completely normal. Children naturally seek connection and may find these interactions pleasurable or special without understanding their inappropriate nature. Your brain was simply trying to make sense of experiences it wasn't developmentally ready for. The fact that you "never said no" doesn't mean anything was your fault. Children don't have the same understanding of boundaries, consent, or the power to enforce them that adults do.</p>
<p><a href="https://ptsdinfo.org/hypersexuality-and-trauma/">Hypersexuality</a> often develops as a way of coping with early sexual trauma. Your brain may have learned to associate sexual behavior with connection, validation, or a way to feel in control. The compulsive sexual behaviors you describe, including online activities, can serve as attempts to fill emotional needs or recreate familiar patterns, even when they don't ultimately provide the connection you're seeking.</p>
<p>Your feelings about your body and femininity being "inherently sexual" reflect how early sexualization can distort your relationship with your own identity. Many survivors struggle with feeling like their worth or identity is primarily sexual, which can make it difficult to experience non-sexual intimacy and connection.</p>
<p>Regarding your therapist, you can start the conversation by saying something like "I've been dealing with some things from my childhood that I think might be affecting me now, and I'd like to talk about them." You could mention that some of these experiences were sexual in nature and ask if they feel comfortable working with you on these issues. If they do not, they can help refer you to someone who specializes in sexual trauma.</p>
<p>Remember that healing is possible. Many people who experienced similar childhood confusion and trauma have found ways to develop healthy relationships with their sexuality and sense of self. Working with a trauma-informed therapist can help you process these experiences, understand their impact, and develop healthier coping strategies. You deserve support and healing, and taking this step to seek help shows tremendous courage. Thank you for reaching out to us. You are not alone.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-in-7th-grade-a-boy-who-knew-i-had-a-crush-on-him-would-make-sexual-jokes-around-me-and-ask-inappropriate-sexual-questions-he-would-offer-me-money-or-snacks-to-get-me-to-answer-these-questio)

**Question:** When I was in 7th grade, a boy who knew I had a crush on him would make sexual jokes around me and ask inappropriate sexual questions. He would offer me money or snacks to get me to answer these questions. I eventually answered because I wanted him to stop asking. I'm wondering if this could be considered grooming, even though we were the same age.

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out to us. What you experienced was absolutely not okay, and I want you to know that your feelings about this situation are completely valid. While the term "<a href="https://theconversation.com/grooming-an-expert-explains-what-it-is-and-how-to-identify-it-181573">grooming</a>" is typically used to describe behaviors by adults toward minors, what you're describing involves harmful sexual behavior that created pressure and discomfort for you.</p>
<p>This boy used tactics that sound manipulative and coercive. He persistently asked inappropriate sexual questions despite your clear discomfort, offered rewards to pressure you into complying, and specifically targeted you when others weren't around. These are concerning behaviors that created an unsafe environment for you. The fact that you eventually answered his questions to make him stop shows how effective his pressure tactics were, not that you did anything wrong.</p>
<p>It's important to understand that adolescents are still developing emotionally, socially, and cognitively. During middle school years, young people are often exploring their developing sexuality while still learning about boundaries, consent, and appropriate social interactions. Many adolescents receive limited education about healthy relationships and may model behaviors they've seen in media, online, or from peers without fully understanding the impact of their actions. This developmental context doesn't excuse harmful behavior, but it helps explain why some young people might engage in sexually inappropriate conduct without fully grasping the consequences.</p>
<p>However, developmental considerations don't diminish the reality of your experience or make his behavior acceptable. Peer-to-peer sexual harassment and coercion can be just as harmful as adult-to-minor dynamics, even when the people involved are the same age. Young people can absolutely engage in sexually harmful behaviors toward their peers, and these experiences can have lasting impacts. Your instinct that something wasn't right about this situation is absolutely correct.</p>
<p>You were not responsible for his behavior, and responding to his pressure doesn't mean you consented or wanted this interaction. You were trying to cope with an uncomfortable situation in the best way you knew how at the time. Many survivors describe similar experiences of compliance as a way to end unwanted attention or pressure.</p>
<p>The impact of experiences like this can vary greatly from person to person. Some people find it helpful to process these experiences with a counselor who specializes in trauma or sexual violence, while others find support through trusted friends, family members, or support groups. Whatever feels right for your healing journey is valid, and you deserve support and care as you process this experience. Thank you for trusting us with that. You are not alone.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/im-starting-therapy-with-a-new-therapist-and-filled-out-a-lengthy-intake-form-that-has-left-me-feeling-overwhelmed-seeing-my-family-history-and-experiences-written-out-there-was-abuse-and-predatory-be)

**Question:** I'm starting therapy with a new therapist and filled out a lengthy intake form that has left me feeling overwhelmed seeing my family history and experiences written out. There was abuse and predatory behavior from multiple men in my extended family, and I've typically stayed surface-level when discussing this with previous therapists. My hypersexuality as a result of generational trauma and my own experiences leaves me feeling ashamed, and I'm scared of others seeing that part of me. Since my sister shared her own childhood experiences a few years ago, my mental health has declined significantly and my body feels affected. I feel like I'm constantly on the verge of panic attacks and don't know how to regulate myself. I'm worried that starting deeper therapeutic work will make things worse and I won't be able to function as an adult because the anxiety feels crippling. What is happening to me and why do I feel so out of control right now?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you so much for trusting us with this question. Everything you're feeling right now makes complete sense given what you've survived and what you're facing.</p>
<p>It sounds like for so long, your mind may have protected you by keeping traumatic experiences separated and manageable, allowing you to function day-to-day. Now, as you approach this therapeutic relationship, your body might be recognizing that you could finally be safe enough to feel the full weight of what you've carried. Your nervous system therefore is shifting from protective numbness into a more activated state, which can feel like constant panic. This shift means your body is preparing to heal rather than just survive, even though it feels frightening.</p>
<p>When you saw everything written out on that intake form, your brain may have started putting fragmented pieces together for the first time. This could feel overwhelming because protective walls are breaking down, but it might also signal that you feel safe enough to begin real healing. It seems like your body has been the keeper of these experiences, and seeing them acknowledged feels like a flood of validation and terror all at once.</p>
<p>I urge you to meet your shame about hypersexuality with gentleness. When early experiences of connection become confused with harm, our developing mind creates sexual responses as ways to find connection, reclaim control, or manage overwhelming feelings. Your brain might have been rewiring itself to find safety and comfort in whatever ways were available. This reflects how creative your system was to survive, rather than anything wrong with who you are.</p>
<p>When your sister shared her experiences, it sounds like it may have shattered remaining illusions about your family while validating your own perceptions. This kind of revelation might make your body start feeling the full impact of stored trauma because you're no longer carrying it alone. The decline in your mental health could be your system finally having permission to feel what happened because the defense of "maybe I was the only one" has been removed.</p>
<p>Your fear about therapy making things harder sounds like it comes from real wisdom. What you're experiencing now - the constant panic, feeling out of control - might be signs that your trauma is already demanding attention. It could be that your nervous system can no longer keep everything contained the same way, which often happens when we feel safe enough to begin healing. As you process trauma, parts of your brain that were disconnected might begin talking to each other more, which could temporarily intensify symptoms while your system learns new ways of handling these experiences.</p>
<p>What's happening might not be you falling apart - it could be your system finally trusting that you don't have to hold everything together alone. You might be beginning the process of moving from surviving to healing, which could involve changing patterns in place for years. This might feel overwhelming, but it could reflect your body's wisdom in feeling ready for deeper work.</p>
<p>The anxiety you're experiencing is not irrational - it is your body responding appropriately to both past trauma and anticipating processing it in new ways. Learning breathing techniques and grounding exercises could help you feel more connected to your own strength during this process. You get to control the pace of your healing completely. Working with a good therapist might mean they'll help you develop ways to manage the intensity while respecting your body's wisdom about what feels safe. Having difficult days doesn't necessarily mean you're going backward - healing might not be a straight line.</p>
<p>You've survived incredible harm and shown remarkable strength. The fact that you're reaching toward healing while feeling scared speaks to your courage. Healing is possible with time and you are taking an important first step. Thank you for trusting us with this. You are not alone.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/is-it-appropriate-for-a-cocsa-child-on-child-sexual-abuse-survivor-to-use-the-csa-child-sexual-abuse-survivor-label-or-should-i-specifically-use-the-cocsa-label-i-often-feel-guilty-using-the-csa-survi)

**Question:** Is it appropriate for a COCSA (child-on-child sexual abuse) survivor to use the CSA (child sexual abuse) survivor label, or should I specifically use the COCSA label? I often feel guilty using the CSA survivor label because others have told me I shouldn't use it since my experiences "weren't as bad" or are different from adult-perpetrated abuse. While I sometimes dismiss my own experiences, COCSA is a form of CSA that has impacted my life similarly to other survivors. I'm confused about whether using the CSA label would be inappropriate.

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for sharing your thoughts and feelings about this with us. Your feelings of confusion about terminology are completely understandable and reflect a common struggle many survivors face when trying to name their experiences. What you experienced as a child was sexual abuse, regardless of the age of the person who harmed you. COCSA (child-on-child sexual abuse) is indeed a recognized form of child sexual abuse, not a separate or lesser category. The "CSA" in COCSA literally stands for child sexual abuse, affirming that your experiences fall under this broader umbrella.</p>
<p>The voices telling you that your experiences "weren't as bad" or that you shouldn't use certain labels are reflecting harmful misconceptions about sexual abuse. There is no hierarchy of trauma that determines whose experiences are "bad enough" to warrant support, healing, or appropriate terminology. Research consistently shows that COCSA can have profound and lasting impacts on survivors, including effects on relationships, self-esteem, sexuality, and mental health that mirror those experienced by survivors of adult-perpetrated abuse.</p>
<p>Labels can be incredibly helpful for some survivors as they provide validation, help connect with others who share similar experiences, and offer a framework for understanding what happened. For others, labels may feel limiting or unnecessary. You are the only person who has the right to decide how to describe your own experiences. Your right to identify with the CSA survivor community is not something that can be taken away by others' opinions about what qualifies as "real" abuse. Many COCSA survivors find connection and healing within broader CSA survivor spaces, while others prefer more specific communities or choose not to use labels at all. All of these choices are valid.</p>
<p>You are not appropriating anything by using the CSA label &ndash; you are accurately describing your lived experience. The tendency to minimize your own experiences is a common trauma response, but it doesn't reflect the reality of what you endured or your legitimate place in survivor communities. Trust your own understanding of what happened to you and what language, if any, serves your healing best. Thank you or reaching out to us.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/how-can-cyberbullying-targeting-lgbtqia-individuals-cause-emotional-harm-and-what-are-the-specific-impacts-this-type-of-online-harassment-can-have-on-mental-health-and-wellbeing-679)

**Question:** How can cyberbullying targeting LGBTQIA+ individuals cause emotional harm, and what are the specific impacts this type of online harassment can have on mental health and wellbeing?

**Answer:** <p>I'm sorry to hear that members of the LGBTQIA+ community are facing cyberbullying. Cyberbullying can have a profoundly negative impact on anyone, but it can be especially damaging for individuals who are already navigating the challenges of understanding and embracing their identity.<br /><br />For LGBTQIA+ individuals, the internet often serves as a vital space for finding community, support, and acceptance, especially if they lack these connections in their offline lives. When cyberbullying occurs, it can shatter that sense of safety and belonging. Hurtful messages, harassment, and discrimination online can lead to feelings of isolation, rejection, and fear.<br /><br />The emotional damage caused by cyberbullying can manifest in various ways. It may contribute to increased anxiety, depression, and stress. Constant exposure to negative comments or hateful language can erode self-esteem and self-worth, making it harder for individuals to feel confident and secure in themselves. This can interfere with their ability to form healthy relationships and pursue opportunities that bring joy and fulfillment.<br /><br />Moreover, cyberbullying can reinforce internalized negative beliefs or fears that some LGBTQIA+ individuals may already be grappling with due to societal stigma. It might make them hesitant to express themselves openly or seek support, further deepening feelings of loneliness.<br /><br />It's important to recognize that these responses are valid reactions to a painful experience. No one deserves to be treated poorly because of who they are. Reaching out to supportive friends, family members, or organizations dedicated to helping LGBTQIA+ individuals can provide comfort and assistance. Connecting with others who understand and affirm their identity can make a significant difference in coping with the effects of cyberbullying.<br /><br />Remember, kindness and understanding can have a powerful impact. Everyone deserves to feel safe and respected, both online and offline.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/im-feeling-invalidated-after-being-raped-the-prosecution-has-refused-to-move-forward-with-my-case-and-hasnt-gotten-my-rape-kit-results-back-yet-i-feel-silenced-and-pushed-aside-what-can-i-do-from-here)

**Question:** I'm feeling invalidated after being raped. The prosecution has refused to move forward with my case and hasn't gotten my rape kit results back yet. I feel silenced and pushed aside. What can I do from here?

**Answer:** <p>I'm so deeply sorry for what you're going through. The pain you're feeling right now - that sense of being invalidated, silenced, and pushed aside - is heartbreaking and so unfair. You came forward with tremendous courage, you trusted a system that was supposed to support you, and instead you've been met with what feels like indifference and abandonment. That's not just disappointing; it's wrong, and your anger and hurt about this are completely justified.</p>
<p>What's happening to you is unfortunately far too common, but knowing that doesn't make your individual pain any less real or significant. You deserved better from the very beginning, and you continue to deserve better now. The way you're being treated is adding trauma on top of trauma, and it's completely understandable that you're feeling lost and questioning what to do next.</p>
<p>First, please know that the prosecution's refusal to move forward says absolutely nothing about the truth of what happened to you or your worth as a person. The criminal justice system is deeply flawed when it comes to sexual violence, and their decision reflects those systemic failures, not the reality of your experience. You know what happened to you, and that truth remains unchanged regardless of what prosecutors decide to do with your case.</p>
<p>The fact that they haven't even gotten your rape kit results back yet adds another layer of harm to what you've already endured. This evidence represents your body, your trauma, and your hope for justice. You have every right to be furious about this. You can contact the crime lab directly to demand information about the status of testing, and victim advocacy organizations can help you push for answers and action.</p>
<p>I want you to know that while the criminal legal system has failed you, there are other paths forward that might feel more empowering and healing. Civil litigation operates under different standards and can sometimes provide a sense of justice and accountability when criminal cases don't move forward. In civil court, you have more control over the process, and the standard of proof is lower, focusing on what's more likely true rather than certainty beyond a reasonable doubt.</p>
<p>Many survivors also find healing and a sense of justice through restorative approaches that center your needs and healing rather than punishment. These might include community accountability processes, facilitated dialogues if and when you're ready, or healing circles that focus on your restoration and the community's response to harm. The power in these approaches is that you get to define what accountability and healing look like for you.</p>
<p>Your voice and your story have power, and many survivors find that advocacy and activism become transformative forms of both healing and justice. When you're ready - and only when you're ready - sharing your experience can help other survivors feel less alone and can push for the systemic changes that are so desperately needed. Whether that's through speaking out, supporting other survivors, working with organizations to change policies, or engaging in community education, your experience can become a force for change.</p>
<p>Creative expression offers another pathway for processing this pain and reclaiming your voice. Writing, art, music, or other creative outlets allow you to tell your story on your own terms and can serve as both personal healing and public testimony. Some survivors find that transforming their pain into art helps them feel less powerless and gives them a way to connect with others who understand.</p>
<p>Right now, though, your healing needs to be the priority. Connecting with a trauma-informed counselor who understands sexual violence can provide you with a safe space to process not just the original trauma, but this secondary trauma of being failed by the system. Support groups with other survivors can help you feel less isolated and remind you that you're not alone in this struggle. Building a network of people who believe you, support you, and validate your experience is crucial for your healing.</p>
<p>You didn't deserve what happened to you, and you don't deserve how you're being treated now. The system's failure is not your failure. Your worth, your truth, and your right to healing and justice remain intact, even when institutions fail you. You have the right to define what justice and healing look like in your own life, and there are people and resources ready to support you in whatever path feels right for you. Your voice matters, your experience matters, and you matter. Thank you for trusting us.</p>

---

I'm tormented by feelings of guilt and fear that I've caused harm. Am I an abuser? Should I seek help or talk to someone about this? **Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/my-cousin-and-i-were-similar-ages-she-was-one-year-older-and-she-would-have-me-touch-and-kiss-her-and-try-to-touch-me-too-i-didnt-refuse-but-felt-manipulated-and-scared-that-if-anyone-found-out-i-woul)

**Question:** My cousin and I were similar ages (she was one year older) and she would have me touch and kiss her and try to touch me too. I didn't refuse but felt manipulated and scared that if anyone found out I would be hated by my family and parents. Is this considered child-on-child sexual abuse (COCSA)?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out to us about this. When children engage in sexual activities, there are many factors that help determine whether the interactions were harmful, including things like coercion, manipulation, power imbalances, or significant differences in knowledge or development between the children involved.</p>
<p>The feelings you describe - feeling manipulated and scared about potential family rejection - are important indicators of how these experiences affected you. Even though you didn't say no, the absence of refusal doesn't mean you felt comfortable or that genuine consent was present. Children often comply with situations they're uncomfortable with due to confusion, fear, or feeling powerless to resist, and this doesn't make them responsible for what happened.</p>
<p>Your fear about family reaction could stem from different sources - it might reflect an intuitive sense that something wasn't appropriate, or it could simply be the natural secrecy children feel around any sexual behavior, even harmless exploration. Many children instinctively know that sexual activities are private adult matters, so feeling scared about discovery doesn't necessarily indicate wrongdoing by either child involved.</p>
<p>It's important to approach these childhood experiences with compassion for all children involved. Your cousin was also very young and may have been acting out behaviors she learned elsewhere, possibly from her own exposure to inappropriate content or experiences. Children who initiate sexual contact with other children are often responding to their own confusion, curiosity, or even trauma, rather than acting with malicious intent. This doesn't minimize your experience or feelings, but recognizing the complexity can sometimes help reduce feelings of anger or blame.</p>
<p>Regardless of how you choose to understand or label these experiences (<a href="https://www.ourwave.org/post/child-on-child-sexual-abuse-cocsa-frequently-asked-questions-and-considerations">COCSA</a> or otherwise), your feelings about what happened matter deeply. If these interactions left you feeling violated, confused, or harmed, those feelings deserve acknowledgment and care. Many people find it helpful to process these experiences with a trauma-informed therapist who understands childhood sexual experiences and can provide a safe space to explore your feelings without judgment. You deserve support and healing, and the label that feels most accurate to you is the one that matters most in your healing journey. Thank you for trusting us with this. We appreciate you.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/im-struggling-with-intrusive-thoughts-and-memories-that-are-causing-me-distress-about-my-past-sexual-behaviors-and-fantasies-when-i-was-younger-i-engaged-in-sexual-play-with-other-children-and-had-fan)

**Question:** I'm struggling with intrusive thoughts and memories that are causing me distress about my past sexual behaviors and fantasies. When I was younger, I engaged in sexual play with other children and had fantasies involving fictional characters. I've been experiencing what I think might be POCD (Pedophile OCD) for several months, and recently more memories have resurfaced that are making me question my identity and worry about my attractions. I'm exhausted from analyzing my thoughts and feelings, and I'm scared about what this means about who I am. I can't feel normal attraction anymore because I'm constantly worried about inappropriate attractions.  I feel like a psychopath for hardly caring about any of my values I used to and continuously thinking about all of this instead. How can I understand what's happening to me and find a way forward?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out to us. What you're experiencing sounds incredibly overwhelming and exhausting, and it's understandable that you're feeling confused and distressed. The intrusive thoughts and constant self-analysis you're describing could be consistent with POCD (<a href="https://www.treatmyocd.com/blog/pocd-symtoms-and-treatment">Pedophile Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder</a>), which is a form of OCD where someone experiences unwanted, distressing thoughts about potential harm to children, leading to excessive checking, analyzing, and mental rituals.&nbsp;However, only a qualified mental health professional can properly assess and determine what you're experiencing, as many conditions can present with similar symptoms.</p>
<p class="whitespace-normal break-words">It's important to understand that childhood sexual behaviors between peers of similar ages are actually quite common and are typically part of normal sexual development and curiosity, not indicative of future problematic behavior. Children and adolescents naturally explore sexuality through play, fantasy, and interaction with peers, and this exploration doesn't predict adult sexual interests or behaviors. The guilt and shame you're carrying about these childhood experiences may be disproportionate to what actually occurred.</p>
<p class="whitespace-normal break-words">Your description of hypersexuality following early trauma is also very common. Many trauma survivors develop patterns of sexual preoccupation or compulsive sexual thoughts as a way of processing their experiences. This doesn't make you dangerous or predatory - it makes you someone whose developing sexuality was impacted by trauma, which is something that can be addressed and healed with proper support.</p>
<p class="whitespace-normal break-words">The exhaustion you're feeling from constantly monitoring your thoughts and attractions is a common response to persistent anxiety and intrusive thinking patterns. When we become hypervigilant about our thoughts, we often create exactly the kind of mental prison you're describing, where normal attractions become impossible to experience because everything gets filtered through the lens of anxiety and self-doubt. This hypervigilance can actually make intrusive thoughts stronger and more frequent.</p>
<p class="whitespace-normal break-words">If what you're experiencing is POCD, it specifically involves intrusive thoughts that go against someone's true values and desires. The very fact that these thoughts distress you so much is actually evidence that they don't represent your authentic self. People with genuine pedophilic attractions typically don't experience this level of distress about their attractions - they may feel conflicted about acting on them, but the attractions themselves don't cause the kind of ego-dystonic horror that you're describing.</p>
<p class="whitespace-normal break-words">You mentioned feeling like a "psychopath" for not feeling as much guilt as you think you should, but emotional numbing and exhaustion are natural responses to prolonged anxiety and intrusive thoughts. Your brain is protecting itself from overwhelm. This doesn't make you callous or dangerous - it makes you human.</p>
<p class="whitespace-normal break-words"><strong>It's crucial that you reach out to a qualified mental health provider as soon as possible.</strong> A therapist or psychiatrist who specializes in adolescent mental health, trauma, and anxiety disorders can provide professional assessment, validation of your experiences, and evidence-based treatment options. They can help you process trauma, manage difficult thoughts, develop healthy coping strategies, and ensure you receive the most effective care for your specific situation. The International OCD Foundation (iocdf.org) has a <a href="https://iocdf.org/ocd-finding-help/how-to-find-the-right-therapist/">therapist directory</a> that is a great place to get started. <strong>Professional support will complement your own insights and provide you with additional tools and perspectives that can be crucial for healing and ensuring everyone's safety, including your own. </strong></p>
<p class="whitespace-normal break-words"><strong>Your willingness to seek help demonstrates your commitment to healing and to being the person you want to be.</strong> Thank you so much for reaching out to us.&nbsp;</p>

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/is-it-still-considered-childhood-sexual-abuse-between-peers-cocsa-if-i-didnt-explicitly-tell-them-to-stop-we-were-the-same-age-and-they-wanted-to-engage-in-sexual-activity-i-said-no-initially-but-they)

**Question:** Is it still considered childhood sexual abuse between peers (COCSA) if I didn't explicitly tell them to stop? We were the same age and they wanted to engage in sexual activity. I said no initially, but they suggested a modified version and proceeded despite my discomfort. I didn't ask them to stop because I was confused about what was happening, but I knew I didn't want it to happen at all. Does this still count as abuse even though I didn't clearly say stop?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for sharing your experience and for reaching out with your question. It's completely understandable to feel confused and unsure about what happened, and you're not alone in feeling this way. What you experienced has characteristics consistent with <a href="https://www.ourwave.org/post/child-on-child-sexual-abuse-cocsa-frequently-asked-questions-and-considerations">Child-on-Child Sexual Abuse (COCSA)</a>, but ultimately, only you can decide how to label your own experience. COCSA can occur when one child engages in sexual activities with another child without their full and informed consent, even if they are the same age.</p>
<p>Your initial "no" was a clear boundary that should have been respected completely. When someone pushes past your stated boundary by suggesting an alternative you didn't agree to, that is not consent - that is manipulation and boundary violation. Consent isn't just about not saying "no," it's also about feeling comfortable and fully understanding what's happening. In your situation, you expressed that you didn't want this sexual contact, and even though they suggested a modified version, it doesn't mean you consented to it. The fact that you didn't stop them afterward doesn't negate your initial refusal or your feelings about the situation.</p>
<p>It's completely understandable that you didn't know how to respond in the moment. Many children and even adults freeze or become confused when someone violates their boundaries, especially when it involves sexual contact they don't understand or want. This freeze response is a normal neurological reaction to threat or violation, not a sign of agreement or participation. As children, it's common not to fully understand what's going on or to know how to react in uncomfortable situations. Feeling unsure, frozen, or unable to speak up is a natural response, especially when you're not certain about what's happening. Your confusion and uncertainty in that moment don't diminish the impact this experience had on you.</p>
<p>While children are still learning about boundaries, consent, and appropriate behavior, and the other child may not have fully understood the harm they were causing, this doesn't change the fact that your boundary was crossed after you clearly expressed your discomfort. Children often act impulsively or may be curious about things they don't fully understand, sometimes without realizing the impact of their actions on others. However, your experience of having your boundary violated is still valid and important, regardless of the other child's intentions or level of understanding at the time.</p>
<p>Boundaries can be difficult to navigate at a young age, and it's not uncommon to look back and question whether you should have done something differently. Please be gentle with yourself. Your feelings about this experience are valid, and it's important to recognize that sexual contact without clear, ongoing consent can be harmful regardless of age or intent. Many survivors of childhood sexual experiences struggle with questions about whether their experience "counts" because they didn't fight back or protest more forcefully. These doubts are common but don't change the reality of what happened to you. Trust your instincts that this felt wrong and unwanted - those feelings are telling you something important about what you experienced. Whatever words feel right to you for describing this experience are the right ones to use.</p>
<p>If you're feeling unsettled or if this memory continues to bother you, it might be helpful to talk to a trusted adult or a professional who can offer support and guidance. They can provide a safe space for you to express your feelings and help you process what happened. You're not alone, and it's okay to seek help. Your feelings are valid, and you deserve understanding and support.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-very-young-7-9-years-i-engaged-in-sexual-touching-with-my-younger-sister-during-bath-time-years-later-when-i-learned-about-inappropriate-sexual-contact-i-felt-disgusted-and-ashamed-about-wh)

**Question:** When I was very young (7-9 years), I engaged in sexual touching with my younger sister during bath time. Years later when I learned about inappropriate sexual contact, I felt disgusted and ashamed about what I had done. Do I deserve to feel this way about my childhood actions?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out to us.&nbsp;When you were between 7 and 9 years old, you were still very much a child learning about the world. At that age, children are naturally curious about their bodies and the differences between themselves and others. This curiosity is a normal part of development, and young children often do not fully understand boundaries or the implications of their actions, especially regarding concepts like sexuality or consent. Children naturally explore their bodies and sometimes engage with siblings or peers in ways that involve touching or looking at genitals. This behavior is typically driven by curiosity rather than sexual intent, as young children don't yet understand sexuality in the way adolescents and adults do.</p>
<p>What distinguishes normal childhood exploration from concerning behavior includes factors like the age difference between children, whether force or coercion was used, and whether the behavior was secretive or shame-based at the time. From your description, this appears to have been a brief incident of childhood curiosity that you didn't understand as inappropriate at the time, which is very different from deliberate harmful behavior.</p>
<p>It's important to recognize that as a child, you didn't have the same awareness, understanding, or intentions that you have now. You couldn't fully comprehend the social and moral implications of your actions back then. The intense shame you're experiencing now likely stems from applying your current adult understanding of appropriate boundaries to actions you took as a very young child. The feelings of disgust and shame you're experiencing now reflect your current maturity and understanding, not the innocence of your younger self. This is common but can be unnecessarily painful, as young children operate from a completely different developmental framework than teenagers or adults, and their actions should be understood within that context.</p>
<p>You don't deserve to carry such heavy guilt for something that occurred when you were a child without full comprehension of your actions. It's okay to acknowledge that, with the knowledge and values you have now, you wouldn't make the same choices. Be gentle with yourself.&nbsp;Remember, our past doesn't define us, but how we learn and grow from it does.&nbsp;</p>

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/im-currently-waiting-for-sentencing-in-my-case-and-today-was-supposed-to-be-the-day-this-is-about-the-fourth-or-fifth-time-it-has-been-rescheduled-and-im-struggling-with-the-delays-this-doesnt-feel-fa)

**Question:** I'm currently waiting for sentencing in my case, and today was supposed to be the day. This is about the fourth or fifth time it has been rescheduled, and I'm struggling with the delays. This doesn't feel fair - I've been waiting for this moment. What can I do to cope with this situation?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out to us during this incredibly difficult and exhausting time. What you're experiencing right now is profoundly challenging, and I want you to know that every feeling you're having - the frustration, disappointment, anger, and weariness - is completely valid and understandable. Waiting for sentencing is emotionally draining under the best of circumstances, and having it rescheduled four or five times feels like a cruel extension of your pain. You're absolutely right that this isn't fair, and I'm so sorry the system is failing you in this way.</p>
<p>You've shown tremendous courage in getting to this point, and each postponement must feel like another blow when you've already summoned so much strength. The repeated delays can reopen wounds and trigger feelings that may echo your original trauma, leaving you feeling powerless all over again. Your nervous system has likely prepared for this monumental day multiple times, and having that emotional energy feel wasted with each reschedule is genuinely heartbreaking. Please know that your exhaustion and frustration don't make you weak - they make you human.</p>
<p>While the court's timeline remains frustratingly out of your control, there are gentle ways to nurture yourself and reclaim small pieces of your power during this waiting period. Self- and community-care isn't just important right now - it's essential. This might look like wrapping yourself in activities that bring even moments of peace: a warm bath, time in nature, creative expression, or simply allowing yourself to rest without guilt. Sometimes the most radical act of self-care is giving yourself permission to feel exactly what you're feeling without judgment.</p>
<p>If you have access to a victim advocate, they can be invaluable in helping you understand what's happening behind the scenes and ensuring your voice is heard in this process. Connecting with a trauma-informed counselor or support group can provide a sacred space where your experience is truly understood. Other survivors who've walked this path often carry wisdom about navigating these impossible delays, and their companionship can remind you that you're not alone in this struggle.</p>
<p>You deserve so much better than this prolonged uncertainty. You deserve closure, peace, and the chance to move forward on your own terms. While you wait for that day to come, please remember that you are surrounded by people who believe in you, who see your strength even when you can't feel it, and who are holding space for your healing. Please be as kind to yourself. We are thinking about you.&nbsp;</p>

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/my-ex-boyfriend-repeatedly-touched-me-inappropriately-in-public-after-i-clearly-asked-him-to-stop-multiple-times-at-the-time-i-convinced-myself-i-was-okay-with-what-happened-but-now-i-realize-how-unco)

**Question:** My ex-boyfriend repeatedly touched me inappropriately in public after I clearly asked him to stop multiple times. At the time, I convinced myself I was okay with what happened, but now I realize how uncomfortable and violated it made me feel. It's been several months since we broke up, and I'm struggling with whether I should address this with him or if it's too late to say anything. How do I process these feelings, and what should I consider when deciding whether to confront him about his behavior?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you so much for reaching out to us and I am so sorry to hear that you are feeling this way about what happened. What you experienced was a clear violation of your boundaries and consent, and your feelings of discomfort and violation are completely valid.&nbsp;</p>
<p>It's incredibly common for people to initially minimize or rationalize boundary violations, especially when they occur within romantic relationships. Sometimes we brush things off to avoid conflict or because we hope the situation will improve, and this psychological response often serves as a protective mechanism to help us cope with difficult experiences in the moment. It's also not uncommon for people to convince themselves that they're okay with something when it's someone they care about. As time passes and you gain more perspective, it's normal for those suppressed feelings to resurface and for you to recognize the impact the experience truly had on you. The fact that you're now recognizing how the experience affected you doesn't mean your initial reaction was wrong - it means you're processing the trauma at your own pace, which is entirely normal and healthy.</p>
<p>Regarding whether to address this with your ex-partner, there is no "right" timeline for speaking up about boundary violations. Deciding whether to reach out to him about what happened is a personal choice that deserves careful consideration. Think about what you hope to achieve by telling him and how it might affect your healing process. Some find closure in expressing how they were affected by previous partners, while others prefer to focus their energy on their own healing journey. If you believe that expressing your feelings to him would provide closure or help you move forward, it might be worth considering. However, since you're no longer in contact, it's also important to think about whether engaging with him again is in your best interest. Remember that his response, should you choose to reach out, may not provide the validation or accountability you deserve.</p>
<p>Your well-being and healing should be the primary focus right now. It might be helpful to talk to someone you trust about how you're feeling - perhaps a close friend, family member, or counselor. Processing these feelings with a trusted person or therapist can be incredibly beneficial as they can provide personalized support for working through complex emotions around consent violations and help you develop healthy coping strategies. Sharing your experience with someone supportive can help you process your emotions and decide on the best course of action for yourself.</p>
<p>What happened to you matters, regardless of when you recognized its impact. Your boundaries deserve respect, and you have every right to process this experience in whatever way feels most supportive for your healing journey. Be gentle with yourself as you navigate through this. Thank you for trusting us with your story.</p>

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-recently-remembered-experiencing-unwanted-sexual-contact-with-another-child-when-i-was-in-kindergarten-the-experiences-continued-for-about-two-years-and-included-various-forms-of-inappropriate-touch)

**Question:** I recently remembered experiencing unwanted sexual contact with another child when I was in kindergarten. The experiences continued for about two years and included various forms of inappropriate touching and contact. The other child was the same age as me. This happened frequently, often in front of other children and adults, and I remember feeling distressed and trying to get away. The adults present seemed to notice but didn't take serious measures to stop what was happening. Until recently, I only remembered parts of what happened, but this week I suddenly remembered much more detail. I'm wondering if this counts as childhood sexual abuse, and how it might have affected me mentally - I remember being depressed as a child but never understood why. Why did it take so long for me to remember everything that happened?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out and sharing your story with us. I'm sorry to hear about what you went through. What you've described certainly sounds like it aligns with characteristics associated with what is called <a href="https://www.ourwave.org/post/child-on-child-sexual-abuse-cocsa-frequently-asked-questions-and-considerations">Child-on-Child Sexual Abuse (COCSA)</a>. The behaviors you experienced&mdash;such as inappropriate touching, forced physical contact, and the violation of your personal boundaries while you were clearly distressed&mdash;represent serious harmful experiences regardless of the ages involved. The fact that these actions were against your will, caused you distress, and happened repeatedly over two years is significant. Your feelings of distress, fear, and attempts to get away clearly indicate that these were not wanted interactions or normal childhood exploration.</p>
<p>Children at such a young age typically lack a full understanding of appropriate boundaries and the implications of their actions. When one child engages in sexually harmful behavior towards another, it can sometimes indicate that they have been exposed to inappropriate sexual content or experiences themselves. It's compassionate of you to consider what the other child might have been through, but it's equally important to acknowledge the serious impact his actions had on you. Your empathy toward him doesn't diminish the reality or severity of what you experienced.</p>
<p>Experiencing such harmful interactions in early childhood can have lasting effects on mental and emotional well-being. The depression you experienced during childhood could certainly be connected to these experiences. Trauma can affect how we see ourselves, how we trust others, and how we process emotions, often manifesting in symptoms like depression, anxiety, difficulty with relationships, and problems with boundaries. These effects can persist into adulthood, sometimes without us fully realizing the connection to past events until memories resurface.</p>
<p>The delayed recall you're experiencing is actually very common among survivors of childhood trauma. It's not uncommon for people to repress or block out traumatic memories, especially when they occur in childhood. This is a protective mechanism our minds use to shield us from overwhelming pain and distress when we lack the cognitive framework to process what's happening to us. Memories of trauma can resurface later in life, often triggered by certain events, stress, developmental milestones, or simply when we feel safer and more mature. The sudden recall of these memories can be confusing and upsetting, but it's a natural process as your mind tries to process and make sense of past experiences. There's nothing unusual about remembering more details now at 18 - your brain may finally feel ready to process these experiences more fully.</p>
<p>It's also crucial to recognize that the adults who witnessed this behavior failed in their fundamental duty to protect you. Regardless of the circumstances, adults have a responsibility to ensure children's safety and to intervene decisively when one child is clearly harming another. Their inaction was a serious failure of the systems that should have kept you safe, and you deserved much better protection than you received.</p>
<p>You deserve support, understanding, and compassion as you work through this difficult time. Consider reaching out to a mental health professional who is experienced in working with childhood trauma. They can help you process what happened, explore how you want to understand and frame your experiences, and work through any ongoing impacts these experiences may have on your life.</p>
<p>Remember, you're not alone in this journey, and what happened to you was absolutely not your fault. You deserve understanding, validation, and support as you navigate these memories and begin your healing process.</p>

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-previously-asked-about-inappropriate-touch-i-engaged-in-when-i-was-9-i-realize-i-didnt-share-the-full-picture-when-i-was-younger-my-brother-and-i-kissed-inappropriately-a-few-times-an-adult-saw-us-a)

**Question:** I previously asked about inappropriate touch I engaged in when I was 9. I realize I didn't share the full picture. When I was younger, my brother and I kissed inappropriately a few times. An adult saw us and told us to stop, and it didn't happen again. During this same time, I tried to kiss my parent in a similar way and was told it wasn't appropriate. I remember later telling my brother we weren't allowed to do that when he tried again. I'm feeling guilty about not sharing this whole truth before and have an overwhelming sense that I'm a horrible person. How do I move forward?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out to us again. What you're describing doesn't change the core truth from your previous question - you were a child exploring boundaries without adult understanding or intent.</p>
<p>At seven years old, children naturally explore relationships and social norms through curiosity. The behaviors you describe are common childhood exploration, which is why the adults in your life responded with gentle correction rather than alarm. Most importantly, you internalized those boundaries and even guided your brother when he tried again, showing healthy development and respect for rules.</p>
<p>The guilt you're carrying now reflects the caring person you've become, but you're applying adult moral reasoning to childhood actions that were developmentally normal. Children who engage in sexual behaviors aren't "bad" children - they're children processing their experiences and learning boundaries. The shame belongs to any circumstances that may have influenced these behaviors, not to you as a person.</p>
<p>If these feelings continue to overwhelm you, a trauma-informed counselor can provide personalized support for processing these complex emotions. You deserve to move forward without carrying shame for childhood misunderstandings that don't define your worth. Be gentle with yourself - you're not a horrible person, and it's okay to let go of this guilt.</p>

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-young-something-happened-between-my-older-cousin-and-me-we-had-a-good-relationship-at-first-we-watched-shows-together-and-played-games-at-some-point-we-started-viewing-explicit-content-toge)

**Question:** When I was young, something happened between my older cousin and me. We had a good relationship at first - we watched shows together and played games. At some point, we started viewing explicit content together. After watching inappropriate content, we both agreed to engage in sexual activity. During this encounter, I noticed my cousin became quiet, but I continued. I attempted certain acts and guided his hand to touch me, though he didn't respond much. I recently learned that my cousin says this was a harmful experience for him. I'm confused about what happened because I was the younger person involved. Can you help me understand this situation?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for sharing your experiences with us. What you're describing sounds like a deeply confusing and painful situation that has stayed with you for a long time, and your desire to understand what happened shows your care for both yourself and your cousin.</p>
<p>When you were 11 years old, you and your cousin were both navigating a time of growth and curiosity. Sexual encounters between children, especially when there's an age difference, are complicated situations that don't fit neatly into adult frameworks of consent and harm. Children and adolescents often explore and imitate what they see in media without fully understanding the implications or boundaries. When children are exposed to sexual content beyond their developmental understanding, it can lead to sexual behaviors that they're not emotionally or cognitively equipped to navigate. At that age, it's common not to grasp the boundaries or the potential impact of certain actions on ourselves or others. This doesn't make you a bad person - it means you were a child processing experiences and information that were too advanced for your age.</p>
<p>The fact that your cousin became quiet and unresponsive during this encounter is significant. Even when someone initially agrees to something, they can change their mind, feel uncomfortable, or freeze up - especially children who may not have the words or understanding to communicate their feelings. Children often don't know how to say no or stop something once it's started, particularly with someone they trust or when they feel confused about what's happening. It's understandable that you're questioning what happened and how your cousin might have felt about it, and learning that he describes the experience as negative can bring up feelings of confusion or guilt.</p>
<p>Each person's experience is unique, and it's possible for two people involved in the same event to perceive it differently. Your cousin's feelings about the experience are valid, just as your feelings and questions are valid. Age differences between children, even just a few years, represent significant gaps in physical, emotional, and cognitive development. What feels like mutual exploration to one child can feel overwhelming, scary, or harmful to another. The fact that your cousin describes this as a harmful experience for him is his truth, and that's valid regardless of your intentions or your age at the time. Trauma isn't determined by intent but by impact.</p>
<p>It's important to recognize that both you and your cousin were children navigating something neither of you was prepared for. This doesn't minimize the impact on your cousin, but it also doesn't make you responsible in the way an adult would be. Children learn about boundaries, consent, and appropriate behavior through guidance and experience --guidance that was missing in this situation. At the same time, carrying guilt and confusion about childhood experiences can be very heavy, and you deserve support in processing these feelings.</p>
<p>Considering the complexity of these feelings and the impact this has had on both of you, it might be helpful to talk to a trusted adult or a mental health professional who specializes in childhood sexual behavior and trauma. They can help you process this experience, understand the complexity of what happened, work through the emotions you're carrying, and explore any concerns you might have. If you feel it would be appropriate and beneficial, you might also consider whether reaching out to your cousin to acknowledge his experience could be healing for both of you, though this should be done with professional guidance.</p>
<p>You are not alone, and it's okay to seek help in making sense of past experiences. Taking care of yourself and acknowledging your feelings is an important step toward understanding and healing. These difficult experiences from childhood don't define who you are as a person today. Thank you so much for trusting us with this.&nbsp;</p>

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/a-friend-touched-my-inner-thigh-without-permission-while-we-were-in-a-hot-tub-together-i-pushed-them-away-and-laughed-it-off-but-it-made-me-uncomfortable-im-wondering-whether-this-would-be-considered)

**Question:** A friend touched my inner thigh without permission while we were in a hot tub together. I pushed them away and laughed it off, but it made me uncomfortable. I'm wondering whether this would be considered assault or harassment.

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for sharing this with us. What you described is a significant boundary violation where your friend attempted to touch you in an intimate area without your consent, which is not okay. <a href="https://rainn.org/articles/what-is-consent">Consent</a> is essential in all interactions, and everyone has the right to have their personal space and boundaries respected.</p>
<p>When someone touches intimate parts of your body without your consent, it crosses important boundaries regardless of the setting or your relationship with that person. Even though you were able to push them away before they went further, the fact that they initiated unwanted physical contact is significant. The fact that you instinctively pushed them away shows that your body recognized this as unwanted contact.</p>
<p>Laughing it off is a very common reaction when we feel uncomfortable or unsure how to respond, but it doesn't diminish how the situation made you feel or minimize what happened. It's very common for people to laugh off uncomfortable situations in the moment, especially when they involve someone we know and trust. Laughing, freezing, or trying to lighten the mood are natural protective responses that many people have when their boundaries are crossed unexpectedly. Your brain was likely trying to process a confusing situation involving someone you considered safe.</p>
<p>Whether this is classified as assault or harassment can depend on legal definitions in different places, but the distinction often depends on specific legal definitions that vary by location. What matters most is not the label but rather acknowledging that your boundaries were violated. Unwanted touching of intimate areas like your inner thigh typically falls under sexual assault in many jurisdictions, as it involves non-consensual sexual contact. However, the most important thing to understand is that your experience was significant and your reaction was completely normal.</p>
<p>You have every right to feel however you feel about this experience. Some people might feel angry, confused, betrayed, or uncertain about what happened. Others might minimize it or question whether it was "serious enough" to matter. All of these responses are normal. What happened to you matters because it mattered to you, and because everyone deserves to have their physical boundaries respected. You deserve to feel safe and respected in all your relationships.</p>
<p>It might be helpful to talk to someone you trust about what happened, whether it's a family member, another friend, or a professional who can provide support. Moving forward, you might consider setting clear boundaries with this friend if you choose to maintain the relationship, or you might decide you need distance. Trust your instincts about what feels right for you. Remember, you're not alone, and it's okay to set boundaries with anyone who makes you feel uncomfortable, even if they're a friend.</p>
