**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-experienced-sexual-abuse-from-ages-6-7-until-11-12-by-a-classmate-during-sleepovers-at-his-house-he-stopped-when-we-reached-seventh-grade-i-didnt-recognize-it-as-abuse-until-i-was-15-though-i-knew-i)

**Question:** I experienced sexual abuse from ages 6/7 until 11/12 by a classmate during sleepovers at his house. He stopped when we reached seventh grade. I didn't recognize it as abuse until I was 15, though I knew it was wrong at the time. Now at 20, I have fragmented memories, including one where his father entered the room during an incident, making me concerned he might have been involved too. What can I do to recover these memories? I've considered contacting my former classmate as we're on cordial terms, though we've never discussed what happened. What might I say to him? I really need to understand what happened.

**Answer:** <p>I'm so sorry to hear about the pain and confusion you're experiencing. First, I want to acknowledge your courage in seeking to understand what happened to you. Childhood sexual abuse is deeply traumatic, and the fragmented memories you're experiencing are a common response to trauma. Your brain may have protected you by blocking certain memories that were too overwhelming to process at the time.</p>
<p>Memory recovery from trauma is complex. Our minds sometimes store traumatic memories differently than everyday ones, making them fragmented or incomplete. Rather than trying to force memory recovery, which can sometimes lead to distress or potentially unreliable memories, many trauma specialists recommend working with a professional who specializes in trauma therapy. Approaches like <a href="https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/22641-emdr-therapy">EMDR</a> (Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing), <a href="https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/therapy-types/trauma-focused-cognitive-behavior-therapy?msockid=1103576693d9679310b645d892bb66b2">trauma-focused cognitive behavioral therapy</a>, or <a href="https://www.verywellmind.com/what-is-somatic-experiencing-5204186">somatic experiencing </a>can help process traumatic memories in a supported environment.</p>
<p>Reaching out to your former classmate might seem like a way to fill in the gaps, but it's important to consider how this could affect you emotionally. Contacting him could bring up unexpected feelings or complicate your healing process. It might also be uncertain how he would respond, which could lead to additional distress. If you do decide to reach out, consider your own safety and emotional wellbeing first. Having a therapist to process this with before and after would be valuable. If you choose to contact him, keep the message simple and direct about your intentions, perhaps indicating that you're working to understand experiences from your childhood that have affected you.</p>
<p>The concern about his father's potential involvement adds another layer of complexity to your experience. This uncertainty can be particularly difficult to carry. A trauma-informed therapist can help you work through these questions and concerns, whether or not complete memories return.</p>
<p>Remember that healing from trauma doesn't necessarily require having a complete narrative of what happened. Many survivors find peace through understanding their responses to trauma, developing coping skills, and reconnecting with their sense of safety and control. Your experience is valid regardless of how much you remember, and support is available to help you process and heal. Be gentle with yourself as you navigate this journey - healing takes time, and it's okay to seek help along the way. You're not alone, and there are people who care about you and want to support you through this process. Thank you for reaching out to us.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/is-it-still-sexual-assault-if-it-was-my-sister-and-she-didnt-have-malicious-intent-when-i-was-around-15-my-sister-grabbed-my-buttcheek-shed-done-it-before-but-it-only-really-felt-bad-this-specific-tim)

**Question:** Is it still sexual assault if it was my sister, and she didn't have malicious intent? When I was around 15, my sister grabbed my buttcheek. She'd done it before but it only really felt bad this specific time. Everyone I know considers this sexual assault, but I feel like I'm appropriating the term calling it that. I feel like it's not because: she was my sister, she was only a year older, it was so statistically improbable, she didn't have malicious intent, and I've read about others with worse experiences who didn't label their experiences as sexual assault.

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for sharing this experience and your thoughtful reflection on it. Navigating uncomfortable physical interactions with family members can be confusing, especially when trying to understand how to contextualize what happened.</p>
<p>What you're describing is a situation where a boundary was crossed that made you uncomfortable. Your discomfort with the experience is valid, regardless of how it might be labeled. Many sibling interactions during adolescence involve boundary testing, and sometimes siblings may not understand the impact of their actions.</p>
<p>The context you've provided&mdash;that this was your sister who was close in age, without malicious intent&mdash;is important information that helps frame the interaction. Family dynamics often include complex patterns of behavior that may not fit neatly into specific categories.</p>
<p>It's worth noting that trauma isn't a competition. Comparing your experience to others or wondering if your situation "counts" enough can sometimes prevent us from acknowledging our own genuine feelings. Your experience doesn't need to meet certain criteria of severity to be valid or worthy of support. Different people process similar experiences in different ways.</p>
<p>Rather than focusing on whether this fits a particular definition, it might be more helpful to consider how you can address these feelings and establish clearer boundaries moving forward. Understanding why this particular incident felt different from previous times could provide insight into your own developing sense of personal boundaries.</p>
<p>What's most important isn't necessarily finding the perfect label, but rather acknowledging how the experience affected you and seeking appropriate support if needed. Some people find that naming their experience helps them process it, while others prefer to describe what happened without using specific terms. Either approach is valid.</p>
<p>If this experience continues to trouble you, it might be helpful to discuss it with a trusted adult, counselor, or therapist who can provide guidance specific to your situation. Remember that your feelings about what happened are valid, and you get to decide how to interpret and process your own experiences in a way that feels right and healthy for you. Thank you for reaching out to us. We appreciate your trust.</p>

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-younger-i-had-frequent-sleepovers-at-my-aunts-house-during-one-visit-i-ended-up-alone-with-her-in-the-houses-mudroom-she-told-me-to-take-off-my-clothes-i-communicated-that-i-didnt-want-to-a)

**Question:** When I was younger, I had frequent sleepovers at my aunt's house. During one visit, I ended up alone with her in the house's mudroom. She told me to take off my clothes. I communicated that I didn't want to and it made me uncomfortable, but she kept insisting it was "fine" because we were both female. I remember her removing her clothes, but my memory is unclear about what happened after that. She is responsible for another unrelated trauma in my life as well.

I'm conflicted about whether I'm glad I don't remember or wish I could recall more to answer the questions in my head. I've never shared this with anyone because I don't know if my experience is valid, and I don't want to invade spaces where I don't belong. Am I right to feel uncomfortable about this experience? I'm just tired of feeling so lost.

**Answer:** <p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Thank you for trusting us with your story. I'm sorry to hear that you've been carrying this experience alone and feeling lost. What you went through with your aunt sounds deeply confusing and unsettling, and it's completely understandable that you're grappling with questions about it now.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">What you've described is absolutely valid, and your feelings of discomfort are completely justified. When someone pressures you to remove your clothing after you've clearly expressed discomfort, that's a violation of your boundaries, regardless of your relationship or gender. An adult asking a child to undress without a clear reason is inappropriate and concerning behavior. It's important to recognize that you had the right to say no, and your discomfort was a natural response to a situation that didn't feel right to you.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Memory gaps around distressing events are common and are often your brain's way of protecting you. These gaps don't make your experience any less real or significant. Many survivors have fragmented memories of traumatic events, and this is a normal response to overwhelming situations. It's okay to feel conflicted about whether you wish to remember more; both feelings are completely valid.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Your hesitation to speak about this experience is understandable. Many survivors question whether their experiences "count" or if they belong in survivor spaces. Please know that there is no minimum threshold of harm that you need to meet to deserve support and healing. If an experience has caused you distress, that alone makes it valid. You're not invading any space by acknowledging what happened to you.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">The fact that this aunt was also responsible for another major trauma in your life suggests a pattern of boundary violations that compounds the impact of these experiences. This context matters and might add weight to your feelings about this specific incident.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Consider reaching out to a therapist who specializes in trauma, as they can provide personalized guidance through these complex feelings. Many survivors find that professional support helps them process memories and emotions at their own pace, without pressure to remember more than feels safe. Sharing your feelings might help alleviate some of the burden you've been carrying.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Remember that healing isn't linear, and questioning your experiences is part of the process. Your feelings matter, your boundaries matter, and your healing journey matters. You deserve compassion, understanding, and support as you work through these difficult memories and emotions. You're not alone, and there are people who care and want to help you navigate through these feelings. Take gentle care of yourself.</p>

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/after-i-was-sexually-assaulted-for-a-short-time-i-thought-i-wanted-it-i-thought-maybe-he-touched-me-because-he-liked-me-does-it-still-count-as-assault-if-i-thought-those-things-at-the-time-am-i-stupid)

**Question:** After I was sexually assaulted, for a short time, I thought I wanted it. I thought maybe he touched me because he liked me. Does it still count as assault if I thought those things at the time? Am I stupid?

**Answer:** <p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Thank you for sharing your thoughts and feelings about such a personal and difficult experience. No, you are absolutely not stupid. What you experienced was absolutely sexual assault, regardless of any thoughts or feelings you had during or after the incident.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">When we experience trauma, our brains try to make sense of what happened in ways that feel less threatening or painful. This is especially true for young people who are still developing their understanding of relationships and boundaries. Thinking that the attention meant the person liked you is a natural response. These thoughts are protective mechanisms, not evidence that the assault didn't happen or that you somehow invited or deserved it.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">It's common for survivors to have complex and confusing feelings about what's happening to them. Your reaction&mdash;wondering if you wanted it or if the person liked you&mdash;is an extremely normal response to trauma. Consent cannot be given by a child, and it's the responsibility of the older person to respect boundaries and protect younger individuals.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Again, you are NOT stupid&mdash;you were responding to a traumatic situation in the best way your mind knew how at the time. Your mind was trying to process a situation that was not your fault. The blame lies entirely with the person who chose to violate your trust and boundaries.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Healing from sexual assault takes time, and your feelings about what happened may continue to change. Being gentle with yourself and recognizing that your reactions were valid survival responses can be an important part of moving forward. The responsibility for the assault lies entirely with the person who chose to violate your boundaries, not with you or your thoughts.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Please consider reaching out to someone you trust or a professional who can offer support as you continue to navigate these feelings. You deserve compassion, understanding, and healing. Remember, what happened to you was not your fault. You are not alone.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/about-6-months-ago-while-on-vacation-with-a-friend-and-her-family-her-older-half-brother-34-made-advances-at-me-21-in-a-hot-tub-when-my-friend-wasnt-looking-the-next-morning-i-woke-up-to-him-standing)

**Question:** About 6 months ago while on vacation with a friend and her family, her older half-brother (34) made advances at me (21) in a hot tub when my friend wasn't looking. The next morning, I woke up to him standing over me. He got on top of me, removed our clothes, locked the door, and had sexual contact with me. I was completely frozen during this—I couldn't move or speak. When it was over, I was in pain and bleeding. I didn't tell anyone except another friend on the trip, believing it was consensual since I didn't stop it. When that friend told my other friend (his relative), she became furious with me.
Since then, I've been in therapy twice weekly with monthly psychiatric visits. At first, I could occasionally go out with a close friend, but recently I've become unable to leave the house for social events. I still go to work and school, but when I tried to join my sister's birthday celebration, I broke down crying. Why can't I go out socially? Am I overreacting after 6 months? I still blame myself and think about it daily. When will I feel safe going out again, and how do I know things will improve?

**Answer:** <p>I'm so sorry to hear about what you've been experiencing. What you've been through is deeply traumatic, and I want you to know that everything you're feeling right now is completely valid. What happened that morning wasn't your fault in any way. When your body froze, it wasn't giving consent&mdash;it was protecting you in the only way it could at that moment. This freeze response is a natural, involuntary reaction to trauma that many survivors experience. Your body was looking out for you, even when you couldn't consciously respond.</p>
<p>Please know that there's absolutely no timeline for healing from something like this. The fact that you're still struggling after six months doesn't mean you're overreacting or doing anything wrong&mdash;it means you're a human being processing a profound violation. The fact that you can go to work and school already shows incredible strength and resilience on your part.</p>
<p>The self-blame you're feeling is something almost every survivor experiences, but I want to gently remind you that nothing about this situation&mdash;not finding him attractive, not the circumstances of the trip, nothing&mdash;made what he did okay or your responsibility. He made a deliberate choice to violate your boundaries and trust.</p>
<p>When you broke down before your sister's celebration, that was your body and mind communicating that they're still healing, still processing. Those tears are not weakness&mdash;they're part of your healing journey. Each time you try, even if it doesn't work out as planned, you're taking a brave step forward.</p>
<p>The path to feeling safe again isn't straight or predictable, but many survivors do find their way back to joy, connection, and feeling secure in the world again. Your work with your therapist is creating the foundation for that healing. On days when it feels impossible, try to be as gentle with yourself as you would be with someone you love deeply who was going through the same thing.</p>
<p>The small victories matter enormously&mdash;getting dressed, putting on makeup, even considering going out. Each of these moments is evidence of your courage and your heart's desire to reclaim your life. Hold onto that, and know that with continued support and deep self-compassion, the weight of this trauma will gradually become lighter to carry.</p>
<p>You deserve all the time and space you need to heal, and you absolutely deserve to feel safe and whole again. That day will come, even if the path there has many twists and turns. Thank you for trusting us with this. Healing is possible.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-14-15-i-engaged-once-in-an-inappropriate-boundary-crossing-game-with-a-much-younger-child-5-that-involved-rubbing-around-private-areas-while-fully-clothed-we-stopped-when-they-asked-and-i-i)

**Question:** When I was 14-15, I engaged once in an inappropriate boundary-crossing game with a much younger child (5) that involved rubbing around private areas while fully clothed. We stopped when they asked, and I immediately told my parents who explained boundaries to me. I had experienced childhood sexual trauma myself and didn't understand self-gratification. Eleven years later, I still feel terrible about this incident, and my OCD causes intrusive thoughts telling me I'm an abuser. I've discussed this with my therapist but continue to struggle. How can I understand and process this childhood mistake?

**Answer:** <p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Thank you for trusting us with this. What happened was inappropriate and crossed important boundaries that should exist between children of different ages and developmental stages. At the same time, I hear the genuine remorse and concern in your words, which speaks to your character today.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">As a 14-15 year old who had experienced your own sexual trauma, your understanding of appropriate boundaries was compromised through circumstances beyond your control. Many survivors of childhood sexual trauma struggle with boundary recognition because they weren't protected themselves and weren't taught healthy models of interaction. This context helps us understand the complexity of what happened, even while recognizing it wasn't okay.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">What shows your character even then was how you responded: you stopped when asked, immediately told your parents rather than hiding it, and most importantly, you integrated their guidance about boundaries so deeply that you've never repeated this behavior. These actions reflect someone who, even in confusion, had an internal moral compass that was seeking direction.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">The deep remorse you feel eleven years later is understandable, but it's also important to recognize how it's being amplified by OCD, which can lock us into cycles of shame that don't allow for growth or healing. OCD often attaches to our deepest values and fears, creating intrusive thoughts that don't accurately reflect who we are but rather what we most fear becoming.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">In trauma recovery, we learn to hold both accountability and compassion&mdash;recognizing that harm occurred while also understanding the wounded child who acted without full comprehension. This isn't about excusing what happened but about placing it in the full context of your life story and allowing yourself to integrate this experience without letting it define you completely.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">The person you are today&mdash;someone who cares deeply about not causing harm, who has maintained proper boundaries for over a decade, who continues to work with a therapist to understand yourself better&mdash;deserves acknowledgment alongside the mistake made by your younger self.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Consider working with your therapist specifically on how trauma and OCD interact, perhaps through approaches like <a href="https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/22641-emdr-therapy">EMDR</a> or specialized OCD treatment that can help process these memories with both accountability and self-compassion. Many people find that addressing both the trauma history and the OCD symptoms together leads to more lasting healing than focusing on either alone.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">While we can't change the past, we can honor it through how we choose to live now, the boundaries we maintain, and the healing we pursue. You are more than your worst moment, especially one that occurred during your own developmental struggle with trauma. Thank you so much for reaching out to us.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-would-like-to-know-if-someone-can-develop-a-fetish-or-kink-around-humiliation-if-the-sexual-abuse-they-experienced-didnt-arouse-them-sexually-when-it-occurred-could-it-be-the-shame-and-humiliation-t)

**Question:** I would like to know if someone can develop a fetish or kink around humiliation if the sexual abuse they experienced didn't arouse them sexually when it occurred. Could it be the shame and humiliation themselves that lead to developing this kink rather than any physical sensation experienced at the time? What's the connection between these experiences? For instance, if someone was coerced into a sexual act they didn't want to perform and felt no physical pleasure from it, could that experience be the cause of developing a specific kink related to that trauma when they begin to develop sexually?

**Answer:** <p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Thank you for reaching out to us. It's entirely possible for someone to develop sexual arousal patterns connected to experiences that were not sexually arousing when they originally occurred. Our brains and bodies have complex ways of processing trauma, and one mechanism that some people experience is called "trauma reenactment" or "traumatic sexualization." This can involve the mind attempting to gain control over or make meaning from traumatic experiences by recreating aspects of them in contexts where the person has more agency and choice.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">The connection between past trauma and later sexual arousal patterns isn't usually about the physical sensations experienced during the traumatic event. Rather, it's more commonly connected to the powerful emotions that accompanied the experience&mdash;emotions like shame, powerlessness, fear, or humiliation. When these intense emotions become linked with sexuality during formative experiences, particularly for young people still developing their sexual identity, they can become part of the person's arousal template.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">In the example you described, being coerced into an intimate act without wanting to participate, and without experiencing physical pleasure, could potentially influence a person's sexual development. The coerced act, combined with the associated emotions of shame or helplessness, might become incorporated into how they understand and navigate their own sexuality later in life. This process is complex and highly individual.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">It's important to understand that this doesn't mean you wanted or enjoyed the traumatic experience. It doesn't diminish the harm that was done to you. Many trauma specialists view these responses as attempts by the mind and body to heal&mdash;by transforming experiences where you had no control into contexts where you have choice, consent, and agency.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Many survivors experience conflicted feelings about these connections between past trauma and current sexuality. Some find that working with trauma-informed therapists specialized in sexual issues helps them understand these patterns and develop a healthier relationship with their sexuality. Others find that simply understanding these connections helps reduce shame and confusion.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Whatever your experience, please know that you're not alone in having these questions, and your feelings are valid. These complex responses to trauma are a normal part of how our minds and bodies attempt to process difficult experiences. Thank you for trusting us with this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-was-inappropriately-touched-at-age-6-by-a-classmate-who-repeatedly-put-his-hand-in-my-skirt-despite-me-telling-him-to-stop-he-only-stopped-when-he-left-the-school-i-barely-remember-details-but-feel)

**Question:** I was inappropriately touched at age 6 by a classmate who repeatedly put his hand in my skirt despite me telling him to stop. He only stopped when he left the school. I barely remember details but feel physically sick recalling his lunchbox. People told me "he must have liked me." I think because of it I've been repulsed by physical contact with other people and very picky when it comes to others, especially boys/men. Now I'm confused - we were both children, but I never consented. Was this coercion? Was it wrong?

**Answer:** <p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Thank you for sharing your experience with us. It sounds like what you experienced was unwanted and inappropriate touching that violated your boundaries, regardless of the age of the other child. When you were six, you were navigating a new school and coping with your parents working abroad, which are significant changes for a child. The boy's actions&mdash;opening your skirt's zipper and touching you without your consent&mdash;were not okay. You told him to stop multiple times, and he disregarded your feelings and continued his behavior. This wasn't just playful or a sign that he "liked you"; it was a violation of your personal boundaries.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Your body remembers this violation even if your mind has gaps in the memory, which is a completely normal trauma response. The nausea you feel when remembering certain details is a natural reaction to an experience where your boundaries were repeatedly crossed despite your clear communication to stop. Many survivors develop protective mechanisms around touch and intimacy following experiences like yours. These are not weaknesses but ways your body and mind have tried to keep you safe.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Children can and do engage in harmful behaviors toward other children, even without fully understanding the impact of their actions. The fact that you repeatedly told him to stop and he continued anyway shows that your boundaries were deliberately ignored, which is a form of coercion. His young age doesn't erase the impact it had on you. You were not at fault, and you did nothing wrong.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">The dismissive responses you received from others ("he must have liked you") reflect harmful cultural narratives that normalize boundary violations as expressions of affection. This kind of response can cause survivors to doubt their own experiences and feelings, which appears to have happened in your case. Minimizing your feelings in that way doesn't acknowledge the distress you experienced.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Your feelings of confusion, repulsion, and selectiveness about physical contact are valid responses to what happened. It's also understandable that this experience might have influenced how you feel about physical contact with others today. Early experiences like this can leave a lasting impression on how we relate to people and how safe we feel in certain situations.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Healing is possible. Many people find that speaking with a trauma-informed therapist helps them process childhood experiences and develop healthier relationships with touch and intimacy. Your experience is valid, your boundaries matter, and the impact on you is real regardless of the other child's age or intentions. You're not alone in this, and reaching out is a brave step forward. Take care of yourself, and know that your well-being matters. Thank you for trusting us with this.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-had-a-relationship-from-ages-10-14-with-someone-who-was-13-17-i-had-previous-trauma-and-was-exposed-to-sexual-content-from-a-young-age-by-my-biological-father-which-led-me-to-develop-hypersexual-ten)

**Question:** I had a relationship from ages 10-14 with someone who was 13-17. I had previous trauma and was exposed to sexual content from a young age by my biological father, which led me to develop hypersexual tendencies. When we met, I quickly developed a crush on this person. Our relationship became sexual both online and physically. They had their own trauma from sexual assault and depended on me emotionally during PTSD episodes and suicidal thoughts. My mental health declined during this relationship. When I questioned the age gap, they said it was okay because I was "mature for my age" and because they had autism they "felt younger." I still talk to this person but feel our relationship wasn't normal. Was this grooming? I feel like it negatively impacted my development, but I also feel partially responsible since I never said no. How should I understand this situation?

**Answer:** <p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Thank you so much for sharing your story with us.&nbsp;First, I want to emphasize that children cannot consent to sexual activities, regardless of how "mature" they might seem. The concept of being "mature for your age" is often used in situations where boundaries are being crossed.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">The age difference between you&mdash;starting when you were 10 and he was 13&mdash;may not seem significant to some, but during those formative years, even a few years can represent a substantial difference in maturity, understanding, and life experience. This can create a power imbalance, even if unintentional. Between ages 10-17, young people go through enormous cognitive, emotional, and social development that amplifies these differences.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">What makes this situation particularly complex is that both of you were navigating difficult traumatic experiences from a very young age. Your partner was dealing with their own significant trauma and mental health challenges. This doesn't excuse the inappropriate nature of the sexual relationship, but it adds layers to understanding the dynamics. They may have been repeating patterns from their own experiences of abuse, which is a common but harmful cycle.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">The emotional burden placed on you to manage their PTSD episodes and suicidal thoughts was not appropriate. No child should be responsible for another person's mental health or safety, especially in the way you described where you were losing sleep and sacrificing your wellbeing. This kind of emotional dependency can be extremely draining and harmful to your own development.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Your feelings that something wasn't right about the relationship are important to acknowledge. Those feelings are your internal wisdom trying to protect you. Trusting your instincts can be a crucial step in understanding your experiences and taking care of yourself. The negative impact on your mental health and development that you've identified is real and valid.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">It's crucial to understand that you are not responsible for what happened. Children are not responsible for maintaining boundaries that adults and older adolescents should be maintaining. Your participation doesn't indicate fault or responsibility - you were navigating complex situations with the tools you had available at the time, while coping with your own trauma.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Moving forward, speaking with a trusted adult&mdash;such as a parent, guardian, teacher, or school counselor&mdash;about how you're feeling could be very helpful. A trauma-informed therapist who specializes in childhood sexual trauma can provide personalized support as you work through these experiences and their impact on your development. Many survivors find that understanding what happened through a developmental and trauma-informed lens helps them develop compassion for themselves and begin healing.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Remember that healing isn't linear, and making sense of complex traumatic experiences takes time. You don't have to figure this out on your own. Reaching out for help is a sign of strength, and there are people who care about you and want to support you. Your questions and reflections show remarkable self-awareness and courage. Whatever labels you ultimately choose to understand your experiences, your feelings are valid, and you deserve to feel safe and understood as you work through these feelings. Thank you so much for trusting us with this. You are not alone.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-younger-8-years-old-another-guy-12-or-13-years-old-who-was-a-few-years-older-sexually-touched-my-private-area-and-performed-oral-acts-on-me-before-he-touched-me-each-time-he-asked-if-he-cou)

**Question:** When I was younger (8 years old) another guy (12 or 13 years old) who was a few years older sexually touched my private area and performed oral acts on me. Before he touched me each time he asked if he could and I just sat there and shook my head yes because of how shocked I was by his question. Is that considered molestation or nothing really?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for reaching out to us. I'm sorry that you went through that situation. To answer your question, what you've described is a form of <a href="https://www.ourwave.org/post/child-on-child-sexual-abuse-cocsa-frequently-asked-questions-and-considerations">childhood sexual abuse</a>. When there is a significant age difference or power imbalance between children, and sexual contact occurs, this is not considered normal childhood exploration or play. Your experience of freezing and nodding yes while feeling shocked is an extremely common trauma response. Many survivors experience this "freeze" response during traumatic situations - it's a biological reaction that happens when we feel threatened and overwhelmed, not a true indication of consent.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">It's important to understand that children cannot give meaningful consent to sexual activities, even when they might nod or appear to agree. True consent requires full understanding, equal power, and the genuine ability to say no without fear or pressure. Your body's response of freezing and nodding while feeling shocked internally shows a disconnect between what you felt able to express and what you were actually feeling.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">To be clear: yes, what you described would be considered molestation. When someone engages in inappropriate touching or sexual activities with a child, it's a serious violation, regardless of whether they asked permission or whether both individuals were minors. At your young age, you were not able to give informed consent to such actions, and the older boy should not have involved you in that behavior.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">None of this was your fault. The responsibility lies entirely with the person who initiated and carried out those actions. Many survivors question whether their experiences "count" as abuse, especially if they didn't fight back or say no. This questioning is a normal part of processing traumatic experiences.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">If you're feeling confused, upset, or have lingering feelings about what happened, it might be helpful to talk to a trusted adult, counselor, or mental health professional who can provide support and guidance. Organizations like <a href="https://hotline.rainn.org/online?_ga=2.244507971.734159730.1741832380-101655522.1716293937">RAINN (Rape, Abuse &amp; Incest National Network)</a> offer resources and support for survivors at any stage of their healing journey. They can help you process these feelings and offer reassurance.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Please know that your experience is valid, and the confusion you might feel about it is completely understandable. Healing from childhood sexual experiences often involves coming to terms with these complicated feelings. Remember that healing is possible, you're not alone, and there are people who care about you and want to help. Please be gentle with yourself as you navigate through these feelings. Thank you for trusting us with them.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/around-age-7-my-cousin-a-girl-7-months-younger-than-me-exposed-me-to-pornography-i-cant-tell-if-that-would-be-considered-child-on-child-sexual-abuse-cocsa-or-normal-childhood-behavior-my-experiences-w)

**Question:** Around age 7, my cousin (a girl 7 months younger than me) exposed me to pornography. I can't tell if that would be considered child-on-child sexual abuse (COCSA) or 'normal' childhood behavior. My experiences worsened throughout childhood - at ages 8-9, I have memories of my older brothers molesting me through inappropriate touching. From age 9 until 21, I was groomed by predators online, which severely impacted my self-perception. I'm working with a therapist and doing art therapy to help my inner child express emotions around these experiences. What other self-healing approaches could I try at home to address this deep-rooted trauma?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for sharing your story with us. I can only imagine how difficult it must be to revisit these experiences. What you've been through is incredibly challenging, and it's completely understandable that you're finding it hard to heal from such deep-rooted trauma.<br /><br />It's important to acknowledge that the experiences you had with your cousin and brothers were not your fault. Being exposed to pornography at such a young age and experiencing unwanted touching can be confusing and distressing, especially when it involves family members. These early experiences can have profound and lasting effects on your emotional and psychological well-being.<br /><br />Your involvement with online grooming from the age of 9 to 21 adds another layer of complexity to your healing journey. The manipulation and exploitation you faced are not a reflection of who you are but rather a testament to the vulnerabilities that can arise from earlier traumatic events. It's courageous of you to recognize these patterns and to seek ways to heal.<br /><br />I'm glad to hear that you're working with a therapist and engaging in art therapy to connect with your inner child. Expressing emotions through creativity can be a powerful tool in processing trauma. In addition to what you're already doing, there are some at-home practices that might support your healing journey.<br /><br />Journaling can be a helpful way to process your thoughts and emotions. Writing down your feelings may provide clarity and insight, allowing you to reflect on your experiences in a safe space. Mindfulness and meditation practices might help ground you in the present moment, reducing anxiety and helping you manage stress. Simple breathing exercises or guided meditations could be a gentle start.<br /><br />Incorporating physical activity into your routine can also be beneficial. Activities like walking, yoga, or dance can improve your mood and help you reconnect with your body in a positive way. Establishing a daily routine that includes self-care activities can create a sense of stability and control, reinforcing your commitment to your well-being.<br /><br />Connecting with supportive people, whether trusted friends or support groups, may provide additional emotional support. Sharing your experiences with others who have faced similar challenges can help reduce feelings of isolation and remind you that you're not alone. There are also books and online resources about healing from trauma that might offer new strategies and perspectives.<br /><br />Above all, practicing self-compassion is essential. Be gentle with yourself, and acknowledge that healing takes time. It's okay to have difficult days. Celebrate small victories, and recognize the strength it takes to confront these experiences and seek out ways to heal.<br /><br />Remember, healing is not a linear process, and it's perfectly okay to take things one step at a time. You've already shown great courage in addressing these experiences and actively engaging in your healing journey. You are not alone, and there are people and resources ready to support you every step of the way. Thank you again for reaching out and sharing your story.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/when-i-was-young-my-older-sister-engaged-in-inappropriate-sexual-contact-with-me-including-making-me-perform-oral-sex-on-her-i-didnt-understand-what-was-happening-at-the-time-but-it-sparked-a-chain-of)

**Question:** When I was young, my older sister engaged in inappropriate sexual contact with me, including making me perform oral sex on her. I didn't understand what was happening at the time, but it sparked a chain of events including an addiction to explicit videos that I'm still struggling with in my 20s. I think she believes I don't remember the incident. I understand the situation is complicated because she was also young, but I can't help feeling angry. I've discussed this in therapy, but I'm having trouble moving past it. What can I do?

**Answer:** <p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">First and foremost, I want to acknowledge your courage in sharing this experience and seeking help. What happened to you was not your fault, regardless of your sister's age at the time. Your feelings of anger are completely valid and a natural response to a boundary violation that occurred during your formative years.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Sexual behaviors between siblings or children with significant age differences often involve power imbalances that can be harmful, even when the older child may not fully understand the impact of their actions. Both you and your sister were children, which creates a complex dynamic where she may have been both an agent of harm while potentially dealing with her own developmental issues or possible exposure to inappropriate sexual content.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">The connection you've made between this early experience and your current struggles with video addiction makes sense from a trauma perspective. Many survivors develop coping mechanisms that can evolve into addictive patterns. This is a common response as the brain attempts to manage distressing emotions, seek comfort, or recreate a sense of control. Early exposure to sexual content can significantly impact how you relate to intimacy, trust, and even your relationship with yourself moving forward.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Working through childhood sexual trauma is rarely a linear process, and "letting go" isn't necessarily the goal. Rather, integrating these experiences into your life narrative in a way that diminishes their power over your present is often more realistic. Your continued work in therapy is an important step. Consider discussing with your therapist whether specialized approaches like <a href="https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/therapy-types/trauma-focused-cognitive-behavior-therapy?msockid=1103576693d9679310b645d892bb66b2">trauma-focused cognitive behavioral therapy</a> (TF-CBT) or <a href="https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/22641-emdr-therapy">EMDR </a>might be helpful for processing these specific memories and addressing both the trauma and addiction components of your experience.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Navigating the mix of emotions&mdash;anger, confusion, hurt, and perhaps even guilt&mdash;is incredibly challenging. These feelings may fluctuate in intensity over time, and that's a normal part of the healing journey. Being patient and compassionate with yourself during this process is essential.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Regarding your sister, you have several options to consider. Some survivors find it healing to address the past directly with the person who harmed them, while others find closure without confrontation. If you do choose to speak with her, preparation with your therapist can help establish boundaries and manage expectations. Remember that her response&mdash;whether acknowledgment, denial, or her own distress&mdash;is not a measure of your experience's validity.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">For your video addiction, seeking specialized support, possibly through a certified sex addiction therapist or support groups, might offer additional tools beyond your current therapy. Support groups&mdash;either in person or online&mdash;can provide a space to connect with others who have faced similar challenges, reducing feelings of isolation and offering practical coping strategies.</p>
<p class="whitespace-pre-wrap break-words">Above all, remember that you're not alone in this journey. Healing from childhood sexual experiences takes time and isn't about forgetting or minimizing what happened, but rather about reducing its impact on your daily life and relationships. Thank you for trusting us with this. We appreciate you.&nbsp;</p>
