Safety plans look different for everyone, and you decide what pieces are useful to you and your situation.</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Living in an unhealthy environment is stressful and predicting when conflict or potential danger might erupt is difficult. In moments of tension, it&rsquo;s hard to think clearly and know how to respond. Developing a safety plan helps you think through and plan for scenarios before they happen.</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">An exit plan can be critical for situations that might become dangerous. It can be helpful to think through belongings you would need to quickly leave, what door is easiest to access, who you will contact after leaving, and where you can go for safety.&nbsp;</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Safe spaces could include the homes of nearby friends or family, a public space that is well populated and where you feel safe, or a housing shelter.&nbsp;</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Safety plans can also be used to meet psychological and emotional needs. If it&rsquo;s not possible or necessary to physically exit your environment, finding a safe and private space within your living space or nearby can be of equal importance for your mental wellbeing.&nbsp;</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">You don&rsquo;t need to have a physical copy of your plan, especially if it would be a risk if found. Mentally planning is just as valid and useful. If you do write it out, try to keep it in a private place or in a password protected note, where it&rsquo;s less likely to be found by others.</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Safety planning is a tool that can help minimize risk, but remember that you are </span><strong>never</strong><span style="font-weight: 400;"> responsible for someone else&rsquo;s violent or abusive actions.&nbsp;</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">When you are unable to leave an unhealthy living situation, it can feel like you&rsquo;re responsible for managing the emotions and behaviors of those around you. It&rsquo;s easy to lose sight of your own needs and worth when your environment does not reflect back how important you truly are.</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Your thoughts and feelings matter. You matter. You are </span><strong>never</strong><span style="font-weight: 400;"> responsible for the hurtful actions of those around you. You are worthy of feeling safe and loved. </span><a href="https://ncadv.org/get-help"><span style="font-weight: 400;">https://ncadv.org/get-help</span></a></p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/how-can-i-deal-with-bad-days-when-i-am-healing-from-trauma-45)

**Question:** How can I deal with “bad days” when I am healing from trauma?

**Answer:** <p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Healing from trauma is not a linear process, and it&rsquo;s common and completely normal to have days that feel a lot worse than others. Having a bad day does not mean you are not improving. It does not mean that you are getting worse.&nbsp;</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">If you take a step back and think about where you were when you first began your healing process, it can help you see the larger picture and find the improvements you have made and the growth you&rsquo;ve experienced.</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Throughout recovery, it&rsquo;s important to be kind to yourself. Some days will be easier than others and some days will be harder. You do not have to hold yourself to the same standards and expectations as your best day. </span><span style="font-weight: 400;">It is okay to be gentle with yourself, to prioritize your mental well-being, and to take it easy&mdash;if you are able.</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Try to engage in an activity that you enjoy or that has helped you feel better in the past when you&rsquo;ve had a difficult day. This could be a calm or relaxing activity like reading a book or watching a tv show, it could also be an opportunity to express how you are feeling through art or writing.&nbsp;</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">If talking to other people is your preferred way to relax and feel better, reach out to a friend, partner, or someone who you trust. It is okay to reach out for help on a difficult day.&nbsp;</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Even years into recovery, it&rsquo;s normal to have a bad day pop up unexpectedly now and again. Sometimes a triggering experience can set off a bad day, but other times they just appear out of the blue.&nbsp;</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">However, the further you get into your recovery journey, the stronger you become at recognizing the signs that a difficult day is happening. You become more resilient and able to cope with the feelings and challenges that accompany a bad trauma day.</span></p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/since-my-attack-ive-had-sex-however-i-cant-seem-to-feelconnected-its-like-im-just-going-through-the-motions-im-afraid-to-open-up-emotionally-to-anyone-and-im-not-sure-how-to-get-past-that-44)

**Question:** Since my attack, I’ve had sex. However, I can’t seem to feel…connected. It’s like I’m just going through the motions. I’m afraid to open up emotionally to anyone, and I’m not sure how to get past that.

**Answer:** <p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Trauma and violence can affect the way you experience sexual intimacy in a multitude of ways. While it may feel different or overwhelming to be with someone sexually in the beginning, it is possible to enjoy sexual intimacy again one day.&nbsp;</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">After experiencing sexual violence, many survivors find that sexual situations make them feel afraid or anxious even when they&rsquo;re with someone they trust. Some survivors experience distressing flashbacks or memories during sex, which can make it hard to relax and feel safe.&nbsp;</span><span style="font-weight: 400;">Other common experiences include feeling disconnected from your body, losing interest, or feelings of guilt &amp; dread. Some people even report hypersexuality after traumatic experiences. It can be hard to feel fully present sexually when you&rsquo;re coping with these challenges.&nbsp;</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">If you encounter any of these things, know that you aren&rsquo;t alone. Over 60% of survivors of sexual assault say they experience some form of difficulty with sexual intimacy during recovery.&nbsp;</span><span style="font-weight: 400;">Sometimes sexual difficulties are related to psychological symptoms of post-traumatic stress, depression, or anxiety--and working through those underlying issues can be helpful. Learning to set boundaries and safely explore pleasure on your own can also increase feelings of control.&nbsp;</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">If you notice yourself getting disconnected during a sexual encounter, remind yourself that you are safe. Practice a grounding activity to get yourself back into your body. For example, counting similar objects in a room, or silently name five things you can see, four things you can touch, three things you can hear, two things you can smell, and one thing you can taste. Tell your partner you would like to take a break if you notice you are feeling disconnected. Get yourself a drink of water. You do not need to continue on with a sexual encounter if it is no longer serving you.&nbsp;</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">You might find that sexual intimacy looks different for you after violence, and that&rsquo;s okay. It is okay to change your sexual boundaries &amp; what things you are &amp; aren&rsquo;t comfortable with. </span><span style="font-weight: 400;">Research has shown that having conversations about boundaries with a sexual partner can build trust, feelings of safety, &amp; improve communication after experiencing sexual violence. While it can be helpful to disclose your trauma to a partner, remember that it is your choice and only you know what&rsquo;s best for you and your situation.</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Your safety &amp; comfort matters. You are allowed to advocate for your needs, set boundaries, and move at a pace that feels right for you. You may also want to explore intimacy in ways other than sex, while you work on feeling comfortable in sexual scenerios again. Sometimes we are just not ready to experience sexual intimacy in a safe and healthy way yet. And that is okay. It is also important to note that if you don&rsquo;t have issues with sexual intimacy, it does not mean what happened to you wasn&rsquo;t serious. You deserve to be in a trusting, healthy relationship.</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Navigating sexual intimacy after violence is challenging and sometimes it feels like it will never get better. Healing takes time, but it is possible. Just because something is difficult </span><em><span style="font-weight: 400;">now</span></em><span style="font-weight: 400;"> does not mean it will be difficult forever. Be patient and kind with yourself. We are here for you.</span></p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-know-someone-who-is-physically-abused-by-her-husband-but-she-wont-ask-for-help-or-file-a-complaint-she-is-scared-because-she-has-2-small-children-and-is-also-not-independent-financially-how-can-i-help-her-43)

**Question:** I know someone who is physically abused by her husband, but she won't ask for help or file a complaint. She is scared because she has 2 small children and is also not independent financially. How can I help her?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for asking this question. It can be extremely challenging to witness someone in your life struggling with intimate partner or domestic violence, particularly if they have financial or family constraints that makes leaving the situation challenging. While it is important to respect their choice to stay, there are many things you can do to support them.</p>
<p>First, recognize that it is not uncommon for someone in an abusive relationship to not feel that leaving that situation is an option for them. This can be due to fears of further violence, worries over how this could affect others in their life, or grief over the loss of what they feel is an important relationship. They may also believe that the abuse is their fault or hope that the abuser will change. On average, it takes someone 7 attempts to fully leave an abusive relationship so be patient and recognize that this is a journey.</p>
<p>It&rsquo;s sometimes most helpful to tell the person you love in an abusive relationship that you&rsquo;re concerned about them and make sure they know help is available for them when they&rsquo;re ready. Reassure them that they do not deserve to be treated in this way and try to stay in their life as much as you can. You can also help them build and maintain a social network outside of their relationship. This will help pull them out of isolation (where abuse can thrive) and provide them with additional positive, supportive networks in their life.</p>
<p>Let them know that there are services to support them. The National Domestic Violence Hotline has call, text and chat options 24/7 which you can find here: <a href="https://www.thehotline.org/get-help/">Get Help | The National Domestic Violence Hotline (thehotline.org)</a>. Be a person they can reach out to for emotional support or if they ever need resources or services. You may even offer to go with them if they want to talk to a counselor, or other family members. If they have to go to the police, court, or a lawyer, you may also offer to go along for moral support.</p>
<p>You can also encourage them to create a safety plan whether they choose to ultimately stay or leave. A safety plan is a set of actions that can help lower their risk of being hurt by their partner. It includes information specific to them and their life that will increase their safety at school, home, and other places that they go on a daily basis. The National Domestic Violence hotline has a great online tool to do this that you can use here: <a href="https://www.thehotline.org/plan-for-safety/">Relationship Abuse Safety Planning | National Domestic Violence Hotline (thehotline.org)</a>.</p>
<p>Remember that leaving an abusive relationship or seeking help are major steps. If the person you love makes the decision to stay in their relationship &ndash; continue to offer them support. Don&rsquo;t deny them help or encouragement for not taking steps that you believe are correct. While it is so challenging to witness someone you love in potential danger, accept that you cannot &ldquo;rescue&rdquo; them. Although it is difficult to see someone you care about get hurt, ultimately the person getting hurt must be the one to decide that they want to do something about it.</p>
<p>You are not alone and also deserve support. You can also reach out to the resources provided above to get more tailored advice or answers to more specific questions. Take care of yourself. You may feel frustration, sadness, hopeless, guilt, or helplessness witnessing the struggle of others. Remember, we support others best when we are healthy, rested, and well.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/what-is-the-cycle-of-abuse-and-how-can-this-cycle-be-broken-41)

**Question:** What is the cycle of abuse and how can this cycle be broken?

**Answer:** <p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Statistically, those who are abused as a child are more likely to grow up to have abusive relationships either where they are revictimized or become abusive themselves. This pattern is known as the </span><em><span style="font-weight: 400;">cycle of abuse</span></em><span style="font-weight: 400;">. Just because this is a common cycle, however, does not mean we cannot break it.</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">There are a variety of factors that can lead to the cycle of abuse including psychological changes related to anger, trust, control, and insecurity. When abuse is all the survivor has known, it is difficult to understand and realize that healthy relationships are possible.</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Survivors may not recognize what healthy relationships look like or they may mistakenly believe that they do not deserve &ldquo;real love.&rdquo; This can cause them to lash out and harm others, or be manipulated by others who want to cause them further harm.</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">For some, harming others may be related to attempting to &ldquo;undo&rdquo; their past trauma by being the one in power and regaining control. However, this is a futile process and often only creates more pain for those involved.</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">This answer is not meant to justify abuse or to blame survivors for what they have experienced. While we can understand why the cycle of abuse occurs, we cannot harm others just because someone harmed us.&nbsp;</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Breaking the cycle of abuse is a challenging topic. As a survivor it is important to recognize signs of cycle abuse, become aware of your risk factors, learn how to identify and manage your emotions, and recognize that healing takes time.</span></p>
<p>&nbsp;</p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Studies show that enhancing safe, stable, and nurturing relationships with intimate partners and with children during early adulthood can decrease the odds that a survivor of sexual assault will become a perpetrator themselves. Work towards developing those healthy relationships once ready.</span></p>
<p>&nbsp;</p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">It is important to acknowledge that while 12% of men who were sexually abused in childhood went on to commit sexual offences, being sexually abused does not </span><em><span style="font-weight: 400;">cause </span></em><span style="font-weight: 400;">someone to become a perpetrator. The majority of boys who are sexually abused do not go on to commit abuse.</span></p>
<p>&nbsp;</p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">We are not responsible for what happened to us, but we are responsible for our own healing. In order to break a cycle, we must recognize it first.&nbsp;</span></p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/how-do-i-make-this-discomfort-and-confusion-to-go-away-39)

**Question:** How do I make this discomfort and confusion to go away?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for asking this question and we are sorry you are dealing with so much discomfort and confusion. Those are some really challenging feelings to work through, but you are not alone.&nbsp;</p>
<p>We want to start by saying that it is perfectly normal to feel discomfort and confusion after experiencing sexual violence. These feelings do not need to last forever, however, and will decrease over time.</p>
<p>Let's start by addressing why you may feel confusion. <strong>When you go through a traumatizing situation, it can be difficult to label what occurred. You might compare your experiences to the experiences you see on TV or read about online, causing you to question whether or not your experience &ldquo;counts&rdquo; as abuse. You also may struggle to remember what happened to you due to the way traumatic memories are stored in the brain, particularly if you were a child and did not have the language to name what was happening to you. </strong><strong>Only you can define your experience, but honor the feelings you are feeling. Sexual abuse can take many different forms, but one thing remains the same: it&rsquo;s never your fault.</strong></p>
<p><strong>Let's now address why you might feel discomfort. Trauma reactions vary by person. Everyone has different thresholds for stress in their lives and has differing reactions and needs as a result. People can respond to trauma in many ways. Some emotional responses you may experience include numbness, mood swings, anxiety, fear, guilt, shame, withdrawing from others, feeling sad or hopeless and even self-blame. Trauma can also manifest as physical symptoms such as insomnia, fatigue, difficulty concentrating, racing heartbeat, and being startled easy. Remember, just because you are experiencing symptoms now, does not mean they will last forever. Typically, symptoms get better with time, although it is common for triggering events to occasionally resurface some of our traumatic feelings. If the intensity of these feelings interferes with your ability to do daily tasks, consider consulting a healthcare professional. Your responses to trauma are normal reactions to abnormal events. Resist the urge to be hard on yourself or compare your experiences to others. Remember that all experiences are valid and it is important to seek help if you need it regardless of the circumstances. You do not need to go through this alone.</strong></p>
<p>One way to manage the discomfort and confusion you are feeling could be learning how to manage the triggers you experience in every day life. Triggers are emotional reactions produced by the part of our brain called the amygdala. Triggers are personal and unique to the individual experiencing them. Our triggers connect to stored memories or feelings. Some triggers can be more obviously linked to traumas from the past such as facing rejection, feeling neglected, someone showing up late, raising their voice, grabbing your arm etc. Other times, however, it can be a taste, smell, or other sensory input that triggers a memory.</p>
<p>If we know our triggers and understand where they come from, we can prepare ourselves and look out for situations that may cause our bodies to go into fight or flight. The event isn't what causes the intense reaction-- the emotions attached to the trigger inspire a reaction. Start by noticing what your body does when it begins to feel triggered. If you can recognize signs of distress in yourself, you may be able to mitigate that response.</p>
<p>You can also begin to identify your triggers by making a list of external events that bring an intense, emotional response that feels out of proportion when looking objectively at the event. Identify the trigger, take space from the trigger, and develop strategies to shift your perspective and find peace (taking a walk, drinking tea, taking deep breaths, etc). Entering back into a state of clarity and balance will enhance your abilities to respond to an event how you choose. Remember: learning to manage triggers takes time, practice, and patience. Be gentle with yourself.</p>
<p>While there is no simple answer to this question, here are some other things you may try. First, safety and security is an important foundation to trauma recovery. If you are able, separate yourself from locations or people that trigger painful memories or perpetuate abuse. Establish a safe and nurturing environment to provide a strong foundation and minimize harm that can occur when you do not have a safe place to heal. Next, build your support network. This can be people in your personal life, trusted professionals, or a community of survivors. Processing trauma can be challenging at times, and is important to have people to fall back on to encourage you to keep going even when things get tough. When you feel ready to tell your story, it may be time to disclose and process your traumatic experience. We suggest doing this with a trained professional, but you can also seek out educational materials on your own, engage in journaling or other self-reflection activities, or share your experience with a trusted friend or family member.</p>
<p>Finally, look inwards with curiosity to reconnect to your true self as you find things that make you feel capable, hopeful, and powerful. While your trauma may always be a part of you, as you find more things that hold meaning to you, it will slowly become less central. Healing is an active process. It may be painful at points and you may have setbacks, but it is possible. Set goals for yourself, acknowledge small victories, and let people in. You deserve so much more than what you have experienced. Thank you for reaching out to us. We are here for you always.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-was-harmed-by-someone-of-my-own-gender-and-now-i-identify-as-lgbtq-does-this-mean-my-experience-made-me-gay-40)

**Question:** I was sexually harmed by someone of my own gender and now I identify as LGBTQ+, does this mean my experience made me gay?

**Answer:** <p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Abuse can disrupt our sense of identity, the relationships we have, and how we feel about our sexuality, particularly when the person who harmed us is of the same gender. Despite this, while your sexual preferences may be influenced by your traumatic experiences, being harmed by someone of the same gender cannot &ldquo;make&rdquo; you gay, bisexual, or transgender. Abuse interrupts our natural development and understanding of ourselves. Therefore it is not uncommon to feel confused about sex and sexuality after experiencing sexual harm.</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">It is important to note that there are good reasons for this confusion outside of the survivor experience. Research has unfortunately shown that </span><span style="font-weight: 400;">LGBTQ+ youth are more likely than their gender-conforming, heterosexual peers to experience childhood abuse. </span><span style="font-weight: 400;">This is commonly attributed to, however, the fact that being a part of a marginalized group in general makes you more likely to be targeted by predatory individuals. This does not mean that these experiences &ldquo;change&rdquo; you from straight to gay, or from cisgender to transgender.&nbsp;</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Laura McGuire, </span><span style="font-weight: 400;">certified sexologist, scholar, and subject matter expert, states: &ldquo;</span><span style="font-weight: 400;">Abuse cannot make you into something you weren&rsquo;t born to be. Yes, abuse confuses everything and can make us question who we are and what we want, but it doesn&rsquo;t change the core of who we are. Being LGBTQ+ is just as natural, normal, and healthy as being cisgender and heterosexual. Live your truth, fight to overcome the world&rsquo;s biases, and help continue to educate your colleagues and peers so that this myth can be eradicated, once and for all.&rdquo;</span></p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/every-year-on-the-anniversary-of-my-trauma-i-feel-a-fresh-pain-deep-down-im-even-triggered-by-holiday-preparations-certain-drinks-and-other-things-that-remind-me-of-the-season-how-can-i-best-support-myself-on-an-anniversary-i-dont-want-to-remember-38)

**Question:** Every year on the anniversary of my trauma, I feel a fresh pain deep down. I’m even triggered by holiday preparations, certain drinks, and other things that remind me of the season. How can I best support myself on an anniversary I don’t want to remember?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you so much for asking this really important question.&nbsp;</p>
<p>Trauma anniversaries can be extremely painful. Triggers may come seemingly out of no where leading up to an anniversary of an event. This can be frightening, frustrating, or just plain inconvenient.&nbsp;</p>
<p>Anniversary reactions occur because of the way a traumatic experience is saved in memory and stored in our bodies and minds. Memories of trauma contain sensory information relating to the danger that the event involved. Such memories may produce strong feelings as well as bodily reactions around the anniversary of an event (even unconsciously) and a psychological reaction&mdash;like a spike in <a class="basics-link" title="Psychology Today looks at depression" href="https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/depression" hreflang="en">depression</a>&nbsp;or&nbsp;<a class="basics-link" title="Psychology Today looks at PTSD" href="https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/post-traumatic-stress-disorder" hreflang="en">PTSD</a>&mdash;can be triggered by things such as weather, lighting, certain smells, or other seasonal reminders (e.g. back to school, the first signs of winter). While biologically this type of reaction is meant to be adaptive, as our body is trying to synthesize information and keep us safe, it can be challenging and confusing to work through now that we are no longer in danger.</p>
<p>You are not alone. Many struggle with similar things leading up to anniversaries of traumatic experiences. For example, <a href="http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/10401455">a study</a> conducted by the Yale University School of Medicine looked at anniversary reactions in veterans of the early 1990s Gulf War.&nbsp;The result? Anniversary reactions are real and occur in survivors of many different types of traumatic experiences. Some common symptoms reported in this particular study included: unwanted memories, getting easily startled by triggering stimuli, heightened avoidance of trauma-related stimuli, emotional distance, irritability, and disturbed sleep.</p>
<p>While you are certainly not alone, that does not make the challenges you face any less real or painful. So how can you handle your own anniversary reaction? <strong>Ellen Hendriksen, Ph.D.</strong>, a psychologist at Boston University's Center for Anxiety and Related Disorders, and our team recommend the following.&nbsp;</p>
<p><strong>1. Prepare</strong></p>
<p>The first year after an event, it&rsquo;s hard to know what will happen. But if you sense you might be vulnerable, or you&rsquo;ve experienced an anniversary reaction before, do your best to plan ahead. Eliminate extra stressors&mdash;try not to move or change jobs around that time of year&mdash;and load up on seeing supportive friends and family. If you see a therapist, arrange to see them more frequently for as long as you need.</p>
<p><strong>2. Commemorate</strong></p>
<p>In addition to delaying stress and upping support, consider making a specific plan that relates directly to your loss or trauma. Get together with other survivors, do a ritual that honors the event, make a donation to a related non-profit, or sign up for an associated charity event. You might feel empowered, liberated, and connected to others who have had similar experiences to you.</p>
<p><strong>3. Remember that it&rsquo;s temporary</strong></p>
<p>Anniversary reactions usually subside within a few weeks. For some, anniversary distress can last as long as a season, but most have a tough couple of weeks and then come out the other side noticeably lighter. Knowing there&rsquo;s a light at the end can make the tunnel less frightening.</p>
<p><strong>4. Find support.</strong></p>
<p>If you weren&rsquo;t fortunate enough to get help after the original trauma, or the help you got didn&rsquo;t go so well, it&rsquo;s common to feel frustrated or ashamed that you&rsquo;re still dealing with your trauma years later. But it&rsquo;s never too late to start looking for support. Shop around for a therapist or counselor that you like and trust. There are proven <a class="basics-link" title="Psychology Today looks at therapy" href="https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/therapy" hreflang="en">therapy</a>&nbsp;techniques out there and medications that can banish nightmares and soothe other symptoms. With a skilled professional, some courage, and some hard work, your latest anniversary reaction could be your last.</p>
<p><strong>5. Be gentle with yourself.&nbsp;</strong></p>
<p>When we experience feelings and symptoms related to our trauma years later, it is easy to get discouraged, frustrated, and down on ourselves. Remember that your body and mind are trying to be helpful, even if they are causing you challenges in this moment. Speak to yourself kindly almost as you would speak to a small child. Try to increase the things you do during your anniversary season that make you feel good about yourself. One strategy may be to create a "crisis kit" where you put in a box things that bring you joy, soothe you, or stimulate positive memories. You can access this kit when times are particuarly challenging.&nbsp;</p>
<p>Overall, remember that healing is possible. I know it may be tough to imagine now, but overtime, your traumatic reactions during your anniversary period will decrease. We are sending you love and light as you prepare for this upcoming challenging season. You have all the tools within you to get through it, but you do not have to get through it all on your own.</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/i-dont-want-to-believe-i-was-assaulted-as-a-young-child-because-i-know-this-person-very-well-and-cant-see-it-in-them-to-act-this-way-but-i-can-recall-details-and-have-vivid-flashbacks-what-should-i-do-how-do-i-seek-help-for-this-37)

**Question:** I don't want to believe I was assaulted as a young child because I know this person very well and can't see it in them to act this way but I can recall details and have vivid flashbacks. What should I do? How do I seek help for this?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you so much for trusting us with this question.</p>
<p>To start, you are not alone. It is quite common for individuals who are processing childhood trauma in adulthood to feel confused, frightened, and frustrated. It is also common for survivors to not want to believe what happened to them was real. Only you know what you have experienced, but here are some of our thoughts below.&nbsp;</p>
<p>It is unfortuntely very common for individuals to know the person who harmed them as children. In fact, around 90% of childhood sexual abuse survivors were harmed by someone they knew, loved, or trusted. Often, these people are people who on the surface might not stand out as an abuser or predator. Many may be well liked or respected in their social circles-- that's often how they gain access to groom or harm young people.</p>
<p>Grooming is when someone builds a relationship, trust, or emotional connection with a young person or their family so that they can manipulate, exploit, and abuse them. Signs of grooming often include:</p>
<p>1. Targeting the young person</p>
<p>2. Building trust with the young person and their family</p>
<p>3. Isolating the young person</p>
<p>4.&nbsp; Initiating sexual abuse and securing secrecy&nbsp;</p>
<p>If this sounds like what you experienced, then it would make sense why you feel so confused. Remember no matter what happened, it is never your fault. Honor the feelings you are having.</p>
<p>It is also not uncommon for memories of childhood trauma to emerge in adulthood and for you to want to seek help for this experience now. In fact, around 70% of child sexual abuse cases are thought to be disclosed during adulthood. Survivors of childhood sexual abuse often have difficulties speaking up about their experience and remembering exact details. Sometimes &lsquo;forgetting&rsquo; becomes your brain&rsquo;s way of surviving and coping.</p>
<p>If you are now remembering details or having vivid memories, it might make sense to try to work through those with a trained mental health professional. You will want to find a therapist who specifically specializes in trauma or posttraumatic stress disorder. You could also reach out to your local rape crisis center. These centers are typically free of charge and can direct you to resources to support you. Once you have some more answers, you can then decide what you would like to do next.&nbsp;</p>
<p>If you have additional questions or want to begin talking through your experience now, you can reach out to the National Sexual Assault Hotline which is available 24 hours a day, 7 days a week: <a href="https://hotline.rainn.org/online">Terms of Service - Online Hotline (rainn.org) . </a>&nbsp;They will listen to what you have to say and will direct you to resources in your local area.</p>
<p>It can be very disorienting to process a traumatic experience that happened when you were a child. While the feelings that you are having are not uncommon, it does not make them any less painful. Trust your gut and honor all the emotions that come along the way. Recognize you have a community of survivors behind you. You are not alone.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/how-can-i-be-in-a-functional-relationship-after-experiencing-trauma-why-do-i-feel-so-ashamed-will-i-always-feel-this-way-36)

**Question:** How can I be in a functional relationship after experiencing trauma? Why do I feel so ashamed? Will I always feel this way?

**Answer:** <p>The trauma from previous experiences can make it extremely difficult to enter new relationships or to connect with someone new. It is not easy to be in a "functional" relationship when your trust has been broken or abused in the past. When you are healing from trauma, honor your feelings. It is okay to slowly build trust and have the people in your life earn it. Be kind and patient with yourself.&nbsp;</p>
<p>The first step to forming new relationships is assessing whether or not your future partner is worthy of your trust.&nbsp; Ask: Do they continuously do untrustworthy or hurtful things in your relationship? Do they have a pattern of acting in a way that causes you to question their character? If yes, consider whether that person is someone you should be working to trust. Seek an objective perspective from a loved one if you are having a hard time discerning if someone brings toxicity into your life.</p>
<p>If you are in a relationship currently, but it doesn't feel "functional," try to be open with your partner about how you are feeling. Giving context as to why it is difficult for you to trust within a relationship may give the other person more confidence to support you. Even a simple disclosure that "someone in the past harmed [you] causing [you] to want to take things slow" can help your new partner learn your needs. If they are not willing to respect your needs, that may be a sign that they might not be the appropriate partner for you at this time. Although another person can't complete the healing process for you, they can encourage you and assure you along the way.</p>
<p>This next part speaks to your question about shame. It is not uncommon to feel "dirty" or "ashamed" after experiencing sexual harm even though it was not your fault. You are not alone. Shame is a frequently cited emotional consequence of sexual violence, with one study noting that 75% of women sexual assault survivors indicated that they felt ashamed about themselves following their assault.</p>
<p>Shame thrives in silence. Learning about how society conditions survivors to feel ashamed can help empower you to recognize that abuse is never your fault, no matter what the circumstances. In addition, talking about your experiences and finding validation from trusted others can begin to&nbsp; decrease the shame you feel in every day life. You may want to also join groups where you can meet other survivors, as their stories and healing succuesses may give you the hope you need to keep going. Separate yourself from people who trigger your shame. You do not need them to cause you any additional pain.</p>
<p>Give yourself grace and honor your past feelings, but remind yourself that while your trauma may always be a part of you, you deserve a healthy and loving relationship external from that trauma. Face your shame head on, and surround yourself with supportive people who reinforce your worth and value. It does get better. Don't give up.&nbsp;</p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/describe-how-would-you-help-a-gender-based-violence-gbv-victim-35)

**Question:** Describe how would you help a gender-based violence (GBV) victim.

**Answer:** <p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Gender-based violence (GBV) is a global health concern and human rights issue. GBV is violence against an individual based on the individual's literal or recognized sex, gender identity, or sexual orientation. GBV is influenced by societal expectations and gendered power dynamics and can encompass such acts as intimate partner violence, sexual violence, child abuse, and stalking. GBV can take many forms, including physical, emotional, economic, and sexual abuse.</span></p>
<p>&nbsp;</p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">While anyone can experience GBV, women face extremely high rates. For example, in the United States, it is estimated that one in four women has been exposed to GBV in some form throughout their lifetime . These numbers highlight the epidemic of GBV, which dispro-portionately impacts women of color, lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, and queer or questioning (LGBTQ+) individuals, and women with disabilities.</span></p>
<p>&nbsp;</p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">When a survivor discloses a GBV experience to you, it can often be difficult to know how to respond appropriately. While every situation is different, the following are suggestions from survivors and their advocates.&nbsp;</span></p>
<p>&nbsp;</p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">First, listen. Rather than starting with doubt, start by believing. Allow the survivor to lead the conversation. Match the terminology of the survivor and do not label their experience for them. Avoid asking questions and let the survivor know you are glad that they told you. It is important to recognize that silence is okay.&nbsp;</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Next, affirm their courage and strength. Ensure that they are safe and ask &ldquo;how can I be helpful?&rdquo; Validate their feelings. Most importantly, respect the decisions the survivor makes even if you do not agree with them. Remember, this person has had their power and control taken away from them so it is important that they have control over this conversation with you.&nbsp;</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Next, refer and connect the survivor to resources. There are a variety of local and national organizations devoted to comprehensive GBV crisis intervention, advocacy, and support. You are not expected to be an expert in this topic, but directing survivors to people who are will help them get continued care and allow them to receive tailored information for their needs.</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Finally, be gentle with yourself. You may feel anger towards the situation or towards the perpetrator. You may feel helpless that you can't relieve the survivor of their suffering. You may feel guilt that you didn&rsquo;t notice the situation sooner. You may even worry that you did not say the right things. Know your feelings are valid and if you need to seek help for these feelings too that is also okay. Just by listening and being there, you are doing enough.</span></p>
<p>&nbsp;</p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Beyond helping an individual survivor, you could also v</span><span style="font-weight: 400;">olunteer your time or donate your money to an organization that is fighting to end GBV and supports survivors. If there is no one in your community advocating for GBV survivors, be the first one. You can volunteer at a community rape crisis center or domestic violence shelter in your area. Most organizations that are working to address GBV rely on volunteers and funding to be efficient.&nbsp;</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Working to end GBV and support survivors is a marathon, not a sprint. It is a process of growing ourselves, challenging our culture, and fighting for justice. You can be a part of the solution. Your action can make a difference.</span></p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/it-is-hard-to-find-much-about-male-survivors-even-harder-to-find-information-about-male-survivors-of-female-perpetrators-it-does-happen-but-it-is-such-a-forbidden-topic-that-no-one-wants-to-talk-or-even-hear-about-it-only-further-isolating-the-victim-it-is-a-lonely-place-34)

**Question:** It is hard to find much about male survivors, even harder to find information about male survivors of female perpetrators. It does happen, but it is such a forbidden topic that no one wants to talk or even hear about it, only further isolating the victim. It is a lonely place.

**Answer:** <p><span style="font-weight: 400;">You are right-- it is hard to find information about male survivors. As a survivor looking for resources online, the majority of support is geared towards women. For example, college students looking for sexual assault resources are often directed towards the school&rsquo;s Women&rsquo;s Center. This is helpful for woman-identifying survivors, but male survivors often feel left behind. Sexual assault can happen to anyone, regardless of gender and sexual orientation, and yet male survivors do not always have a specific place to go in the same way many woman survivors do. Sexual assault is a human issue, not just a women's issue. Luckily, many sexual violence organizations are beginning to realize that and change their language and programming.&nbsp;</span></p>
<p>&nbsp;</p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Although the presence of other male survivors may not be as noticeable, I promise you are not alone. </span><a href="https://www.nsvrc.org/resource/2500/national-intimate-partner-and-sexual-violence-survey-2015-data-brief-updated-release"><span style="font-weight: 400;">Nearly a quarter (24.8%) of men in the U.S. experience some form of contact sexual violence in their lifetime</span></a><span style="font-weight: 400;">. Additionally, </span><a href="https://www.rainn.org/statistics/victims-sexual-violence"><span style="font-weight: 400;">1 out of every 10 rape victims are male</span></a><span style="font-weight: 400;">. The gap in support for male survivors is largely due to outdated stereotypes and harmful attitudes related to masculinity. According to </span><a href="https://1in6.org/"><span style="font-weight: 400;">1in6</span></a><span style="font-weight: 400;">, &ldquo;The social stigma and silence around male sexual abuse and assault results in a lack of awareness and understanding about the effects of these experiences, and what men need to move forward.&rdquo; It is important to directly acknowledge that men also experience sexual assault and need healing. Male survivors face similar emotions to woman survivors, but they often experience more stigma from others when they express them.&nbsp;</span></p>
<p>&nbsp;</p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Society tends to assume that men crave sexual experiences and that they cannot be harmed by sexual encounters. This is not true. Consent is always important for both parties involved. Yet if a woman perpetrator assaults a man, other men might tell them that they were "lucky" they had that encounter - which further contributes to those harmful stereotypes. Instances of sexual assault and abuse is often, as you stated, &ldquo;a forbidden topic&rdquo; for men which is difficult for the survivor to disclose their experiences and find healing. This creates conflicting emotions after men feel violated and perpetuates shame for wanting to express those negative emotions when society tells them they should feel positively. There are not nearly enough men who share their stories due to this.</span></p>
<p>&nbsp;</p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">In addition to the resources on the Our Wave website, </span><a href="https://malesurvivor.org/about-malesurvivor/"><span style="font-weight: 400;">MaleSurvivor</span></a><span style="font-weight: 400;">&rsquo;s platform provides a space specifically for male survivors, &ldquo;Every man who has experienced sexual assault or sexual abuse deserves access to a judgment-free space where he can heal on his own terms and without shame.&rdquo; They have discussion boards you can read to learn from others who may have had experiences like yours. If you need immediate help, free and confidential support is available 24/7 from:</span></p>
<ul>
<li style="font-weight: 400;" aria-level="1"><span style="font-weight: 400;">The </span><a href="https://1in6.org/helpline"><span style="font-weight: 400;">national chat-based helpline</span></a><span style="font-weight: 400;"> for men who were sexually abused or assaulted</span></li>
<li style="font-weight: 400;" aria-level="1"><span style="font-weight: 400;">The </span><a href="https://rainn.org/about-national-sexual-assault-telephone-hotline"><span style="font-weight: 400;">national telephone hotline</span></a><span style="font-weight: 400;"> for all survivors of sexual assault at 800-656-HOPE (4673).</span></li>
</ul>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">Additionally, we believe stories are powerful and can help survivors feel less alone. You can view </span><a href="https://1in6.org/bristlecone"><span style="font-weight: 400;">men&rsquo;s stories</span></a><span style="font-weight: 400;"> on the 1in6 website or use our filter option to specifically view male survivor stories on Our Wave&rsquo;s platform to read about other male survivor experiences.&nbsp;</span></p>
<p><span style="font-weight: 400;">The purpose of Our Wave is to amplify the stories of <strong>all</strong> surviors of sexual violence and harm. This we hope will begin to change the conversation around survivorship. Thank you for reaching out to us. You are not alone and your story matters.</span></p>

---

**Link:** [View on Our Wave](https://community.ourwave.org/answer/what-strategies-can-south-africans-put-into-place-to-prevent-gender-based-violence-30)

**Question:** What strategies can South Africans put into place to prevent gender-based violence?

**Answer:** <p>Thank you for this question! We consulted a gender-based violence expert in South Africa who recommended the following blog to answer your question:</p>
<p><a href="https://nam10.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.saferspaces.org.za%2Funderstand%2Fentry%2Fgender-based-violence-in-south-africa&amp;data=05%7C01%7Claura.sinko%40temple.edu%7C97647c50bf114701d37908da600acd63%7C716e81efb52244738e3110bd02ccf6e5%7C0%7C0%7C637927896773138449%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C3000%7C%7C%7C&amp;sdata=QcLdMnTYnSgD6Br%2B8n0fcpVMfBDxwLMiH%2FtEKkjf3wQ%3D&amp;reserved=0">https://www.saferspaces.org.za/understand/entry/gender-based-violence-in-south-africa</a></p>
<p>She also recommended some non-profit organisations in South Africa dealing with gender-based violence who you can reach out to if you want to get involved or just learn more!</p>
<p><a href="https://nam10.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fgenderjustice.org.za%2Fproject%2Fpolicy-development-advocacy%2Fstop-gender-violence-national-campaign%2F&amp;data=05%7C01%7Claura.sinko%40temple.edu%7C97647c50bf114701d37908da600acd63%7C716e81efb52244738e3110bd02ccf6e5%7C0%7C0%7C637927896773138449%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C3000%7C%7C%7C&amp;sdata=OzcjZB6b8IXdhYU3RnAxXM0wO%2BFNJITjynz9hPnPbzI%3D&amp;reserved=0"><em><span style="text-decoration: underline;"><strong>1. SONKE GENDER JUSTICE</strong></span></em></a></p>
<p><a href="https://nam10.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Frapecrisis.org.za%2Four-work%2F&amp;data=05%7C01%7Claura.sinko%40temple.edu%7C97647c50bf114701d37908da600acd63%7C716e81efb52244738e3110bd02ccf6e5%7C0%7C0%7C637927896773138449%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C3000%7C%7C%7C&amp;sdata=lWi3zKR2RAnHpP%2FLBLWTAG1ptcD%2F4TaUElI5cb%2FpX%2BQ%3D&amp;reserved=0"><em>2. RAPE CRISIS CAPE TOWN TRUST</em></a></p>
<p><a href="https://nam10.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.facebook.com%2FTearsFoundationSA%2F&amp;data=05%7C01%7Claura.sinko%40temple.edu%7C97647c50bf114701d37908da600acd63%7C716e81efb52244738e3110bd02ccf6e5%7C0%7C0%7C637927896773138449%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C3000%7C%7C%7C&amp;sdata=bpTsd2YOEmL8Okf%2F828dff%2BMGI9P%2FuY0wObMZnLOd%2FQ%3D&amp;reserved=0"><em>3. TEARS FOUNDATION</em></a></p>
